Microsoft’s custom silicon has been a string of setbacks: Maia 100 (2023) never reached high-volume production; Maia 200’s tape-out slipped to late 2024 and is “assessed a failure internally,” with software development stopped entirely; Maia 300 (2nm) is the earliest credible next attempt, “late 2027” at best. Microsoft’s fallback is IP access (excluding consumer hardware) to OpenAI’s own Broadcom-designed “Titan” ASIC line [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, SA-PAID].

Microsoft’s real disclosure strength is Fairwater, covered in depth in Ch14 and Ch18: the “Azure AI superfactory” blog links Fairwater Wisconsin and Atlanta into one logical training fabric via a dedicated “AI WAN” built on 120,000+ new fiber miles in a year, with Azure CTO Mark Russinovich on record: “The amount of infrastructure required now to train these models is not just one datacenter, not two, but multiples of that… Nobody’s really run into that problem yet because they haven’t gotten to the scale we’re at now” Microsoft, “Infinite scale: The architecture behind the Azure AI superfactory” [OFFICIAL-BLOG], Nov 2025. Each Fairwater campus pairs a ~300MW GPU building (>150k GB200 GPUs) with a 48MW CPU/storage building — a ~1:6 CPU:GPU power ratio SemiAnalysis expects to worsen in the Rubin generation [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, SA-PAID]. Fairwater 2 Atlanta’s rail-only topology — splitting each compute tray’s 3,200G into 32 planes rather than per-GPU — reaches 524,288 GPUs on a 2-layer network using 24,576 switches (same 21.3 GPU/switch ratio as Stargate’s 131,072-GPU network, at 4x the GPU count), at the cost that same-tray GPUs can no longer talk over the scale-out network and must route through NVLink, handled by Microsoft’s own MRC implementation (Ch18) [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, SA-PAID, “Microsoft’s AI Strategy Deconstructed”], Nov 2025. Satya Nadella, on Dwarkesh (Nov 2025), summarized the incremental-buildout philosophy: “you want to be scaling in time as opposed to scale once and then be stuck with it” [SPOKEN-CLAIM].

What the pattern across six labs says

Table 2.1 collects the load-bearing disclosed numbers; note how unevenly they cluster — hardware topology is disclosed far more freely than data, routing, or failure attribution.

Three patterns fall out of assembling this record end to end. First, networking is the disclosure of choice: five of six labs have put real, quantified fabric detail into the public record (MRC, Virgo, Fairwater, Llama 3’s Clos topology, Colossus’s Spectrum-X), because a fabric paper reads as an industry-collaboration story rather than a competitive-secrets leak. Second, failure attribution is almost entirely absent from official channels: where it exists at all, it comes from a single paid-newsletter analyst team working from partial access, not from any lab’s own retrospective — Meta’s Behemoth account is the only detailed technical postmortem for a frontier MoE pretrain failure in this corpus, and it is second-hand, single-sourced, and unquantified on its most load-bearing claim. Third, the compute-scale numbers that dominate headlines are increasingly unreliable as a measure of current frontier position: Epoch’s tracker has no FLOP estimate for Gemini 3 Pro, any Claude Opus 4.x model, Claude Sonnet 4/4.5, or GPT-5.1 as of its July 2026 pull, and its “Confident”-rated large-scale estimates belong not to any lab in this chapter but to DeepSeek, GLM, and Kimi (Ch3) — precisely because they publish real technical reports. The closed frontier is not only closed on data and algorithms; as of mid-2026 it is functionally closed on the compute-scale question too.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

Four disclosures here should shape planning assumptions elsewhere in this book. First, MRC’s design choices — NIC-side routing state, avoidance of a second adaptive-routing layer, static SRv6 paths — are the most concrete production answer yet to the fabric-design question the book’s own strawman (Ch16, Ch18) must resolve independently, since no framework ships this out of the box. Second, the Gemini 1.5 report’s 85%→97% goodput improvement via in-memory state replication is the cleanest existence proof that a 10T/200B run’s fault-tolerance layer (Ch19) should treat in-memory redundancy as the default, not a fallback. Third, Meta’s Behemoth postmortem is the most actionable cautionary data point this book surfaces for MoE design: do not switch routing paradigms mid-run, do not swap data-ingestion pipelines mid-run without stress-testing dedup at target scale, and build long-context/reasoning evals before locking in a local-attention-majority architecture, not after (Ch5, Ch10). Fourth, every lab here treats compute-scale disclosure as a marketing lever, not a transparency obligation — the ratio of hardware-topology disclosure to failure-mode disclosure is roughly ten to one. A team building toward 10T/200B on 100k GB200s should expect to generate its own failure taxonomy from scratch: the public record from six labs that have done this at scale amounts to one paper (Llama 3), one paid-newsletter postmortem (Behemoth), and a scattering of podcast asides — the argument for Ch4’s DeepSeek deep-dive and Ch20’s operations chapter, where the open stack and the war-story genre are what is left to learn from.

Chapter 3 · Part I — The Evidence Base

The Open Frontier: China's Labs

Ten labs shipped trillion-class or near-trillion MoE reports between mid-2025 and mid-2026, and no two disclosed the same slice of their stack. One hands you a full MFU waterfall and a closed-form scaling law; the next publishes 36 trillion tokens of curriculum detail and omits every hardware fact down to the GPU vendor. Read as a set, these reports triangulate something none states outright: what training a multi-hundred-billion-active-parameter MoE on export-controlled Hopper silicon actually costs, and which architectural bets the field has already made and unmade in public.

This chapter covers every major Chinese lab except DeepSeek, detailed enough to warrant its own chapter (Ch4) and used here only as a comparison anchor. The throughline is disclosure asymmetry: the labs that publish infrastructure detail (Moonshot, Ant, Baidu) and the labs that publish almost none (Alibaba, ByteDance’s current generation, Tencent) are not the ones you’d predict from model quality alone, and the gaps are as informative as the numbers.

Moonshot AI: Kimi K2 and the MuonClip Disclosure

Kimi K2 is a 1.04T-total / 32.6B-active MoE — 384 routed experts with 8 active per token, a 48:1 sparsity ratio, Multi-head Latent Attention with 64 heads (half of DeepSeek-V3’s 128), and 128K context via YaRN extension — trained on 15.5T tokens with, per the paper’s own claim, zero loss spikes across the full run Kimi K2: Open Agentic Intelligence [PAPER, Jul 2025]. Of every report surveyed in this chapter, K2’s is the most infrastructurally complete: H800 GPUs, 8 per node with 2TB RAM, nodes linked by 8×400Gbps RoCE; a parallelism recipe of 16-way pipeline parallelism (with virtual stages) × 16-way expert parallelism × ZeRO-1 data parallelism, with the model-parallel group spanning 256 GPUs and per-device memory for parameters/gradients/optimizer states held to roughly 30GB; a 67M-token global batch Kimi K2 §2.4.2 [PAPER, Jul 2025]. And yet the report never states an aggregate cluster size or an MFU figure — the single most-cited “transparent” Chinese infra disclosure of the period still withholds the two numbers a capacity planner would want first. Treat this as a pattern, not an oversight: see the “conspicuously undisclosed” discussion below.

The headline algorithmic contribution is MuonClip: Muon (Newton-Schulz-orthogonalized updates with RMS rescaling) combined with a post-update weight clip on the attention query/key projections, applied per head as γ_h = min(1, τ/S_max^h) with τ=100, where S_max^h is that head’s maximum attention logit Kimi K2 §2.1 [PAPER, Jul 2025]. Moonshot’s own research-diary blog — kexue.fm, run by Jianlin Su, a Moonshot researcher writing in first person about “our newly released trillion-parameter model Kimi K2” — published the QK-Clip derivation ten days before the technical report itself, adding training telemetry the paper omits: roughly 220,000 total steps, the first head crossing τ=100 around step 7,000, and a roughly 70,000-step “tug of war” between Muon (pushing MaxLogit up) and QK-Clip (pulling it down) before every head settled below threshold on its own QK-Clip / MuonClip [BLOG, Jul 2025]. Su’s structural explanation: Muon’s orthogonalized update msign(M) is exactly full-rank, while Adam’s empirical updates are low-rank; when a full-rank update’s singular vectors align with a weight matrix’s existing ones, they add constructively and the spectral norm — hence the attention logit ceiling — inflates. He also states QK-Clip was directly inspired by DeepSeek’s own auxiliary-loss-free load-balancing strategy, a documented case of technique cross-pollination via public writing rather than personnel movement kexue.fm/archives/11126 [BLOG, Jul 2025]. (Ch7 covers the general Muon-at-scale stability story; this is K2’s specific disclosure.)

Three further Moonshot data points matter for a 10T/200B planner. Activation-memory engineering stacks selective recomputation (LayerNorm/SwiGLU/MLA up-projections, MoE down-projections), FP8-E4M3 compression (1×128 tiles, FP32 scales) for MoE up-projection inputs, and CPU offload of remaining activations with copy-engine-overlapped streaming Kimi K2 §2.4.3 [PAPER, Jul 2025]. A concrete number on synthetic rephrasing versus repetition: K2’s own ablation shows 10× single-epoch LLM-rephrased knowledge data beating 10-epoch repetition of the same raw data, 28.94% vs. 23.76% SimpleQA accuracy — one of the only quantified answers in the corpus to a question every lab gestures at (Ch11 covers this in the curation context) Kimi K2 Table 1 [PAPER, Jul 2025]. And checkpoint-engine, Moonshot’s open-sourced RL weight-broadcast middleware, updates the full 1T-parameter K2 model in place across thousands of GPUs in roughly 20 seconds and underpins both K1.5 and K2 production RL — a concrete number for a problem most labs mention only qualitatively checkpoint-engine [CODE, 2025].

Moonshot’s architecture line continued past K2 with Kimi Linear (Kimi Delta Attention, KDA) — a hybrid design at a 3:1 layerwise ratio of KDA to full MLA layers, which Moonshot claims “for the first time” outperforms full attention under a fair comparison, reporting up to 75% KV-cache reduction and 6x decode throughput at 1M context, since validated to scale into K2-class models Kimi Linear: An Expressive, Efficient Attention Architecture [PAPER, Oct 2025] — the direct counter-datapoint to MiniMax’s attention retreat below, and a live, unresolved architectural fork. K2 Thinking (~Nov 2025) applied quantization-aware training to MoE weights specifically, producing native INT4 checkpoints at roughly 2x generation speed and half the FP8 variant’s memory at near-FP16 quality, a technique LMSYS independently reverse-engineered for RL rollout within about two months Squeezing 1TB Model Rollout into a Single H200 [OFFICIAL-BLOG, Jan 2026]. Kimi K3 — reportedly targeting 2.5-4T parameters, Kimi Linear attention, ~1M native context, expected Q3 2026 — remains unreleased as of this writing and is [RUMOR] beyond that framing.

Alibaba Qwen: Efficiency Claims Without Infrastructure

Qwen3’s flagship report — 235B-total/22B-active, 36T tokens across 119 languages in a three-stage curriculum (general → reasoning-emphasis → long-context), 94 layers, 128 experts with 8 active and no shared expert (a departure from both DeepSeek-V3 and Kimi K2), QK-Norm added, a 151,669-token BBPE vocabulary — discloses architecture and data curriculum in real depth, then says nothing about hardware, cluster size, parallelism, MFU, throughput, precision, optimizer, LR schedule, or batch size anywhere in the document Qwen3 Technical Report [PAPER, May 2025]. Against Ling-1T’s and Kimi K2’s infra transparency, this is a total blackout from the single most commercially deployed open-weight Chinese model family.

Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B compensates with an efficiency claim rather than an infra disclosure: hybrid attention at a 3:1 layer ratio (75% Gated DeltaNet, 25% standard gated attention), 512 total experts with 10 routed + 1 shared active (~3.7% activation, sparser than the 235B flagship’s 6.25%), and the claim that Qwen3-Next-80B-A3B-Base needed under 10% of the GPU-hours of dense Qwen3-32B — 9.3% of its compute cost — while outperforming it downstream, trained on 15T tokens sampled from the 36T Qwen3 corpus Qwen3-Next: Towards Ultimate Training & Inference Efficiency [OFFICIAL-BLOG, Sep 2025]. This directly rebuts the assumption that once a lab has settled on “MoE at 5-8% activation,” architecture is a solved axis: Alibaba claims a further order-of-magnitude efficiency step from hybrid linear attention plus deeper sparsification, resting purely on relative GPU-hour accounting with no hardware disclosure behind it. Qwen3.5 (Feb 2026, 397B-A17B flagship) and Qwen3.5-Omni (Apr 2026, hundreds-of-billions-parameter omni-modal line trained on 100M+ hours of audio-visual content) continue the pattern — parameter counts and benchmarks public, pretraining infrastructure absent — and beyond the Omni report itself Qwen3.5-Omni Technical Report [PAPER, Apr 2026] no primary source with training methodology exists for either release.

Zhipu/Z.ai: GLM-4.5’s Muon Recipe and the WSD Dissent

GLM-4.5 (355B-total/32B-active) and GLM-4.5-Air (106B/12B) trade width for depth relative to DeepSeek-V3 and Kimi K2 — a narrower 5,120 hidden dimension against 89 MoE layers and 96 attention heads, a choice the team states directly was because “deeper models exhibited better reasoning capacity” GLM-4.5: Agentic, Reasoning, and Coding (ARC) Foundation Models §2.1 [PAPER, Aug 2025]. The paper’s own comparison table places GLM-4.5 alongside DeepSeek-V3 and Kimi K2 on total params, active params, dense/MoE layer counts, and QK-Norm usage — one of the few Chinese-lab reports to formally cross-reference competitors’ architecture rather than only its own. Pretraining runs 23T tokens across four stages: 15T tokens general corpus at 4K sequence length, 7T tokens code-and-reasoning continual pretraining, then three mid-training phases (500B repo-level code at 32K, 500B synthetic reasoning at 32K, 100B long-context/agent data at 128K) GLM-4.5 §2.2-2.4 [PAPER, Aug 2025]. Notably the team explicitly did not use best-fit packing during core pretraining — finding random truncation a useful data-augmentation effect at that stage — but did apply it during mid-training to avoid truncating reasoning chains or repo-scale code, a curriculum-staged packing decision Ch12/Ch13 should weigh against.

GLM-4.5’s optimizer section is one of the most complete Muon hyperparameter recipes published by any lab: Newton-Schulz iterations N=5, momentum μ=0.95, update RMS scaled to 0.2, batch-size warmup from 16M to 64M tokens over the first 500B tokens, weight decay 0.1, no dropout, aux-loss-free routing-bias update rate 0.001 for the first 15T tokens then 0.0 GLM-4.5 §2.4 [PAPER, Aug 2025]. The team explicitly chose cosine LR decay over WSD after early experiments showed WSD-scheduled models underfit on SimpleQA and MMLU — a named data point against a scheduling trend otherwise gaining adoption in the same window (Ant’s Ling 2.0 sidesteps the question with checkpoint-merging “Warmup-Stable-Merge” instead of LR decay; MiniMax-M1 uses neither cosine nor WSD but a two-phase constant-then-decay schedule) — worth weighing against Ch6’s treatment of WSD-law practice. Separately, Su’s optimizer-adoption-guide post confirms the Moonlight-variant of Muon is now used in both Kimi K2 and GLM-4.5 — cross-lab technique adoption from Moonshot to Zhipu via public blog rather than shared codebase Muon优化器指南 [BLOG, Nov 2025]. GLM-4.5’s report gives essentially none of the hardware, cluster-size, parallelism, or MFU detail Kimi K2 and Ling-1T do; the RL infrastructure behind the GLM-4.x/5.x line, slime (Megatron-LM for training, SGLang for rollout), is separately open-sourced by THUDM but documents RL-phase engineering, not pretraining parallelism slime [CODE, 2025-2026]. GLM-4.6 extended context 128K→200K without a fresh infra disclosure, and per third-party coverage was the first Chinese flagship reported running inference on Cambricon chips at FP8+Int4 — a domestic-silicon deployment datapoint, not a training-hardware one IntuitionLabs secondary coverage [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, ~Sep 2025].

MiniMax: The Full-Attention Retreat

MiniMax-M1, shipped June 2025, was a 456B-total/45.9B-active hybrid-attention model: one softmax (transformer) block following every seven lightning-attention (transnormer) blocks, native 1M context, built via continual pretraining of MiniMax-Text-01 on 7.5T additional tokens MiniMax-M1: Scaling Test-Time Compute Efficiently with Lightning Attention [PAPER, Jun 2025]. Long-context extension was reported as genuinely dangerous for this architecture: aggressive extension caused sudden gradient explosions, attributed to earlier transformer layers lagging later layers in parameter optimization because lightning-attention layers at different depths carry different decay rates — mitigated only by a smoother four-stage extension from 32K up to 1M. On the RL side, M1 introduced CISPO (Clipped Importance-Sampling Policy Optimization), which clips the importance-sampling ratio itself rather than token-level updates, motivated by the observation that reflective tokens (“However,” “Recheck,” “Wait”) are rare and low-probability and were getting clipped out under GRPO/DAPO in the hybrid-attention setting — a 2x step-count speedup over DAPO was reported on a controlled Qwen2.5-32B-base study, and the full RL training run (512 H800 GPUs, three weeks) cost roughly $534,700 MiniMax-M1 §3 [PAPER, Jun 2025].

Roughly a year later, MiniMax-M2 reversed course entirely: 229.9B-total/9.8B-active, full multi-head attention (GQA, 48 query/8 KV heads) across all 62 layers, no hybrid or linear component anywhere, pretrained on 29.2T tokens The MiniMax-M2 Series §2 [PAPER, ~2026]. This is the load-bearing datapoint of this chapter, and MiniMax’s own accompanying blog post is unusually candid about why. On saturated benchmarks — MMLU, BBH, MATH, LongBench — the M1-era hybrid lightning attention “looked just as good as pure full attention,” but at larger scale “clear deficits in complex, multi-hop reasoning tasks” emerged, meaning the very benchmarks used to validate the architecture decision were the blind spot. M1’s RL training separately exposed “serious precision issues”: linear attention is far more sensitive to numerical precision than full attention, a direct problem for low-precision KV-cache and state-storage schemes at inference. Performance degraded noticeably as context length grew in hybrid sliding-window-attention variants, unacceptable for agentic use. Infrastructurally, linear-attention variants were frequently memory-bound even during training, leaving GPU FLOPs on the table, and the theoretical compute-favorable crossover point (a few thousand tokens) never materialized without simultaneously solving prefix caching, speculative decoding, and low-precision state storage. Finally — and this is the part with the most direct 10T/200B-planning consequence — global attention patterns (retrieval heads, induction heads) are established early in pretraining and “resist mid-training architectural changes,” making a late-stage swap to selective full attention impractical once you’ve committed Why did M2 end up as a full attention model? [OFFICIAL-BLOG, Nov 2025].

MiniMax quantified the regression rather than just asserting it, rare enough to flag on its own: on RULER-128K’s “CWE” metric, hybrid sliding-window attention scored 72.0 against full attention’s 90.0; on a translation in-context-learning benchmark (MTOB), scores roughly halved, from 60.0/44.8 to 45.0/27.2 MiniMax-M2 Series Table 2 [PAPER, ~2026]. Within 32K context the gap was small or reversed on short-horizon tasks — the failure mode is specifically long-context, multi-hop, agentic degradation, invisible on the benchmark suites that dominated 2024-2025 architecture validation. M2’s own MoE uses sigmoid gating rather than softmax top-k, dropping the zero-sum constraint across experts so several can activate confidently at once, reducing reliance on auxiliary balancing loss MiniMax-M2 Series §2 [PAPER, ~2026]. MiniMax’s own framing is worth carrying forward: “efficient” attention isn’t dead, it’s just not mature enough to be the default for a system that has to do code, math, agents, multimodality, long-chain reasoning, and RL simultaneously — direct counter-evidence to Kimi Linear’s claim of clean superiority and to Qwen3-Next’s efficiency framing (Ch5 owns the design-space synthesis; this is the empirical retreat that constrains it).

StepFun: Step-3 and the Economics of Decode

Step-3 is a 321B-parameter VLM (316B LLM + 5B vision encoder), 38B activated per token, whose real contribution is not a pretraining report but a model-system co-design paper for cheap inference, relevant here because the design choices feed back into how a future trillion-scale MoE should be architected from the start Step-3 is Large yet Affordable [PAPER, Jul 2025]. Its Multi-matrix Factorization Attention (MFA) uses 64 query heads sharing a single K/V head at dimension 256 with a low-rank-factorized query path, reaching an “effective rank” of 16,384 — matching DeepSeek-V3’s MLA and doubling Qwen3-MoE-235B’s GQA. Attention-FFN Disaggregation (AFD) physically splits attention and FFN layers onto separate GPU pools run as a three-stage pipeline targeting a 50ms time-per-output-token SLA; the headline result is 4,039 tokens/s/GPU versus DeepSeek-V3’s 2,324 under an identical setup, with DeepSeek-V3 needing 320 GPUs per decoding instance against Step-3’s 32.

The paper’s real value here is its cross-lab decode-cost comparison table — DeepSeek-V3, Kimi K2, Qwen3-MoE-235B, Qwen3-32B, Llama-4-Maverick, MiniMax-M1, ERNIE-4.5, Huawei’s Pangu Pro MoE, and Step-3 itself, normalized onto a single $/1M-decoded-tokens axis across H800/H20/A800/Ascend-910B — one of the only places this comparison exists at all Step-3 [PAPER, Jul 2025]. Two conclusions carry forward: neither total nor active parameter count reliably predicts decode cost (Qwen3-32B, smaller than DeepSeek-V3 or Step-3, has the highest decode cost among the compared models on most hardware), and attention design dominates decode cost more than FFN/MoE design, the gap widening sharply with context length. A February 2026 follow-up complicates the AFD story: extending a communication-aware roofline model, it finds a bandwidth-limited “dead zone” where scaling out FFN instances stops improving hardware utilization because throughput becomes capped by scale-out interconnect bandwidth rather than compute — AFD’s benefits are more cluster-topology-contingent than Step-3’s framing suggests Revealing the Challenges of Attention-FFN Disaggregation [PAPER, Feb 2026].

ByteDance Seed: From MegaScale to MegaScale-MoE

ByteDance’s most-cited infra number is still MegaScale — 55.2% MFU training a 175B dense model on 12,288 GPUs, a 1.34x improvement over a comparable Megatron-LM baseline (~41%) — and it is from February 2024, describing a dense architecture, not the current MoE line MegaScale: Scaling LLM Training to More Than 10,000 GPUs [PAPER, Feb 2024]. For 2025-2026’s Doubao Seed 2.0/2.1 generation, ByteDance has published essentially nothing at that level of detail — no cluster scale, no GPU generation, no MFU, no parallelism layout — despite operating one of the largest GPU fleets in China, a meaningful gap relative to Ant Group and Moonshot. Seed 2.0 Pro is reported in secondary coverage as a roughly 1T-parameter Gemini-style natively multimodal MoE (98.3 AIME 2025, 88.9 GPQA-Diamond, 76.5 SWE-bench Verified), but treat the exact architecture as [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY], since no technical report with an architecture table was located technode.com Doubao Seed 2.0 coverage [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, Feb 2026].

What partially closes this gap is a MoE-specific training-systems paper from the same lineage, published outside the Doubao release cadence: MegaScale-MoE, accepted at EuroSys ’26 MegaScale-MoE [PAPER, May 2025]. It measures a 352B MoE model at 1,440 H800 GPUs reaching 1.41M tokens/s at 27.89-32.48% MFU, 1.88x over Megatron-LM, and separately confirms production use training a proprietary 200B-total/20B-active MoE across 10,000+ GPUs for months with stable convergence, “saving millions of GPU-hours” — the closest thing to a current-generation ByteDance MoE infra disclosure in the public record, even though it isn’t badged as a Doubao/Seed release. Its two headline mechanisms: sequence parallelism (rather than tensor parallelism) for the attention/dense path, cutting communication to roughly a quarter of TP’s latency within an 8-GPU NVLink domain; and, for the FFN/expert path when top-k exceeds EP world size, an allgather + local-scatter + reduce-scatter sequence replacing standard all-to-all dispatch, matching or beating it while improving GroupedGEMM utilization — a different mechanism from both DeepSeek’s DeepEP and NVIDIA’s HybridEP (Ch15/Ch17 own the kernel-level mechanics; this is production-scale evidence it works at frontier scale). FP8 forward/backward uses per-token quantization because SwiGLU’s dynamic range breaks naive FP8; gradient reduce-scatter downcasts FP32→BF16 with FP32 local accumulation preserved.

Two smaller Seed-line points round this out. Seed-OSS-36B, a dense 36B model, trained on only 12T tokens — a third to half the 15.5-36T-token norm elsewhere in this chapter — with ByteDance framing the resulting competitive benchmark performance as a data-quality argument rather than a scale argument, worth treating skeptically absent a data-composition disclosure Seed-OSS-36B [CODE, Aug 2025]. And ByteDance’s HybridFlow RLHF framework, open-sourced as verl, has become a broad multi-organization project — adopted by the Qwen team and Shanghai AI Lab among others — reporting 1.5-20x throughput improvements over prior RLHF baselines HybridFlow [CODE, 2024].

Ant Group: Ling-1T and the Efficiency Leverage Law

Ling 2.0 is Ant Group/InclusionAI’s three-model family sharing one design — Ling-mini-2.0 (16B/1.4B), Ling-flash-2.0 (103B/6.1B), and Ling-1T (1T-total/51B-active, 80 layers, hidden 8,192) — all using 256 routed experts plus 1 shared expert with 8 active (roughly 3.5% activation), GQA, QK-Norm, and partial RoPE on only the first 64 dimensions Ling 2.0: Every Activation Boosted Table 1 [PAPER, Oct 2025]. This is, by a clear margin, the richest infrastructure disclosure surveyed in this chapter. Ant fit power-law relationships between compute budget and both optimal batch size and learning rate separately for MoE and dense architectures from roughly 1,000 experiments up to 3e20 FLOPs, finding MoE systematically prefers larger batch sizes and lower LR than dense at matched compute, attributed to sparse per-expert gradients needing larger batches for stable statistics. The “Ling Wind Tunnel” runs five standardized experiments spanning 500M-8B parameters (a 100x range), fully determined by the fitted laws, then extrapolates 100x further to predict Ling-1T’s behavior — reportedly predicting final training loss to within 0.01 at 35% of the compute cost of a naive single-scale ablation Ling 2.0 §2.3 [PAPER, Oct 2025]. This simulate-before-you-burn-GPU-hours methodology is directly reusable for a 10T/200B build’s own scaling-law program (Ch6 covers the general practice).

The centerpiece is Efficiency Leverage (EL), a closed-form scaling law defined as the compute-cost ratio for a dense model to match a given MoE configuration’s validation loss, derived from a 300+-model empirical study up to 28B parameters: EL(A, G, C) = Â^(α + γ(log G)² + β log G), where A is a saturating transform of activation ratio, G is expert granularity, and α = a + d·log(C) — meaning EL itself grows with training compute, so sparsity’s efficiency advantage compounds at larger scale rather than staying fixed Ling 2.0 §2.3.2 [PAPER, Oct 2025]; companion paper Towards Greater Leverage [PAPER, Jul 2025]. Activation ratio is the dominant driver, following a robust power law down to 1/128 activation; expert granularity has a nonlinear, largely compute-independent effect with an empirically identified optimal range of 8-12 activated experts (Ling 2.0’s choice of 8 sits inside this band); shared-expert count and layer placement are secondary. All three Ling 2.0 models’ 256-expert/8-active/3.5%-activation configuration delivers over 7x measured efficiency leverage versus a dense equivalent (Ch5 should weigh this directly against DeepSeek’s and Kimi’s own activation-ratio choices when deriving the 10T/200B strawman’s sparsity).

Infrastructure: a modified Megatron 0.11, FP8 training with native MTP support, 2,016 Hopper GPUs, TP1/EP8/PP21/VPP2, sequence length 4K, full activation recomputation. The MFU waterfall is the rarest artifact in this entire corpus — most labs report a single final number, if any:

Ling 2.0 §5, Fig. 14 [PAPER, Oct 2025]

FP8 alone contributes +15% MFU over BF16 and holds within 0.25% relative loss deviation from a BF16 baseline over a 900B-token run, tracked in real time by a “FP8 Training Safeguard System” monitoring per-layer quantization underflow and distortion as a first-class training metric (Ch8 owns precision recipes in full; this is Ling’s specific numbers). Operationally, distributed checkpoint save time was cut from 269s to 30s via a metadata cache avoiding rank-0 recomputation, and checkpoint interval is set to 48 minutes as a calculated failover-loss/overhead tradeoff (Ch19’s territory). A “Warmup-Stable-Merge” scheduler replaces LR decay with checkpoint merging entirely, reportedly delivering 1-2% average benchmark gains that persist through post-training — a third distinct answer, alongside GLM’s cosine-over-WSD and MiniMax’s two-phase schedule, to the same “how do you end training” question.

Tencent Hunyuan and the Mamba Detour

Hunyuan-Large (389B-total/52B-active, 64 layers, 16 specialized experts with top-1 routing plus 1 shared expert) trained on 7T tokens plus roughly 1.5T tokens of synthetic data, combining GQA with Cross-Layer Attention (KV cache shared every 2 layers) for a claimed ~95% KV-cache reduction versus standard MHA Hunyuan-Large [PAPER, Nov 2024]. Its one genuinely novel disclosure is expert-specific learning-rate scaling: the shared expert uses the standard optimal rate, while each specialized expert scales to roughly 0.31x that rate, justified by unequal per-iteration token load — a detail no other lab in this chapter discloses. The report gives no hardware, parallelism, or MFU figures anywhere.

Hunyuan-TurboS (560B-total/56B-active, 128 layers in an “AMF/MF” interleaved Attention-Mamba-FFN pattern, 16T-token corpus) is Tencent’s hybrid Transformer-Mamba line, motivated explicitly by pure-transformer models’ struggles with long-text O(N²) costs Hunyuan-TurboS: Mamba-Transformer Synergy [PAPER, May 2025]. Its infra opacity is unusually well-confirmed: MiniMax’s own M1 paper independently calls out Hunyuan-T1/TurboS as the one exception among reasoning models using a non-Transformer backbone, noting “this model is not open-sourced and few details are disclosed” — a competitor’s citation graph corroborating a disclosure gap MiniMax-M1 §1 [PAPER, Jun 2025]. Beyond the architecture concept, no MFU, hardware, or parallelism detail exists for TurboS anywhere.

Baidu ERNIE and PaddlePaddle’s Parallel Universe

ERNIE 4.5 is architecturally unremarkable relative to its peers — plain GQA (64/8 query/KV heads for the 300B-A47B flagship, no MLA-style latent compression), 54 layers, a heterogeneous modality-isolated MoE where text and vision tokens route through structurally distinct expert pools — but its infrastructure disclosure is the second-richest here after Ling-1T, once gap-fill research resolved an initial fetch failure against Baidu’s self-hosted PDF ERNIE 4.5 Technical Report [PAPER, Jun 2025]. ERNIE-4.5-300B-A47B was pretrained on 2,016 NVIDIA H800 GPUs with RoCE interconnect, sequence length 4,096, global batch size 15,120, reaching 47% MFU — higher than any other Chinese lab here discloses. The parallelism is 8-way expert parallelism × 12-way pipeline parallelism × ZeRO-1 data parallelism at sharding degree 168, tensor parallelism held at 1 for the text-only model, cross-verified against the open-sourced training YAML — EP=8 is deliberately sized to fit inside one 8-GPU H800 node, so the MoE all-to-all never crosses a node boundary, using plain NCCL primitives yet claimed to reach throughput “comparable to DeepEP-based MoE implementations” github.com/PaddlePaddle/ERNIE [CODE, verified Jul 2026].

Baidu trains on PaddlePaddle, not Megatron or PyTorch — the one frontier Chinese lab here not building on the Megatron lineage — but the disclosed 47%-MFU run used PaddlePaddle’s older, manual “Fleet” explicit-parallelism path, functionally equivalent to Megatron’s mpu, rather than the framework’s own GSPMD-descended Auto Parallel system. Auto Parallel is real, documented, and shipped (paddle.distributed.shard_tensor/shard_layer/process_mesh), with its own working reference implementation of the identical 300B-A47B model requiring only 56 H800 GPUs — but that implementation is explicitly annotated “more advanced optimizations are on the way,” i.e. not yet the production path at full scale github.com/PaddlePaddle/ERNIE/examples/auto_parallel/README.md [CODE, checked Jul 2026]. FP8 is explicitly modeled on DeepSeek-V3’s recipe: E4M3, block-wise weight quantization plus tile-wise activation quantization, retained FP8 activations across backward, FP8-precision first all-to-all ERNIE 4.5 Technical Report §5.3 [PAPER, Jun 2025]. Fault tolerance is framework-native: automatic recovery under 8 minutes, and a projected >98% effective training time at a hypothetical 10,000-GPU scale assuming linear failure-rate scaling (Ch19’s territory; a claim, not a measured result). Training hardware is confirmed 100% NVIDIA H800 — no Kunlun, no Ascend — despite a separate, unconnected 30,000-card Kunlunxin P800 cluster Baidu lit up two months earlier, apparently serving its inference/fine-tuning cloud business rather than pretraining ERNIE 4.5 Technical Report [PAPER, Jun 2025]. ERNIE 5.0 (Feb 2026) scaled EP from 8-way to 64-way, switched from intra-node-only NCCL EP to DeepEP for necessarily cross-node communication, and added context parallelism — but disclosed zero MFU, hardware, or cluster-size figures, a regression in infra transparency worth flagging for anyone treating 5.0 as a clean successor data point ERNIE 5.0 Technical Report [PAPER, Feb 2026].

The Master Comparison Table

Sources as cited in the preceding sections; DeepSeek-V3 row per DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report [PAPER, Dec 2024] (Ch4 detail). *DeepSeek-V3’s MFU figures are not a single disclosed number: they are backed out from the paper’s own 2.664M-GPU-hour pretraining-cost figure and its separately reported 250 GFLOPs/token training-compute estimate, cross-checked against an independent third-party recompute that converges on the same ~19.5-21%/~39-43% range — [CALCULATED]/[CREDIBLE-HEARSAY], not [PAPER]-tier; see Ch4 for the derivation.

What the Aggregate Reveals

Read across all these disclosures, three points of convergence and one genuine, unresolved divergence stand out. FP8 training is now the default wherever precision is disclosed at all — Ling-1T (full, fine-grained blockwise), ERNIE 4.5 (full, DeepSeek-modeled), MegaScale-MoE (full, per-token quantized) — with Kimi K2 as the chapter’s explicit counter-example rather than a partial-adoption data point: K2’s GEMM compute ran in BF16 with FP32 gradient accumulation throughout pretraining (params never touched FP8), and its one disclosed FP8-E4M3 usage is a memory-engineering technique — compressing recomputed MoE up-projection activation inputs to cut activation memory, not a compute-precision choice — layered on an explicit, disclosed decision to decline FP8 pretraining compute after a preliminary study flagged degradation risk (Ch4 covers the full recipe and its cross-lab implications). Every lab reporting an FP8-vs-BF16 validation gives the same order-of-magnitude tolerance, well under 0.5% relative loss deviation over hundreds of billions of tokens. Where MFU is disclosed at all, it clusters in a wide 17-47% band with no obvious correlation to model size; the single highest number (ERNIE’s 47%) comes from the lab using the least MLA-like architecture and the least Megatron-conventional framework, a reminder that MFU is as much a function of engineering effort and cluster topology as of architecture. Activation ratios span a wide range even among labs converged on “sparse MoE”: Kimi K2 at 3.1%, Ling-1T at 3.5%, MiniMax-M2 at roughly 4.3%, DeepSeek-V3 at 5.5%, GLM-4.5 at 9%, Hunyuan-Large at 13.4%, ERNIE-4.5 at 15.7% — Ling-1T’s ~3.5% is the one figure stated as a percentage in its own report; the rest are this chapter’s own division of each paper’s disclosed active/total parameter counts, not verbatim percentages from the sources. Ant’s Efficiency Leverage law argues the sparser end should keep winning as compute scales further, but no lab here has published a controlled ablation isolating activation ratio at trillion-parameter scale the way Ling’s sub-28B study does.

The genuine divergence is attention architecture. MiniMax’s retreat to full attention, backed by quantified long-context degradation, sits directly against Kimi Linear’s claim of a fair-comparison win for hybrid linear attention, Qwen3-Next’s order-of-magnitude efficiency claim from a 3:1 linear-hybrid ratio, and Hunyuan-TurboS’s undisclosed-but-shipped Mamba hybrid. No two labs that tried a non-full-attention architecture at scale report the same verdict, and MiniMax is the only one that quantifies a negative result rather than only reporting positive ones — worth weighting accordingly.

What is conspicuously undisclosed forms its own pattern:

  • Aggregate cluster size and MFU for Kimi K2 — the single most detailed parallelism and memory-budget disclosure in the chapter never states either number.
  • Any hardware, parallelism, or precision detail for Qwen3, the most commercially deployed open-weight family here — a complete blackout against otherwise thorough architecture and data disclosure.
  • ByteDance’s current-generation (Seed 2.0/2.1) training infrastructure — despite likely operating one of the largest GPU fleets in China, the newest matched infra detail (MegaScale-MoE) documents a 352B benchmark model and a separately cited 200B/20B production model, neither badged as the actual Doubao flagship.
  • MiniMax-M2’s pretraining infrastructure — the M1→M2 reversal is extensively documented; the hardware and parallelism used to actually train M2’s 29.2T tokens are not.
  • Hunyuan’s hardware entirely, across both Hunyuan-Large and TurboS — confirmed opaque even by a competitor’s own paper.
  • iFlytek’s Spark line — no technical report or credible engineering post with any pretraining-relevant detail exists at all, a genuine gap rather than a search failure.
  • GB200/Blackwell training anywhere in this corpus. Every hardware figure here — H800, H20, A800, Hopper generically — is pre-Blackwell, mostly export-controlled-tier Hopper. None of these MFU numbers, parallelism layouts, or FP8 recipes transfer directly to a GB200 NVL72 build without translation through Ch14 and Ch16; they are evidence about MoE methodology at scale, not about Blackwell utilization.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

The aggregate argues for three concrete positions. First, the Efficiency Leverage law’s compute-dependence term (EL grows with log compute) is the strongest available evidence that a 10T/200B design sitting an order of magnitude above Ling-1T’s training compute should push sparser than Ling’s own 3.5% activation, not merely match it — this should anchor Ch5’s activation-ratio decision, with Kimi K2’s 3.1% as a second, independently-arrived-at data point in the same direction. Second, MuonClip and the broader Muon-adoption pattern (confirmed independently at Moonshot and Zhipu, and — per Ch4 — adopted by DeepSeek itself in V4) means any 10T/200B optimizer plan should treat Muon-class orthogonalized updates plus a QK-clip-style safety valve as the field’s converged answer to trillion-parameter attention-logit stability, not a Moonshot curiosity; budget per-head MaxLogit monitoring as a first-class training metric regardless of whose exact recipe you follow. Third, and most consequentially: MiniMax’s full-attention retreat warns against locking in a hybrid or linear-attention architecture based on saturated 2024-2025-era benchmarks, because the specific failure mode — long-context, multi-hop, agentic degradation — is exactly the capability profile a 10T/200B model is presumably being built to have, and MiniMax’s own account states plainly that the relevant architectural commitment (global attention patterns fixed early in pretraining) cannot be cheaply undone mid-run. A strawman that bets on hybrid attention needs a validation suite built to catch this failure mode before the architecture is frozen, not after.

Chapter 4 · Part I — The Evidence Base

DeepSeek: The Reference Open Stack

No other near-frontier lab has published a training stack in the granularity DeepSeek has: a full FP8 numerics section with the exact hardware bug it works around, a pipeline schedule with a bubble-vs-memory table, an all-to-all comm budget quantified down to the SM, and — almost uniquely — the raw PyTorch Profiler traces from the actual production run. This chapter treats the V3/V3.2/V4 lineage as what it functionally is for this book: the reference implementation against which every other design choice in Chapters 5-19 gets checked, not because DeepSeek is the biggest lab, but because it is the only one that shows its work.

The V3 cluster: parallelism without tensor parallelism

DeepSeek-V3 was trained on 2,048 NVIDIA H800 GPUs, 8 per node, NVLink+NVSwitch intra-node and InfiniBand inter-node DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report, arXiv:2412.19437 §3.1 [PAPER] 12/2024. The production parallelism recipe is 16-way pipeline parallelism, 64-way expert parallelism spanning 8 nodes (EP64 = 8 nodes × 8 GPUs, routed experts uniformly spread across those 64 GPUs), and ZeRO-1 data parallelism — with no tensor parallelism anywhere in training 2412.19437 §3.2 [PAPER]. This is a real departure from 2024-era Megatron defaults, which lean on TP as the default intra-node lever; DeepSeek’s argument is that once EP is already saturating the interconnect, adding TP’s all-reduce/all-gather traffic on top is pure overhead, and the memory pressure TP normally exists to relieve is instead absorbed by recomputation and CPU-offload tricks (below). The training framework is HAI-LLM, the direct successor to HaiScale from the pre-V3 Fire-Flyer cluster 2412.19437 §3.2 [PAPER]. For a 10T/200B design that will almost certainly need TP somewhere (Ch16), DeepSeek’s precedent is a useful existence proof that TP is not structurally required at even 64-way EP — the open question is whether the same recompute/offload substitutions scale to 100k GPUs and 200B active parameters, or whether TP’s absence at 2,048-GPU scale is itself scale-dependent.

DualPipe: bidirectional scheduling for near-zero all-to-all overhead

DeepSeek’s own framing of the problem: cross-node EP communication and compute run at roughly a 1:1 ratio without overlap, meaning naive execution would nearly double wall-clock step time 2412.19437 §3.2.1 [PAPER]. DualPipe’s answer splits each micro-batch chunk into four components — attention, all-to-all dispatch, MLP, all-to-all combine — with backward chunks further split into “backward-for-input” and “backward-for-weights” in the style of ZeroBubble/ZB1P, then feeds micro-batches from both ends of the pipeline simultaneously, manually tuning the SM split between comm and compute so both all-to-all and PP communication are fully hidden behind compute 2412.19437 §3.2.1 [PAPER].

github.com/deepseek-ai/DualPipe README; 2412.19437 Table 2 [CODE][PAPER]

The price is explicit: DualPipe carries two copies of the model’s parameters per device, tolerable specifically because a large EP degree already keeps the effective per-GPU parameter count small 2412.19437 Table 2 [PAPER]. DualPipeV, a V-shaped derivative credited to Sea AI Lab’s “cut-in-half” blog post rather than DeepSeek itself, halves device count for the same bubble bound — a genuine third-party contribution folded back into the official repo, and worth noting as an early data point on how quickly the open Chinese ecosystem iterates on DeepSeek’s own releases (see Ch3 for the broader lab-by-lab picture) github.com/deepseek-ai/DualPipe, HackMD “Cut-in-half” post cited in repo [CODE].

The genuinely underused artifact here is github.com/deepseek-ai/profile-data [CODE]: DeepSeek open-sourced the actual PyTorch Profiler traces — chrome://tracing-loadable JSON — for training (EP64, TP1, 4K sequence length, 4 MoE layers per chunk, PP comm excluded for clarity), prefill (EP32), and decode (EP128). This is the literal DualPipe overlap pattern from the real V3 pretraining run, not a synthetic reproduction, and almost no third-party analysis engages with the raw trace files directly — a high-value resource for anyone doing overlap-schedule engineering for a 10T/200B build.

The 20-SM comms budget: routing topology as the binding constraint

DeepSeek’s network asymmetry on H800 is NVLink at 160 GB/s versus InfiniBand at 50 GB/s, roughly 3.2:1 2412.19437 §3.2.2 [PAPER]. Their routing rule caps each token at dispatch to at most M=4 nodes (“node-limited routing”), selected by summing top-K_r/M affinity scores per node; a token crosses IB to the GPU with matching in-node index on the target node, then NVLink-forwards to the GPU(s) actually hosting its target experts, with IB and NVLink phases fully overlapped. The paper states this scheme lets each token reach an average of 3.2 experts per node — meaning the same comm-cost envelope that supports V3’s actual 8-of-256 expert selection could support up to 4 nodes × 3.2 experts/node = 13 activated experts with zero added communication cost 2412.19437 §3.2.2 [PAPER]. This is one of the more consequential numbers in the whole corpus for a 10T/200B strawman (Ch5): DeepSeek is explicit that the routing topology, not per-token compute, is the binding constraint on activation count at their scale, and they chose to leave that slack on the table rather than spend it — presumably trading against quality-per-FLOP rather than comms.

The mechanism itself: 20 of 132 SMs on H800, split into 10 communication channels via warp specialization. During dispatch, separate warps handle IB sending, IB→NVLink forwarding, and NVLink receiving, with warp counts dynamically rebalanced by load; combine mirrors this in reverse with NVLink sending, NVLink→IB forwarding+accumulation, and IB receiving+accumulation, using custom PTX and auto-tuned chunk sizes to avoid L2 pollution from comm kernels sharing SMs with compute 2412.19437 §3.2.2 [PAPER]. DeepSeek’s own hardware-reflections paper calls this SM allocation a tax outright: “using SMs for communication results in significant inefficiencies, as tensor cores remain entirely under-utilized,” and asks for a GPU/network co-processor analogous to NVIDIA SHARP to absorb IB↔NVLink forwarding, RDMA buffer transport, all-to-all-combine reduction, and chunked memory-layout management directly, unifying scale-up and scale-out into one addressable domain Insights into DeepSeek-V3, arXiv:2505.09343 §3.5.1 [PAPER] 05/2025.

FP8: the recipe, precisely, and why it isn’t naive

DeepSeek’s mixed-precision framework runs Fprop, Dgrad, and Wgrad for every Linear operator in FP8, while embedding, output head, MoE gate, normalization ops, and attention itself stay in BF16/FP32 2412.19437 §3.3.1 [PAPER]. Two design choices separate this from a naive cast:

Granularity and format. Activations are scaled 1×128 (per-token, per-128-channel tile); weights are scaled 128×128 (per-128-input-channel × per-128-output-channel block), both computed online per step rather than from a delayed/historical amax 2412.19437 §3.3.2 [PAPER]. Format is E4M3 uniformly — a deliberate break from the common E4M3-forward/E5M2-backward convention, made viable because fine-grained block scaling already compensates for E4M3’s narrower dynamic range by sharing exponent budget within small groups (“Mantissa over Exponents,” 2412.19437 §3.3.2 [PAPER]). NVIDIA’s own later choice for Nemotron-H-56B — the simplest Transformer Engine recipe, per-tensor current scaling with the conservative E4M3/E5M2 split and the first/last four GEMMs held in BF16 — shows “the FP8 recipe” is not yet a converged industry answer (Ch8).

The accumulation fix. DeepSeek found H800’s tensor-core FP8 accumulation empirically retains only about 14 mantissa bits, not full FP32, producing up to ~2% relative error on a K=4096 GEMM without correction. The fix promotes partial WGMMA results to FP32 CUDA-core registers every NC=128 accumulated elements (4 WGMMAs), described as “the minimal accumulation interval that can significantly improve precision without introducing substantial overhead” — and because H800 can run two WGMMAs concurrently, the promotion of one overlaps the MMA of the other, hiding the extra CUDA-core work 2412.19437 §3.3.2 [PAPER]. Colfax Research’s independent analysis confirms the same ~14-bit accumulation limitation on Hopper tensor cores and notes the CUDA-core promotion pattern is now standard practice in CUTLASS ≥3.2 research.colfax-intl.com/deepseek-r1-and-fp8-mixed-precision-training [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY], and also flags that DeepSeek found activation-only blockwise scaling (without matching weight-block scaling) insufficient, with documented instability in the V3 paper’s Appendix B.2.

Two sensitive spots stay off the FP8 path even in the forward-cached direction: inputs to the post-attention Linear are stored in a custom E5M6 format with power-of-2 scale factors, re-tiled from 1×128 to 128×1 for backward; SwiGLU-in-MoE inputs are recomputed rather than cached. Dispatch-direction MoE activations are FP8 with power-of-2 scales; the reverse combine direction stays BF16 to protect accuracy 2412.19437 §3.3.3 [PAPER]. This recipe was validated at DeepSeek-V2-Lite/V2 scale (~1T tokens) at <0.25% relative loss error versus BF16 before being trusted at full V3 scale 2412.19437 §3.3 [PAPER] — and it has since become production-standard well beyond DeepSeek: NVIDIA’s Transformer Engine ships it as --fp8-recipe blockwise in Megatron-Core, citing validation “at DeepSeek-V3 class” scale plus MiniMax-M2 and Ant Ling-2.0, and recommends it as the standard for Hopper-generation training, versus MXFP8 for Blackwell NVIDIA TE docs, “FP8 Blockwise Scaling” [OFFICIAL-BLOG] 2026. Nebius independently reproduced DeepSeek-V3-671B (and a 16B MoE variant) on a 256×B200 TorchTitan cluster using native MXFP8 rather than the original blockwise scheme, claiming ~41% faster tokens/sec versus BF16 combined with DeepEP github.com/nebius/ml-cookbook [CODE] 2026 — a rare independent confirmation that DeepSeek’s precision philosophy transfers across a hardware generation even where the exact format changes. Worth flagging as a counter-example: Kimi K2, a comparably-sized MoE trained months after V3, explicitly declined FP8 for pretraining compute after a preliminary study suggested degradation risk, casting to blockwise FP8 only post-hoc for inference weights — “DeepSeek’s FP8 recipe works” is not the same claim as “FP8 pretraining is a solved default” at this scale huggingface.co/moonshotai/Kimi-K2-Instruct/discussions/30, referencing arXiv:2507.20534 [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 07/2025.

MLA, MTP, and aux-loss-free balancing: the mechanisms behind the headline architecture

MLA’s own paper framing is well trodden; the hardware-reflections paper’s framing is less commonly seen and matters more for a systems reader. Its argument is that LLM decode is GEMV-bound, not GEMM-bound, because KV-cache reads dominate — “the computation shifts from GEMM to GEMV, which has a much lower compute-to-memory ratio” — and MLA’s low-rank joint KV compression cuts per-token cache footprint from 516 KB (LLaMA-3.1-style MHA) to 70.272 KB for V3, a 7.28× reduction, reframing MLA primarily as a memory-bandwidth optimization rather than a parameter-efficiency trick 2505.09343 [PAPER] 05/2025. Su Jianlin’s independent, controlled ablations at Moonshot (Ch5) sharpen this further: matching MHA’s loss at 1/7th the KV cache decomposes into three separable effects — larger effective head dimension (128+64 → effective ~192) is the single biggest lever, bigger than increasing head count or MLP width at matched parameters; Partial RoPE helps independently; KV-sharing helps but is the smallest and least-validated-at-scale of the three Transformer升级之路 #20 [BLOG] 05/2025, #21 [BLOG] 07/2025. His theoretical argument: under NoPE, any of MHA/GQA/MQA is expressible as some head-dims-K, KV-shared MQA via matrix absorption, so for a fixed KV-cache budget the best decode-time model is head-dims-equals-cache-size KV-shared MQA, while for fixed compute the best model is head-dims-K unshared MHA — MLA is precisely the LoRA-style bridge between those two optima, behaving like each depending on whether you’re compute-bound (train/prefill) or memory-bound (decode), using the same weights. For a reader not copying MLA verbatim: the free variables that matter are head dimension and Partial RoPE fraction, not the MLA formulation per se.

Multi-Token Prediction in V3 runs at depth D=1 (one extra token predicted beyond next-token), implemented as a complete causal chain — sequential MTP modules each combining the prior depth’s hidden state with the next token’s embedding via a projection matrix, unlike Gloeckle et al.’s parallel-independent-head design. The loss weight λ follows an explicit, rarely-reproduced schedule: 0.3 for the first 10T tokens, 0.1 for the remaining 4.8T 2412.19437 §2.2, §3.2.3 [PAPER]. Embedding and output head are physically shared with the main model by co-locating the shallowest and deepest layers on the same PP rank under DualPipe, saving both parameter and gradient memory 2412.19437 §3.2.3 [PAPER]. At inference — not training, but the number that retroactively justifies the training-time investment — second-token acceptance runs 80-90%, yielding 1.8× TPS via speculative-decode-style use of the MTP head 2505.09343 [PAPER] 05/2025.

Aux-loss-free load balancing uses sigmoid affinity scores with a per-expert bias term b_i added only to the top-K routing decision, never to the gating value itself. Bias update speed γ is 0.001 for the first 14.3T tokens, frozen at 0.0 for the final 500B; a small complementary sequence-wise balance loss (α=0.0001) supplements it, deliberately tiny per the paper’s own note that “too large an auxiliary loss will impair model performance” 2412.19437 §2.1.2, §4.2 [PAPER]. Result: zero token drops across all of V3 training. Su Jianlin’s MoE环游记 series rigorously re-derives this update rule from an Aux-Loss-plus-straight-through-estimator starting point, showing it is not actually loss-free in spirit — the same objective, with gradient flow surgically routed to touch only the bias parameter — and flags a causality requirement DeepSeek’s paper doesn’t spell out: model parameters must update on LM loss first, then b updates on the resulting batch statistics, since updating b first risks future-information leakage MoE环游记：3 [BLOG] 03/2025. Su’s successor, Quantile Balancing, reframes balanced top-k routing as a linear program and claims to beat DeepSeek’s Loss-Free method by dropping BIP’s inequality-constraint clipping (“can only rob the rich, not save the poor”) MoE环游记：6 [BLOG] 02/2026 — one of several points in this book where the Chinese technical blogosphere runs ahead of, not just behind, the papers it comments on.

The remaining memory-saving techniques that let V3 dispense with TP: recomputing RMSNorm and MLA up-projections in the backward pass rather than persisting their activations, a CPU-resident EMA of parameters updated asynchronously at no extra GPU cost, and the shared embedding/output-head placement noted above 2412.19437 §3.2.3 [PAPER].

Full V3 hyperparameter table

61 transformer layers, hidden dim 7168; MLA with n_h=128 heads, d_h=128/head, KV compression dim d_c=512, query compression dim d_c’=1536, decoupled RoPE head dim d_h^R=64. MoE: all FFNs past the first three dense layers, 1 shared expert + 256 routed experts/layer, expert intermediate dim 2048, top-8 routing, node cap M=4. 671B total / 37B active parameters. AdamW β1=0.9, β2=0.95, weight_decay=0.1, grad-clip norm 1.0. Sequence length 4K throughout pretraining, 14.8T tokens total. LR: linear warmup 0→2.2e-4 over 2K steps, held to 10T tokens, cosine decay to 2.2e-5 over the next 4.3T tokens, held for 333B tokens, final step to 7.3e-6 for the last 167B tokens. Batch size ramps 3072→15360 over the first 469B tokens, then flat. Context extension uses YaRN across two extra 1000-step phases, 4K→32K→128K, config identical to V2’s 2412.19437 §4.2-4.3 [PAPER] — pulled directly from the PDF’s full text, since most secondary coverage compresses this table away.

Training cost, and the MFU-denominator trap

2412.19437 Table 1 [PAPER]. At $2/GPU-hour rental this is DeepSeek’s headline $5.576M figure 2412.19437 §1 [PAPER] — a rental-market spot-price number for the training run only, excluding R&D salaries, failed/ablation runs, and CapEx. SemiAnalysis’s January 2025 rebuttal citing ~$1.3B total server CapEx is not actually in conflict with this: that figure is total CapEx across DeepSeek’s full H800/H100/H20 fleet, not a V3-run-specific number, and the two are answering different questions [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] SemiAnalysis, 01/2025 (via secondary reporting; original paywalled).

The MFU calculation is worth reconstructing explicitly because the headline “DeepSeek trains at ~40% MFU” claim circulating publicly is, on the numbers in the paper’s own hardware-reflections companion, misleading by a factor of two. Using the paper’s own 250 GFLOPs/token training-compute figure 2505.09343 Table 2 [PAPER] × 14.8T tokens gives 3.7×10²⁴ total pretraining FLOPs; dividing by 2.664M GPU-hours × 3600 s/hr = 9.59×10⁹ GPU-seconds gives 385.8 TFLOPS/GPU average achieved. H800’s dense (no-sparsity) peaks are 1979 TFLOPS FP8 and 989.5 TFLOPS BF16 (H800 has identical compute to H100 — only NVLink bandwidth is cut for export-control reasons). That yields FP8-normalized MFU ≈ 19.5%, BF16-normalized MFU ≈ 39.0% [CALCULATED, 07/2026, from PAPER data]. A third-party recompute using profiler-measured forward FLOPs (×3 for fwd+bwd+update) instead of the 250 GFLOPs/token approximation lands at 424.3 TFLOPS/GPU achieved — 21.4% FP8-MFU / 42.9% BF16-normalized MFU — closely bracketing the independent estimate medium.com, dlrover/ATorch team, “What is the MFU for DeepSeek-V3 training” [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY], methodology described, undated (~2025). Because H800/H100 FP8 dense peak is exactly 2× the BF16 dense peak, any FP8-trained model’s MFU swings by 2× depending on which peak you normalize against; public claims almost always use the BF16 peak despite the GEMMs actually executing in FP8. Every FP8-MFU number cited elsewhere in this book (see Ch16, Ch21) should be checked against this same denominator trap before being taken at face value.

Fire-Flyer AI-HPC: the cluster DeepSeek built to afford this

Before V3, DeepSeek operated Fire-Flyer 2, a roughly 10,000-GPU cluster whose central architectural bet was that PCIe A100s beat SXM DGX-A100 nodes on cost-performance for LLM-scale workloads: node cost at 60% of DGX-A100, TF32/FP16 GEMM at 83% of DGX-A100’s per-GPU throughput, power at 40% less, for a cost-performance ratio of 1.38 versus 1.0 for the DGX design Fire-Flyer AI-HPC, arXiv:2408.14158 [PAPER] 08/2024. A two-layer fat-tree with 800-port zones ×2, integrating compute and storage traffic onto the same fabric, cut switch count to 122 versus 1,320 for an equivalent 3-layer DGX-style topology and network CapEx to $350 relative units versus $4,000 2408.14158 §Network Topology [PAPER]. The centerpiece is HFReduce, a custom allreduce doing intra-node CPU-side reduction (GDRCopy async D2H/H2D plus SIMD reduction across FP32/FP16/BF16/FP8) before an inter-node RDMA double-binary-tree allreduce — entirely bypassing NCCL’s GPU-kernel reduction path and its PCIe P2P limitations, measured at 6.3-8.1 GB/s inter-node bandwidth versus NCCL’s 1.6-4.8 GB/s under identical conditions, against a theoretical ceiling of ~13.3 GB/s bottlenecked by EPYC Rome’s shared PCIe host-bridge bandwidth 2408.14158 §HFReduce [PAPER]. HaiScale, HAI-LLM’s predecessor, hit 88% parallel scalability from 32→512 GPUs on VGG16 with the HFReduce backend (~50% faster than PyTorch-NCCL DDP), 91% efficiency on LLaMA-13B, and 76.14%/92.92% on DeepSeekMoE-16B at 640/320 GPUs 2408.14158 §HaiScale [PAPER].

Two more Fire-Flyer data points earn their place in any 100k-GPU reliability plan (Ch19): a one-year hardware-failure taxonomy — NVLink errors (Xid74) at 42.57% of all GPU failures, software/memory errors (Xid13/31/43/45) at 33.48%, ECC memory errors at ~2%, IB link failures at 30% of the non-Xid74 remainder 2408.14158 §Fault Tolerance [PAPER] — a rare public large-cluster reliability dataset with Xid-code granularity that essentially no other lab has published; and the original 3FS deployment (180 nodes, 2,880 SSDs, 20+ PiB mirrored) sustaining 9 TB/s outbound IB capacity and 8 TB/s achieved read, checkpointing every 5 minutes via a batch API 2408.14158 §3FS [PAPER]. The paper’s stated 2024 roadmap — 1:1 GPU:NIC ratio, multi-plane fat-trees, 400Gbps RoCE, 32,768 GPUs — is DeepSeek pre-announcing the design direction that shows up a year later as the Multi-Plane Fat-Tree in the hardware-reflections paper 2408.14158 §Future Direction [PAPER].

V3.2-Exp and DSA: sparse attention as a cheap retrofit

DeepSeek Sparse Attention, shipped in V3.2-Exp, pairs a lightning indexer with a top-k selector inside MLA’s MQA mode. The indexer score is I_{t,s} = Σ_j w^I_{t,j}·ReLU(q^I_{t,j}·k^I_s), computed in FP8 with H^I indexer heads and learnable per-head weights — O(L²) but cheap per pair (a ReLU-plus-dot-product versus full attention math) — feeding a top-k=2048 selection into the real MLA attention, which then runs O(Lk) DeepSeek-V3.2, arXiv:2512.02556 [PAPER] 12/2025. The continued-pretraining curriculum from V3.1-Terminus is deliberately tiny: 1,000 dense-warmup steps on 2.1B tokens (indexer-only KL-divergence loss aligning the indexer to the main attention distribution), then 15,000 sparse-training steps on 943.7B tokens (LM loss on the main model, indexer gradients detached) — roughly 946B tokens total, about 6.4% of V3’s original 14.8T-token budget 2512.02556 [PAPER]. Kernel support shipped same-day: FlashMLA PR #98 for sparse attention kernels, DeepGEMM PR #200 for indexer MQA-logit scoring, and a TileLang reference implementation github.com/deepseek-ai/FlashMLA, DeepGEMM [CODE] 09/2025.

FlashMLA’s own FP8 sparse-decode deep-dive found the kernel is dequantization-bound, not MMA-bound: MMA for 64 query heads costs ~34 cycles/token versus ~50 cycles/token for FP8→BF16 dequantization of the KV cache. The fix pairs CTAs in clusters of two sharing dequantized K/V via Distributed Shared Memory so each CTA only dequantizes half the cache, reaching 410 TFLOPS at batch=128/topk=2048 on H800 SXM5, up to 460 TFLOPS at topk=32768, against a dense BF16 decode baseline of 640 TFLOPS — sparse overtakes dense past a sequence length of roughly 3,000 tokens FlashMLA deep-dive doc [CODE] 09/2025. The per-token FP8 KV-cache byte layout is 512 bytes quantized “NoPE” content (e4m3) + 16 bytes scale factors + 128 bytes unquantized RoPE part (bf16) = 656 bytes/token [CODE]. DSA is a case study in how cheaply an architectural retrofit can land relative to pretraining from scratch — worth weighing against building sparse attention in from step zero for a 10T/200B design (Ch5, Ch9).

V4: the deltas that matter for infra

V4 (published as preview April 24, 2026, full report April 27, 2026) ships two variants — V4-Pro (1.6T total / 49B active, 33T tokens) and V4-Flash (284B total / 13B active, 32T tokens), both natively 1M-token context DeepSeek-V4, arXiv:2606.19348 [PAPER] 04/2026. Four deltas are load-bearing for a systems reader; three others are worth flagging as design signal.

Attention: hybrid CSA+HCA replaces MLA+DSA-only. Compressed Sparse Attention compresses every m tokens into one entry (softmax-weighted over 2m overlapping source tokens) then applies DSA-style lightning-indexer top-k selection over the compressed entries, using shared-KV MQA and grouped output projection to cut projection cost; Heavily Compressed Attention uses the same compression machinery at a much larger ratio with no sparse selection at all, applying dense attention directly over the heavily-compressed cache. Layers interleave CSA and HCA, plus a sliding-window branch, partial RoPE on only the last 64 dims, Q/K RMSNorm pre-attention (replacing V3’s need for QK-clip), and a learnable attention-sink logit. At 1M context, V4-Pro uses 27% of V3.2’s per-token inference FLOPs and 10% of its KV cache; V4-Flash uses 10% FLOPs / 7% KV cache 2606.19348 §Hybrid Attention [PAPER].

Optimizer: Muon replaces AdamW for most weights. Momentum accumulation followed by hybrid Newton-Schulz orthogonalization — 8 fast iterations at coefficients (3.4445, −4.7750, 2.0315) plus 2 stabilizing iterations at (2, −1.5, 0.5) — with RMS-rescaled updates, Nesterov momentum, and weight decay applied directly to Muon params; AdamW is retained only for embeddings, prediction heads, mHC’s static biases/gates, and RMSNorm weights. MoE gradient sync for the all-to-all is quantized to BF16 with stochastic rounding, halving communication, with a two-phase all-to-all plus local FP32 summation replacing tree reduce-scatter for robustness. No QK-Clip is needed because Q/K RMSNorm handles logit blowup directly 2606.19348 §Muon [PAPER]. This continues the public Muon lineage Su Jianlin has built at Moonshot since late 2024 (Ch7); DeepSeek adopting it for V4 is one data point in a broader pattern of Chinese frontier labs converging on spectral-norm-flavored optimizers over AdamW.

mHC: residual mixing constrained to the Birkhoff polytope. Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections generalizes the residual stream with three dynamically-generated linear maps (A_l, B_l, C_l) per layer; the residual-mixing matrix B_l is projected onto the doubly-stochastic-matrix manifold via 20 Sinkhorn-Knopp iterations, guaranteeing spectral norm ≤1 and stabilizing backprop through very deep stacks, at a measured 6.7% wall-time overhead on an already-overlapped 1F1B stage via fused kernels and selective checkpointing 2606.19348 §mHC [PAPER]. Su Jianlin’s March 2026 posts frame this as a design space DeepSeek got to first and validated at scale — Moonshot’s own internal rival, Attention Residuals (AttnRes), replaces residual streams with cross-layer attention entirely and reports ~5% extra compute for ~25% of the full benefit in its “Block” variant, not yet shipped as of the source date Attention Residuals 回忆录 [BLOG] 03/2026 — two frontier Chinese labs racing the same architectural problem from different angles, in public.

EP overlap goes wave-based. V3’s DualPipe targets full-pipeline-level comm hiding; V4 instead splits Dispatch→Linear1→Linear2→Combine per MoE layer into waves, where each wave’s compute starts only once all that wave’s expert comms finish, targeting comm-time < compute-time within a single layer rather than across the whole pipeline. The compute/comm boundedness threshold for V4-Pro is stated explicitly as 6hd FLOPs per token-expert pair against 3h bytes of communication, requiring compute/byte ≤ 2d = 6144 FLOPs/byte to stay comm-bound-safe. Claimed speedups are 1.50-1.73× for general inference, up to 1.96× for RL rollouts, shipped as the open-sourced “Mega MoE” fused kernel in DeepGEMM (fusing dispatch+L1+SwiGLU+L2+combine via symmetric memory, requiring PyTorch ≥2.9) 2606.19348 + DeepGEMM PR #304/#316 [PAPER][CODE] 04/2026. The paper’s own framing language leans toward this being primarily a post-training/RL-serving optimization rather than core pretraining infra, though it does not state this definitively either way.

Three more V4 deltas worth a sentence each. Precision defaults to BF16 for training, with the KV cache mixing BF16 (RoPE dims) and FP8 (rest), the indexer running FP4 at extreme context lengths, and routed-expert weights using FP4 — framed explicitly as “equivalent to FP8×FP8 on current hardware, theoretically 1/3 more efficient on future hardware,” a forward-compatibility bet rather than a speed win today, with FP4 QAT applied post-training 2606.19348 §Precision [PAPER]. The TileLang kernel/determinism infrastructure moves host-side contract checks into codegen (per-kernel overhead to <1μs), adds Z3 SMT-solver integer-range analysis, and disables fast-math by default — bitwise-reproducible training runs by default, a deliberate departure from the speed-over-reproducibility norm, with dedicated deterministic kernels for attention backward, MoE backward, and mHC matmul 2606.19348 §Training Infra [PAPER]. And Su Jianlin separately reports V4 replaces V3’s “first_k_dense” strategy with “first_k_hash” — early MoE layers routed by a fixed, precomputed token-id-to-expert-id lookup table rather than the learned router, undocumented in the V4 report itself and reverse-engineered by Su as a plausible greedy frequency-based construction, corroborated externally by a Hugging Face discussion referencing an all-zeros tid2eid tensor in a leaked V4-Flash checkpoint DeepSeek V4的tid2eid是怎么来的？ [BLOG] 05/2026 — the strongest evidence here that at extreme expert counts, structurally-guaranteed deterministic balance for early layers is displacing learned routing, not just supplementing it.

V4’s training hardware is a genuinely unresolved question this book should not paper over: reporting ranges from all-Huawei-Ascend-950 claims to DeepSeek’s own alleged H800+Ascend-910C mix to a U.S. government allegation of smuggled Blackwell chips, timed suspiciously close to launch. NIST’s CAISI evaluation of V4-Pro (May 2026) discloses no training-hardware information — only that CAISI itself served the model from cloud H200/B200 for evaluation — and the official DeepSeek V4 model card is a data-sourcing/licensing document, not a hardware disclosure CAISI evaluation [OFFICIAL] 05/2026; DeepSeek V4 model card, fe-static.deepseek.com [OFFICIAL] 04/2026. Treat any specific-chip claim about V4’s training hardware as [RUMOR].

The Open Infra Index: production kernels, not reference code

DeepSeek’s repo family is worth treating as a shared vocabulary rather than a set of one-off tools, because each piece has been revised at least once against real production feedback.

DeepGEMM is JIT-compiled (NVRTC-optional, up to 10× faster compile) and CUTLASS/CuTe-inspired but template-light, hitting 1550 TFLOPS on H800 (April 2025). SM90/SM100 support landed in a July 2025 refactor; SM120 (RTX 5090/Pro 6000) is explicitly unsupported as of mid-2026, with an official warning against enabling it. Scaling-factor format differs by architecture — SM90 wants FP32 scales, SM100 wants packed UE8M0 (four packed per torch.int), a direct consequence of Blackwell’s native microscaling tensor-core support — and the grouped-GEMM API groups only the M-axis (N, K fixed), deliberately tailored to same-shaped MoE experts rather than general grouped GEMM github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepGEMM [CODE].

EPLB ships two placement policies: hierarchical (pack expert groups to nodes evenly, replicate within-node, then pack to GPUs — used for prefill with smaller EP) and global (replicate regardless of groups, pack directly to GPUs — used for decode with larger EP), the open-sourced version of the redundant-experts strategy from V3’s inference-deployment section (32 redundant experts for prefill, one extra per GPU beyond its native 8; decode treats the shared expert as a 9th always-selected routed expert) github.com/deepseek-ai/EPLB; 2412.19437 §3.4 [CODE][PAPER].

DeepEP went through a full v1→v2 rewrite: the NVSHMEM backend was replaced by a lighter NCCL “Gin” backend (header-only, reuses existing NCCL communicators), separate high-throughput/low-latency APIs were unified into a single ElasticBuffer interface, analytical SM/QP-count calculation replaced v1’s auto-tuning, and the EP domain scaled to EP2048. SM usage for “V3-like legacy training” dropped from 24 SMs to 4-6 in v2. On SM100 (Blackwell) with pure NVLink, it hits 726 GB/s dispatch / 740 GB/s combine at 64 SMs, or 643/675 GB/s at the minimum 24-SM configuration github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepEP README [CODE] 2026. The fork ecosystem is more active than commonly assumed: uccl-ep adds heterogeneous NVIDIA/AMD and EFA/Broadcom/CX7 NIC support, Mori-EP is an AMD ROCm backend, AntGroup’s “Normal-SMFree” branch removes SMs from the RDMA path entirely (a stronger claim than DeepSeek’s own v2 reduction), and Hybrid-EP uses TMA instructions for even lower SM usage plus NVFP4 support — real infra convergence across the Chinese frontier-adjacent ecosystem contributing back into DeepSeek’s repo, not just consuming it [CODE]. Read alongside ByteDance’s independently-engineered COMET/Flux/Triton-distributed overlap stack and MegaScale-MoE’s SP-for-attention/EP-for-FFN split — a materially different philosophy (thread-block-level fusion versus DeepEP’s dedicated dispatch/combine kernels) achieving comparable ends — DeepEP is clearly not the only viable answer to this problem MegaScale-MoE, arXiv:2505.11432 [PAPER] 05/2025.

FlashMLA’s performance progression moved from an early-2025 kernel to an April 2025 rewrite hitting 660 TFLOPS dense decode (5-15% gain, H800 SXM5) via a “seesaw” schedule distinct from FlashAttention-3’s ping-pong — splitting the output matrix into two 64×256 halves (32,768 of 65,536 total registers needed per output matrix), alternating two warpgroups on different KV blocks with a single shared running max, ~2% slower than ping-pong in memory-bound regimes but a deliberate trade for the compute-bound-dominant real workload. Dense decode reaches up to 3000 GB/s memory-bound or 660 TFLOPS compute-bound on H800 SXM5; NVIDIA-reported dense MHA prefill on SM100/B200 reaches 1460 TFLOPS forward / 1000 TFLOPS backward; sparse MLA prefill reaches 640 TFLOPS (H800) / 1450 TFLOPS (B200, CUDA 12.9) github.com/deepseek-ai/FlashMLA + deep-dive docs [CODE].

3FS (Fire-Flyer File System) sustains 6.6 TiB/s aggregate read on a 180-node cluster (16×14TiB NVMe + 2×200Gbps IB per node) even with background training traffic running concurrently — a number that has become the industry reference point for AI-workload parallel filesystems — and completed a GraySort of 110.5 TiB in 30m14s (3.66 TiB/min) on 25 storage + 50 compute nodes via smallpond, a DuckDB-on-3FS data-processing framework, with KVCache-over-3FS peaking at 40 GiB/s across clients github.com/deepseek-ai/3FS README [CODE] 02/2025. Ch13 covers dataloading infrastructure requirements in full; 3FS is the concrete existence proof that a single filesystem can serve both bulk training I/O and background checkpoint/data traffic at this throughput without a dedicated separate fabric.

Scaling-law lineage

DeepSeek’s own hyperparameter scaling law — η_opt = 0.3118·C^(-0.1250), B_opt = 0.2920·C^(0.3271) — predates V3 by nearly a year DeepSeek LLM, arXiv:2401.02954 [PAPER] 01/2024, and the batch-size ramp and LR schedule actually used in V3’s hyperparameter table above are visibly downstream of it. Ch6 covers the broader scaling-law landscape (muP, Step Law, Farseer, WSD laws) in depth; the point worth flagging here is that DeepSeek’s practice of publishing the compute-optimal LR/batch-size fit alongside the model that used it is itself unusual — most labs disclose the resulting hyperparameters without the law that produced them, which is part of why this stack functions as a reference rather than just a data point.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

What borrows close to verbatim: the FP8 blockwise recipe (1×128/128×128, uniform E4M3, NC=128 FP32 promotion) is now productionized in Megatron-Core’s Transformer Engine and validated at DeepSeek-V3 scale plus at least two other frontier-adjacent MoE labs — little reason to reinvent this for Hopper-class hardware, and Ch8 should be read as extending this recipe onto Blackwell’s native MXFP8/NVFP4 paths rather than replacing its logic. The aux-loss-free balancing mechanism, and its Loss-Free/Quantile-Balancing lineage as refined in the open Chinese blogosphere, is similarly close to a solved problem worth adopting rather than re-deriving — though a 10T/200B build should budget Su’s causality requirement (update model params on LM loss before updating the bias) as a correctness constraint, not an optimization. EPLB’s hierarchical/global placement split and the 3FS throughput target (6.6 TiB/s-class aggregate read under live training load) are directly reusable specs for Ch13 and Ch19.

What needs redesign, not adoption: DualPipe’s 2× parameter-memory cost is a bet that only pays off at DeepSeek’s specific EP-heavy, TP-free 2,048-GPU configuration — a 100k-GPU/200B-active build at a materially different point in the parallelism space (Ch16) needs to re-derive whether the same bubble/memory tradeoff holds, or whether TP re-enters once EP degree, node count, and NVLink domain size (NVL72 versus H800’s 8-GPU domain) all change simultaneously. The 20-SM comms budget and its 3.2:1 NVLink:IB asymmetry are H800-specific artifacts of export-control-driven NVLink throttling; GB200 NVL72’s 900 GB/s-class unidirectional NVLink changes this ratio enough that the entire warp-specialization design — and the “13 experts at zero extra cost” slack calculation — needs rerunning from scratch, not porting (Ch16, Ch18). And V4’s wave-based EP overlap, Muon adoption, and mHC are recent enough (April 2026) that none have the multi-generation validation history the V3 stack has; they are the most promising near-term upgrades to evaluate, not yet default choices to inherit.

Part IIThe Model

Chapter 5 · Part II — The Model

The MoE Design Space and a 10T/200B Strawman

Every other chapter in this book measures a lab’s disclosures against a fixed architecture; this one has to fix the architecture. By July 2026 the frontier has converged on more of the MoE design space than most retrospectives credit — dropless routing, aux-loss-free balancing, and multi-token prediction are no longer live debates — but the axes that actually determine whether a 10T-total/200B-active host trains stably at 100,000 GB200s (sparsity ratio, attention mechanism, expert granularity) are still fought over in public, with at least one frontier lab publishing a named retreat from its own architecture. This chapter reads that fight from the primary sources and ends where the brief demands: a fully justified spec table.

The Sparsity Frontier: How Far Does 2% Go?
