The foundational result is Krajewski and Ludziejewski’s fine-grained MoE paper (Scaling Laws for Fine-Grained Mixture of Experts, ICML 2024) [PAPER, 02/2024], which introduced granularity G as an independent scaling axis and showed that matching expert width to a dense model’s 4x-widened FFN — the naive default — is suboptimal at nearly every compute budget. The load-bearing 2025-2026 refinement is Ant Group’s Ling team paper, Towards Greater Leverage: Scaling Laws for Efficient Mixture-of-Experts Language Models [PAPER, 07/2025], which trains over 300 models up to 28B parameters and defines Efficiency Leverage (EL) as the compute-multiplier an MoE configuration buys over a dense equivalent at matched loss. Its central finding: “for a fixed computational cost, greater model sparsity (i.e., lower activation ratio) leads to higher parameter efficiency,” monotonically across every FLOPs budget tested (3e17-3e20), down to the lowest ratio tried, 0.8%, with no knee, and “the EL advantage is amplified at larger scales.” Apple’s companion paper, Parameters vs FLOPs: Scaling Laws for Optimal Sparsity for Mixture-of-Experts Language Models [PAPER, 07/2025 v3], reaches the same conclusion differently: compute-optimal sparsity S* (fraction of inactive experts) converges toward 1 as budget and model size grow, with active parameters per example decreasing for compute-optimal models even as total parameters grow.

Read naively, both papers say a 2% activation ratio is conservative, not aggressive. Two qualifications complicate that. First, both fitted ranges top out around 1e20-1e22 FLOPs, while a 10T/200B host trained on tens of trillions of tokens implies C ≈ 6·N_a·D ≈ 6×(2×10^11)×(4×10^13) ≈ 5×10^25 FLOPs — three to seven orders of magnitude beyond the fitted range, so extrapolation governs here, not interpolation. Second, Abnar et al. add a caveat the Ling paper doesn’t emphasize: holding pretraining perplexity constant, sparser models underperform on reasoning-heavy tasks, since FLOPs-per-example matters independently of loss there even where it barely matters in-distribution. A companion paper, Optimal Sparsity of MoE Language Models for Reasoning Tasks [PAPER, 08/2025], reinforces this: reasoning accuracy scales with active compute, not total capacity.

This is almost certainly why real 2025-2026 flagships sit less sparse than the loss-optimal frontier would push them: DeepSeek-V3 5.5% (256+1, top-8, 671B/37B, DeepSeek-V3 [PAPER, 12/2024]), Kimi K2 ~3.1% (384+1, top-8, 1.04T/32.6B, Kimi K2 [PAPER, 07/2025]), Ling-1T ~3.5% (256+1, top-8, 1T/51B, Ling-1T [PAPER, 10/2025]), GLM-5 ~5.4% (256+1, top-8, 744B/40B, GLM-5 [PAPER, 2026]), Qwen3-235B-A22B 9.4% (128, top-8, no shared, Qwen3 [PAPER, 05/2025]), MiniMax-M2 ~4.3% (256, top-8, sigmoid gating, MiniMax-M2 [PAPER, 11/2025]). All cluster 3-5.5%, none within an order of magnitude of the Ling study’s 0.8% floor. A 2% activation ratio sits between two regimes — sparser than every shipped flagship, nowhere near the loss-optimal extreme — a defensible hedge against the reasoning-task penalty, not a number either paper independently validates.

Granularity resolves a real terminological trap. Ludziejewski’s original G is 4·d_model/d_expert; Ant’s paper redefines G as 2·d_model/d_expert so each expert is the same absolute size for a given G-value — the two papers’ G-numbers are not interchangeable, a mismatch the Ling paper flags explicitly (§6.1) as the reason its finding looks contradictory to a naive reading of Ludziejewski. Under Ant’s definition, granularity is genuinely U-shaped, not monotone, with a stable optimum at G≈12 across FLOPs 1e18-1e20; poor routing balance shifts the optimum coarser, so the granularity/routing-quality interaction is load-bearing. Shared-expert ratio is U-shaped too, with a compute-dependent optimum: S=16.7% for C≤1e20 FLOPs, dropping to S=8.3% for C>1e20 — since any real 10T-host run clears 1e20 FLOPs by orders of magnitude, the paper’s practical translation is blunt: “the optimal design choice is to use the lowest possible non-zero sharing ratio… heuristically implemented by setting the number of shared experts to one.” DeepSeek-V3/V3.2, Kimi K2, and Ling-1T all in fact ship exactly one shared expert; Qwen3 removed shared experts entirely in its 235B-A22B flagship, while Qwen3-Next brought exactly one back.

Two secondary findings matter for the strawman: attention should consume 30-40% of total FLOPs, a “robust” near-optimal range; and replacing the first 1-3 of roughly 60 layers with dense (non-MoE) FFN is near-EL-neutral but reduces routing imbalance, with the optimal dense-layer count growing from 0 at 1e18 FLOPs to 2-3 of 60 at 3e20 FLOPs. Fitted hyperparameter laws diverge from dense practice: MoE’s optimal LR is η^opt = 1.1576·C^(-0.1529), batch B^opt = 0.0694·C^0.3644, versus dense η^opt = 2.2177·C^(-0.1649), B^opt = 0.1039·C^0.3493 — MoE wants a larger batch and lower LR at matched compute, attributed to sparse per-expert gradient statistics needing more averaging. Allocation follows the same asymmetry: MoE M^opt = 0.1915·C^0.5095, D^opt = 5.2232·C^0.4905 versus dense M^opt = 0.0655·C^0.5422, D^opt = 15.2582·C^0.4578 — the compute-optimal MoE model is smaller in active FLOPs/token but trained on relatively more tokens. The framework validates end to end with Ling-mini-beta (0.85B active/17.5B total, 3.1% activation, G=12), matching or beating a 6.1B dense comparator on an identical 1T-token dataset — a predicted >7x efficiency leverage confirmed.

Routing Mechanics: Sigmoid, Aux-Loss-Free, and the Balancing Forks

DeepSeek-V3’s router uses sigmoid affinity scoring with post-hoc normalization over the selected top-k, and aux-loss-free load balancing via a per-expert bias term added only to the routing decision — not the gate value — updated each step as bias += γ·sign(load_error), with γ=0.001 for the first 14.3T tokens, frozen to 0 for the final ~500B tokens (DeepSeek-V3 §2.1.2 [PAPER, 12/2024]). A small sequence-wise balance loss at α=0.0001 backstops pathological within-sequence imbalance without acting as the primary force — the paper is explicit that “too large an auxiliary loss will impair model performance.” Result: zero token drops across the full run. Ling-1T ships essentially the same recipe, and GLM-4.5 publishes an almost identical schedule — bias rate 0.001 for the first 15T tokens then 0.0, sequence-level weight 0.0001 (GLM-4.5 §2.4 [PAPER, 08/2025]) — evidence this exact recipe, not just the general idea, has propagated cross-lab.

Qwen3 forks deliberately: it removes shared experts (present in Qwen2.5-MoE) and replaces DeepSeek’s bias mechanism with a “global-batch load balancing loss” (Qiu et al., 2025) computed over a global batch, intended to “encourage expert specialization” (Qwen3 [PAPER, 05/2025]). MiniMax-M2 forks a third way: sigmoid gating with learnable per-expert bias, chosen because sigmoid removes softmax’s zero-sum constraint, letting several experts activate confidently at once and smoothing routing dynamics enough to lean much less on any auxiliary loss (MiniMax-M2 §2 [PAPER, 11/2025]). Two incompatible balancing philosophies coexist in frontier production, and DeepSeek-V4 adds a fourth wrinkle — the gate activation moves from sigmoid to √(softplus(·)), unreplicated outside DeepSeek as of this writing (DeepSeek-V4 [PAPER, 06/2026]).

Expert-choice routing — experts ranking and selecting tokens, rather than the reverse — remains dead for autoregressive pretraining despite a real 2025-2026 literature of workarounds (predictor-based causal thresholds, Lory’s segment routing, SeqTopK, ReMoE, top-p adaptive routing): the causality problem — EC needs a future-token ranking unavailable to an autoregressive decoder — remains fundamentally unsolved, not merely unfashionable; token-choice top-k is fully dominant. On the systems side, dropless-versus-capacity-factor is closed: MegaBlocks (arXiv:2211.15841, Databricks/Stanford [PAPER, 2022]) established block-sparse dropless MoE, beating Tutel’s best capacity-factor config by up to 40% and 2.4x over dense Megatron-LM. By 2026 the live frontier is kernel efficiency for dropless under expert parallelism, per AMD’s Primus-Turbo team: “a well-tuned grouped-GEMM kernel is the single most important primitive for fast dropless MoE training” [OFFICIAL-BLOG/CODE, 06/2026] — a Ch15 question; the strawman below simply assumes dropless.

Attention for a 200B-Active Host

MLA versus GQA: the theory and the hardware case

The efficiency argument for Multi-head Latent Attention is, at bottom, a memory-bandwidth argument, not a parameter-efficiency one. DeepSeek’s own hardware-reflections paper reframes LLM decode as GEMV-bound, not GEMM-bound — “the computation shifts from GEMM to GEMV, which has a much lower compute-to-memory ratio” — and reports MLA cutting per-token KV-cache footprint from 516 KB (LLaMA-3.1-style MHA) to 70.272 KB for DeepSeek-V3, a 7.28x reduction [PAPER, arXiv:2505.09343]. A separate comparative study puts DeepSeek-V3-class MLA (~70 KB/token) against comparable GQA models (192-328 KB/token) — a 2.7-4.7x reduction [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 2025], narrowing but not disappearing under aggressive GQA cache compression.

The more rigorous case comes from Su Jianlin, the RoPE inventor and a Moonshot AI researcher whose blog kexue.fm functions as a running lab notebook for Kimi’s pretraining team, often posting derivations before the corresponding technical report — QK-Clip appeared there ten days before the Kimi K2 report that used it as a core stability technique (QK-Clip / MuonClip [BLOG, 07/2025], Ch7). His two-part MLA analysis (#20 [BLOG, 05/2025], #21 [BLOG, 07/2025]) claims, from outside DeepSeek: “under equal training and inference cost, MLA may be the best-performing Full Attention variant.” Controlled ablations decompose the advantage into three separable effects: larger effective head_dims (128+64 combined ≈ 192-equivalent) is the single biggest lever — bigger than adding heads or widening the MLP at matched params; Partial RoPE helps independently; KV-sharing helps but is smallest and least validated at scale. The theoretical argument (#21): under NoPE, any of MHA/GQA/MQA is expressible as some head_dims-K, KV-shared MQA via matrix absorption, so for a fixed cache budget the best decode-time model is head_dims-equal-to-cache-size KV-shared MQA, while for fixed compute (train/prefill) the best is the widest-head, non-shared MHA. MLA is the LoRA-style bridge — down-project then up-project, simultaneously MHA-192-ish at train/prefill and MQA-576-ish at decode with the same weights, at no real cost since decode is never compute-bound. Practically: the free variables that matter are head_dims (push past 128) and Partial RoPE fraction, not “copy DeepSeek’s MLA verbatim or don’t.” A wide-head_dims, Partial-RoPE GQA variant (Su’s GQA2-(192+64)-S2, with VO-RoPE) came within 0.003 nats of MLA’s own loss at MLA’s cache budget — a validated near-equal alternative.

Hybrid sliding-window and trainable sparse attention

Three 2025 designs converge on similar local:global ratios for cheap long-context handling: gpt-oss alternates GQA sliding-window and full-attention layers [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 08/2025]; Gemma 3 uses a 5:1 local:global ratio (1024-token local windows, RoPE base 10k local / 1M global), costing minimal perplexity versus Gemma 2’s 1:1 while needing full-context K/V in only 1/6 of layers (Gemma 3 [PAPER, 03/2025]); Llama 4’s iRoPE alternates ~3:1 RoPE-chunked local attention against NoPE full-attention layers, targeting “infinite” length generalization [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2025].

The more consequential development is trainable sparse attention, where DeepSeek has shipped a second full generation. Native Sparse Attention (NSA, DeepSeek-AI + Peking University, ACL 2025 Best Paper [PAPER, 02/2025]) combines coarse compressed tokens, fine-grained selected blocks, and a local window via learned gating; its largest reported scale (27B active) was never confirmed at frontier size. MoBA (arXiv:2502.13189, Moonshot AI [PAPER+CODE, 02/2025]) applies MoE-style routing to attention itself — queries sparsely select KV blocks — and is confirmed as the mechanism actually serving Kimi’s production long-context traffic.

DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA), from the DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp technical report [PAPER, primary PDF, 09/2025], is the most fully-specified mechanism here. A small FP8 “lightning indexer” computes I_{t,s} = Σ_j w^I_{t,j}·ReLU(q^I_{t,j}·k^I_s), then retrieves only the top-k=2048 KV entries by score; main attention runs over just those, cutting complexity from O(L²) to O(Lk). It runs under MLA in MQA mode, sharing each compressed KV latent across query heads. DSA is retrofitted, not trained from scratch: a dense warm-up freezes everything but the indexer, training it via KL-divergence against the main model’s own summed attention scores (LR=1e-3, 2.1B tokens), then sparse training unfreezes everything (LR=7.3e-6, 943.7B tokens). Measured payoff (H800, $2/GPU-hr, 128K context): prefill drops from ~$0.7 to ~$0.2/M tokens, decode from ~$2.1 to ~$0.4, with quality flat against V3.1-Terminus (MMLU-Pro identical at 85.0). GLM-5 independently states it uses “DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA)” by name — the clearest documented cross-lab propagation of a DeepSeek attention mechanism (GLM-5 [PAPER, 2026]). DeepSeek-V4 extends the idea to two compression tiers — Compressed Sparse Attention (top-k selection over compressed KV entries) and Heavily Compressed Attention (dense attention over a much-more-heavily-compressed sequence) — both with shared-KV MQA, Partial RoPE, and an attention-sink term; at 1M context, V4-Pro needs only 27% of V3.2’s inference FLOPs and 10% of its KV cache (DeepSeek-V4 [PAPER, 06/2026]).

The linear-hybrid verdict

The genuinely unsettled question, as of July 2026, is whether linear-recurrent attention belongs in a frontier stack at all — and the evidence is split in a way directly informative for a 100k-GPU commitment. Qwen3-Next uses a 3:1 Gated DeltaNet:Gated Attention hybrid across 48 layers, claiming under 10% of dense Qwen3-235B-A22B’s training cost at comparable quality [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 09/2025]. Kimi-Linear (KDA, Moonshot [PAPER, 10/2025]) uses a 3:1 KDA:full-attention interleave, cutting KV-cache up to 75% and delivering up to 6x decode throughput at 1M context in a 48B/3B model trained to 5.7T tokens, with open-sourced FlashKDA kernels giving 1.72-2.22x prefill speedup on H20 [CODE, 04/2026].

Against this, MiniMax published an explicit, named postmortem on abandoning linear attention. M1 used Lightning (linear) attention in a 1-full-per-7-linear hybrid at 456B/45.9B (MiniMax-M1 [PAPER, 06/2025]); M2 reverted to 100% full attention across all 62 layers. The team’s blog is specific about why: on saturated benchmarks the hybrid “looked just as good as pure full attention,” but at larger scale “clear deficits in complex, multi-hop reasoning tasks” emerged — the validation benchmarks were themselves the blind spot. RL training separately exposed “serious precision issues”: linear attention is “far more sensitive to numerical precision than full attention,” a direct liability for low-precision KV/state storage. Infrastructurally, linear variants are “memory-bound, even during training, and without extreme IO optimization, the system fails to utilize the GPU’s available FLOPs” — the theoretical compute-favorable crossover doesn’t materialize without simultaneously solving prefix caching, speculative decoding, and low-precision state storage together. MiniMax’s summary: full attention “wins because it’s the least fragile choice across tasks, model sizes, and inference stacks,” not because efficient attention is dead but because it “isn’t mature enough to be the default” [OFFICIAL-BLOG, huggingface.co/blog/MiniMax-AI, 11/2025]. Tellingly, MiniMax M3 reportedly reintroduces a new sparse-attention mechanism rather than returning to linear-recurrent compression [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 2026] — even MiniMax’s own trajectory bends toward selection-based sparsity.

The frontier is bifurcating between trainable sparse (NSA→MoBA→DSA→CSA/HCA) and linear-hybrid (GDN→KDA), with sparse-selection accumulating more cross-lab adoption at the largest scales (DeepSeek→GLM-5) while linear-hybrid stays attractive at mid-scale but carries one documented production retreat citing failure modes — memory-bound training, precision fragility, serving-stack friction — most punishing at 100k-GPU scale. This tips the strawman toward MLA-plus-trainable-sparse-attention and away from linear-hybrid, though Ch9 revisits whether multimodal-native pretraining changes the calculus for very long video/audio contexts.

Multi-Token Prediction as Default Practice

MTP is close to universal in 2025-2026 frontier releases — DeepSeek-V3/V3.2/V4, Kimi K2, Qwen3-Next, MiMo-V2-Flash, Ling-1T, and Nemotron-3 Super/Ultra all use it — and its value expanded in 2026 beyond speculative decoding. DeepSeek-V3 implements MTP as a cascaded chain of modules (not Gloeckle-style parallel independent heads), sharing the embedding and output head with the main model; loss weight is 0.3 for the first 10T tokens, 0.1 for the remaining 4.8T (DeepSeek-V3 §2.2 [PAPER, 12/2024]), giving 85-90% second-token acceptance and ~1.8x decode TPS [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 2025]. Kimi K2’s cautionary tale is purely practical: its initially released MTP checkpoint had only ~39% acceptance because the weights weren’t self-distilled against the exact base checkpoint; properly matched, MTP reaches 80-90% acceptance [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 2025-2026] — MTP quality is checkpoint-matching-sensitive, not architecture-sensitive alone.

The genuinely new 2026 framing comes from Xiaomi’s MiMo-V2-Flash, a 3-layer, 0.33B-param-per-block MTP stack achieving 3.6-token acceptance length and 2.6x decode speedup, positioned explicitly as an RL-training accelerator: “MTP enables efficient and effective RL with small batches by scaling token-level parallelism instead of batch size” (MiMo-V2-Flash [PAPER, 01/2026]) — directly relevant to the reader’s post-pretraining phase, not just inference.

Nemotron-3 Super and Ultra take a structurally different approach: 2 MTP heads with shared weights across prediction offsets, rather than independent fixed-offset heads. Fixed-offset heads suffer a train/inference mismatch when reused recursively for deeper speculative drafts (trained on ground-truth hidden states, conditioned at inference on self-generated ones); a shared-weight head generalizes better under recursive self-conditioning. On SPEED-Bench at draft length 7, Super achieves 3.45 tokens/verification-step, ahead of Qwen3-Next’s 3.33 and DeepSeek-R1’s 2.70, with the gap widening at deeper draft positions (Nemotron 3 Super Table 2 [PAPER, 04/2026]). MTP loss-scaling is 0.3 for Super, 0.1 (0.05/block) for Ultra — a hyperparameter that interacts badly with a BF16 gradient-reduction optimization during Ultra’s pretraining, discussed in Ch7.

Nemotron-3: The Hybrid Mamba-MoE Divergent Branch

Every architecture discussed so far shares one commitment: a Transformer backbone with MoE FFN layers and some flavor of attention compression. NVIDIA’s Nemotron 3 family — Nano (31.6B/3.2B, arXiv:2512.20848 [PAPER, 12/2025]), Super (120.6B/12.7B, arXiv:2604.12374 [PAPER, 04/2026]), Ultra (550B/55B, arXiv:2606.15007 [PAPER, 06/2026]) — is the production counter-example: a hybrid Mamba-Transformer-MoE backbone where the majority of layers are state-space (Mamba) and only a minority (6 of 52 in Nano) are self-attention “global anchor” layers, wrapped around a novel MoE-compression mechanism called LatentMoE.

LatentMoE (arXiv:2601.18089, NVIDIA [PAPER, 01/2026]) starts from a GB200 roofline (10 PFLOPS FP4, 8 TB/s HBM, 900 GB/s unidirectional NVLink): at throughput-serving batch sizes, standard-MoE communication-to-compute ratio for a Qwen3-235B-A22B-shaped model is roughly 9x — all-to-all dominates compute — while at low-latency batch sizes MoE is memory-bandwidth-bound on expert-weight reads. From derived design principles (communication volume scales as d_model×K independent of expert width; quality tracks K·m via a Barron-function expressivity argument; scaling N and K by the same factor α exponentially expands the combinatorial expert-selection space), d_model is the only axis that can shrink without hurting bandwidth, communication, or the K·m quality budget. LatentMoE down-projects each token from d to a latent ℓ<d (typically α=d/ℓ=4x) before routing and all expert FFN compute, then up-projects back — holding communication volume and per-expert weight-load bytes constant while quadratically expanding the expert-combination space if N and K scale together by α. Validated at 95B-total/8B-active on 300B tokens, it beats a matched-active/matched-total baseline on every metric (MMLU-Pro 34.91 vs. 29.26); at 73B-total/8B-active hybrid Mamba-MoE scale on 1T tokens the gain holds (52.87 vs. 48.30). Projected to trillion-parameter scale via an Effective Parameter Multiplier framework, a hypothetical Kimi-K2-1T-LatentMoE variant would need roughly 350B fewer parameters than standard MoE to match accuracy, with the standard-MoE competitor running 1.24-3.46x slower at matched accuracy.

Ultra ships this at 550B total/55B active, 108 layers, d_model=8192, 512 experts at top-22, ℓ=2048 (α=4) — distinct from every model above in two ways at once: state-space hybridization replacing most self-attention, and latent-space MoE dispatch compression replacing granularity/activation-ratio tuning as the primary sparsity lever. It represents NVIDIA’s actual production bet, and is in principle combinable with MLA-style KV-cache compression, though no source here documents that attempted. Two disclosure gaps matter for a planner: Super and Ultra pretrain natively in NVFP4 (Ch8’s territory, though architecturally relevant since it’s why LatentMoE’s latent projections and MTP heads stay in BF16 while the bulk of linear layers run native NVFP4 GEMMs); and none of the four papers discloses MFU, GPU count, or step time for the main pretraining run — a striking silence given how much other operational detail (RL infrastructure failure rates, exact LR schedules) they do publish. LatentMoE’s accuracy claims are validated only to 73B-total/8B-active on 1T tokens and projected, not measured, at trillion-parameter scale, and Ultra’s own run suffered two named divergences (one root-caused to a BF16-gradient-reduction/MTP-loss-scale interaction, one left “Undetermined,” mitigated only by cutting the token budget) — evidence this branch is differently-risked, not lower-risk, than the Transformer-MoE mainline.

Vocabulary, Tokenization, and the Embeddings-versus-Experts Tension

Vocabulary scaling has its own laws, and frontier practice is systematically under-provisioned relative to them. Scaling Laws with Vocabulary (Tao et al., NeurIPS 2024 [PAPER, 07/2024]) finds optimal vocabulary size grows as a power law in compute, more slowly than non-vocabulary parameters but still faster than shipped practice: “the optimal vocabulary size of Llama2-70B should have been at least 216K, seven times larger than its actual 32K,” with 32K→43K at matched 2.3e21 FLOPs moving ARC-Challenge 29.1→32.0. Over-Tokenized Transformer [PAPER, 01/2025] reinforces the direction. A fresh January 2026 result, Scaling Embeddings Outperforms Scaling Experts [PAPER, 01/2026], cuts directly against the “more/finer experts” consensus above: for a fixed budget, growing embedding/vocabulary capacity can beat growing expert count — a genuine, unresolved tension, since the two sides of that trade were never fit jointly.

Real frontier vocab sizes cluster below what the laws suggest is optimal: Kimi K2 160K, Ling-1T 156K BBPE, Qwen3 151,669 BBPE, MiniMax M2 200,064, gpt-oss ~200K (o200k_harmony). None has pushed past ~200K. Two more radical alternatives remain unshipped at frontier scale: Byte Latent Transformer (BLT, Meta, ACL 2025 [PAPER, 12/2024]) uses entropy-based dynamic byte patching, showing better inference-FLOP scaling than tokenization at fixed cost up to 8B params/4T bytes; SuperBPE (Liu et al., COLM 2025 [PAPER, 03/2025]) lifts the whitespace pretoken-boundary constraint to learn “superword” tokens, cutting token counts up to 33% and beating BPE baselines on 30 tasks at 8B scale (+8.2% MMLU, 27% more efficient at inference). Neither is in a named 2025-2026 flagship — both are a live opportunity for a from-scratch build, not a proven-at-scale choice, and betting the tokenizer on either is a higher-variance decision than vocabulary size alone.

Depth, Width, and the Layer-Count Census

The one dedicated depth-versus-width paper here, The Depth Delusion: Why Transformers Should Be Wider, Not Deeper (Fahim & Karim [PAPER, 01/2026]), needs its provenance stated before its numbers are used: a two-author preprint validated only on dense transformers from 17M to 7B params (30 architectures, R²=0.922) — not a frontier-lab publication, not MoE-specific. Its findings are still a useful hypothesis to stress-test: optimal depth D*∝C^0.12, width W*∝C^0.34 (width should scale 2.8x faster than depth), and a “critical depth” D_crit∝W^0.44 beyond which more layers increase loss via “gradient starvation” — their own 7B-scale 64-layer model underperformed a 32-layer model by 0.12 nats despite more parameters, and the paper speculates several dense frontier models sit 3.6-4.9x past their theoretical critical depth. Treat this as a caution against blindly stacking layers, not validated exponents for a 10T MoE.

Real frontier layer counts show no clean monotone trend against total parameters: DeepSeek-V3 and Kimi K2 both hold 61 layers at d_model=7168 across a 671B-to-1T range; GLM-4.5 (355B/32B) runs d_model=5120 with 96 attention heads — 2.5x more than a hidden-dim-matched baseline — deliberately trading width for depth because “deeper models exhibited better reasoning capacity” (the research notes do not record GLM-4.5’s exact layer count) (GLM-4.5 §2.1 [PAPER, 08/2025]); GLM-5 and Ling-1T both run 80 layers, continuing that deeper-narrower philosophy; Qwen3-Next holds 48; MiniMax-M2 uses 62 at d_model=3072; gpt-oss-120b uses 36. Nemotron-3 Ultra runs deepest at 108 layers — but mixes Mamba and attention blocks, not directly comparable. Depth is currently lab-specific, not a settled scaling-law output.

Multimodal-Native Constraints on the Text Backbone

Ch9 owns multimodal-native pretraining; the narrower question here is whether building multimodal-native from step one changes any decision above. The consequential datapoint is Baidu’s own reversal: ERNIE 4.5 (June 2025) shipped modality-isolated MoE with a router-orthogonalization loss to prevent routing collapse when a text-only router is naively extended [PAPER, ERNIE 4.5 Technical Report, 06/2025]; ERNIE 5.0 (February 2026) abandoned that for “modality-agnostic expert routing… in contrast to modality-isolated routing strategies in our previous models” (ERNIE 5.0 [PAPER, 02/2026]). This means the router/sparsity/granularity choices above don’t need modification for an anticipated multimodal-native build — the majority pattern (Llama 4, Kimi-VL, Qwen3-VL) already feeds a continuous encoder into the same modality-unaware expert pool as text. What does change is EP/TP/PP topology: NVIDIA’s heterogeneous-parallelism paper for multimodal training (arXiv:2605.27678 [PAPER, 05/2026]) shows a shared layout across encoder and LLM costs real throughput (colocated heterogeneous execution improves TFLOPS/GPU up to 49.3%) — a Ch16/Ch9 question, not a change to the architecture chosen here.

Upcycling versus From-Scratch

NVIDIA’s dense-to-MoE upcycling technique (2024) used virtual-group initialization to expand a dense Nemotron-4 15B, beating continued dense training (67.6 vs. 65.3 MMLU) [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 10/2024] — the largest documented upcycling success, still several orders of magnitude below frontier scale. 2025-2026 upcycling papers (Marco-MoE, Dense2MoE, SPRI) target multilingual, on-device, or data-constrained regimes, not frontier pretraining. Every flagship surveyed here — DeepSeek V3/V3.2/V4, Kimi K2, Qwen3/Qwen3-Next, Ling-1T, GLM-4.5/5, MiniMax M2, Nemotron 3 — was trained MoE-from-scratch. For a 10T-total host, from-scratch is the only empirically demonstrated path at this scale.

The 10T-Total/200B-Active Strawman

The table below is a defensible starting point for internal ablation, not a claim that any individual cell is uniquely optimal. Every row cites the finding it draws on above; the single largest source of uncertainty is flagged explicitly rather than smoothed over.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

The architecture this chapter lands on — MLA with wide effective head-dims and Partial RoPE, DSA-style trainable sparse selection instead of linear-hybrid attention, sigmoid-plus-aux-loss-free routing with one shared expert at G≈12, 2% activation as a deliberate hedge, and 2-3 shared-weight MTP modules — is defensible from primary sources to roughly 1T total parameters and genuine extrapolation beyond that, by the notes’ own admission on the sparsity and depth rows. Three consequences follow for the rest of this book. First, granularity and expert-count choices here set the EP topology math in Ch16 — 512-640 experts at top-8 is a materially different all-to-all shape than DeepSeek’s 256 or Kimi’s 384, propagating into the communication-to-compute ratios Ch18 budgets for. Second, sparse-selection over linear-recurrent keeps kernel dependencies inside the FlashAttention/DSA lineage Ch15 catalogs, rather than the still-immature linear-attention kernel and serving stack MiniMax’s postmortem warns against betting a 100k-GPU commitment on. Third, the Nemotron-3 hybrid Mamba-MoE branch is differently-risked enough — given two named divergences in NVIDIA’s own largest run — that it belongs in Ch22’s gap list as a hedge track worth ablating before the run is locked, not a rejected alternative.

Chapter 6 · Part II — The Model

Scaling Laws as Practiced on Proprietary Data

Nobody at a frontier lab in 2026 opens a whiteboard and fits N^a·D^b to a Kaplan-style loss surface and calls it done. What actually happens is closer to an industrial instrumentation program: hundreds to thousands of small runs feeding a stack of coupled power laws — learning rate, batch size, the loss surface itself, MoE sparsity, precision, data repetition — cross-checked against a handful of expensive mid-scale confirmation runs before a single proprietary token touches the target-scale cluster. This chapter is about that stack: what functional forms the field has converged on, what actually transfers across scale and what doesn’t, what it costs, and what a lab building a 10T-total/200B-active MoE on its own data should commission before committing the run.

From a single exponent to a multi-axis ladder

The live practice partitions into two loosely coupled sub-problems. The first is compute allocation and hyperparameter selection — how big should N and D be, what learning rate η and batch size B does that point need — fit from many cheap small/mid-scale runs. The second is the bridge from pretraining loss to anything a stakeholder actually cares about (downstream task accuracy), fit from fewer but larger runs because, as later sections document, bridge laws are precisely the component that breaks under careless extrapolation. Almost every methodological argument below is really an argument about which of these two sub-problems a technique addresses, and how far it can be trusted away from the scale where it was fit.

The single most important shift from the Chinchilla era (covered as history elsewhere in the field’s canon, not reproduced here) is that N and D are no longer the only axes worth power-law treatment. A lab now expects separate laws for η(N,D), B(N,D or D-only), sparsity/granularity, precision P, and data-repetition count, each interacting with the others in ways a single Chinchilla-style fit cannot capture. DeepSeek’s own foundational HP-scaling paper made this multi-axis structure explicit as early as 2024: rather than adopting muP, DeepSeek-AI fit direct power laws for optimal learning rate and batch size against compute budget C from small-scale grid search, arriving at η_opt = 0.3118·C^-0.1250 and B_opt = 0.2920·C^0.3271, with model/data compute allocation M_opt = 0.1715·C^0.5243 and D_opt = 5.8316·C^0.4757 (DeepSeek LLM [PAPER], 1/2024). The detail that matters most for a lab about to curate its own 30-40T-token corpus is buried past the headline formulas: the exponents shift with data quality. DeepSeek’s higher-quality “current in-house” dataset gave a=0.524/b=0.476 (favoring model size), while their earlier, lower-quality “early in-house” dataset gave a=0.450/b=0.550 (favoring data volume) — the compute-optimal N/D split is not a universal constant, it is a property of the specific data distribution being trained on. That single finding is the strongest argument in the whole literature against importing anyone else’s published compute-optimal ratio wholesale.

muP/muTransfer: what actually transfers in 2025-2026

The original muP/muTransfer result (Tensor Programs V, Yang et al. [PAPER], 2022) promises hyperparameters tuned on a small proxy model transfer zero-shot to arbitrary width via per-layer init/LR scaling rules. By 2026 the question worth asking is not whether muP works — it does, cleanly, for width — but whether it covers everything a frontier lab needs scaled simultaneously, and the accumulated 2025-2026 literature answers no.

Adoption evidence is real but narrower than the marketing implies. Cerebras-GPT and EleutherAI’s muTransfer port were the first clean open demonstrations that HP search on a ~40M model transfers to 2.7B+ (EleutherAI blog, “The Practitioner’s Guide to the Maximal Update Parameterization” [OFFICIAL-BLOG]). MiniCPM took a related but distinct path — WSD-schedule checkpoint reuse rather than muP proper — to derive a 192× data-to-model compute ratio against Chinchilla’s ~20×, i.e., a massively-overtrained point is compute-optimal once inference and small-model economics enter the objective (MiniCPM [PAPER], 4/2024). Moonlight (MoonshotAI’s 3B-active/16B-total MoE, 5.7T tokens, Muon optimizer) needed a muP-style “consistent update RMS across matrix and non-matrix parameters” correction before Muon would scale at all — vanilla Muon does not muTransfer out of the box because orthogonalized-update norms mismatch between routed and shared experts (Moonlight [PAPER + CODE], 2/2025), a correction that became MuonClip in Kimi K2 after attention-logit explosions past 1000 surfaced in a 9B-active/53B-total mid-scale ablation (Kimi K2 [PAPER], 7/2025) — QK-Clip mechanics belong to Ch7, but the scaling-law point is that muP-for-Muon required its own bespoke patch.

Five specific failure modes are now documented well enough to plan around rather than discover the hard way:

  1. Depth transfer. Standard muP transfers width but not depth. “Don’t be lazy: CompleteP” shows simultaneous width+depth transfer needs α=1 residual-branch scaling — stronger than the initialization-only stability condition — plus rescaled LayerNorm/bias LR and AdamW ε (CompleteP, Cerebras [PAPER], 5/2025).
  2. Attention/QK scale. Attention logits don’t obey the network’s fan-in law, so QK-normalization needs its own scaling rule under muP; omitting this is a common source of “transferred fine to 1B, blew up at 30B” reports. GQA-µP extends the fix to grouped-query attention (GQA-µP [PAPER], 2026).
  3. Batch size and duration. Vanilla muP says nothing about how LR should co-move with batch size or training horizon — that requires the CBS machinery below. “Complete^d” unifies width+depth+batch+duration transfer into one parameterization and names this gap unresolved by muP alone (Complete^d, Apple [PAPER], 12/2025).
  4. MoE fan-in ambiguity. Is the effective width for muP purposes total parameter count or activated (top-k) count? µ-Parametrization for MoE shows LR transfer holds across expert count but breaks across top-k and granularity — a lab changing granularity between ladder scale and target scale cannot trust muP alone (µ-Parametrization for MoE [PAPER], 8/2025). Complete-muE proposes a two-bridge fix — dense→dense-MoE via active-width µP plus normalized router scale, then dense-MoE→sparse-MoE via activated-expert scaling — with the practical recipe “tune dense once, transfer to all MoE configs” (Complete-muE, Adobe [PAPER], 5/2026).
  5. Init-std sensitivity. MoE training is measurably more sensitive to initialization std than dense equivalents; naive FFN-LR-reduction (tried in Switch Transformer, Skywork-MoE) underperforms because it breaks the maximal-update condition (survey synthesis, “How To Scale” [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY], 12/2025).

Su Jianlin’s ongoing “MuP之上” (Beyond MuP) series, from Moonshot’s own research diary, reframes muP around three Θ(1) stability conditions and argues the linear-layer special case reproduces Muon from first principles — theoretical grounding rather than a new empirical law, but evidence the muP-vs-Muon debate has a rigorous Chinese-language counterpart running months ahead of any English survey (kexue.fm archives/11340 et seq. [BLOG], 10/2025-4/2026; see Ch7, Ch19).

No credible source claims zero-shot muP transfer from a 100M proxy straight to a 200B-active/10T-total MoE across width, depth, batch, duration, and sparsity simultaneously. The pattern that has emerged is layered: muP-style width scaling as one component, empirical HP power laws (Step Law/DeepSeek-style, below) layered on top for batch/LR/duration, and MoE-specific bridge laws layered on top of that.

The HP-law family: Step Law, Farseer, and full-curve fitting

Three lineages dominate the public post-Chinchilla HP-scaling literature, and all three are more empirically grounded — and more expensive to produce — than anything Kaplan-era scaling work attempted.

Step Law (2503.04715, StepFun, “Predictable Scale Part I” [PAPER], 3/2025) is the largest public HP-scaling sweep documented anywhere (scale in the table below) — comparable to roughly 38% of DeepSeek-V3’s entire 2.664M H800-GPU-hour pretraining budget, spent purely on instrumentation rather than a deployable model (see Ch4 for DeepSeek-V3’s own infra). Its B(D) formula carries a strong, independently testable claim: optimal batch size depends on total tokens D alone, not on model size N. For MoE, Step Law validates active parameter count N_a over total N as the correct HP-scaling variable, cutting relative prediction error to 0.5% versus >4× larger error using a DeepSeek-style total-N formula, and its 0.94‰ deviation from exhaustive grid search dominates OpenAI-style (9.51‰), Microsoft-style (9.25‰), DeepSeek-style (9.26‰), and Porian-style (3.71‰) laws. The loss landscape under fixed (N,D) is convex but broad near the optimum — the practical payoff of a precise HP law is mostly about avoiding gross misallocation, not squeezing out the last 0.1%.

Farseer (2506.10972, StepFun, “Predictable Scale Part II” [PAPER], 6/2025) attacks the loss surface itself rather than HP selection, replacing Chinchilla’s separable L(N,D) with a form where the data-scaling exponent is itself a function of N (full form in the table below), fit via a three-stage “Differential Piecewise + Multi-round Iterative” procedure across ~1,000 models and ~3M H100-hours.

LR-annealing full-curve fitting (2408.11029, Tencent [PAPER], 8/2024) is the mechanism that makes the whole ladder economically tractable: L(s) = L₀ + A·S₁^-α - C·S₂, where S₁ is an “area under the LR curve” generalization of token count and S₂ the extra loss reduction attributable to LR decay. Fitting this from just 1-2 actual runs predicts the entire loss curve at every step under any scheduler — a stable-phase WSD checkpoint plus a short decay run stands in for a from-scratch cosine run at that budget. This is the mechanism underneath MiniCPM’s cheap ladder and the reason WSD (not cosine) has become the ladder-default schedule field-wide; the recipe below returns to it as the single highest-leverage cost lever available.

Gemstones (2502.06857, “A Model Suite for Multi-Faceted Scaling Laws” [PAPER, NeurIPS 2025], 2/2025) is the field’s built-in caution against over-trusting any of the above: 4,000+ open checkpoints, transformers to 2B params, 10T+ cumulative tokens, deliberately varying LR, cooldown schedule, and width/depth ratio within the ladder. Its central finding is that scaling-law prescriptions — the compute-optimal N/D split, in particular — are highly sensitive to which checkpoint subset and which LR/cooldown regime the fit is run on, a direct warning against copying a published law’s numeric constants onto a differently-shaped architecture.

Critical batch size: not what gradient-noise-scale predicts

The classical McCandlish et al. (2018) framework gives η*(B) = η_max/(1+B_noise/B) for SGD, with the noise scale estimated cheaply from per-device gradient variance. Su Jianlin’s kexue.fm derivation extends this to Adam via a SignSGD mean-field approximation and corrects a mid-2024 paper’s claim about the Adam-specific “Surge phenomenon” (2405.14578 [PAPER]): past a second, higher critical batch size β_noise, the optimal learning rate for Adam-family optimizers can decrease as batch size increases further, rather than merely saturating as SGD’s η* does. The original paper’s per-coordinate approximation forces the conclusion Surge “almost always” occurs; Su’s mean-field treatment shows the reversal is conditional on β_noise < 1, and derives a second, Hessian-free noise-scale estimator from measured (steps, examples) pairs (kexue.fm/archives/10542 [BLOG, high-SNR independent technical blog], 11/2024).

Ai2’s “Critical Batch Size Revisited” (2505.23971, NeurIPS 2025 Spotlight [PAPER], 5/2025) tests directly whether gradient-noise-scale is a usable CBS proxy at LLM scale and finds it is not: for OLMo 7B the noise-scale estimate does not even track the qualitative trend of the empirically-measured CBS, underestimating true CBS by orders of magnitude at both 1B and 7B. Their branched-training protocol — train at batch k·B and LR f(k)·η over a fixed 2B-token window, find the max k with ≤ε loss degradation — measures CBS directly instead, finding it starts near zero at initialization, rises rapidly over the first ~50K tokens, then plateaus (≈4096 documents / ~16M tokens in their setup). This motivates batch-size warmup as standard practice: start small, double batch size (with √k LR rescaling for Adam) whenever measured CBS exceeds 2× the current batch size. Applied to OLMo-1B, this produced a run reaching slightly better final loss in 43% fewer gradient steps than a fixed-small-batch baseline, while a naive fixed-large-batch run used 75% fewer steps but landed at a measurably worse loss — a concrete quantification of the cost of getting CBS scheduling wrong in either direction.

Optimizer choice materially shifts CBS: multiple 2025-2026 sources converge on Muon roughly doubling to tripling usable CBS versus AdamW at matched validation loss (survey synthesis citing Moonlight-lineage results, howtoscalenn.github.io [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY], 12/2025 — not independently verified against primary sources; theoretical treatment in “Convergence Bound and Critical Batch Size of Muon Optimizer” [PAPER]). AdamW’s CBS tends to plateau around ~10M tokens for many setups, a soft ceiling on data-parallel scale-out before switching optimizer families becomes the higher-leverage move — relevant to the DP/EP composition math in Ch16.

Batch-size schedule, not just the CBS point estimate, has its own recent literature. “How to Allocate Your Tokens?” proposes a three-term law L(N,M,K) = E + A/N^α + B/M^β + C/K^γ (M = batch tokens, K = steps, D = MK) whose optimum M* = [βB/γC]^(1/(β+γ)) · D^(γ/(β+γ)) depends only on total token budget D, not on N — empirically M* = 0.67·D^0.566, closely matching Step Law’s independently-derived B(D) = 0.58·D^0.571 despite a different methodology, needing only 2 batch sizes per (N,D) point (28% of a full grid) to recover the same law (2607.01487 [PAPER], 2026). “Fast Catch-Up, Late Switching” finds the optimal within-run batch-ramp shape is task-dependent: easy objectives favor continuously increasing batch size, hard/late-saturating objectives favor staying small then switching late (2602.14208 [PAPER], 2026).

MoE-specific scaling laws: efficiency leverage and the joint form

MoE scaling in 2025-2026 is treated as its own joint law over (N or N_active, D, granularity/sparsity, shared-expert ratio) rather than a dense law with a correction bolted on — the choices quantified here feed directly into the architecture decisions in Ch5. Three independent efforts converge on compatible pictures.

Ant Group’s Ling team defines Efficiency Leverage (EL) — the compute ratio between a dense model and its loss-equivalent MoE counterpart, so EL=5 means the MoE needs 1/5 the FLOPs for equal loss — from 300+ models trained to 28B params. EL is driven by activation ratio and total compute budget via clean power laws, while granularity is a non-linear modulator with a genuine interior optimum rather than “more granular is better,” predicting a 3.1%-activation, granularity-12 MoE achieves >7× EL at 1e22 FLOPs, validated by training Ling-mini-beta (0.85B active) to match a 6.1B dense model on 1T identical tokens using >7× less compute (Ling EL, 2507.17702 [PAPER], 7/2025) — a result that directly informed the production Ling-2.0/Ling-1T configuration: 256 routed experts, 8 active plus 1 shared (~3.5% activation), up to 7× active-compute efficiency versus dense at full-scale FP8 (Ling-1T, 2510.22115 [PAPER/OFFICIAL], 10/2025).

“Joint MoE Scaling Laws” fits a joint L(N_active, D, Ê) power-law form, where Ê is a monotonic transform of expert count, across 280+ experiments up to 2.7B active/5B total params, and lands on a genuinely counter-intuitive headline: MoE can be more memory-efficient than dense at matched loss, contradicting the usual framing that MoE trades memory for compute savings (2502.05172, ICML 2025 [PAPER], 2/2025).

The “Comprehensive MoE Scaling Law” runs 446 controlled experiments across five factors — D, N, N_active, active-expert-count G, and shared-expert-ratio S — and finds optimal G and S are independent of model architecture and data size, i.e., a granularity/shared-ratio choice tuned once transfers across scale, a rare genuinely scale-invariant MoE knob. But optimal N_active/N shrinks (gets sparser) as N grows: a 10T-param model’s compute-optimal active fraction is not the same fraction a lab would pick at a 100B-param ladder rung, and must be swept as its own function of scale (2509.23678 [PAPER], 9/2025).

Cross-checked against Step Law’s finding that active-parameter-count HP formulas cut relative error 4× versus total-parameter-count formulas, the field is converging hard on N_active — not N_total — as the correct primary MoE scaling variable, with granularity and shared-ratio as secondary, largely scale-invariant knobs, and top-level activation ratio as the variable that must itself follow its own power law in N. This is a materially different picture from “fit Chinchilla on active params and call it done.”

Data-constrained scaling at 30T+ token budgets

Muennighoff et al.’s foundational data-constrained law established the reference points every later paper measures against: up to 4 epochs of repetition is roughly equivalent to unique data, and the value of additional compute decays toward zero beyond roughly 16 epochs, tested to 900B tokens/9B params (2305.16264 [PAPER], 5/2023). “Practical Scaling Laws: Converting Compute into Performance in a Data-Constrained World” extends this with a three-term decomposition L(N,D,T) = E + (L₀-E)·h/(1+h) separating undercapacity, undertraining, and overfitting, distinguishing unique examples D from total-examples-seen T, and recommending WSD schedules with log-spaced compute checkpoints — the same checkpoint-reuse trick underlying MiniCPM and Tencent’s LR-annealing law, applied to the data-constrained regime — so a single long run yields many (N,D,T) loss measurements cheaply. In its extreme-scarcity regime (ρ_D/ρ_C = 10^13), the fit implies optimal model size shrinks ~20× versus the data-unconstrained optimum, optimal epoch count climbs to ~1,250, and ~86% of compute budget should go to data repetition (2605.09189 [PAPER], 5/2026) — an extreme-regime number, but directionally the lesson frontier labs training 15-18T+ token corpora with multiple passes over higher-quality subsets already lean on. For a 30-40T-token target corpus, unique high-quality web/code/book supply is the binding constraint before parameter count is — see Ch10, Ch12.

Precision-aware scaling laws: the 7-8 bit claim and its pushback

Kumar et al.’s “Scaling Laws for Precision” introduces an effective parameter count N_eff(P) ≈ N·(1-e^(-P/γ))³ that lets loss degradation from low-precision training or post-training quantization be predicted as if the model simply had fewer parameters. Its two headline claims: jointly optimizing N, D, P under fixed compute gives P* ≈ 7-8 bits as compute-optimal, quantifying that BF16 is somewhat wasteful and full INT4 somewhat too aggressive; and a PTQ degradation formula that increases with D — meaning past some token budget, additional pretraining data actively harms a model destined for low-precision deployment (Kumar et al., 2411.04330, ICLR 2025 [PAPER], 11/2024).

A competing Chinese-lab law, self-named “Capybara,” independently derives a cost-optimal FP precision range of 4-8 bits — wider and lower than Kumar’s — and confirms the qualitative critical-data-size finding, while decomposing the loss dependence into exponent-bit versus mantissa-bit contributions, finding exponent bits matter slightly more (2501.02423, ICML 2025 [PAPER], 1/2025). Neither paper anticipated native FP4 training working well: “Quartet” reports FP4-native recipes with proper stochastic rounding and scaling reaching parity with higher precision, meaning the practical precision floor moved below both papers’ stated optimum within about a year (Quartet, 2505.14669 [PAPER], 5/2025) — the field’s clearest illustration of a durable functional form (effective-parameter framework, data-size-dependent PTQ degradation) attached to a numeric constant with a shelf life measured in single-digit months.

In practice, frontier labs mostly bypass the “pick one global precision” framing both laws assume: DeepSeek-V3 and Ling-2.0 both run full-scale FP8 mixed-precision training with fine-grained block/tile-wise scaling factors rather than a single uniform P. DeepSeek’s own hardware-co-design retrospective flags FP8’s constrained Tensor Core accumulation precision on Hopper as a live stability issue requiring architecture-level workarounds — the precision-scaling-law numbers are necessary but not sufficient; engineering reality is dominated by which fine-grained quantization scheme a given accelerator generation supports well (DeepSeek-V3 hardware retrospective, 2505.09343 [PAPER/OFFICIAL], 5/2025). Ch8 covers the full FP8→MXFP8→NVFP4 recipe landscape; the point to carry forward here is that a lab should fit its own effective-parameter-count curve on its own hardware/recipe rather than import Kumar’s or Capybara’s numeric optimum.

Inference-aware and distillation scaling laws

Two adjacent law families matter for a lab that will also serve the model it trains. Sardana et al.’s “Beyond Chinchilla-Optimal” modifies compute-optimal N,D selection to account for lifetime inference volume rather than training FLOPs alone, finding loss keeps improving as tokens-per-parameter is pushed to extreme ranges — up to 10,000 — i.e., overtraining relative to Chinchilla is often globally optimal once serving cost is counted; a lab expecting ~2T inference tokens over a model’s lifetime could cut total train+serve compute 17% by training a 7B model instead of Chinchilla-optimal 13B (Sardana et al., 2401.00448 [PAPER], 1/2024). MoE-specific inference-aware laws remain immature in the public literature — a gap worth flagging. Busbridge et al.’s Distillation Scaling Laws give a compute-optimal teacher/student FLOPs allocation and resolve the “capacity gap” (student quality degrading as teacher grows) as an addressable learning-capacity gap, not a fixed penalty: with many students to distill or an existing teacher, distillation beats from-scratch training up to a threshold scaling predictably with student size; with exactly one student and a teacher that must also be trained, direct pretraining is usually preferable (Busbridge et al., 2502.08606, Apple [PAPER], 2/2025) — relevant to a 10T-param flagship alongside a family of smaller served models.

How labs actually build ladders, and what it costs

Three open-lab programs document the ladder-construction process in enough detail to serve as templates (numbers in the table below).

Ai2/OLMo runs a two-step prediction methodology — fit N,D → intermediate loss, then intermediate loss → downstream task accuracy, because direct N,D → task-accuracy fits are noisier (Bhagia et al., 2412.04403, COLM 2025 [PAPER], 12/2024), using the OLMES eval framework to stabilize the second-stage fit target.

Marin (Stanford CRFM) makes the entire ladder process public, including a standing GitHub issue tracking scaling-law experiments across extraction method, mixture ratio, and quality-filter choice. Its Speedrun leaderboard ranks submissions by “Projected C4-EN BPB at 1e22 FLOPs” — the slope of a method’s scaling curve extrapolated to a fixed reference budget, not raw score at whatever scale was trained (marin.community [OFFICIAL-BLOG + CODE], 5/2025 ongoing) — a rigor upgrade worth adopting for internal ablation programs generally.

HuggingFace’s SmolLM3 / “Smol Training Playbook” gives the most transparent public accounting of ladder cost as a fraction of final-run cost: the primary ablation setup trains the full 3B target architecture on 100B tokens instead of the final 11T, shrinking the token budget rather than the model — a different ladder-axis choice than Ai2’s, costing “more than half the cost of our main training run” by the playbook’s own accounting. Its methodological caution is worth carrying forward: small-scale ablations are trustworthy for negative results, much less trustworthy for positive ones, which need confirmation at a reasonable token count before being trusted (HF Smol Training Playbook [OFFICIAL-BLOG], 10/2025).

Step Law’s own ladder is the most extreme documented ratio: ~1M H800-GPU-hours on the law-fitting sweep alone against DeepSeek-V3’s entire 2.664M H800-GPU-hour pretraining budget for a comparable-era, comparable-hardware MoE effort — instrumentation-only spend producing no deployable model at all. This is the strongest evidence available that serious HP-law fitting for a frontier-scale run is a double-digit-percentage line item against the final training budget, not a small ablation, and should be staffed accordingly.

Why ladders fail: extrapolation breakdown and downstream unreliability

Multiple independent 2025-2026 results converge on the same warning from different directions. A meta-analysis of published downstream scaling-law fits finds predictable scaling holds only about 39% of the time, with failure modes including emergence-like jumps, inverse scaling, and — most operationally relevant — high sensitivity to seemingly benign choices like prompt format, eval-set choice, and checkpoint selection (2507.00885, EMNLP 2025 Findings [PAPER], 7/2025). Gemstones, discussed above, delivers a companion warning purely about methodology: the same training-run distribution, refit on different LR-schedule/checkpoint subsets, yields materially different compute-optimal prescriptions. Related work frames the problem as a two-stage compounding-error process — N,D→loss, then loss→accuracy — where models with near-identical loss can differ substantially in downstream performance, making loss necessary but insufficient as a ladder target (2512.08894 [PAPER], 12/2025).

Two cross-check methodologies are worth knowing. Observational scaling laws exploit the roughly 100 existing public model families as an implicit, free scaling sweep, fitting cross-family correlations to predict downstream behavior as capability scales; emergent-seeming benchmark jumps turn out to follow smooth sigmoidal curves once plotted against a suitable capability axis rather than raw compute, reconciling with the earlier “Are Emergent Abilities a Mirage?” argument that apparent discontinuities are measurement artifacts of non-smooth metrics (Ruan et al., 2405.10938, COLM 2024 [PAPER], 5/2024). Loss-to-loss prediction fits shifted power-law relationships directly between the losses of different models or datasets rather than fitting N,D→loss separately per dataset; these laws extrapolate well to 20× the largest fit FLOP budget, sometimes beating a single-dataset N,D law directly (Brandfonbrener et al., 2411.12925, TMLR 2025 [PAPER], 11/2024).

The composite lesson: loss-based ladders are comparatively trustworthy; anything downstream of loss needs its own validated bridge law, validated per-lab and per-eval-suite, and re-validated whenever checkpoint or schedule choices change. No lab should assume a bridge law fit at 1B tokens-per-param survives a schedule change, let alone a 100×+ scale jump.

The recipe: commissioning a laws program for a 10T/200B run

Synthesizing the above into a concrete ladder design — the “how to build your own laws” deliverable this chapter promised, not any single paper’s prescription:

1. Split the ladder into two decoupled sub-problems. (a) The N,D compute-allocation law and HP power laws (η, B) — cheap-ish, many small runs. (b) The loss→downstream-task bridge law — fewer but larger runs, since bridge laws are unreliable at small scale.

2. Sweep axes, in the order a 10T/200B lab should budget for them:

  • N × D grid at 5-7 log-spaced model sizes (Step Law used 60M→1B; a lab targeting 10T total should extend one or two rungs higher, e.g. to 3-8B, given Gemstones’ warning that shape/schedule sensitivity grows with extrapolation distance) crossed with 5+ data scales.
  • Active-parameter ratio and expert granularity as their own axis at fixed N and D, not inferred from a dense law. Expect granularity’s optimum to be roughly scale-invariant (tune once) but N_active/N’s optimum to be a function of N and swept across the ladder’s full range.
  • Batch size and LR jointly, via direct grid search at each (N,D) point (Step Law’s approach) or the cheaper branched CBS-measurement protocol (Ai2’s 2B-token windows) plus the token-budget-only three-term law as a cross-check — the latter needs only ~2 batch sizes per (N,D) cell instead of a full grid.
  • Precision as a genuine sweep axis at 2-3 model sizes if the run is FP8/FP4-bound: fit an in-house effective-parameter-count curve rather than importing Kumar’s or Capybara’s numeric P*, since the published constants are already stale relative to native FP4.
  • Data mixture and epoch count via the data-constrained scaling framework, since a 10T-param model on tens of trillions of tokens is likely to hit the unique-high-quality-token ceiling before a parameter ceiling — fit the multi-epoch decay law on the corpus’s own quality tiers rather than assuming Muennighoff’s public-web constants transfer (see Ch10, Ch12).

3. Run the entire ladder on WSD (or another checkpoint-reuse-compatible schedule), not cosine. Each run yields many (N,D) loss observations along its stable-phase trajectory plus a cheap decay-phase re-measurement via the Tencent LR-annealing law, instead of a fresh cosine run per grid point — the single highest-leverage cost-reduction lever in the entire practice.

4. Budget the ladder at 30-60% of final-run compute, not a rounding error. SmolLM3’s own accounting (58%) and Step Law’s absolute scale (~38% of a DeepSeek-V3-scale run) both land in this band; plan cluster time accordingly.

5. Fit at least 2-3 decades of compute before trusting extrapolation past the largest ladder rung, and treat any extrapolated number with Gemstones/2507.00885-grade skepticism — refit on a different schedule or checkpoint subset as an internal consistency check before committing the full run’s HPs.

6. Never assume a loss→downstream bridge law survives from ladder scale to target scale unchanged. Use the two-step Ai2-style prediction validated at 2+ intermediate scales, or loss-to-loss prediction as a lower-variance intermediate signal, or accept downstream-metric extrapolation is only ~39% likely to hold — let the loss-based law drive N/D/sparsity allocation, treat downstream predictions as directional.

7. Documented failure modes to plan around, not rediscover:

  • Fitting muP transfer on width alone and assuming it covers depth, batch, duration, or MoE granularity.
  • Trusting gradient-noise-scale as a CBS proxy — off by orders of magnitude at LLM scale.
  • Importing a published precision-scaling constant (7-8 bit, or 4-8 bit) instead of refitting on the run’s own hardware and quantization recipe.
  • Fixing MoE granularity or activation ratio at the value optimal at ladder scale and never re-deriving it at target N.
  • Trusting a positive small-scale ablation result without a large-enough token budget — SmolLM3’s most common ablation failure mode.
  • Fitting the loss→downstream bridge once and reusing it across eval-set or prompt-format changes.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

A laws program for this scale is not optional pre-work squeezed into idle cluster capacity; on the evidence above it is a 30-60%-of-final-run line item needing its own budget, cluster allocation, and schedule milestone before the target run is greenlit. The highest-leverage single design decision is running the entire ladder on WSD rather than cosine, since the LR-annealing full-curve law converts every stable-phase checkpoint into a free additional (N,D) observation — closer to Step Law’s economics than to a naive from-scratch grid, and worth adopting regardless of which other lever is chosen. Muon (or any spectral-optimizer choice, see Ch7) changes the CBS and muP picture enough that a lab cannot simply reuse an AdamW-fit ladder; both the batch-size ceiling and the width-transfer rules shift, and QK-Clip-style stabilization needs budgeting into the ladder’s mid-scale confirmation runs, not discovered at target scale as Kimi K2’s team was. The MoE-specific axes — N_active as the primary scaling variable, granularity/shared-ratio as a scale-invariant knob tunable once, activation ratio requiring its own scale-dependent sweep — should be fit on proprietary data explicitly, since Step Law and the comprehensive-MoE-law both show total-parameter-count formulas carry multiple-times-larger error than active-parameter-count formulas for the architecture class (see Ch5) this run targets. Finally, given DeepSeek’s own compute-optimal N/D split moved by roughly 7 percentage points between two in-house datasets of different quality (Δa = Δb ≈ 0.074, from a=0.450/b=0.550 on the lower-quality corpus to a=0.524/b=0.476 on the higher-quality one — arithmetic on the raw exponents from [DeepSeek LLM, 2401.02954], not a percentage-point figure the paper itself states), no published constant here — Step Law’s exponents, Kumar’s 7-8 bit optimum, Muennighoff’s epoch knee — should be treated as more than a starting prior; the corpus this run trains on is proprietary precisely where it matters most.

Chapter 7 · Part II — The Model

Optimizers and Stability at 10T Parameters

The optimizer line in a training-run design document is usually the shortest, least-argued-over entry in the whole spec — until the run is one-shot, costs several billion dollars, and the largest public validation of either candidate optimizer sits at a fraction of the target active-parameter count. For a 10T-total/200B-active MoE, the optimizer choice is a bet made under genuine extrapolation, and 2025-2026 supplied the first real evidence base for making it rather than guessing. This chapter lays out that evidence: Muon’s mechanics and trillion-parameter track record, the distributed-systems engineering required to run Newton-Schulz at 100,000-GPU scale, the adversarial re-benchmarks that punctured and then partially restored Muon’s headline speedup claims, the strict validation ceiling no optimizer has cleared above roughly 50B active MoE parameters, and the stability toolkit — QK-Norm, QK-Clip, spike taxonomies, automated rollback — any choice will need regardless.

Muon Mechanics and the Trillion-Parameter Evidence Base

Muon (MomentUm Orthogonalized by Newton-Schulz), due to Keller Jordan (2024), takes an SGD-momentum update matrix and replaces it with its nearest semi-orthogonal matrix via a quintic Newton-Schulz (NS) iteration rather than an exact SVD [OFFICIAL-BLOG, kellerjordan.github.io/posts/muon, verified 7/2026]. Five fixed-coefficient NS steps (a=3.4445, b=-4.7750, c=2.0315) drive singular values toward 1, run stably in bf16, and apply independently per 2D parameter matrix — never to embeddings, the LM head, or scalar/vector parameters, which stay on AdamW in every production implementation found [PAPER, arXiv:2502.16982; PAPER, arXiv:2507.20534]. FLOP overhead scales as Θ(T·m/B), measured at roughly 0.7% at NanoGPT-speedrun scale and estimated at ~0.5% at Llama-405B scale [OFFICIAL-BLOG, kellerjordan.github.io/posts/muon].

Su Jianlin’s kexue.fm — effectively Moonshot AI’s public research diary — supplies the cleanest first-principles account: an ideal optimizer’s update should minimize Tr(GᵀΔW) subject to a stability bound ρ(ΔW)≤η under some norm; SGD is the Frobenius-norm solution, Muon the spectral-norm solution, a strictly tighter bound on output perturbation [BLOG, kexue.fm/archives/10739, 2/2025]. Su also proves the special case β=0 of 2018’s Shampoo is exactly msign(G) — Muon and Shampoo coincide at that limit [BLOG, kexue.fm/archives/10592, 12/2024].

Moonlight (Moonshot, arXiv:2502.16982) is the load-bearing scaling-law evidence: fitted curves give Muon: 2.506·C^(-0.052) vs AdamW: 2.608·C^(-0.054), translating to the claim that Muon needs only ~52% of AdamW’s training FLOPs to match loss at compute-optimal allocation [PAPER, arXiv:2502.16982, 2/2025]. Two changes were required to scale raw Muon past small models: weight decay (orthogonalized updates carry no natural shrinkage the way AdamW’s adaptive denominator does), and a per-parameter update-scale correction, since raw NS output has RMS ≈1/√max(A,B) for an [A,B] matrix — Moonlight rescales each update by 0.2·√max(A,B) to match AdamW’s typical update RMS, letting teams reuse existing LR schedules without per-layer-shape re-tuning [PAPER, arXiv:2502.16982]. Su flags at least four distinct Muon scaling-factor conventions circulating in the wild, and that conflating them — or inverting d_in/d_out between PyTorch’s and Keras’s conventions, a bug he documents as present in Keras 3.12’s built-in Muon — is a common, silent source of bad results; with every convention but Moonlight’s, peak LR needs roughly a 10x multiplier over Adam’s [BLOG, kexue.fm/archives/11416, 11/2025]. Moonlight itself: 2.24B active/15.29B total MoE, 5.7T tokens, weight decay 0.1, momentum 0.95; Muon-trained weights show measurably higher singular-value entropy than Adam-trained weights, the same signal Su later uses to explain Muon’s MaxLogit-explosion propensity — though he has since begun publicly questioning whether higher entropy is actually good [PAPER, arXiv:2502.16982; BLOG, kexue.fm/archives/11767, 5/2026].

Kimi K2 (1.04T total/32.6B active, MLA attention, 15.5T tokens) is the largest confirmed pure-Muon pretraining run and the origin of MuonClip [PAPER, arXiv:2507.20534, 7/2025]. During a 9B-active ablation, max pre-softmax attention logits “rapidly exceed 1000” under vanilla Muon, “more frequently with Muon but less with AdamW.” Soft-capping bounds only the effect of logit growth, and QK-Norm is “incompatible with MLA’s non-materialized keys,” since MLA’s compressed KV representation isn’t directly normalizable at inference time [PAPER, arXiv:2507.20534]. QK-Clip operates in weight space, post-hoc: after each Muon step, if a per-head max softmax input S_max^h exceeds τ=100, rescale that head’s Q/K weights by γ_h = min(1, τ/S_max^h) — √γ_h to the head-specific MLA components, γ_h to the rotary component, with the shared rotary component untouched to avoid cross-head interference [PAPER, arXiv:2507.20534]. Su’s account, published ten days before the K2 report, adds telemetry the paper doesn’t: across ~220,000 training steps, the first head crossed τ around step 7,000, and a roughly 70,000-step “tug of war” between Muon (pushing MaxLogit up) and QK-Clip (pulling it down) preceded all heads settling below τ, after which QK-Clip essentially stopped firing — evidence, in Su’s reading, that weight decay alone would eventually have solved the problem, but QK-Clip is what lets the model survive early training [BLOG, kexue.fm/archives/11126, 7/2025]. He credits DeepSeek’s Auxiliary-Loss-Free MoE balancing as QK-Clip’s conceptual ancestor, and flags that per-head clipping requires reassembling logically-per-head slices of a sharded weight matrix, “not hard on top of Muon, noticeably more complex on top of Adam-style per-shard state” [BLOG, kexue.fm/archives/11126, 7/2025]. K2’s Figure 3 shows the full per-step loss curve for the entire 15.5T-token run with no visible spikes — one of three “zero loss spikes” data points now on record (with DeepSeek-V3/AdamW and, implicitly, DeepSeek-V4/Muon), a sharp break from PaLM-era norms of roughly twenty manually-handled spikes per run [PAPER, arXiv:2204.02311].

DeepSeek-V4 is the single most consequential — and least publicized relative to K2 — data point in this chapter. DeepSeek-V3 used plain AdamW (β1=0.9, β2=0.95, weight decay 0.1) and reported zero loss spikes and zero rollbacks across its full 14.8T-token run [PAPER, arXiv:2412.19437, 12/2024]. DeepSeek-V4 switched to Muon — “we employ the Muon optimizer for the majority of modules in DeepSeek-V4 series due to its faster convergence and improved training stability” — reusing prior AdamW hyperparameters after the RMS-matching rescale [PAPER, arXiv:2606.19348, 6/2026]. V4-Pro (1.6T total/49B active) and V4-Flash (284B total/13B active) trained on 32-33T tokens, more than double V3’s budget. Where K2 solves attention-logit explosion with a post-hoc weight-space clip, V4 solves it architecturally, applying RMSNorm directly to attention queries and KV entries — no QK-Clip needed [PAPER, arXiv:2606.19348]. This suggests QK-Clip may be a workaround for MLA’s incompatibility with standard QK-Norm rather than a generically necessary Muon companion — an inference from DeepSeek’s own architectural choice, not a confirmed causal finding by either lab, since neither has published the counterfactual (QK-Clip retrofitted onto a non-MLA model, or direct RMSNorm-on-QK retrofitted onto MLA) that would isolate the variable. The right stability mechanism depends on attention architecture (Ch5), not optimizer alone. Two other named V4 techniques, “Anticipatory Routing” and “SwiGLU Clamping,” lack enough surviving detail to describe precisely; treat as open questions. On distributed engineering, V4 bin-packs dense-parameter matrices to ZeRO ranks and optimizes each expert independently, quantizing MoE gradients to bf16 during synchronization [PAPER, arXiv:2606.19348] — detail Ch4 covers in full. No spike count is disclosed for V4; given DeepSeek’s stability discipline, the silence is more plausibly “still very few” than an admission, but that is an inference, not a fact.

GLM-4.5/4.6/4.7 (355B total/32B active, Muon + QK-Norm) supply the most complete published Muon hyperparameter recipe of any lab: NS iterations N=5, momentum μ=0.95, update RMS scaled to 0.2, Muon on all parameters except embeddings/bias/RMSNorm, weight decay 0.1, no dropout [PAPER, arXiv:2508.06471, §2.4, 8/2025]. Su confirms this is the Moonlight-Muon variant specifically — cross-lab propagation of Moonshot’s implementation into Zhipu’s stack via blog post and open code [BLOG, kexue.fm/archives/11416, 11/2025]. GLM-4.5 also found WSD-scheduled models underfit relative to cosine decay on SimpleQA/MMLU and chose cosine deliberately [PAPER, arXiv:2508.06471, §2.4]. “Muon: Training and Trade-offs with Latent Attention and MoE” independently confirms the same three-way split found in Moonlight, K2, and DeepSeek-V4 — router on Adam, experts on Muon, embeddings/norms/LM head on Adam, embarrassingly parallel across experts since NS has no cross-expert coupling [PAPER, arXiv:2509.24406, 9/2025]. This is now close to a converged default for any lab adopting Muon.

Distributed Newton-Schulz at 10T Parameters

Running NS orthogonalization across a tensor-parallel-sharded momentum buffer at 100,000-GPU scale is a solved-but-nontrivial engineering problem, not an open research question, as of mid-2026. NVIDIA’s Megatron Core “Emerging-Optimizers” library ships three sharding modes: duplicated (all-gather momentum across the TP domain once, every GPU runs full NS locally — one comm op, redundant compute, latency-optimized), distributed (NS itself distributed across TP ranks, an all-reduce after each iteration’s first matmul), and blockwise (no cross-GPU communication; each GPU orthogonalizes only its local block, approximate) [OFFICIAL-BLOG, developer.nvidia.com, 2026]. A companion layer-wise distributed optimizer assigns whole layers, not individual elements, to DP ranks, so each GPU holds complete matrices for its layers and NS runs without cross-rank gather. Measured throughput on GB300 NVL72 is close to a wash against AdamW and, by one accounting convention, favorable [OFFICIAL-BLOG, developer.nvidia.com, 2026]:

The “MFU is higher with Muon” framing is a methodological subtlety: part of the reported gain is an artifact of counting NS’s own matmuls as useful FLOPs, though raw throughput is still favorable either way. torch.optim.Muon shipped in PyTorch 2.9 core but does not itself support distributed training — the Megatron sharding layer above is a necessary addition, exposing a match_rms_adamw flag implementing Moonshot’s RMS-rescaling trick directly [CODE, PyTorch docs; GitHub issue #148819]. Megatron-LM’s roadmap targets layer-wise distributed Muon with torch_dist checkpoint format in Q1 2026 and Muon+Megatron-FSDP integration in Q2 2026 [CODE, github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM issues #4003/#4997, 2026].

The gap that matters most for a 10T/200B design: no explicit MoE-expert-parallel-interaction detail was surfaced in the available NVIDIA documentation, despite EP-heavy configurations appearing in the library’s own experimental setup [OFFICIAL-BLOG, developer.nvidia.com, 2026]. The Qwen3-30B-A3B-shaped measurement is an MoE model showing overhead proportionally similar to the dense case — reassuring evidence per-expert NS doesn’t blow up disproportionately with expert count — but not a measurement anywhere close to 200B-active, high-expert-count.

Dion (Microsoft, arXiv:2504.05295) is the leading alternative distributed design, replacing NS with amortized power iteration on a low-rank approximation of the momentum buffer via decoupled momentum buffers, so orthonormalization never needs a fully-materialized gradient matrix on any device, compatible with simultaneous DDP+FSDP+TP [PAPER, arXiv:2504.05295, 4/2025]. Validated only to 3B parameters, with benefits reported to improve with scale, but no adoption in any named frontier model as of mid-2026 — a research proposal, not a production choice.

One memory angle for the HBM budget: 8-bit blockwise-quantized Muon optimizer states report up to 62% memory reduction with zero measured accuracy loss to 2.7B parameters, since Muon’s momentum-only state is uniquely compatible with simple linear quantization [PAPER, arXiv:2509.23106, 9/2025] — a single-paper result with no cross-lab or larger-scale replication found in the corpus, so treat it as a provisional lever pending independent confirmation, not a settled one; still under-discussed and relevant where 200B-active optimizer-state memory is itself a serious HBM line item.

The Adversarial Re-Benchmarks and the Critical-Batch-Size Case

The “Muon is 2x AdamW” headline that circulated through 2025 does not survive careful re-benchmarking intact. “Fantastic Pretraining Optimizers and Where to Find Them” (Sept 2025) retested 11 optimizers with fair per-optimizer tuning and found matrix-based optimizers’ advantage over AdamW shrinks to roughly 1.1x at 1.2B parameters, with claimed 2x speedups in prior papers traced to under-tuned AdamW baselines [PAPER, arXiv:2509.02046, 9/2025]. A NeurIPS 2025 follow-up partially reverses that: with proper muP-based hyperparameter transfer, Muon and Shampoo sustain 1.4x and 1.3x compute-equivalent speedups over AdamW up to 1.4B parameters [PAPER, arXiv:2512.05620, 12/2025], and a June 2026 “Hyperball” follow-up pushes Muon’s advantage to 20-30% and growing with scale [PAPER, arXiv:2606.16899, 6/2026]. The lesson: any single-paper speedup number is provisional until it survives adversarial re-tuning, and the most recent evidence tilts back toward a real, scale-persistent Muon edge — but “persistent to 1.4B parameters” is still five orders of magnitude below a 200B-active target.

Essential AI’s “Practical Efficiency of Muon” supplies the claim most relevant to a 100,000-GPU plan, since it’s about batch size rather than parameter count: Muon needs 10-15% fewer tokens than AdamW to reach the same loss at batch sizes up to 16M tokens, and the relative advantage is non-decreasing — often growing — as batch size increases past the point where AdamW’s own data efficiency starts degrading (the critical-batch-size regime) [PAPER, arXiv:2505.02222, 5/2025]. At tested scales (100M-4B parameters, batch 128K-16M tokens, Gemma3 architecture), the absolute AdamW token deficit reaches ~2B extra tokens — the strongest evidence Muon’s data-efficiency edge doesn’t erode at exactly the extreme-batch regime a 100,000-GPU plan is forced into. The paper’s muP telescoping protocol (doubling calibration width while shrinking the search grid by 4^(-1/k) per doubling, tuning overhead O(C·log N)) reports transfer holding to 3.7B for both peak LR and weight decay, a claim contested at MoE-specific scale below. Their recipe: Muon on all matrix layers, AdamW for embeddings/norms, tune only peak LR and weight decay.

The modded-nanogpt speedrun repository — cloned and inspected directly for this research — is a load-bearing, dated primary source for which optimizer micro-techniques survive contact with a competitive, third-party-timed benchmark (Prime Intellect times every record, FineWeb, 8×H100) [CODE, github.com/KellerJordan/modded-nanogpt, verified 7/13/2026]. Muon has been present in every one of 84 records since #3 (10/4/24), and the record has fallen from 45 minutes to 1.32 minutes: Polar Express replaced Newton-Schulz at #38 (9/29/25); NorMuon was adopted at #41 (10/24/25), 18 days after its arXiv posting; per-head-pair Q/K orthogonalization landed at #80 (4/8/26). The README’s own point — any contributor was free to reset the baseline to well-tuned AdamW across “12 records set by 7 different researchers” and didn’t — is revealed preference under adversarial, third-party-audited conditions.
