NorMuon adds per-neuron second-order-statistics normalization on top of orthogonalization, reporting 21.74% fewer steps than AdamW at 1.1B parameters and roughly half of AdamW’s memory footprint [PAPER, arXiv:2510.05491, 10/2025] — unvalidated, like everything here, above the 1-2B class.

AdamW: The Incumbent’s Actual 2025-2026 Track Record

AdamW remains the only optimizer with a disclosed, completed validation above 400B parameters, and it is worth tabulating what frontier labs actually shipped with rather than assuming the field has moved on:

Adafactor (Shazeer & Stern, 2018) was Google’s standard for T5 and the unfactored PaLM variant, but no public evidence surfaced of Adafactor use in any Google frontier model since — Gemini’s optimizer remains undisclosed. A recurring secondary-literature claim that “higher-order preconditioning has been applied in large-scale training runs, such as Gemini-1.5 Flash” traces back to citations of Google’s own Distributed Shampoo research, not to a primary source confirming Shampoo actually trained Gemini; treat as [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, unconfirmed as of 7/2026]. The qualitative pattern across every AdamW-disclosing lab is close to universal — β1=0.9, β2=0.95, weight decay 0.1, gradient clip 1.0 — varying mainly in peak LR, warmup length, and batch-ramp schedule. The 2025-2026 shift is Muon displacing AdamW specifically for matrix parameters; no lab has gone “all-Muon,” and AdamW remains the fallback for embeddings, norms, router, and head in every Muon-using system found, including DeepSeek-V4.

Shampoo, SOAP, and the Preconditioning Road Not (Yet) Taken

MLCommons AlgoPerf 2024 remains the strongest controlled evidence that non-diagonal preconditioning beats Adam on wall-clock, not just step-count: Distributed Shampoo (Meta) won the external-tuning ruleset, 28% faster wall-clock than the baseline, while Schedule-Free AdamW won the self-tuning ruleset [OFFICIAL-BENCHMARK, mlcommons.org, 8/2024]. Despite this, no frontier lab has publicly confirmed using Distributed Shampoo, SOAP, or PSGD for a >10B-parameter production run; Meta’s actively-developed optimizers/distributed_shampoo repository signals continued internal interest [CODE, github.com/facebookresearch/optimizers], but signal, not deployment. MARS/MARS-M and Lion likewise show no frontier-scale validation, Lion specifically degrading with scale in the adversarial benchmark above and surviving only as a niche in vision-language contrastive pretraining [PAPER, arXiv:2411.10438; arXiv:2510.21800; arXiv:2509.02046]. Schedule-free approaches (ScheduleFree+, arXiv:2605.19095, 6/2026) claim a 31% improvement over WSD at 1000 tokens/param but have no confirmed frontier adoption. On the theory frontier, Thinking Machines’ “Modular Manifolds” (Bernstein, Sept 2025) constrains weight matrices to the Stiefel manifold and derives “Manifold Muon” via dual ascent plus msign retraction, with a composability calculus for budgeting per-layer LR — a rival to muP, validated only on toy models [OFFICIAL-BLOG, thinkingmachines.ai/blog/modular-manifolds, 9/2025]. Scion (arXiv:2502.07529) generalizes the family to arbitrary norm-ball linear-minimization oracles, reporting ~30% fewer ViT/ImageNet epochs with momentum interpolating directly into weight decay — again with no MoE-scale validation. None of this is production-ready for a 10T run; worth tracking, not betting on.

The Strict Validation Ceiling

Being strict about “publicly validated” (named model, disclosed optimizer, disclosed scale, completed run) and strict about active, not total, parameters, the record as of mid-2026 is stark:

No optimizer choice has a public, disclosed, completed-training validation above ~50B active parameters for an MoE, or above ~540B for a dense model. A 200B-active design sits 4x past the largest validated MoE active-parameter count for Muon. GPT-4 (rumored ~280B active of ~1.76T total, never confirmed) [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, widely repeated 2023-2026] and Llama 4 Behemoth (288B active target, never shipped) are the closest “almost-precedents,” and neither has a disclosed optimizer. Comparisons citing total parameters (where 1-2T totals are now unremarkable) systematically launder over this active-parameter gap.

The 2026 Stability Toolkit

The field has converged on “normalize Q/K somehow” as the dominant attention-stability strategy, and abandoned pure logit soft-capping:

A numerics-level, architecture-independent complement: Su Jianlin reviews evidence for a BF16 round-to-even failure — a “two big, one tiny, same-sign” accumulation pattern structurally common in softmax-attention output, producing a systematic positive rounding bias that compounds over training [BLOG, kexue.fm/archives/11371, reviewing an external paper, 10/2025]. The mitigation widens the softmax denominator’s subtracted max (S − β·S_max, β≥2) so residual terms underflow cleanly; Su flags it as complementary to, not a substitute for, QK-Clip/QK-Norm.

Spike response has moved from PaLM’s fully manual playbook — roughly 20 spikes on the 540B run, each handled by rolling back ~100 steps and skipping the triggering batch [PAPER, arXiv:2204.02311, 2022] — to automated, tiered systems distinguishing narrow, self-recovering spikes from wide, persistent ones, escalating only the latter to full rollback. AdaGC operationalizes this with per-tensor adaptive thresholds from an EMA of each tensor’s clipped-gradient-norm history, reducing spike incidence to zero and improving downstream accuracy 1.3-2.5% over global clipping [PAPER, arXiv:2502.11034, 2/2025]. Root-cause attribution is now a named discipline: “Flare” categorizes divergence into data, numerics, and optimizer-state buckets [PAPER, arXiv:2502.05413, 2/2025], and OLMo2 traced a concrete data-side cause — long repeated n-gram sequences — to an n-gram-repeat filter added to the pipeline [PAPER, arXiv:2501.00656, 1/2025], a reminder not every spike is an optimizer problem.

Stas Bekman’s ml-engineering supplies a complementary, field-tested taxonomy: seven named spike patterns, most dangerous of which is a silent repeat-data false-plateau — a PyTorch-Lightning DataSampler resume bug caused exact data repetition across stitched training segments, producing what looked like loss improvement, caught only by manually re-stitching wandb runs to find the “progress” was pure memorization [CODE, training/instabilities/training-loss-patterns.md, github.com/stas00/ml-engineering, verified 7/2026]. It is the one pattern with no visible anomaly, likeliest to survive undetected through the dozens-to-hundreds of resumes a 10T-MoE run will require; the only defense is deliberately testing early resumes for repeat data. The same source’s STD-init recommendation — sqrt(1/(3·NHIDDEN)) over Megatron’s naive 0.02 default — is credited as one reason BLOOM-176B succeeded where a pre-BLOOM 104B fp16 attempt with the default init never got past a few thousand steps; and a study of Adam divergence up to 546B parameters found the fix for time-correlated divergence is setting epsilon to 0, not raising it [PAPER, arXiv:2304.09871, cited in ml-engineering, 07/2026].

Worth stating plainly: full-run EMA of weights maintained continuously throughout pretraining is not standard frontier practice as of mid-2026. What is used is post-hoc averaging of only the last few checkpoints — a final-model-quality technique, not a continuous-training-time stability tool.

WSD vs Cosine, muP-for-MoE Transfer, and Per-Block LR

The field has not converged on a single LR-schedule shape, and the disagreement is recent and live. DeepSeek-V3 holds LR constant after a 2000-step warmup until 10T of its 14.8T tokens, then cosine-decays the remainder; Kimi K2 does the identical shape (constant 2e-4 for 10T of 15.5T tokens, then cosine decay to 2e-5) — both label this inconsistently (K2 calls it “WSD,” V3 doesn’t name it), but functionally both are WSD variants with a cosine-shaped decay phase [PAPER, arXiv:2412.19437; PAPER, arXiv:2507.20534]. Nemotron-3 uses an explicit WSD schedule [PAPER, arXiv:2604.12374/arXiv:2606.15007]. The practical payoff, per the broader WSD literature, is that the token budget doesn’t need to be committed at step zero — teams can extend the stable phase and branch checkpoints for different downstream budgets (Ch12). Llama 3.1 405B instead uses pure cosine from step 1 (peak 8e-5, 8000-step warmup, decay to 8e-7 over 1.2M steps) [PAPER, arXiv:2407.21783] — the schools coexist, WSD-style concentrated in 2025-2026 Chinese-lab reports, pure cosine still present in 2024-era Meta practice. GLM-4.5 directly contradicts the WSD trend: its team found WSD-scheduled models underfit relative to cosine decay on SimpleQA/MMLU — “indicating underfitting” — and chose cosine deliberately [PAPER, arXiv:2508.06471, §2.4]. Ant’s Ling-1T uses a third mechanism, “Warmup-Stable-Merge,” avoiding LR decay entirely via checkpoint merging [PAPER, arXiv:2510.22115, §2.2]. Three frontier labs, three answers, inside a twelve-month window — though part of the disagreement may be semantic rather than purely empirical: GLM’s ablation compared its own named “WSD” schedule against cosine, not literally against K2’s or V3’s constant-then-cosine shape, and since K2/V3 are WSD-shaped only in function (both decline to use the label), it is not established that GLM’s underfitting result would reproduce against the specific schedule K2 and V3 actually ran. No settled consensus for a 10T design to inherit uncritically either way.

muP-for-MoE transfer — does a learning rate fitted at small expert count and width hold as both scale up? — is genuinely contested as of mid-2026. In favor: dedicated derivations (arXiv:2508.09752) provide theoretical feature-learning guarantees across width, and a June 2026 follow-up (arXiv:2605.14200) reports transfer holding unless fine-grained granular expert scaling is introduced — it works scaling expert width, becomes unreliable scaling expert count, with optimal LR shifting slightly downward as count increases even where transfer nominally holds. Essential AI separately reports transfer holding to 3.7B for both LR and weight decay [PAPER, arXiv:2505.02222]. Against it: a Qwen-affiliated paper tested muTransfer against a fitted power-law LR(N,D) = 38.4588·N^(-0.2219)·D^(-0.3509) on a Qwen3-MoE (12B total/1.3B active) and found the fitted law beat muTransfer by 1.28%/2.23% on MMLU/CMMLU after 500B tokens — and found no significant gain from fine-grained per-module LR tuning once QK-Norm is present [PAPER, arXiv:2601.05049, 1/2026]. Both of the most scale-relevant papers report real deviations from clean transfer in the large-expert-count regime a 10T/200B design occupies — treat muP-for-MoE transfer as directionally useful for narrowing a search space, not a scaling-law substitute. On per-block LR, the field has converged toward not needing separate LR beyond a two-group split (Adam-group: embeddings/norms/router/head; Muon-group: everything else) — Essential AI and the Qwen paper agree despite disagreeing on almost everything else. Hunyuan-Large’s expert-specific LR scaling (shared expert at the standard rate, specialized experts at ≈0.31x, justified by unequal per-iteration token load) is the one documented counterexample [PAPER, arXiv:2411.02265, §2.4].

Batch Ramping

Batch-size ramping over the first several hundred billion to a few trillion tokens is universal: DeepSeek-V3 ramps 3072→15360 sequences over the first 469B tokens [PAPER, arXiv:2412.19437]; Llama 3.1 405B doubles 4M→8M→16M tokens at 252M and 2.87T tokens [PAPER, arXiv:2407.21783]; GLM-4.5 ramps 16M→64M tokens over the first 500B tokens [PAPER, arXiv:2508.06471]. BLOOM-176B’s ramp — GBS=16 at only 8 TFLOPs/s (PP-bubble-dominated), GBS=192 at 73 TFLOPs/s, GBS=2048 at 150 TFLOPs/s — shows strongly diminishing marginal throughput gain well before GBS saturates memory [CODE, ml-engineering, training/hparams.md, 07/2026]: a 2022-era number, but the pattern is worth re-verifying rather than assuming linear gains at 10T-MoE scale, where PP is also structurally required (Ch16). Weight decay is near-universal at 0.1 across AdamW and Muon alike, and for Muon it is not merely regularization — per Moonlight’s proof, it keeps ‖W‖ bounded and MaxLogit growth in check.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

Safe choice: AdamW, with the recipe DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3, and Llama 3 converged on — β1=0.9, β2=0.95, weight decay 0.1, grad clip 1.0, a WSD-shaped or pure-cosine schedule with a fitted-scaling-law peak LR rather than naive muTransfer, batch ramp over the first ~500B-3T tokens, QK-Norm for attention stability. This is the only choice with a disclosed, completed validation above 400B active-or-dense parameters. The cost is real: leaving Muon’s FLOPs/token efficiency on the table, and inheriting AdamW’s own critical-batch-size degradation risk at 100,000-GPU global-batch scale.

Alpha choice: Muon (or a QK-Clip/RMSNorm-on-QK-equivalent stability companion), applied to all 2D matrix parameters, AdamW retained for embeddings/norms/router/head. This is no longer a research bet — it is the choice made by the two largest confirmed MoE pretraining runs on record, Kimi K2 at 32.6B active and, more tellingly, DeepSeek’s own V3→V4 transition: the lab with arguably the best track record for MoE stability, having validated AdamW at 37B active with zero spikes, deliberately moved to Muon next. The risk is pure scale extrapolation: every public Muon validation tops out at 49B active; 200B is a 4x extrapolation with no precedent for any optimizer at that count on an MoE. Three risks need de-risking: (a) attention-logit blowup at larger active width, solved differently by every lab that has hit it — validate your own variant, don’t assume K2’s or DeepSeek-V4’s transfers untested; (b) distributed NS at your specific EP/TP/PP topology, where the MoE-EP interaction is exactly the undocumented gap in NVIDIA’s tooling; (c) muP-for-MoE LR transfer reliability at high expert count, which 2026 evidence says is not solved.

The de-risking ablation ladder:

  1. 50M-300M active, ~10B tokens, architecture-matched, 4-way sweep — AdamW vs. Muon vs. Muon+your-chosen-QK-stability-mechanism vs. Muon+QK-Clip-exact-as-K2. Confirm attention-logit blowup reproduces at small scale (per K2’s report of hitting it at 9B active) and that your fix suppresses it; cheap enough (<$50K at 2026 GB200 rates) to run all four arms to convergence.
  2. 1B-4B active, ~50-100B tokens, muP-telescoping (Essential AI) and fitted-scaling-law (Qwen) protocols side by side on the actual target architecture — the two disagreed in the literature because they were tested on different architectures; a head-to-head at your own MoE shape is required before trusting either for the final LR.
  3. 8-15B active, ~1-2T tokens, full distributed-Muon systems test at production EP/TP/PP topology — validate the NS-sharding-mode choice against your fabric and expert-parallel degree, and get the first read on whether the ~1-5% GB300-class Muon-overhead number holds at very high active-param, very high expert-count, since that regime sits outside every public measurement.
  4. ~50-80B active (>1x the largest public Muon validation of 49B), 3-5T tokens — full end-to-end rehearsal, the primary go/no-go gate: if attention-stability, muP-transfer, and distributed-NS-overhead all hold clean here, the marginal risk of continuing to 200B active becomes “does the same shape of finding continue” rather than “will something qualitatively new break.”

If step 4 shows any qualitative instability that doesn’t trace to a known, fixable cause, fall back to AdamW for the full run. The FLOPs/token cost of that fallback is bounded — Moonlight’s own number is that Muon needs only ~52% of AdamW’s FLOPs to match loss, so the downside is a known, priced inefficiency. An unrecovered instability discovered only at 100,000-GPU full scale is not bounded the same way, and that asymmetry — a priced fallback against an unpriced failure mode — should govern the final decision more than any individual benchmark in this chapter.

Chapter 8 · Part II — The Model

Precision: FP8 to MXFP8 to NVFP4

Numerics is the one design axis where a 10T-parameter run cannot simply copy a published recipe and move on: every source in this beat that reached frontier scale did so by inventing at least one non-obvious workaround for a specific piece of silicon, and the workarounds do not transfer cleanly across Hopper, Blackwell, and the FP4 frontier beyond it. What follows is the evidence base — DeepSeek V3’s blockwise FP8 as the still-unbeaten reference point, NVIDIA’s four-recipe Transformer Engine zoo and what its own flagship model actually shipped with, MXFP8 as the Blackwell-native successor with one real 256-GPU corroboration, and NVFP4 as a 12B/10T-token existence proof that is nowhere near frontier scale — followed by the GB200 hardware ground truth this book treats as authoritative, and a verdict on what a $2B run in July 2026 should bet on versus merely pilot.

The reference point: DeepSeek V3’s blockwise FP8 recipe

DeepSeek-V3’s mixed-precision framework is the most load-bearing precision recipe in this literature because it is the only one validated at genuinely frontier parameter count with a clean ablation against a BF16 baseline. All three GEMMs inside every Linear operator — Fprop, Dgrad, Wgrad — run in FP8; the embedding, output head, MoE gating module, all RMSNorm operators, the attention softmax itself, master weights, weight gradients, and optimizer states stay in BF16/FP32 DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report [PAPER, Dec 2024]. The granularity is fine-grained and asymmetric: activations use 1×128 tile quantization (per-token, per-128-channel group), weights use 128×128 block quantization, both computed online per iteration rather than from a delayed/historical amax buffer. The format choice is uniform E4M3 for every GEMM operand — a deliberate break from the “E4M3 forward / E5M2 backward” convention that dominated earlier FP8 practice, made viable specifically because fine-grained per-block scaling already compensates for E4M3’s narrower dynamic range (see Ch4 for the full DeepSeek stack context).

The single most consequential engineering detail is the accumulation fix. DeepSeek found H800’s Tensor Core accumulates FP8 partial sums natively at roughly 14 bits — not full FP32 — which produced up to ~2% relative error on a K=4096 GEMM without correction. Their fix promotes partial WGMMA results to CUDA-core FP32 registers every Nc=128 accumulated elements (four WGMMAs), overlapping the promotion of one interval with the MMA of the next so the extra CUDA-core work is largely hidden DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report [PAPER, Dec 2024]. Colfax Research’s independent analysis confirms the same ~14-bit accumulation limitation on Hopper tensor cores and notes the CUDA-core promotion pattern is now standard practice in CUTLASS ≥3.2 Colfax Research, DeepSeek-R1 and FP8 Mixed-Precision Training [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 2025]. Net result, validated at ~1T-token comparison scale before being trusted for the full V3 run: relative loss error <0.25% versus a BF16-trained reference — “well within the range of training randomness” in the paper’s own framing.

Two numeric details rarely reproduced elsewhere matter for anyone reimplementing this: inputs to the post-attention Linear are stored in a custom E5M6 format with power-of-2 scale factors, re-tiled from 1×128 to 128×1 for the backward pass, because that specific activation proved sensitive to naive FP8 casting; and MoE dispatch-direction activations are FP8 while the reverse combine direction stays BF16 to protect accuracy on the accumulation-heavy reduction DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report §3.3.3 [PAPER, Dec 2024]. This is the level of granularity a 10T/200B recipe needs to match or exceed — DeepSeek’s own Appendix B.2 documents that blockwise-only-on-activations, without the matching weight block scheme, produced training instability DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report, Appendix B.2 [PAPER, Dec 2024], a finding independently corroborated by Colfax Research [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 2025].

The industrialized recipe zoo — what NVIDIA actually ships, and what its own model chose

Transformer Engine (TE 2.16/2.17, mid-2026) exposes four selectable FP8 recipe classes via an fp8_recipe config string:

Per-Tensor and Per-Block Scaling Strategies for Effective FP8 Training [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2025]; Transformer Engine docs [CODE/OFFICIAL-DOCS, 2026].

What’s instructive is which recipe NVIDIA’s own flagship chose. Nemotron-H-56B-Base — described as “the first Nemotron model fully pretrained with an FP8-based recipe” — used the simplest option in the zoo: per-tensor current scaling, with a hybrid format (E4M3 for weights/activations, E5M2 for gradients — the more conservative choice DeepSeek explicitly avoided), and the first and last 4 GEMMs of the network kept in BF16 Nemotron-H Technical Report [PAPER, Apr 2025]. In 8B-scale ablations run to 15T tokens, this per-tensor recipe matched or beat BF16 on downstream accuracy. NVIDIA’s own framing for current scaling over delayed scaling: instantaneous adaptation to real-time distribution shifts, at the cost of a live max-reduction per tensor per step — a bet that per-step overhead is cheaper than the risk of a stale delayed-scaling factor clipping an outlier. Production adoption of DeepSeek’s exact blockwise recipe has since spread: TE ships it as the standard recommendation for Hopper-generation MoE training, validated at DeepSeek-V3 scale plus MiniMax-M2 and Ant Ling-2.0 NVIDIA TE docs, “FP8 Blockwise Scaling” [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2026].

Why naive FP8 breaks, and where FP8 attention stands

The literature past the headline “FP8 matches BF16” results is more instructive for a one-shot 10T-parameter run than any single success story. “To FP8 and Back Again” found that removing even a single exponent bit from a float format prevents training entirely — mantissa bits are cheap to lose, dynamic-range bits are not — and identified the LM head and SwiGLU activation outputs as specific divergence sources under naive quantization. The same paper found ~10% of nanoGPT BF16 baseline runs (18/188) diverged more than 5% into training purely from random seed variation, a useful caution against attributing every reported FP8 instability to the format itself rather than baseline variance To FP8 and Back Again [PAPER, May 2024].

The “FOG” paper pushed FP8 into the attention operator itself — Q·Kᵀ and P·V in FP8, TE delayed scaling with history length 1024, E4M3 forward / E5M2 backward — and found architecture, not just numerics, is the lever that makes this stable: pre-normalization must become post-normalization/LayerScale, trainable QK-RMSNorm gains must be frozen (they explode under low precision), softmax scale must move from 1/√d to γ₀²/√d, and gated SwiGLU activations should be swapped for non-gated ones. With those changes, “FOG-flash” (8B params, 20B tokens) hit 12,764 tokens/sec/GPU versus 9,105 for BF16 — a 35–43% gain, rising to +43.5% at 8K context Towards Fully FP8 GEMM LLM Training at Scale [PAPER, May 2025]. FlashAttention-3’s FP8 forward path (block quantization plus a Hadamard-style “incoherent processing” step) reaches ~1.2 PFLOP/s on H100 with 2.6× lower numerical error than naive per-tensor FP8 attention, but Tri Dao’s own writeup explicitly flags that its effect on large-scale training dynamics — as opposed to inference — was not validated in that paper FlashAttention-3 [PAPER, Jul 2024]. Net for a frontier run: FP8 attention forward is workable with FA3/FA4-class kernels (see Ch15 for the kernel-level detail — FA4 CuTe remains forward-only for FP8 on SM100 as of July 2026, with no MXFP8/NVFP4 attention path at all), but a fully FP8 attention training path that’s proven at scale requires FOG-style architectural intervention, not a kernel swap.

One further counter-example belongs here because it directly contradicts the “FP8 pretraining is settled” narrative that dominates 2025–2026 commentary: Kimi K2 (1T-parameter MoE, July 2025) explicitly declined to use FP8 in pretraining compute after a “preliminary study” showed risk of degradation, training in higher precision and only casting to blockwise FP8 post-hoc for inference weights Kimi K2 Technical Report [PAPER, Jul 2025]; Kimi-K2-Instruct HF discussion #30 [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY]. This does not refute DeepSeek V3’s result — DeepSeek is direct counter-counter-evidence at comparable scale — but it is a named, citable data point that FP8 pretraining at trillion-parameter MoE scale is not universally considered safe by every frontier lab, and the reasons for Kimi’s choice (routing topology, MuonClip/QK-Clip interactions, or something else) were never published in enough detail to generalize.

MXFP8: the Blackwell-native successor

NVIDIA’s “Recipes for Pre-training LLMs with MXFP8” is the primary MXFP8 evidence base and its core technical insight is that quantization must run along both the row and column axis separately, because a weight or activation tensor is consumed in both its natural orientation (fprop) and transposed orientation (dgrad/wgrad), and MX block scales are axis-local — a single quantization pass does not suffice the way it can for a coarser per-tensor scheme Recipes for Pre-training LLMs with MXFP8 [PAPER, Jun 2025, v2 Aug 2025]. The format is E4M3 uniformly, including for activation gradients — an explicit departure from Nemotron-H’s E5M2-for-gradients choice — justified by an 843M-model ablation showing E5M2 gradients caused “substantial degradation” at 8B scale; at a 32-element block granularity the per-block dynamic range is already covered by roughly 17.8 binades, so E4M3’s extra mantissa bit is worth more than E5M2’s wider exponent range once per-block scales absorb the range problem. Scale factors use UE8M0 (power-of-two only) and are computed by rounding up (ceiling), deliberately deviating from the OCP MXFP v1.0 spec’s round-down convention, because round-down can let a post-scale value exceed the destination format’s representable maximum and silently overflow.

Because MX block granularity is fine enough, MXFP8 quantizes essentially all linear/GEMM layers — softmax, activation functions, and residual-add stay high precision, but there is no first/last-N-layers BF16 carve-out the way per-tensor FP8 recipes need. Validated to 8B params / 15T tokens, MXFP8 sustains <0.5% perplexity gap versus BF16 through the entire run, with MMLU/ARC/RACE/PIQA/Winogrande/HellaSwag/OpenBookQA/SocialIQA/CommonsenseQA all matching. The paper’s own efficiency numbers claim up to 22% training-time reduction, 14% lower peak memory, 19% higher throughput versus BF16 — but worth flagging that these large ablation runs were executed on 3072 Hopper GPUs via emulation/kernel support, not native Blackwell silicon, so the loss-curve evidence and the hardware-throughput evidence come from different generations of hardware.

The real-world corroboration is more useful than the paper’s own numbers for run-planning purposes. PyTorch’s TorchTitan team combined MXFP8-quantized grouped GEMMs (via TorchAO’s _to_mxfp8_then_scaled_grouped_mm) with DeepEP-overlapped expert-parallel communication on a DeepSeek-V3-671B-architecture run, 256×B200 GPUs (TP2/PP2/EP32, seq_len 8192, local batch 64): DeepEP overlap alone gave +32.0% throughput (651→859 tok/s), MXFP8+DeepEP together gave +41.0% (651→918 tok/s), against a BF16+standard-EP baseline PyTorch/TorchTitan blog, MXFP8 + DeepEP for DeepSeek-V3 on B200 [OFFICIAL-BLOG, Mar 2026, updated May 2026]. The disclosed caveat matters as much as the headline: MXFP8 could not be applied to the dense (non-expert) linear layers in the 671B config, because of a torch.compile+tensor-parallel incompatibility — the 41% figure is grouped-GEMM (expert) MXFP8 only, not full-model MXFP8. A companion 16B-scale, 1500-step run showed MXFP8 converging equivalently to BF16 with no meaningful divergence. Nebius independently reproduced the same DeepSeek-V3-671B architecture on a 256×B200 TorchTitan cluster with MXFP8 replacing the original 1×128/128×128 blockwise recipe, reporting the same ~41% figure Nebius ml-cookbook [CODE, 2026]. This is a genuine second organization landing on the same number for the same architecture — real corroboration by this beat’s standards — but the caveat is worth stating plainly: Nebius ran its reproduction on TorchTitan itself, meaning it likely shares TorchTitan’s MXFP8-grouped-GEMM-plus-DeepEP code path rather than being an independently-implemented measurement. Read it as a second team’s confirmation that the technique reproduces on their hardware and cluster config, not as two fully independent implementations converging on the same figure by coincidence.

NVFP4 and the FP4 pretraining frontier

NVIDIA’s flagship FP4 result is the largest published FP4 pretraining run as of mid-2026: a 12B hybrid Mamba-Transformer (62 blocks: 6 self-attention, 28 FFN, 28 Mamba-2), 10T tokens, NVFP4 for the majority of linear layers Pretraining Large Language Models with NVFP4 [PAPER, Sept 2025]. The recipe’s own ablation shows all four of the following techniques are necessary — removing any one measurably degrades convergence:

  1. Two-level block scaling: 16-element blocks (versus MXFP4’s coarser 32) with an E4M3 block scale, plus one additional FP32 per-tensor scale that first remaps the whole tensor into the FP4×FP8-representable range before the per-block E4M3 scale does fine adjustment.
  2. Random Hadamard transforms (16×16), applied only to the inputs of the weight-gradient (Wgrad) GEMM, using a fixed random sign vector held constant across the entire training run.
  3. 2D scaling for weights (16×16 blocks) versus 1D scaling for activations/gradients (1×16) — asymmetric specifically to preserve forward/backward chain-rule consistency, since a weight tensor is read in two different contractions.
  4. Stochastic rounding restricted to gradient tensors during the FP4 cast; weights and activations use plain round-to-nearest-even.

Plus a structural carve-out: 16% of linear layers stay BF16 — the first 2 blocks and final 8 of 62, a front/back sandwich rather than a uniform sprinkling. Results: relative loss error versus an FP8 baseline stays <1% through the stable-LR phase, widening to ~1.5% during LR decay — the decay phase is where 4-bit’s information loss bites hardest, consistent with the general finding that late-training fine adjustments are the most precision-sensitive part of a run. A late-training precision upgrade — switching to BF16 for the final 18% of tokens (from the 8.2T/10T mark) — recovers most of the gap, down to 0.5% relative error, while keeping ~94% of total FLOPs in FP4. MMLU-Pro comes out at 62.58% (NVFP4) versus 62.62% (FP8) — a wash. A smaller 8B/1T-token NVFP4-versus-MXFP4 head-to-head in the same paper found NVFP4’s relative error (~1.5%) beating MXFP4’s (~2.5%), with MXFP4 needing 36% more tokens (1.36T vs 1T) to match NVFP4’s loss — the paper’s own accounting of NVFP4’s format advantage.

The academic literature has moved fast in the roughly ten months since and has not converged. Quartet argues native all-MXFP4 training can be compute-optimal on Blackwell once real kernel speedups are accounted for, but uses deterministic round-to-nearest for the backward pass rather than stochastic rounding — a direct disagreement with NVFP4’s design choice — and is validated only to 430M params (7B as a spot check), far below the scale needed to trust its “compute optimal” framing Quartet [PAPER, May 2025]. Quartet II revisits unbiased gradient estimation specifically for NVFP4-style two-level scaling, suggesting the field is converging toward that variant over plain MXFP4 Quartet II [PAPER, Jan 2026]. TetraJet-v2 frames the two dominant FP4-training failure modes as weight oscillation and activation outliers, proposing “OsciReset” and “OutControl” for fully-NVFP4 linear layers including gradients TetraJet-v2 [PAPER, Oct 2025, ICML 2026 Spotlight]. “Four Over Six” is a sharp, small fix worth flagging as under-circulated: NVFP4’s E4M3 block scale, set to a block’s max value, leaves a dead zone where no FP4 code point can represent 66.6–100% of the block’s own max — the fix quantizes each block twice (scale=6 and scale=4) and keeps whichever has lower MSE, closing 22.3% of the NVFP4-to-BF16 loss gap on a Nemotron-3-Nano architecture for under 15% compute overhead Four Over Six [PAPER, Dec 2025]. “Elucidating the Design Space of FP4 Training” systematically swept technique combinations and concluded Hadamard + tensor scaling + stochastic rounding is the best accuracy/overhead trade-off, with a more hedged verdict than NVIDIA’s or Quartet’s papers: FP4 training is achievable with careful configuration, not a drop-in replacement Elucidating the Design Space of FP4 Training [PAPER, Sept 2025]. Cross-vendor validation exists but only at small scale: MXFP4 on AMD MI355X native FP4 hardware is validated to just 1B params / ~10B tokens Pretraining LLMs with MXFP4 on Native FP4 Hardware [PAPER, May 2026]. “Characterization and Mitigation of Training Instabilities in Microscaling Formats” documents why naive MX-format training diverges — block-exponent overflow, quantization-noise floor effects late in training, and critically, Adam’s momentum/second-moment accumulators compounding quantization error over iterations rather than averaging it out Characterization and Mitigation of Training Instabilities in Microscaling Formats [PAPER, Jun 2025] — a good citation for why the field converged on elaborate multi-technique recipes rather than “just quantize and go.”

The bottom line for a 10T-parameter design: NVFP4’s best public evidence is two orders of magnitude below frontier scale, the technique stack is intricate enough that a single missed component measurably degrades convergence per the paper’s own ablations, and the field disagrees — as of early-to-mid 2026 — on whether stochastic rounding or deterministic RTN is correct for the backward pass. This is not a settled question dressed up as one.

DeepSeek-V4’s FP4: a memory play, not a compute play — and a live gap

DeepSeek-V4 (two variants — V4-Pro at 1.6T total/49B active/33T tokens, V4-Flash at 284B total/13B active/32T tokens) is the first frontier model to publicly commit to FP4 for MoE expert weights, but the report’s own text undercuts the headline reading DeepSeek-V4 Technical Report [PAPER, Jun 2026]. It states explicitly that “peak FLOPs for FP4×FP8 operations are currently the same as FP8×FP8 on existing hardware” — the FP4 choice for experts is a memory-footprint optimization (roughly halving expert-weight storage versus FP8), not a compute-throughput win, on the hardware the report describes. KV cache uses a mixed format: BF16 for RoPE dimensions specifically, FP8 for the rest, cutting KV cache size roughly 2× versus pure BF16. Attention inside the “lightning indexer” (the sparse-attention selection mechanism, see Ch4 for the CSA/HCA architecture detail) runs in FP4 explicitly to accelerate the indexer at million-token context.

The load-bearing caveat: “FP4 Quantization-Aware Training” is applied to MoE expert weights and the indexer’s QK path, but this is described in Section 5.2.1, framed as part of the post-training stage — the report does not disclose the numeric recipe used for the primary 32–33T-token pretraining run itself. Anyone downstream citing “DeepSeek does full FP4 pretraining” should be corrected to: DeepSeek stores/serves and QAT-refines expert weights in FP4; the base pretraining precision is undisclosed as of the June 2026 report. Separately, the report states Muon’s Newton-Schulz orthogonalization iterations “remain stable when computed with BF16 matrix multiplications” — a specific, checkable numerics claim relevant to Ch7’s optimizer discussion — and MoE gradient synchronization across ranks uses stochastic rounding when casting down to BF16 for the all-reduce/all-gather, i.e., SR is applied not just at FP4/FP8 quantization boundaries but at the BF16 communication-compression boundary too.

Where FP32 islands persist, and why the router is non-negotiable

Every source in this beat converges on the same short list of precision-protected components, and the reasoning is consistent enough to treat as settled practice rather than caution:

  • Router/gating logits: FP32 is the default in Megatron-Core’s MoE implementation specifically because “router logits computation should use FP32 instead of BF16 to enhance numerical stability,” with softmax/sigmoid over router logits forced to FP32 even when the rest of the model runs BF16/FP8 [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, synthesized from Megatron-LM/DeepSpeed GitHub issues, 2025-2026]. Direct empirical support comes from an FP8-RL ablation showing FP8 routers produce materially higher KL-divergence mismatch in expert-selection distributions than BF16 routers, while BF16→FP32 gives only marginal further improvement — implying BF16 is the real numerical floor for routing and FP32 buys little extra beyond it FP8-RL [PAPER, Jan 2026]; lmsys.org FP8-RL blog [OFFICIAL-BLOG]. A closed Megatron-LM GitHub issue is the concrete cautionary tale: applying a naive --fp8-format hybrid recipe to Mixtral-8x7B without protecting the router landed at val loss 7.71 (PPL 2222) versus BF16’s 7.25 (PPL 1410) — a ~57% perplexity blowup from an under-specified MoE FP8 config Megatron-LM issue #1152 [CODE]. This is the single clearest MoE-specific numerics failure mode in the entire corpus, and it is exactly the trap a 10T/200B-active design must not walk into.
  • Attention probabilities / softmax: kept in high precision (BF16 minimum, often FP32 internally) uniformly across DeepSeek V3, MXFP8, and Nemotron-H — none of the production recipes surveyed quantize the softmax operator itself, only its Q/K/V GEMM inputs.
  • Residual/accumulation stream: “To FP8 and Back Again” explicitly keeps residual connections in FP32; the general pattern is that the additive backbone of a transformer is treated as more precision-sensitive than the multiplicative GEMM operators feeding into it.
  • Master weights and optimizer states: universally BF16/FP32 across every recipe surveyed — DeepSeek V3, Nemotron-H, the NVFP4 paper, the MXFP8 paper. Nobody quantizes optimizer state below BF16 for pretraining.

Stochastic rounding follows one consistent pattern across every recipe that uses it: restricted to gradient tensors only, never applied to weights or activations in the forward pass. This matches “To FP8 and Back Again”’s finding that SR helps unbiased gradient estimation for backprop but actively hurts forward-pass convergence, where round-to-nearest has lower quantization error.

Stochastic rounding hardware, and the determinism tax

Stochastic rounding is now a real hardware primitive on Blackwell, not a software convention. PTX cvt.rs on B200/B300 (sm_100a/sm_103a) performs hardware RNG-driven stochastic rounding for FP32→{FP16, BF16, FP8-E5M2/E4M3, FP6, FP4-E2M1} conversions, including a 4-wide cvt.rs.satfinite.e2m1x4.f32 variant for packed NVFP4 output, consuming 13 bits of random source for FP16 and 16 bits for BF16 and the 8-bit formats Limited-Precision Stochastic Rounding [PAPER/CODE, Mar 2026]. Because the random source itself is limited-precision, realized rounding probabilities diverge slightly from mathematically exact SR — a second-order bias effect the same paper characterizes formally. No source found in this pass confirms or denies an equivalent hardware SR primitive on TPU or Trainium; treat this as an open question specific to NVIDIA silicon.

Determinism is a distinct, less-quantified cost. NCCL 2.27 (2026-era) computes AllReduce/AllGather/ReduceScatter reductions with FP32 accumulators (FP16 specifically for FP8 tensors over NVLink Switch/NVLS) to improve determinism, but true bitwise reproducibility across runs still requires pinning NCCL_ALGO/NCCL_PROTO — floating-point sum is non-associative, and algorithm choice changes reduction order — plus a verified fixed collective ordering NCCL 2.27 blog [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2026]. DeepSeek-V4 separately claims “deterministic kernel implementations with bitwise reproducibility across training/inference” without detailing the throughput cost — the report describes deterministic attention backward (per-SM accumulation buffers replacing atomic-add), deterministic MoE backward (per-rank token-order preprocessing), and a deterministic mHC matmul reduction, all opt-in against a fast-math-disabled-by-default IEEE-754-compliant baseline DeepSeek-V4 Technical Report §Training Infra [PAPER, Jun 2026]. Note that Thinking Machines Lab’s widely-discussed “Defeating Nondeterminism in LLM Inference” work is scoped to inference batch-invariance, not training, and is frequently over-cited in training-determinism discussions simonwillison.net summary [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, Sept 2025] — the actual training-time determinism tax remains a cost most labs pay only for targeted debugging replays, not production pretraining, given known interactions between fixed collective algorithms and communication-performance tuning.

Precision-aware scaling laws: directional, not load-bearing

Kumar et al.’s “Scaling Laws for Precision” is the most-cited general theory here, proposing that training in lower precision effectively shrinks a model’s usable “effective parameter count,” and separately that post-training-quantization degradation increases with the amount of pretraining data seen — beyond some critical data budget, additional pretraining tokens make a model destined for later quantization actively worse off. The paper’s fitted law suggests a 7–8 bit compute-optimal precision band for pretraining Scaling Laws for Precision [PAPER, Nov 2024, ICLR 2025]. The load-bearing caveat: this is fit over ≤1.7B parameters and ≤26B tokens across 465 runs — nobody has published a re-derivation of its coefficients within two orders of magnitude of a 10T-parameter, tens-of-trillions-of-token run. Every large-scale empirical result surveyed above (DeepSeek V3’s 671B/~1T-comparison-token FP8 run, NVIDIA’s 12B/10T-token NVFP4 run, MXFP8’s 8B/15T-token run) reports near-zero loss gaps at 8-bit and even 4-bit — broadly consistent with Kumar’s claim that 7-8 bits sits comfortably above the compute-optimal-precision floor, but none of these are true post-hoc-quantization setups (they are precision-matched throughout training), so none test Kumar’s more provocative claim directly. Treat the qualitative direction — more data and lower target precision genuinely interact — as trustworthy, and the specific numeric coefficients as not predictive at 10T-parameter scale.

Hardware ground truth: GB200 NVL72, reconciled

This chapter treats 30-gb200-nvl72-peak-flops-reconciliation.md as the single authority on GB200 peak-FLOPS numbers, because it resolves a real conflict present elsewhere in this corpus’s underlying research passes — the jax-scaling-book research note and the kernels-attention research note both originally conflated the two SKUs, an error subsequently corrected in this book’s own Ch16 (critical-batch and expert-parallelism-viability thresholds) and Ch15 (GB200 MFU) drafts — between the liquid-cooled, 1,200W GB200 NVL72 SKU and the air-cooled, 1,000W HGX B200 SKU. Both are the same reticle-limited Blackwell die (208B transistors, TSMC 4NP); the difference is power envelope and cooling, not silicon NVIDIA “Blackwell” datasheet #3384703 [OFFICIAL-DOCS, Dec 2024]. NVIDIA’s own side-by-side table, with the critical footnote “all Tensor Core numbers except FP64 with sparsity” (i.e., every Tensor Core row is a 2:4-structured-sparse figure — dense is exactly half):

Three independent primary/near-primary sources cross-validate this table exactly: NVIDIA’s own datasheet PDF, live-fetched HTML from NVIDIA’s GB200 NVL72 and DGX B200 product pages, and Glenn K. Lockwood’s independently-compiled technical wiki glennklockwood.com/garden/processors/b200 [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, Apr 2026]. The correction is a clean, uniform +11.1% on every tensor-core dense precision (5000/4500 = 2500/2250 = 10000/9000 = 10/9 exactly), but only +6.7% on the non-tensor-core FP32 vector path and +8.1% on FP64 — meaning the two SKUs’ published tensor-core numbers are independently-chosen round marketing figures rather than a single clock multiplier applied uniformly, and NVIDIA does not publish an official clock speed for either SKU.

This reconciliation matters for this chapter specifically because the JAX Scaling Book — a source cited elsewhere in this book (Ch16) — uses the HGX B200 dense figures (bf16=2.3e15, fp8=4.5e15, fp4=9.0e15 FLOPs/s/chip) under an undifferentiated “B200” label, while its own Quiz Question 6 separately reasons from the GB200-class 80 TFLOPS FP32 figure without flagging the SKU switch. A second, independent instance of the same conflation was found in this corpus’s kernels-attention research: a Megatron-Core MoE technical report’s 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU sustained figure for DeepSeek-V3-685B-scale training on GB200, divided against the wrong (HGX B200) 2,250 TFLOPS BF16 denominator, yields 46.6% MFU; divided against the correct GB200 NVL72 figure of 2,500 TFLOPS, it is 41.9% — a ~4.7-percentage-point, ~10% relative overstatement Megatron-Core MoE technical report [PAPER, Mar 2026], reconciled in 30-gb200-nvl72-peak-flops-reconciliation.md [this corpus] and now correctly stated as 41.9% in this book’s own Ch15. Any MFU number quoted elsewhere in this book against a “B200” or “GB200” denominator should be checked against this table before being trusted.

The achievable-fraction reality: marketing peaks versus MoE training shapes

The more consequential finding for a 200B-active MoE design is not which SKU’s peak is correct, but how far actual training GEMMs fall short of any peak. Independent microbenchmarking of a standalone B200 at a realistic GEMM tile shape (m64n8k16) measured 3,850.6 TFLOPS FP8 — 96.3% of that SKU’s own (lower, HGX-class) theoretical peak — with FP6 at 96.0%, FP4 at 96.2%, BF16 at 96.3%, and INT8 at 98.2% Microbenchmarking NVIDIA’s Blackwell Architecture [PAPER/BENCHMARK, Dec 2025]. The same paper found B200 delivers a consistent ~1.27× speedup over H200 across precisions at the raw tensor-core level — meaning most of the “Blackwell is 2-4× faster” headline comes from the new narrower dtypes (FP4/FP6 not existing as fast paths on Hopper) and from memory bandwidth/capacity gains, not from Blackwell tensor cores being dramatically faster than Hopper’s at a fixed dtype.

That 96%+ efficiency is for isolated, well-shaped GEMM tiles. MoE training GEMM shapes are nowhere close. DeepGEMM reports up to 1,550 TFLOPS on H800 for well-shaped problems, but a vLLM benchmark at a realistic per-expert training shape (M=128, N=4096, K=7168 — a small-M grouped-GEMM slice typical of a single expert’s token batch) measured only 190.49 TFLOPS for CUTLASS on H800 DeepGEMM [CODE]; vLLM PR #13917 [CODE/BENCHMARK, 2025]. This is the crux of why sparse-TFLOPS marketing numbers are close to irrelevant for MoE pretraining planning: a 10T-total/200B-active run’s realized TFLOPS/GPU will be dominated by per-expert GEMM shape efficiency — which scales with expert capacity and batch size, not peak tensor-core throughput — far more than by which precision format’s peak appears on a spec sheet. Ch15’s kernel-level worklog on B200 NVFP4 grouped GEMM corroborates this directly: a naive implementation runs at 238µs geomean across representative expert shapes, a hand-optimized persistent-kernel version reaches 23.8µs (~10×), and even that optimized kernel only achieves ~50% average tensor-core utilization and 12.4% SM occupancy, because TMEM (the accumulator plus scale-factor tensor) fits only one CTA per SM at small M — a hardware constraint precision format choice does not fix.

Field MFU data corroborates the same lesson from a different angle: on H100, FP8 MFU runs consistently lower than BF16 MFU as a percentage of each format’s own peak — 39.5% versus 54% on GPT-3-175B/128×H100, 35.5–38.1% versus 53.7–54.5% on Llama3-70B, ~43% versus ~54% on Llama3-405B SemiAnalysis, “H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks” [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, partial paywall, 2025]. This is not a contradiction of FP8’s value — it still wins on absolute tokens/s and $/token because its peak is roughly 2× higher — but it directly rebuts any planning heuristic assuming FP8 achieves the same utilization fraction as BF16. The same source notes GB200-generation FP8 MFU improved from 29.5% to 39.5% “through software gains alone” over an unspecified maturation window, meaning roughly a third of Blackwell’s realized FP8 throughput gain over its early software state came from kernel/scheduling maturity, not hardware — a caution against reading any July-2026 MFU snapshot as a hardware ceiling. Note 30 separately flags that the specific “38-46% sustained FP8 training MFU on NVL72” figure sometimes attributed to NVIDIA could not be verified against any retrievable primary source in this pass and should be treated as unconfirmed pending a located citation, not propagated as an official NVIDIA number.

The verdict: what to bet the run on in July 2026

For a 10T-total/200B-active MoE on ~100,000 GB200 GPUs:

Bet the run on:

  • FP8, DeepSeek-V3-style blockwise (1×128 activation / 128×128 weight, uniform E4M3) or NVIDIA’s TE blockwise recipe, as the default GEMM format across attention QKV/output projections and MoE expert FFNs — the only recipe with published evidence at genuinely frontier scale, corroborated independently at 8B/15T tokens by MXFP8 and Nemotron-H, landing consistently under 0.5-1% loss gap across every reproduction.
  • On GB200/Blackwell specifically, prefer MXFP8 over legacy per-tensor/delayed FP8 where the integration gap allows: it needs no first/last-N-layers BF16 carve-out, gives a real (not just theoretical) throughput win independently corroborated by both TorchTitan and Nebius at 256×B200, and is architecturally the native Blackwell path. Budget explicitly for the torch.compile+TP integration gap both groups hit on dense layers — this needs custom kernel work, not a drop-in flag, to reach 100% of layers rather than expert GEMMs only.
  • Router/gate logits and softmax in FP32, non-negotiable. Every piece of evidence — Megatron’s own defaults, the closed GitHub issue’s 57%-perplexity-blowup case, the FP8-RL routing-KL ablation — says this is cheap insurance against a catastrophic, hard-to-diagnose failure mode, and router GEMMs are tiny relative to expert FFNs at MoE scale, so the cost is negligible.
  • Master weights, optimizer states, residual stream, attention softmax in BF16/FP32, following universal practice across every recipe surveyed.
  • Stochastic rounding restricted to gradient tensors at FP8/FP4 quantization boundaries and to the BF16-compression step in gradient all-reduce, using Blackwell’s native cvt.rs where the format pair is supported — this is now near-free hardware, not a software tax.
  • A late-training precision upgrade as scheduled insurance: switching some fraction of the final 15-20% of tokens to BF16 recovers most of the accumulated low-precision loss gap for a small FLOP cost, and every paper in this beat finds precision-sensitivity peaks during LR decay.

Only pilot, not bet the full run on:

  • NVFP4 (or any FP4 variant) for the dense/attention path. The best public evidence is 12B/10T tokens — two orders of magnitude below a 10T-parameter model — and the field has not converged on stochastic-rounding versus deterministic-RTN for the backward pass, an active disagreement between concurrently publishing groups as of mid-2026.
  • FP4 for MoE expert weights specifically, following DeepSeek-V4’s disclosed direction, but note it is a memory play, not a compute-speed play, on current hardware, and DeepSeek applies it as post-training QAT rather than as the pretraining-time format. A reasonable pilot: FP4 for expert-weight storage/QAT refinement after an FP8-pretrained base, not as the from-scratch pretraining format for a first frontier run.
  • FP8 inside the attention softmax/probability computation itself. FA3/FA4’s FP8 forward paths are validated for inference-grade accuracy, not large-scale training dynamics, and FOG’s fully-FP8-attention training result required architectural changes beyond a kernel swap. Pilot with FOG-style mitigations built in from the start.
  • Treat Kimi K2’s counter-example as a genuine, unresolved data point rather than noise — a team training a genuine trillion-parameter-class MoE explicitly declined FP8 pretraining compute after a stability study, and the specifics of why were never published.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

The precision decision that most directly threatens a $2B run is not the FP8-vs-MXFP8-vs-NVFP4 choice — it is getting the MoE-specific numerics wrong: an under-specified router precision, or a naive recipe applied uniformly across dense and expert layers without DeepSeek-V3’s or Nemotron-H’s carve-outs, is the documented path to a 57%-perplexity-class blowup, not a subtle degradation. The router-FP32 rule, the BF16 combine-direction convention, and the first/last-N-layers BF16 sandwich are all cheap, well-evidenced, and should be treated as fixed requirements rather than tunable hyperparameters. Second, the GB200 hardware ceiling this book uses throughout (Ch16, Ch21) must be the reconciled dense figures in the table above — 5,000 TFLOPS/GPU dense FP8, 2,500 dense BF16, 10,000 dense NVFP4 — not the HGX B200 numbers that have already contaminated at least two independent derivations in this corpus’s own source notes. Third, and most consequential for cost modeling: the precision format chosen matters less to realized $/token than the small-M MoE grouped-GEMM shape-efficiency problem documented in Ch15 and here — a 200B-active design with narrow per-expert batches will leave far more throughput on the table from kernel/scheduling immaturity than from choosing FP8 over MXFP8, or MXFP8 over NVFP4. Ch16’s parallelism math and Ch21’s MFU/cost derivations should be read with this precision chapter’s ceiling numbers and its shape-efficiency caveat both in hand.

Chapter 9 · Part II — The Model

Multimodal-Native Pretraining as the 2026 Default

A text-only frontier pretraining run is now the exception a lab has to justify, not the default it can assume. Every architecture family surveyed in this book’s China-frontier and closed-lab chapters (Ch2, Ch3) ships some form of native vision and, increasingly, audio ingestion from step one — and the labs that have published enough detail to compare (Baidu, Meta, Moonshot, Alibaba, Ant, Google) disagree sharply on how modality should touch the MoE router, the tokenizer, and the parallelism layout. This chapter treats that disagreement as data: it is not a solved design space, one lab has already reversed its own flagship choice within eight months, and a 10T/200B host planning a primarily-text run still has to make load-bearing infrastructure decisions now to avoid an expensive retrofit later.

The 2026 baseline: why multimodal-native is no longer optional

The evidence for “multimodal-native is the default” is not a single number but a convergence: Baidu’s ERNIE 4.5 and 5.0, Meta’s Llama 4/Behemoth, Moonshot’s Kimi-VL, Alibaba’s Qwen3-VL and Qwen3-Omni/Qwen3.5-Omni, Google’s Gemini 2.5, and ByteDance’s Doubao Seed 2.0/2.1 are all disclosed or credibly reported as natively multimodal from pretraining, not vision-bolted-on-after (see Ch3, Ch6 for the lab-by-lab architecture tables). Meta’s own framing of Llama 4 is explicit: “more than 30 trillion tokens… text, image, and video,” with “10x more multilingual tokens than Llama 3” The Llama 4 herd [OFFICIAL-BLOG, Apr 2025]. Google states plainly that “the Gemini 2.5 models are sparse mixture-of-experts (MoE)… transformers with native multimodal support for text, vision, and audio inputs” Gemini 2.5 Technical Report [OFFICIAL-REPORT, 2025]. Even labs whose primary published artifact is a text benchmark leader — Kimi K2, GLM-4.5, MiniMax-M2 — sit inside product families (Kimi-VL, GLM’s broader Zhipu multimodal line, MiniMax’s own omni efforts) that ship vision and audio variants close behind the text flagship.

The practical consequence is not that a 10T/200B run must be multimodal — it is that the design decisions this chapter covers (routing, tokenizer, dataloader, parallelism topology) are being made by every peer lab regardless, and several are cheap to provision for now and expensive to retrofit later. The closing section turns that observation into a checklist.

Three architectures for wiring modality into MoE

There is no single answer to “how does a vision (or audio) token interact with an MoE router,” and the corpus supports three genuinely distinct, coexisting design families rather than one converged pattern.

Pattern A — modality-isolated expert pools within a single MoE backbone. Baidu’s ERNIE 4.5 (Jun 2025) is the most fully specified public example. Text and vision tokens route to structurally distinct expert pools (text experts, vision experts, shared experts) inside every MoE layer, while both modalities pass through all self-attention layers, which stay dense. Vision experts are quantitatively cheaper: their FFN intermediate dimension is one-third that of text experts, yielding roughly a 66% reduction in per-token FFN FLOPs for a routed vision token relative to a routed text token ERNIE 4.5 Technical Report §2.1 [PAPER, Jun 2025]. Vision experts are excluded entirely from the final Transformer layer, “as their weights do not contribute to the cross-entropy loss,” and are introduced only in later stages of the training curriculum specifically to avoid routing collapse — ERNIE’s own ablations show naively extending a text-only router to multimodal inputs degrades textual capability ERNIE 4.5 Technical Report §2.1 [PAPER, Jun 2025]. The stability machinery behind the split is a router-orthogonalization loss applied directly to router weight vectors with an AdamW-style decoupled update (coefficient 1e-3, not LR-scaled), plus a token-balanced loss that normalizes cross-entropy by total sequence length rather than valid-token count, because in multimodal sequences only text tokens contribute to loss and their proportion varies wildly sample-to-sample — otherwise “induces substantial gradient variance” ERNIE 4.5 Technical Report §3.4 [PAPER, Jun 2025].

Modality-isolated MoE did not originate at Baidu. Meta FAIR’s MoMa (Jul 2024) publishes it first, with FLOPs-accounting rigor ERNIE’s paper doesn’t reproduce: a 1.4B model with 4 text + 4 image experts hits 3.7× overall FLOPs savings (2.6× text, 5.2× image) at a 1T-token budget, versus 3× for a standard 8-expert mixed-modal MoE (3× text, 2.8× image); adding mixture-of-depths pushes the isolated design to 4.2× overall MoMa: Efficient Early-Fusion Pre-training with Mixture of Modality-Aware Experts [PAPER, Jul 2024]. Image tokens benefit disproportionately — the paper’s interpretation is that they are structurally more redundant, so a narrower expert suffices without quality loss. MoMa’s efficiency section also surfaces an operational cost ERNIE’s paper glosses over: under FSDP, the text:image token ratio varies across GPUs and iterations, creating a “cascading straggler effect” bounding step latency by the slowest GPU’s worst-case modality mix. The fix is dataloading-level, not routing-level — pre-align the text:image data-mix ratio to the per-GPU expert ratio, plus sequential expert execution by modality to overlap compute with weight prefetch MoMa §3.1–3.2 [PAPER, Jul 2024].

The load-bearing update to this whole line is that ERNIE 5.0 (Feb 2026) explicitly reversed it. Its own text: “modality-agnostic expert routing, where routing decisions are conditioned on unified token representations rather than modality identifiers… In contrast to modality-isolated routing strategies in our previous models (ERNIE 4.5)” ERNIE 5.0 Technical Report [PAPER, Feb 2026]. ERNIE 5.0 pairs this with an ultra-sparse design (activation rate below 3%) and a novel elastic training paradigm — within one pretraining run the model simultaneously learns sub-networks varying in depth (75% full-depth / 25% reduced-depth), expert width (80% full-expert / 20% sampled-subset), and routing sparsity (80% standard top-k / 20% varied top-k), reportedly preserving near-full performance at 53.7% activated parameters and 35.8% total parameters ERNIE 5.0 Technical Report [PAPER, Feb 2026]. Its disclosed parallelism is 4-way TP, 12-way PP with virtual stages, 64-way EP, ZeRO-1 DP, plus context parallelism, and it disaggregates vision/audio tokenizers onto separate GPU nodes from the MoE backbone — a disaggregation choice absent from ERNIE 4.5. No GPU count, MFU, or per-modality token budget was disclosed for 5.0. This is a rare, citable case of a frontier lab publicly walking back its own architectural bet on multimodal MoE routing within eight months of shipping it, and it should reframe modality-isolated MoE as a contested, actively-being-abandoned-by-its-inventor’s-successor design point rather than a converged answer.

Pattern B — continuous encoder feeding a modality-unaware shared MoE. This is the majority pattern in practice, despite “native multimodal” marketing language suggesting something architecturally novel. Llama 4, Kimi-VL, and Qwen3-VL all do this: a vision encoder (MetaCLIP for Llama 4; MoonViT, a NaViT-style native-resolution packer, for Kimi-VL; continuously-trained SigLIP-2 for Qwen3-VL) produces continuous embeddings, an adapter projects them into the LLM’s hidden dimension, and the resulting tokens flow through the same expert pool as text with no router modification. Meta’s own description of Llama 4’s MoE layer — “128 routed experts and a shared expert. Each token is sent to the shared expert and also to one of the 128 routed experts” — makes no distinction for image tokens The Llama 4 herd [OFFICIAL-BLOG, Apr 2025]. Kimi-VL’s MoE decoder (Moonlight, 16B total / 2.8B active) uses standard 4D parallelism (DP + EP + PP + CP) with no modality-conditioned routing logic disclosed Kimi-VL Technical Report [PAPER, Jun 2025 rev.]. Qwen3-VL frames its novelty as DeepStack — injecting visual features from three distinct ViT depths into the first three LLM layers via dedicated mergers — and interleaved MRoPE, not routing; its MoE variant’s expert pool is otherwise unmodified from the text-only Qwen3 MoE Qwen3-VL Technical Report [PAPER, Nov/Dec 2025].

Pattern C — dual independent MoE stacks split by function, not modality. Qwen3-Omni and its April 2026 successor Qwen3.5-Omni use a “Thinker-Talker” architecture where both halves are independently sparse MoE. The Thinker handles multimodal understanding and text generation; the Talker consumes only audio/visual features (not the Thinker’s text hidden states) and autoregressively predicts streaming multi-codebook speech tokens via an MTP-style multi-track codec, with waveform synthesis via a lightweight causal ConvNet (“Code2Wav”) rather than a diffusion model, running audio codes at 12.5 Hz for low-latency synthesis Qwen3-Omni Technical Report / Qwen3.5-Omni Technical Report [PAPER, Sep 2025 / Apr 2026]. This is architecturally distinct from A and B — the modality split happens at the macro level, two independent transformer/MoE stacks with different objectives, rather than at the expert level within one stack.

Gemini 2.5 is confirmed sparse-MoE native-multimodal with no further routing-mechanism disclosure, trained on TPUv5p pods of 8,960 chips each with synchronous data-parallel training across multiple datacenters Gemini 2.5 Technical Report [OFFICIAL-REPORT, 2025]. Amazon’s Nova report is essentially silent on architecture beyond confirming a Transformer base and multilingual/multimodal pretraining data, with Nova Canvas/Reel disclosed as architecturally separate latent-diffusion models with a VAE The Amazon Nova Family of Models [OFFICIAL-REPORT, Mar 2025]. Both are genuine disclosure gaps, not search failures.

Two very recent papers, both positioned explicitly as responses to ERNIE 4.5’s limitations, suggest the field is converging on a middle ground. SMoES (“Soft Modality-Guided Expert Specialization in MoE-VLMs,” Apr 2026) argues hard modality routing causes “sharp specialization at the cost of rigid boundaries that hinder adaptation to cross-modal features,” and proposes continuous, layer-adaptive soft modality scores plus an “expert binning” mechanism for EP-friendly device placement, reporting +4.2% average on language-only tasks, +0.9% on multimodal tasks, a 56.1% reduction in EP communication overhead, and 12.3% throughput improvement across four MoE-VLM backbones SMoES [PAPER, Apr 2026]. MoIIE (“Mixture of Intra- and Inter-Modality Experts”) pursues a related hybrid where some experts are modality-specific and others shared cross-modal MoIIE [PAPER, Aug 2025]. Net read: treat “hard isolation vs. full sharing” as a tunable spectrum with a real, now-quantified EP-communication cost at the isolated end, not a binary commitment.

Tokenization: discrete codebooks, continuous embeddings, and vision-as-compression

Two tokenizer families coexist and interact with MoE routing very differently. Chameleon (Meta, May 2024) represents the discrete, unified-vocabulary approach: a 512×512 image becomes 1,024 discrete tokens from an 8,192-entry codebook, and those 8,192 codes live inside a single 65,536-token vocabulary shared with text, so image and text tokens are literally interchangeable positions in the same embedding table and the same next-token objective applies uniformly Chameleon: Mixed-Modal Early-Fusion Foundation Models [PAPER, May 2024]. This genuinely early fusion — image and text tokens flow through identical weights from layer 1 — required two non-obvious stability interventions: QK-norm (LayerNorm on query/key vectors pre-softmax, to control attention-logit growth, worse under mixed-modal training) and a z-loss term (1e-5 · log²Z penalizing softmax-partition-function growth), plus, for the 34B variant, reordered norm placement to bound feedforward-block norm growth. ERNIE 5.0 uses a related but more sophisticated discrete scheme — a “causal 2D multi-scale tokenizer” for images, “inflated into 3D for video,” with bit-wise quantization and progressive tokenizer switching from low-bit to higher-bit variants (a curriculum over discretization granularity, not a fixed codebook), while audio uses hierarchical Residual Vector Quantization codec tokens with distilled semantic tokens from Whisper and “Next-Codec Prediction” across codec dimensions ERNIE 5.0 Technical Report [PAPER, Feb 2026].

The majority pattern — ERNIE 4.5, Kimi-VL, Qwen3-VL, Llama 4 — does not discretize images into a shared vocabulary at all; continuous ViT features are projected into the LLM’s embedding space, so there is no “image vocab size,” and the interaction with the 200K+ text vocabulary is purely at the embedding-dimension level. ERNIE 4.5’s vision encoder is an adaptive-resolution ViT (independently resizes height/width to the nearest patch multiple rather than forcing square resizing) using 2D RoPE for spatial position and an image-packing technique to batch variable-length images; its adapter does spatial compression (2×2 patches → 4× token reduction) and temporal compression (2×) via pixel-shuffle + MLP, treating static images as synthetic two-frame video to unify handling. For the fused sequence it uses a genuinely novel 3D RoPE — temporal axis gets the lowest (slowest-varying) frequencies, spatial height/width axes get the interleaved remaining frequencies, and text tokens default to standard 1D RoPE ERNIE 4.5 Technical Report §2.2–2.4 [PAPER, Jun 2025]. It also encodes video time via literal timestamp text rendered onto frames rather than a position-embedding or token-based scheme, claiming this is “flexible to any frame rate, consumes no extra tokens.” Qwen3-VL converged on a related but token-based solution — explicit timestamp tokens (e.g. <3.0 seconds>) prefixed to each temporal patch — because tying temporal position IDs to absolute time, as Qwen2.5-VL did via MRoPE, “produces excessively large and sparse temporal position ids for long videos” Qwen3-VL Technical Report §2.3 [PAPER, Nov 2025]. Kimi-VL’s MoonViT instead relies purely on interpolated + 2D-RoPE positional embeddings with no timestamp mechanism, its NaViT-style patch-packing flattening variable-resolution images into 1D sequences that “share the same core computation operators and optimization as a language model,” explicitly to preserve FlashAttention-compatible variable-length training throughput Kimi-VL Technical Report §2.1 [PAPER, Jun 2025].

The most consequential idea in this beat for a text-centric host is DeepSeek-OCR’s reframing of vision tokens as a context-compression primitive rather than a modality-understanding mechanism. Its encoder is a serial SAM-base (80M, window attention) → 16× convolutional token compressor → CLIP-large (300M, dense global attention) pipeline: a 1024×1024 image becomes 4,096 SAM patch tokens, compressed to 256 before the CLIP stage. The headline compression curve: within 10× compression, roughly 97% OCR decode accuracy; at roughly 15× compression (64 vision tokens per ~1,000-token document), 79–96.5% depending on document; at roughly 20× compression, accuracy drops to about 60% DeepSeek-OCR: Contexts Optical Compression [PAPER, Oct 2025]. Training data mix was 70% OCR (30M PDF pages + 20M scene-text images), 20% general vision, 10% text-only. The paper poses vision-token compression as a general answer to “for a document containing 1,000 words, how many vision tokens are at least needed for decoding,” and floats progressive optical downsampling of older conversational or document history as a token-economics technique — a pretraining- and serving-time context-budget lever, not an OCR trick, directly relevant to a 200B-active host’s long-document token-budget planning even absent any product need for OCR (see Ch12).

Data mixing at scale: what the disclosed curricula actually show

Clean, comparable percentage-of-tokens-by-modality tables are the scarcest resource in this literature — most 2025–2026 reports disclose stage structure far more consistently than stage token-count percentages, a genuine and consistent reporting gap relative to, e.g., Llama 3’s clean 50/25/17/8 text-domain split (checked directly against ERNIE 4.5/5.0, Qwen3-VL, Kimi-VL, Chameleon, and Llama 4’s own materials — the absence is real, not a search failure). What the corpus does give is stage-by-stage token budgets from four labs:

Two patterns cross all four. First, every non-Chameleon lab stages modality introduction — text-only or text-heavy pretraining first, vision/multimodal data introduced or ramped later — rather than mixing from token zero the way Chameleon did; ERNIE 4.5’s paper is explicit that vision experts are added late “to avoid routing collapse,” and Kimi-VL’s multimodal proportion is described as “gradually increasing” from a pure-text start toward a ~40% ceiling. Second, Qwen3-VL’s total disclosed pretraining budget (~2.17T tokens) is far below the 15T+ text-only budgets typical of contemporaneous pure-LLM training (see Ch6, Ch8’s ladder-recipe discussion), suggesting that VL pretraining built on top of a strong existing text base is token-cheap relative to training multimodal capability from scratch — consistent with Qwen3-VL’s own square-root-normalized per-token loss, adopted specifically to prevent multimodal and text losses from imbalancing each other during joint training on a comparatively small joint-training budget Qwen3-VL Technical Report §3.1 [PAPER, Nov 2025].

MoMa’s efficiency section adds an operational point orthogonal to curation: under modality-isolated routing, the data-mix ratio isn’t purely a curation decision, it is a distributed-systems constraint that must be co-designed with the per-GPU expert ratio to avoid stragglers MoMa §3.1 [PAPER, Jul 2024] — an easy coupling to miss if data mixing and MoE architecture are organizationally owned by different teams, which they usually are.

The input pipeline: packing, load balance, and deterministic replay

Chapter 13 owns dataloading requirements in full; this section covers only what multimodal data specifically breaks. Megatron’s dedicated multimodal loader, Megatron-Energon, is WebDataset-lineage — a standardized on-disk format optimized for high-speed multi-rank reads, dataset mixing, and, critically, savable/restorable iteration state via get_savable_loader(), so a long multimodal run can checkpoint which shard, which sample within shard, and worker RNG state, then resume deterministically after preemption Megatron-Energon [OFFICIAL-DOCS/CODE, 2026]. This state-save design is architecturally closer to MosaicML StreamingDataset’s or Grain’s continuous-index approach than to Megatron-Core’s precompute-then-stream GPTDataset model, and it becomes load-bearing the moment any multimodal or agentic-data component enters a 10T host’s mix even at low weight.

The core breakage is that fixed-length sequence packing assumes roughly uniform per-sample compute cost — an assumption that holds for text and fails hard under variable-resolution images and, worse, variable-duration video. ERNIE 4.5’s paper documents this directly: naive packing causes “extreme computational and memory imbalances between different data parallel ranks of the ViT encoder,” because “video data presents more severe imbalance due to temporal length variations” than image resolution does. Their fix is hierarchical: a coarse level sorts all packed sequences by token count within the ViT encoder’s DP group and round-robin-distributes them for balanced total token count per device; a fine level, “packed sequence parallelism” (PSP, distinct from Megatron’s TP-based sequence parallelism), concatenates and evenly partitions packed sequences along the sequence-length dimension outside attention operators, then performs an all-to-all to exchange sequence-length and attention-head dimensions before and after attention (which needs the full sequence-length dimension per packed sequence). Measured result: up to 32% overall end-to-end multimodal training throughput improvement versus no load balancing ERNIE 4.5 §5.1.2 [PAPER, Jun 2025].

ByteDance’s MegaScale-Data paper quantifies the same failure mode independently and more starkly, in a pure dataloading context rather than an encoder-specific one: because attention FLOPs scale with sequence length squared, non-uniform per-rank sample-length distributions from multi-source data create compute imbalance even when sample counts are balanced across ranks. In an 8-GPU VLM trial, max microbatch FLOPs were 3.2× higher (image) and 6.9× higher (full sequence) than the minimum — pure dataloader-composition-induced bubbles, invisible to naive samples/sec metrics MegaScale-Data [PAPER, EuroSys 2026]. Their actor-model redesign, which decouples preprocessing scheduling from the standard iterator model, delivers 4.5× end-to-end training throughput and 13.5× CPU memory reduction versus PyTorch DataLoader, tf.data, Ray Data, Cachew, and Pecan baselines at up to 4,096 GPUs, with context-length-dependent gains (1.71× at 4K, 3.09× at 16K) and fault tolerance via shadow-loader hot failover with differential checkpointing plus elastic resharding for mid-run parallelism changes. This is the closest thing in the public literature to “what a dataloader built for a 10T-MoE-scale run with any multimodal component should look like” — and notably it is not Megatron-Core, Energon, StreamingDataset, or Grain; it’s a bespoke, unreleased system.

torchtitan’s own maintainers flag a smaller but concrete version of the same problem: multimodal loading — image download, decode, resize, pad — is CPU-intensive enough to bottleneck a PyTorch-native reference trainer, with “more DataLoader workers” as the first-line mitigation torchtitan issue #650 [CODE, GitHub]. NVIDIA DALI exists as a GPU-accelerated answer to exactly this class of problem but its reader/decoder library is overwhelmingly vision/audio-centric (JPEG/video decode, augmentation) with no text-pretraining-specific readers, making it relevant mainly if interleaved data is tokenized to image patches rather than for the text stream itself.

ERNIE 4.5 solves a second, dataloading-adjacent problem worth flagging independent of its multimodal motivation: reproducible, non-duplicating data consumption under elastic or interrupted training. REEAO (“Record Everything Everywhere All at Once”) is a bitwise-deterministic data-flow manager guaranteeing the exact token sequence is fixed before training starts and stays reproducible “even under complex scenarios such as changes in the number of training nodes, distribution strategies, global batch size, or context length” ERNIE 4.5 §3.2 [PAPER, Jun 2025]. ERNIE’s own framing is that multimodal data’s heterogeneity specifically motivated building this, but the guarantee is valuable for any 10T-scale run with mid-run elasticity, text-only or not (cross-ref Ch19’s fault-tolerance and checkpoint-replay coverage).

Parallelism topology: decoupling encoder and backbone

Does a vision encoder change EP/TP/PP composition? Yes, and a very recent NVIDIA paper is the first to quantify it systematically rather than gesture at it. “Heterogeneous Parallelism for Multimodal Large Language Model Training,” released May 28, 2026, identifies the exact failure mode a 10T/200B host would hit forcing one shared TP/CP/PP/DP/EP layout onto both encoder and LLM: LLM-scale tensor-parallel degrees introduce all-reduce overhead that “can outweigh compute and memory savings” for a small encoder; LLM context parallelism shards the fused sequence, but encoder sequence length is bounded by image/video resolution rather than context window, so inheriting LLM-CP wastes shards that could instead be encoder-DP replicas processing more samples per step; and pipeline parallelism confines the encoder to the LLM’s first PP stage, forcing encoder-heavy workloads to inherit that stage’s TP degree and overload it Heterogeneous Parallelism for Multimodal Large Language Model Training [PAPER, May 2026].

Their solution is a Megatron-LM extension in which each module — encoder, LLM — independently picks TP/CP/PP/DP/EP, connected via autograd-aware “boundary communicators” that materialize forward activations in the destination layout and route backward gradients back to the source layout. Measured on an H100 80GB cluster (900 GB/s NVLink intra-node, 8×400 Gb/s ConnectX-7 IB inter-node): for a 1B encoder + 14B LLM at 8K context on 72 GPUs, the best homogeneous configuration is a shared 4/1/1/18 TP/CP/PP/DP layout, while the best heterogeneous configuration splits to encoder 2/1/1/36 and LLM 2/1/2/18 at identical GPU count and peak memory. Across the full sweep, colocated heterogeneous execution improves TFLOPS/GPU by up to 49.3% over the homogeneous shared-layout baseline. For a 120B LLM, where encoder memory residency starts competing with LLM pipeline layout, non-colocated execution — encoder and LLM on physically disjoint GPU islands — becomes the better operating point, improving aggregate token throughput by up to 13.0% and TFLOPS/GPU by up to 9.6%. The crossover from colocated-optimal to non-colocated-optimal happens starting around a 6B-parameter encoder paired against a 120B LLM. Convergence parity (FP32 step-level numerical tolerance plus longer BF16 LLaVA-style runs) is validated, and the system ships open-source as a Megatron-LM extension arXiv:2605.27678 App. A [PAPER, May 2026].
