This paper directly extends the parallelism-composition math this book develops for the text backbone alone in Ch16 — an EP/TP/PP/DP layout tuned for a 200B-active MoE at 100K GPUs was never going to be encoder-optimal even for a small encoder, and the crossover point (~6B encoder parameters) gives a concrete threshold for when to stop trying to force colocation.

Compute accounting: what vision tokens actually cost

Three independent quantifications converge on the same nuanced answer: a vision token is cheaper per-token in FFN FLOPs than a text token under modality-isolated routing, but that advantage can reverse once encoder overhead, EP communication tax, and architecture-driven (not modality-driven) throughput effects are counted.

  • ERNIE 4.5: vision-expert FFN intermediate dimension is one-third of text-expert dimension, giving roughly 66% lower per-token FFN FLOPs for a routed vision token versus a routed text token, since FFN FLOPs scale with the product of input and intermediate dimension ERNIE 4.5 §2.1 [PAPER, Jun 2025].
  • MoMa: under FLOPs-matched training, image tokens gain more from modality-specific parameter allocation than text tokens do — 5.2× FLOPs savings for image versus 2.6× for text in the 4-text/4-image configuration — interpreted as image tokens being structurally more redundant, so a narrower expert suffices without quality loss arXiv:2407.21770 [PAPER, Jul 2024].
  • Kimi-VL: the vision encoder itself (MoonViT, 400M params) is a rounding error relative to the 16B-total MoE backbone, and Kimi-VL’s own comparison states overall training throughput is “around 60% higher” than a 7B dense VLM at comparable capability — but this delta is architecture-driven (MoE sparsity), not modality-driven, and shouldn’t be read as a vision-specific efficiency number Kimi-VL Technical Report [PAPER, Jun 2025].
  • DeepSeek-OCR inverts the framing entirely, asking not “what does a vision token cost” but “how few vision tokens can replace N text tokens” — landing on a 10–20× token-count compression ratio at 60–97% fidelity depending on target compression. This is a token-count reduction lever, orthogonal to per-token FLOPs cost, and arguably the more consequential number for a 200B-active host doing long-context or long-document pretraining arXiv:2510.18234 [PAPER, Oct 2025].
  • SMoES supplies the missing cost on the other side of the ledger: its expert-binning fix for modality-isolated-style routing reduces EP communication overhead by 56.1% and improves throughput by 12.3% relative to unbinned modality-aware routing under expert-parallel deployment — direct evidence that Pattern A (modality-isolated MoE) carries a nontrivial, now-quantified EP communication tax versus Pattern B (modality-agnostic routing) that a 10T-host planner should budget for if choosing hard isolation arXiv:2604.23996 [PAPER, Apr 2026].

Two production-scale throughput datapoints round out the picture. Meta’s only disclosed Behemoth efficiency number is 390 TFLOPs/GPU using FP8 on 32,000 GPUs The Llama 4 herd [OFFICIAL-BLOG, Apr 2025] — notable here mainly because Meta discloses no vision-specific FLOPs breakdown, consistent with Pattern B’s “no modality-aware anything” design. ByteDance Seed’s VeOmni (Aug 2025, feeding AAAI 2026’s “OmniScale”) decouples parallel-strategy choice (FSDP/HSDP/SP/EP) from model definition via a “parallel plan” API and reports a 30B omni-modal MoE training at over 2,800 tokens/sec/GPU scaling to 160K context on 128 GPUs, a 7B dense VLM hitting 61.5% MFU at 192K context on 8 GPUs (FSDP+SP4), and a 72B dense VLM hitting 54.82% MFU at 96K context on 128 GPUs VeOmni [PAPER, Aug 2025] — the most complete public MFU accounting for omni-modal training infrastructure in this corpus, though its link to any specific lab’s frontier production run, including ByteDance’s own Doubao Seed 2.x line, is unconfirmed.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

A primarily-text 10T/200B host is unlikely to ship without some multimodal capability eventually, given every disclosed or credibly-reported peer architecture in Ch2/Ch3 already has one. The infrastructure decisions here split cleanly into cheap-now/expensive-later and can-defer categories:

  • Decouple encoder and backbone parallelism from day one, even with zero vision data in the launch mix. Building the LLM’s TP/CP/PP/DP/EP composition (Ch16) as a module-level choice rather than a single fixed layout is the single highest-leverage provisioning move this chapter surfaces — NVIDIA’s heterogeneous-parallelism paper shows the retrofit cost (forcing a shared layout, then re-deriving a decoupled one later) is exactly the 49.3% TFLOPS/GPU gap between homogeneous and colocated-heterogeneous execution arXiv:2605.27678 [PAPER, May 2026].
  • Default to Pattern B (continuous encoder into a modality-unaware shared MoE) if and when multimodal data is added, not Pattern A. It is both the majority pattern among 2025–2026 shipped systems and the one ERNIE 5.0’s own reversal, and SMoES’s quantified EP tax, argue against hard isolation as a durable choice. Treat modality-isolated routing as a research-frontier option requiring its own EP-communication budget, not a safe default.
  • Provision Energon-class dataloader state-save semantics and hierarchical packing/load-balance logic even for a text-only launch. The measured cost of not having this — up to 32% end-to-end throughput lost to naive packing under ERNIE’s regime, and the 3.2×/6.9× per-rank FLOP imbalance MegaScale-Data measures from dataloader composition alone — recurs the moment any variable-length, variable-cost data source (video, long documents, agent trajectories) enters the mix, which is a near-certainty for a run planning midtraining and agentic data curricula (Ch12, Ch13).
  • Build bitwise-deterministic data replay (REEAO-style) as a fault-tolerance primitive, not a multimodal one. It composes directly with Ch19’s checkpoint and elastic-restart architecture and pays for itself independent of any modality decision.
  • Treat vision-token compression (DeepSeek-OCR’s framing) as a context-budget lever worth reserving data and encoder-path headroom for, even in a text-centric model. A 10–20× token-count reduction at 60–97% fidelity is a lever on effective context length and long-document training cost that has nothing to do with shipping OCR as a product feature.
  • Budget FLOPs and EP-communication accounting separately if any modality-isolated routing is considered. The ~66% per-token FFN saving ERNIE 4.5 reports is real but is not free — SMoES’s 56.1% EP-communication-overhead reduction from expert binning implies the unbinned baseline tax is substantial, and that tax must be modeled against Ch16’s EP topology before committing to Pattern A over Pattern B.

None of this requires shipping a multimodal model on day one of a 10T/200B run. It requires not architecting the parallelism stack, the dataloader, and the checkpoint/replay layer as if text is the only modality that will ever exist — because on the evidence of every peer lab covered in this corpus, it will not be for long.

Part IIIThe Data

Chapter 10 · Part III — The Data

The Data Supply Chain: Crawlers, Licensing, and the Stock

Every parallelism formula in Ch16 and every optimizer schedule in Ch7 presupposes an input that this chapter treats as anything but given: 25-50T tokens of text, arriving on schedule, at a quality bar that survives Ch11’s filters. By mid-2026 that assumption is the shakiest load-bearing wall in the whole plan. The open web is enclosing in real time, the two labs with the cleanest legal book-acquisition playbooks got there by either destroying merchandise at industrial scale or settling for $1.5B, and the public estimate of the entire addressable human-text stock is measured in the same units as a single frontier training run’s appetite. This chapter reconciles what a 10T-parameter, 200B-active MoE actually needs against what demonstrably exists, and specifies what a new entrant would have to build to get it.

The own-crawler economy: who crawls, how much succeeds, and what that reveals

By 2026 every frontier lab operates a dedicated, named, robots.txt-addressable crawler fleet distinct from search indexing — GPTBot/OAI-SearchBot/ChatGPT-User (OpenAI), ClaudeBot/Claude-SearchBot/claude-web (Anthropic), Google-Extended/Googlebot/GoogleOther (Google), Meta-ExternalAgent (Meta), Amazonbot, Bytespider (ByteDance), PetalBot (Huawei) — directly measurable via Cloudflare’s Radar bot-traffic telemetry, which classifies verified bots by declared purpose (training / search / user-action) [OFFICIAL-BLOG, blog.cloudflare.com/from-googlebot-to-gptbot, 06/2025].

The volume numbers move fast and reshuffle hard. Cloudflare’s May 2025 snapshot put request-volume share at Googlebot 50%, Bingbot 8.7%, GPTBot 7.7%, ClaudeBot 5.4%, GoogleOther 4.3%, Amazonbot 4.2%, Bytespider 2.9% [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 06/2025]. Year-over-year growth from May 2024 to May 2025 was wildly uneven: GPTBot +305%, ChatGPT-User +2,825%, PerplexityBot +157,490% off a tiny base — while ClaudeBot fell -46%, Bytespider fell -85%, Amazonbot fell -35% [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 06/2025]. Among AI-only crawlers specifically, Bytespider’s share collapsed from 42% to 7.2% over the same window while GPTBot’s rose from 5% to 30% [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 06/2025]. That picture inverted again within a year: by June 2026, ClaudeBot surged +66% month-over-month to roughly 19.8-20% of AI-bot traffic, becoming the #2 AI crawler behind Googlebot and ahead of both Meta-ExternalAgent (~10.2%) and GPTBot (~9.4-9.6%); with Claude-SearchBot added, Anthropic’s combined footprint reached ~23%, nearly matching Googlebot’s 24.9-27.5% share of AI-bot-classified traffic in Q2 2026 [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, Cloudflare Radar via secondary aggregation, 06-07/2026]. Bytespider’s brief 2025 resurgence reversed again, dropping back to ~7.3% by June 2026 [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 06-07/2026]. The month-to-month volatility — GPTBot and ClaudeBot trading the #1 AI-crawler spot repeatedly across April-June 2026 — is a genuinely underappreciated operational signal: it suggests labs are throttling and bursting crawl fleets on a cadence plausibly tied to training-run data-refresh cycles, rather than running steady-state background crawls, meaning a competitor’s crawl telemetry is itself a leading indicator of their training calendar.

Crawl-purpose breakdown confirms training dominance is durable, not a one-time spike: 52.3% of AI crawler requests in the 28 days to June 22, 2026 were for model training, versus only 2.6% live user fetches [OFFICIAL-BLOG, Cloudflare Radar, 06/2026], consistent with Cloudflare’s July 2025 finding that training crawling ran 79-82% of total AI crawl volume across Jan-Jul 2025, up from 72% in July 2024 [OFFICIAL-BLOG, blog.cloudflare.com/crawlers-click-ai-bots-training, 08/2025].

The single sharpest metric of extractive-versus-reciprocal crawling behavior is the crawl-to-refer ratio — pages fetched per human visitor eventually referred back — and Anthropic is the extreme outlier. Cloudflare measured Anthropic’s ratio at 286,930:1 in January 2025, falling to a still-enormous 38,065:1 by July 2025, against OpenAI’s much flatter 1,217:1 → 1,091:1 over the same window [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 08/2025]. This gap is a direct artifact of ClaudeBot being almost purely training-oriented with negligible consumer-search product driving referrals back, versus OpenAI’s ChatGPT search integration — and it is exactly the kind of asymmetry that plausibly shaped both site-level blocking decisions and the political pressure behind Cloudflare’s September 2026 default-block policy (below).

Vercel’s hosted-site network corroborates the picture independently (December 2024) [OFFICIAL-BLOG, vercel.com/blog/the-rise-of-the-ai-crawler]: Googlebot 4.5B fetches/month, GPTBot 569M, Claude 370M, AppleBot 314M, PerplexityBot 24.4M — GPTBot plus Claude combined at roughly 20% of Googlebot’s volume. AI crawlers post markedly higher 404 rates than Googlebot (ChatGPT 34.82%, Claude 34.16%, versus Googlebot’s 8.22%) [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 12/2024], a sign these fleets still hit stale or broken URL lists more than a mature scheduler should — an engineering-maturity gap the greenfield spec below should not replicate.

Common Crawl’s slow-motion enclosure

The foundational academic study of the commons closing is Longpre et al., “Consent in Crisis: The Rapid Decline of the AI Data Commons” (Data Provenance Initiative / MIT Media Lab) [PAPER, arXiv:2407.14933, 07/2024]. Auditing more than 14,000 domains across C4, RefinedWeb, and Dolma, the paper finds that over 28% of the most actively-maintained, high-quality C4 source domains are now fully robots.txt-disallowed, and roughly 45% of C4’s underlying data is now restricted by Terms of Service even where robots.txt stays silent — a split-brain problem, since TOS restrictions are legally live but technically invisible to a crawler that respects only robots.txt. The paper dates the inflection to fall 2023, coincident with OpenAI and Google publishing dedicated AI-training user-agent strings that made selective blocking legible for the first time.

By 2025-2026 the trend has compounded, per secondary aggregation of Cloudflare’s own robots.txt corpus scans [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY throughout this paragraph — SEO/content-marketing sites reprocessing Radar data, not primary researchers, treat point estimates with caution]: AI-blocking among “reputable” sites rose from roughly 23% (September 2023) to roughly 60% (May 2025), median blocking site now disallowing about 15.5 distinct AI user-agents; more than 2.5M sites had fully disallowed AI training and more than 1M Cloudflare customers had activated AI-crawler blocking as of August 2025. The Q1 2026 DISALLOW-rule frequency ranking is the sharpest single data point in this beat: GPTBot is the most-blocked bot (5.52% of all disallow rules), then CCBot (5.08%) — Common Crawl’s own bot is blocked nearly as often as OpenAI’s dedicated training crawler — then ClaudeBot (4.88%), Google-Extended (4.44%), Bytespider (4.23%) [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, Q1/2026]. This directly undercuts the assumption that “just use Common Crawl” sidesteps the consent fight: CC is caught in the same dragnet, at nearly the same rate, as the labs it supposedly serves as a neutral alternative to.

Common Crawl remains large in raw terms — current monthly snapshots run roughly 2.0-2.2B pages and 350-400 TiB uncompressed (CC-MAIN-2026-25, June 2026: 2.10B pages; CC-MAIN-2026-21, May 2026: 2.16B pages), cumulative corpus in the hundreds-of-petabytes / 300B+-page range since 2008 [OFFICIAL/PRIMARY, commoncrawl.org, verified 07/2026] — but the quality-weighted usable fraction is shrinking, because the domains that matter most for quality filtering (news, forums, reference sites, precisely the ones with well-maintained robots.txt) are disproportionately represented among the newly-disallowed. Whether CCBot actually honors a disallow directed at AI-training bots specifically, or continues crawling under a “research use” theory, is not resolved by any source found in this pass; the best available inference is that CC respects Disallow: CCBot literally, meaning the 5.08% blocked-rule figure translates fairly directly into lost quality-domain coverage in every corpus (C4, RefinedWeb, FineWeb, DCLM) built on top of it — a medium-confidence inference, not a confirmed policy (see Ch11 for what those downstream corpora look like once built).

Cloudflare’s own structural interventions compound the picture. Pay Per Crawl (private beta July 1, 2025, GA August 2025) lets a domain owner set a flat per-request price and return HTTP 402 Payment Required to non-paying crawlers; named early adopters include Condé Nast, Time, AP, BuzzFeed, Reddit, Pinterest, and Stack Overflow, and by 2026 Cloudflare reports customers network-wide sending more than 1 billion HTTP 402 responses per day to AI crawlers — a lower bound on crawl attempts now actively monetization-gated [OFFICIAL-BLOG, blog.cloudflare.com/introducing-pay-per-crawl, 07/2025]. The Content Signals Policy (September 24, 2025) adds three machine-readable robots.txt signals — search, ai-input, ai-train — auto-injected into managed robots.txt, defaulting to search=yes, ai-train=no, live on more than 3.8 million domains as of mid-2026 [OFFICIAL-BLOG, blog.cloudflare.com/content-signals-policy, 09/2025]. Google has reportedly stated the signal has no binding effect on its crawling behavior [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, reported via SEO trade press; not independently re-verified — flag accordingly], underscoring these remain voluntary courtesy signals: a crawler operator can ignore ai-train=no with no more consequence than ignoring classic robots.txt Disallow today — legal and reputational risk only, not a technical barrier.

The most consequential near-term change is dated: starting September 15, 2026, “mixed-use” crawlers blending search, agent, and training signals will be blocked by default from any ad-carrying page, for all new customers, new sites, and existing free-tier accounts — pure search crawlers are exempted, and Google-Extended remains the AI-training opt-out channel [OFFICIAL-BLOG + TechCrunch, 07/2026]. Cloudflare’s stated rationale: more than 50% of AI crawler traffic re-fetches unchanged pages, and per CEO Matthew Prince, “the majority of traffic on the internet is now non-human” [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 07/2026]. Cloudflare is separately piloting “Pay Per Use” — compensation tied to actual content citation rather than raw fetch count, with partners Ceramic.ai and You.com, broad availability undated as of July 2026 [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2026]. The direction is unambiguous: crawl access is moving from an opt-out-by-default commons to an opt-in, metered, increasingly enforced marketplace, and a run planned for late 2026 or 2027 should assume the September 15 change is live for a meaningful fraction of the commercial web by the time crawling starts.

The licensing economy: real money, bounded scale

Licensing deals are a real revenue stream for publishers and a real legal-risk-mitigation play for labs, but they do not move the token-volume needle for a 25-50T-token budget. The table below compiles press-reported terms; exact figures are frequently undisclosed by the parties, so treat all dollar amounts as [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] unless noted otherwise.

The T&F and Wiley deals drew significant author backlash over the absence of opt-out or author compensation mechanisms — a governance failure mode a new entrant negotiating similar deals should design around explicitly. OpenAI alone reportedly holds roughly 20 verified publisher partnerships covering 160+ outlets in 20+ languages, per an independent aggregator not independently re-verified [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY]. But aggregate industry-wide licensing spend is dwarfed by a different budget line: multiple sources converge on each frontier lab spending on the order of $1B/year on human-provided training and evaluation data via Scale AI, Surge AI, and Mercor [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 2026]. Licensing deals are principally about real-time grounding, brand safety, and pre-empting lawsuits — not about closing a multi-trillion-token pretraining gap; budget the line item as legal and product expense, not data-volume strategy.

Books: two legal templates, one clean and one expensive

The clearest bifurcation in 2025-2026 data law separates how you acquire copyrighted text from whether training on it is fair use, and Bartz v. Anthropic produced the reference case. In June 2025, Judge William Alsup (N.D. Cal.) ruled that training on legally acquired books is “quintessentially transformative” and protected fair use — but that downloading and retaining pirated copies, even of books never used for training, is independently infringing, separate from any training use [legal filing, widely reported, 06/2025].

Anthropic’s own acquisition pipeline — internally codenamed “Project Panama” and surfaced via litigation discovery, reported by the Washington Post (01/2026), The Bookseller, and Futurism [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] — is the cleanest available template for a new entrant. The company purchased books in bulk, sometimes tens of thousands at a time, from resellers such as Better World Books and World of Books, then destructively scanned them: hydraulic cutting machines to debind, production-grade scanners, with the now-unbound, unsellable physical remains sent to recycling. Internal documents reportedly cite throughput of up to 8,000 books and 2 million pages per day, and an internal note stating “We don’t want it to be known that we are working on this” [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 01/2026]. The judge’s fair-use finding rested partly on three facts: the books were purchased rather than pirated, scanning was done in-house, and physical destruction prevented resale or redistribution of the source — a legally clean but operationally and reputationally expensive template, an industrial physical-logistics operation, not a script.

The other half of the case is cautionary. Anthropic separately settled for $1.5B (announced August 2025, court approval through September 2025) [widely reported] covering roughly 500,000 pirated works from LibGen and the lesser-known “PiLiMi” shadow library that the company held but had not necessarily used for training — the largest copyright settlement in US history, roughly $3,000 per class work. Anthropic settled this despite having won the fair-use argument for training use itself; the exposure came entirely from the piracy-acquisition claim, where statutory damages could reach $150,000 per work, implying theoretical liability above $70B once the class was certified. The lesson for a new entrant is explicit and three-part: purchase physical or licensed-digital copies rather than downloading shadow-library dumps; digitize in-house, destructively or DRM-respectingly; and do not retain pirated copies even “just in case” — retention itself, independent of training use, is the primary post-Bartz liability surface.

Meta’s parallel exposure is diverging toward risk, not settling toward safety. Kadrey v. Meta (June 2025, Judge Chhabria) found Meta’s training use fair use, but on a narrow, fact-specific record (plaintiffs failed to show market harm on that record), and Chhabria explicitly signaled future plaintiffs could win with better evidence. Discovery established Meta employees torrented roughly 82TB (Kadrey record) to as much as 267TB+ (per a newer suit’s allegations) of pirated books and journals via LibGen, Z-Library, and Anna’s Archive, including — per the newer suit — deliberately masking IP addresses while torrenting and stripping copyright management information, a distinct DMCA §1202 claim independent of fair use [court filings via press]. On May 5, 2026, five major publishers (Elsevier, Cengage, Hachette, Macmillan, McGraw Hill) plus author Scott Turow filed a fresh class action alleging Meta considered and abandoned licensing negotiations in April 2023 on direct instruction from Zuckerberg — evidence, if proven, that cuts hard against fair use’s good-faith factor [PAPER-ADJACENT/LEGAL, 05/2026]. As of July 2026 this is unresolved litigation, not settled law: a new entrant should not read Kadrey’s summary judgment as a green light for pirated-corpus acquisition, since the acquisition method itself now faces a second, more targeted legal challenge independent of the fair-use question.

A licensed, non-litigation alternative worth flagging alongside these two cases: Harvard’s Institutional Books 1.0, released by the Institutional Data Initiative in June 2025, digitizes 983,004 public-domain volumes from Harvard Library’s collections (part of the original Google Books scanning effort), spanning more than 250 languages and roughly 400M pages, tokenized to 242B tokens with OpenAI’s o200k_base scheme [PAPER, arXiv:2506.08300, 06/2025]. It is architecturally distinct from every web-crawl or PDF corpus in this chapter — pre-2006-digitized institutional scan OCR, refined, with historical-document register and out-of-copyright-era language distributions no Common-Crawl-derived corpus can supply. The catch for a commercial 10T-parameter run: despite the public-domain status of the underlying books, the initial release carries a non-commercial license, reflecting unresolved uncertainty around subsequent, non-Harvard re-digitizations of the same works — meaning it cannot simply be ingested today without a separate licensing conversation, unlike Common Pile v0.1’s openly-licensed sources (see Ch11 for that corpus’s specific, benchmark-identifiable quality gap on commonsense-reasoning tasks) [PAPER, 06/2025].

The gray zones: YouTube, audio, and the expert-data channel

A parallel, non-web data supply chain has scaled to a size that should be treated as available capacity, not hypothetical headroom. NYT reporting (widely cited, not independently re-verified in the underlying research pass — [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY]) states that OpenAI, having assessed by 2021 it was running short of high-quality text, built Whisper specifically to transcribe audio and video at scale, and used it to transcribe over 1 million hours of YouTube video as GPT-4-era training data, with internal awareness this arguably violated YouTube’s terms of service and a decision to proceed on a fair-use theory anyway [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, ~2024 reporting]. This is distinct from Whisper’s own published training corpus — the original model used 680,000 hours of multilingual/multitask supervised audio [OFFICIAL-BLOG, openai.com/index/whisper, 09/2022], and large-v3 scaled to 1M hours weakly-labeled plus 4M hours Whisper-v2-pseudo-labeled [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 11/2023] — two separate million-hour-scale audio datasets easy to conflate. The operational lesson: audio/video-derived text is a proven, board-approved-scale channel, but carries the same ToS/consent exposure as web scraping — available capacity with real legal tail-risk, not free lunch (Ch9 covers the audio/video-native pretraining case where this becomes a first-class modality rather than a text-extraction source).

By 2026 the human-data vendor market has bifurcated from cheap crowd labeling toward credentialed specialist networks explicitly marketed for pretraining-scale use, not just RLHF: Mercor (roughly 300,000 vetted experts, “off-the-shelf, license-ready” pretraining datasets), Scale AI’s Expert Match, Surge AI’s premium expert pipelines [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 2025-2026]. This is now materially a data-generation function: expert-written worked solutions, textbook-style explanations, and domain Q&A at this scale function as a synthetic-adjacent, high-density pretraining source distinct from both web crawl and licensed-publisher archives. The aggregate spend figure — each frontier lab reportedly on the order of $1B/year — is the same number cited for licensing context above, and it is worth stating plainly: this vendor channel, not publisher licensing, is where the serious money in acquired (as opposed to crawled) data actually goes.

The stock math: reconciling Epoch AI against a 25-50T budget

Epoch AI’s “Will We Run Out of Data? Limits of LLM Scaling Based on Human-Generated Data” (originally November 2022, substantially revised June 2024) remains the load-bearing public estimate of the addressable stock [PAPER, arXiv:2211.04325, 06/2024]. The revised numbers: an effective stock of quality- and repetition-adjusted human-generated public text of roughly 300 trillion tokens, 90% confidence interval 100T-1000T, and an 80% confidence interval for full exhaustion between 2026 and 2032. The 2024 revision roughly quintupled the 2022 estimate (which had projected exhaustion by 2024), for two operationally relevant reasons: careful filtering makes raw web data outperform curated corpora more than expected, and multi-epoch repetition — several epochs “without significant degradation” — expands the effective stock 2-5x over the naive single-pass count. At Chinchilla-style compute-optimal training the stock supports models up to roughly 5×10^28 FLOP, a level Epoch projected around 2028, though this FLOP-year mapping predates the overtraining trends that have since dominated frontier practice and should be treated as dated. Under aggressive overtraining — 100x past Chinchilla-optimal token:parameter ratio — the stock could be exhausted as early as 2025; Epoch’s own model flags that overtraining, exactly the strategy frontier labs have pursued since Llama 3 on dense/effective-parameter terms, burns through the stock fastest.

Reconciling this against the reader’s stated 25-50T-token target requires being explicit about what “tokens” means. Two hundred billion active parameters at even a moderate 20-40x overtraining ratio — well below extreme small-model ratios like Llama 3 8B’s roughly 1875x tokens/param, but plausible for a frontier-scale active-parameter budget — implies a token target in the 4-8T range on active-parameter Chinchilla logic alone (Ch6 covers the scaling-law machinery behind this arithmetic). The 25-50T figure is consistent with aggressive overtraining on active parameters, multi-epoch repetition of a smaller high-quality core, and/or heavy synthetic augmentation — all three of which the Epoch framework treats as stock-multipliers, not stock-consumers. At 25-50T unique-equivalent tokens, a 10T-parameter MoE would consume roughly 8-17% of Epoch’s central 300T-token estimate in a single run — plausible only if multi-epoch counting is used generously (true unique tokens closer to 10-20T, repeated 2-3x) and/or synthetic/rephrased data supplies a meaningful fraction of the nominal count. The instruction that follows: treat “25-50T tokens” as requiring an explicit synthetic/multi-epoch accounting from day one, not as 25-50T tokens of freshly-crawled unique human text — that volume of unique, quality-filtered fresh crawl very likely does not exist.

The corpus-scale evidence assembled elsewhere in this research base supports that conclusion directly. No single open web-crawl-derived corpus tops out above the low-teens of trillions of tokens even at maximal scale — Essential-Web v1.0 reaches 24T tokens across 23.6B documents from Common Crawl [PAPER, arXiv:2506.14111, 06/2025], and DCLM’s own raw pool of 240T tokens filters down to 3.8T retained (2.6T used for the headline run) [PAPER, arXiv:2406.11794, 06/2024] — a filtering ratio of roughly 1-1.6% retained, the single most concrete illustration of how far raw crawl volume overstates usable stock (Ch11 covers the filtering-pipeline mechanics behind that ratio). Nemotron-CC’s full pipeline, including 2T tokens of synthetic augmentation, reaches 6.3T [OFFICIAL-BLOG+PAPER, arXiv:2412.02595, 12/2024], and its v2 successor reaches 6,585.8B tokens with 879.1B + 1,629.1B tokens of additional synthetic rephrase [OFFICIAL/HF, 2025]. Frontier runs are already consuming a meaningful fraction of what’s publicly documented to exist: Qwen3 at 36T tokens across three curriculum stages [PAPER, arXiv:2505.09388, 05/2025], Kimi K2 at 15.5T [PAPER, arXiv:2507.20534, 07/2025], DeepSeek-V3 at 14.8T [PAPER, arXiv:2412.19437, 12/2024]. Multi-epoch repetition is consequently not a fallback for a 25-50T-token run but a structural necessity: the load-bearing scaling law remains Muennighoff et al.’s finding that up to roughly 4 epochs causes negligible degradation, meaningful gains persist to roughly 16 epochs, and value is largely exhausted by roughly 40 epochs [PAPER, “Scaling Data-Constrained Language Models,” 2023, cited widely through 2025-26]. A 2026 refinement extends the domain-mixture scaling-law framework to model per-domain repetition as a first-class variable once any single mixture component becomes data-limited, rather than a single global scalar [PAPER, arXiv:2605.12715]. Kimi K2’s own ablation is the sharpest single data point on the alternative to brute-force repetition: 10x knowledge-domain rephrasing lifts SimpleQA from 23.8% to 28.9%, beating naive 10-epoch repetition on the same underlying text [PAPER, arXiv:2507.20534, 07/2025] — synthetic rephrasing is now a demonstrated superior alternative to repetition for knowledge-dense subsets, not merely a hedge against running out of unique tokens.

DatologyAI’s BeyondWeb result quantifies the same substitution from the compute-efficiency side: a rephrased-web recipe beats Cosmopedia by up to 5.1pp and Nemotron-Synth by 2.6pp average accuracy across 14 benchmarks, with a 3B model on the recipe beating an 8B baseline at equal token budget, and 7.7x training speedup against open web data [OFFICIAL-BLOG, datologyai.com/blog/beyondweb, 08/2025]. As a data-efficiency multiplier rather than a parameter-count multiplier: for a fixed token budget, a synthetic-rephrasing pipeline can substitute for roughly 2.5x more parameters — a lever a 10T/200B design should weight explicitly against simply adding more raw, unrephrased crawl tokens, bearing directly on the ladder-recipe cost accounting in Ch6.

Two additional stock-side data points sit genuinely outside standard web-crawl accounting. FinePDFs (HuggingFace, September 2025) demonstrates PDFs as a systematically denser source than HTML web text — 475M documents, 1,733 languages, 3T tokens total (1.1T English alone), median document length roughly 5.3k characters, about 2x typical web corpora, with a much heavier tail (95th percentile ~68k characters versus 11-13k for HTML-derived corpora) [PAPER/OFFICIAL-BLOG, huggingfacefw-finepdfsblog.hf.space, 09/2025]. A 1.67B-parameter ablation shows a FinePDFs/FineWeb-Edu/DCLM blend at just a 0.25 mixing ratio beating Nemotron-CC-v2, the prior public-SOTA baseline [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 09/2025]. CCI4.0 (BAAI, June 2025) illustrates a different kind of stock-stretching: a 35T-token bilingual corpus that reuses Nemotron-CC’s already-curated 22.5TB English subset wholesale and adds a from-scratch 5.2TB Chinese pipeline, rather than re-deriving an independent English pipeline [PAPER, arXiv:2506.07463, 06/2025] — a reminder that the effective global stock is not additive across labs’ independent crawls the way a naive sum would suggest, since major curated English pools are increasingly reused rather than re-crawled.

Building a greenfield crawler in 2026: the technical spec

Extraction quality is now a measured, first-order lever on effective stock, not commoditized the way most practitioners assume. DCLM’s own ablation found resiliparse and trafilatura both beat raw WET-file extraction by at least 2.5 CORE points, similar to each other in downstream quality but resiliparse roughly 8x faster — why DCLM standardized on it for cost, not quality, reasons [PAPER, arXiv:2406.11794, NeurIPS 2024 D&B track, 06/2024]. More surprising: a February 2026 Apple/Stanford/UW paper found that at a fixed post-curation filtering pipeline, resiliparse, trafilatura, and jusText each keep substantially different pages — only 39% of surviving pages are kept by more than one extractor, 61% survive via exactly one [PAPER, arXiv:2602.19548, 02/2026]. At 7B-model/1x-epoch scale from the identical underlying WARC pool: resiliparse yields 193B tokens (Core_V2 42.6, MMLU 39.5), trafilatura 135B tokens (41.9, 37.7), jusText 124B tokens (41.2, 34.5) — a greater than 50% token-yield spread between extractors on identical input [PAPER, 02/2026]. Taking the union of multiple extractors’ output increases DCLM-Baseline token yield by up to 71%, falling to roughly 58% after cross-extractor deduplication, at equal benchmark performance [PAPER, 02/2026] — running two or three extractors in parallel and deduping the union is a close-to-free yield increase essentially no major open pipeline appears to be doing as of publication. A November 2025 Shanghai AI Lab/OpenDataLab paper (AICC) goes further, replacing heuristic extraction with a 0.6B-parameter sequence-labeling model (MinerU-HTML) that lifts ROUGE-F1 content-match from trafilatura’s 63.6% to 81.8%, with far better structured-content preservation (code blocks 90.9%, formulas 94.0%, categories heuristic extractors are known to mangle) [PAPER, arXiv:2511.16397, 11/2025], producing a 7.3T-token corpus from just two CC snapshots and a measured +1.08pp average benchmark gain over an identically-processed trafilatura baseline [PAPER, 11/2025]. Whether any frontier lab already runs a proprietary model-based extractor is undisclosed by all of them, but given the magnitude of these gaps it would be surprising if none has — a genuine open question for anyone benchmarking against published open-corpus numbers.

JS-rendering economics are the second design axis, and they are memory-bound rather than compute-bound: the reported rule of thumb is roughly 250MB RAM per headless-Chrome browser context, so 1,000 concurrent rendered pages costs roughly 250GB RAM, and practitioners converge on Chromium’s multi-context-per-process model plus horizontal scale-out across many small nodes as both faster and cheaper than fewer large machines [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, practitioner-blog consensus, 06/2026]. Webrecorder’s Browsertrix (open-source, Puppeteer/CDP-driven, Docker-packaged, billing by browser-window-time) is the closest thing to a standard reference implementation for high-fidelity JS-rendering crawl-to-WARC pipelines outside the major labs [CODE/OFFICIAL, github.com/webrecorder/browsertrix-crawler, 07/2026]. This matters structurally because Common Crawl itself does not render JS at all — CCBot is Nutch-based (Hadoop/MapReduce scheduling, WARC output, adaptive rate-limiting) and fetches raw HTTP responses only [OFFICIAL, commoncrawl.org engineering documentation] — systematically under-representing JS-heavy modern sites (SPAs, paywalled news, app-shell architectures) relative to a lab running its own headless-render fleet, a gap arguably as important a reason frontier labs run proprietary crawlers as the robots.txt-blocking gap above.

Politeness and scheduling round out the spec. Common Crawl’s architecture (Hadoop/Bigtop on spot EC2, historically roughly 20 nodes at 2 cores/32GB RAM for the fetch stage) makes strict per-host fetch-delay queuing, not compute, the actual throughput bottleneck [OFFICIAL, commoncrawl.org engineering writeups]. A greenfield 2026 crawler needs the same primitives, plus one CC lacks: per-host adaptive rate limiting respecting Retry-After/429 and empirically inferring safe QPS; a separately-scheduled freshness/recrawl policy — Cloudflare’s >50% unchanged-page-refetch finding indicts the industry’s recrawl-scheduling maturity, implying a change-detection/etag-aware scheduler is not yet standard even at major labs [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 07/2026]; crawl-time dedup via URL-canonicalization plus content-hash/SimHash ahead of the offline pass in Ch11; and a content-signals parser for Cloudflare’s search/ai-input/ai-train extension alongside classic robots.txt, unenforced as it remains. Brave Search’s independently-built index and Mojeek’s smaller but fully independent crawler are useful existence-proofs that non-Google-scale independent web indexes remain viable in 2026, though neither publishes a page-count metric comparable to Common Crawl’s.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

The data-supply chain analysis compresses to a small number of hard planning constraints:

  • Do not budget on Common Crawl staying a free, comprehensive commons. CCBot’s 5.08% DISALLOW-rule share (nearly matching GPTBot’s 5.52%) and the September 15, 2026 Cloudflare default-block change mean the effective quality-adjusted CC yield will keep shrinking through the run’s crawl-planning window; treat any CC-derived corpus size as a ceiling that degrades monthly, not a fixed asset.
  • Budget an explicit multi-epoch and synthetic-augmentation accounting for the token target, not a unique-token count. A 25-50T nominal token budget against Epoch’s ~300T central stock estimate is only defensible if the true unique-token core is closer to 10-20T, repeated 2-3x and/or extended via rephrasing on the Kimi K2/BeyondWeb model — plan the data pipeline around this from the start rather than discovering the shortfall mid-run.
  • Treat book acquisition as a build-vs-license decision with a known cost structure on both sides. The Anthropic Project Panama template (purchase, in-house destructive digitize, no retention of pirated material) is legally clean but requires standing up an industrial physical-logistics operation; Harvard’s Institutional Books 1.0 offers 242B tokens of comparable historical-register text but needs a separate commercial-licensing conversation before use.
  • Do not treat licensing deals as a data-volume lever. Aggregate industry licensing spend is a rounding error against the ~$1B/year each frontier lab separately spends on human-provided data via Scale/Surge/Mercor; size the licensing budget for legal risk mitigation and product integration, not token count.
  • Run 2-3 text extractors in parallel and dedup the union. The measured 50-71% token-yield spread between resiliparse, trafilatura, and jusText on identical WARC input is close to free to capture and is not standard practice even at major labs — one of the few remaining near-costless yield increases available before touching filtering strategy proper (Ch11).
  • Design the crawler’s freshness scheduler before its throughput. Cloudflare’s >50% unchanged-page-refetch finding indicates raw crawl QPS is not the binding constraint most fleets face; a change-detection/etag-aware recrawl policy is higher-leverage than additional crawl capacity.
  • Treat YouTube/audio transcription as available but legally live capacity, appropriate for the multimodal-native design discussed in Ch9, and size any reliance on it against the same ToS/consent exposure pattern that is currently reshaping web crawling itself.

Chapter 11 · Part III — The Data

Curation SOTA and What Lies Beyond Essential-Web

Ch10 priced the token stock and the legal terrain for acquiring it. This chapter asks the narrower, more technical question: once the raw tokens are on disk, what is the state of the art for turning them into a curated corpus, and where — precisely — does the published literature say the remaining headroom lives? The answer as of July 2026 has a settled center of gravity (taxonomy-annotate everything, filter with SQL) and a genuinely unsettled frontier (reject-pile recovery, dynamic influence selection, per-language bespoke curation) that a 10T-parameter build should be positioned to exploit rather than rediscover.

Essential-Web v1.0, dissected

Essential-Web v1.0 is the reference architecture for 2025-26 curation: 24 trillion tokens across 23.6 billion documents, built from Common Crawl, with every document tagged by a 12-field taxonomy rather than a single quality scalar Essential-Web v1.0, arXiv:2506.14111 [PAPER, Jun 2025]. The taxonomy groups into five families, documented in the project’s own repository Essential-AI/eai-taxonomy [CODE, checked Jul 2026]: a Free Decimal Correspondence field (a public-domain Dewey-Decimal analogue, three hierarchy levels); Bloom’s Knowledge Domain and Bloom’s Cognitive Process (the 2001 Anderson/Krathwohl revision of Bloom’s Taxonomy, applied literally to web documents, both primary+secondary); Document Type v1 (17 categories) and Document Type v2 (25 refined categories, built directly on WebOrganizer’s format taxonomy — see below); Extraction Artifacts and Missing Content flags; Reasoning Depth, Technical Correctness, and Education Level scales; 30+ RedPajama-v2-style continuous quality metrics; and six fastText classification scores (DCLM, English, Educational, Math, Web Code). The combinatorics are the point: 14.1 million unique primary-label combinations, 1.2 billion including secondary labels — every document sits at a coordinate in a 12-dimensional space that downstream users slice with SQL rather than retraining a classifier per use case arXiv:2506.14111, via HF summary [PAPER, Jun 2025].

The labels are produced cheaply: EAI-Distill-0.5b distills a Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct annotator down to 0.5B parameters, agreement reported within 3% of the 32B teacher at roughly 50x the inference speed, with infrastructure optimization (compression, prompt trimming, output condensation) raising throughput from 1.4 to 70 requests/second per GPU. Labeling the full corpus cost ~90,000 AMD MI300X GPU-hours arXiv:2506.14111 [PAPER, Jun 2025] — at a wholesale rate of roughly $2-2.50/GPU-hour (an estimate from the research notes, not a disclosed Essential-AI or AMD figure), that implies roughly $180k-225k, about $0.0000075-0.00001 per token, excluding upstream crawl/extract/dedup [data-processing-infra note, rate estimate] [Derived, 2025-26]. The domain-subset wins are the paper’s real claim to fame — simple SQL filters, zero domain-specific classifier training:

A single generic taxonomy matches or beats three of four bespoke domain pipelines and comes within single digits on the fourth (math) arXiv:2506.14111 [PAPER, Jun 2025]. That is the strongest published evidence that “annotate once, filter many ways” beats building N domain-specific classifiers — and it is the reason Essential-Web functions as this chapter’s reference point rather than merely one entry in the dataset table below.

The lineage question: WebOrganizer as the direct comparison

The instinct to compare Essential-Web against WebOrganizer resolves as lineage, not rivalry. WebOrganizer arXiv:2502.10341 [PAPER, Feb 2025], from Princeton/Ai2/UC Berkeley/UW, predates Essential-Web by four months and defines two complementary, near-independent 24-category taxonomies — Topic (subject matter) and Format (venue/style) — with normalized mutual information ≈0.10 between them, empirically confirming the two axes are close to orthogonal. Its 140M-parameter classifiers (built on gte-base-en-v1.5, two-stage distillation from Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct then Llama-3.1-405B-Instruct) drive a RegMix-style mixture search across 512 small (50M-param, 1B-token) proxy models; combined Topic×Format mixing improves average downstream accuracy from 51.6% to 54.6%, and layered on a FineWeb-Edu quality filter, mixing alone recovers 84% of FineWeb-Edu’s own gain without any quality classifier at all arXiv:2502.10341 [PAPER, Feb 2025]. Essential-Web’s own Document Type v2 (25 categories) is explicitly built on WebOrganizer’s format taxonomy — so “Essential-Web vs. WebOrganizer” is really “incorporates a component, but does not adopt the second axis (Topic) or the mixture-weighting methodology.” This lineage claim is the research note’s own cross-reference of Essential-Web’s taxonomy documentation against WebOrganizer’s paper — neither primary paper states the relationship in these terms — so treat it as a well-grounded inference rather than a directly-sourced fact [data-processing-infra note, cross-referenced against arXiv:2506.14111 taxonomy docs] [DERIVED/SYNTHESIS, 2025-26]. That leaves an open combination for a 10T-scale build: SQL-filter subsetting and training-time mixture weighting are complementary techniques nothing in the published literature has combined in one pipeline.

The open-dataset frontier, mapped

Essential-Web sits inside a crowded 2024-26 field of crawl-to-corpus pipelines, each making a different bet on filtering methodology, dedup granularity, or source mix:

Three of these deserve a beat of comment beyond the table. FinePDFs is the sharpest 2025-26 evidence that document source format, not just filtering quality, is a first-order lever: PDF-derived documents run roughly 2x the median character length of HTML-derived web text and a much heavier tail (95th percentile ~68k characters vs. ~11-13k), and a 1.67B-parameter/36B-token ablation shows a FinePDFs/FineWeb-Edu/DCLM blend at just a 0.25 mixing ratio beating the current public-SOTA baseline, Nemotron-CC-v2 huggingfacefw-finepdfsblog.hf.space [OFFICIAL-BLOG, Sept 2025]. Common Pile v0.1 has a real, mechanistically-explained quality gap rather than a generic one: at 7B/1T tokens Comma beats budget-matched Pile/OSCAR/FineWeb baselines on knowledge and code tasks but is specifically worse on HellaSwag, PIQA, and CommonSenseQA — the paper’s own diagnosis is that openly-licensed sources systematically under-represent personal blogs, tutorials, hobby content, and sports writing, exactly the register those benchmarks draw from, and the gap persists as a structural signature of the source mix rather than closing with scale arXiv:2506.05209 [PAPER, Jun 2025]. Institutional Books 1.0 is architecturally unlike everything else in the table — pre-2006 institutional library-scan OCR rather than crawl-derived text — but its non-commercial initial license, driven by unresolved uncertainty around non-Harvard re-digitizations of the same public-domain works, means a commercial 10T-parameter run cannot ingest it without a separate licensing conversation arXiv:2506.08300 [PAPER, Jun 2025].

One upstream lever sits underneath every entry in this table and is easy to under-weight: text extraction itself is not a solved commodity. Feb 2026 work found HTML extractors (resiliparse/trafilatura/jusText) keep substantially different pages from the same WARC pool — only 39% of surviving pages are retained by more than one extractor — and taking the union increases DCLM-Baseline token yield by up to 71% at fixed benchmark quality; a Nov 2025 Shanghai AI Lab paper (AICC) replaces heuristic extraction with a 0.6B sequence-labeling model and lifts ROUGE-F1 content-match from trafilatura’s 63.6% to 81.8% [arXiv:2602.19548 + arXiv:2511.16397, per crawling-supply notes] [PAPER, Feb/Nov 2025-26]. This is Ch10’s territory in full, but it bears directly on curation SOTA: every classifier and dedup number in this chapter is computed downstream of an extraction step that the 2025-26 literature has only just started treating as its own model-training problem rather than a regex.

Technique deltas: three generations of selection

The field has moved through three generations of data-selection methodology in roughly eighteen months. Generation one is static, single-axis classifiers — DCLM’s fastText scorer, FineWeb-Edu’s Llama-3-70B-derived regression head. Generation two is static, multi-axis taxonomies — Essential-Web, GneissWeb, WebOrganizer, Meta-rater. Generation three is dynamic, model-state-aware, group-level influence selection, and it is the one with genuine unexploited headroom for a 10T-scale build, because nobody has published it validated past 400M-7B-parameter proxy scale.

Ensembles reliably beat single-axis classifiers where the comparison has been run directly: GneissWeb’s ensemble (not single-annotator) quality filters beat FineWeb 1.1.0 by +2.73pp averaged over 11 benchmarks at 7B/350B tokens arXiv:2502.14907 [PAPER, Feb 2025], and a GneissWeb-adjacent survey source found only 368 of the top 1,000 domains overlap between the DCLM fastText classifier and the FineWeb-Edu classifier — the two widely-used single-axis scorers agree on fewer than 37% of their own top-ranked domains, meaning a single classifier’s “top tier” is closer to one opinion than a ground truth [data-curation note, GneissWeb-adjacent survey source, 2025]. Meta-rater arXiv:2504.14194 [PAPER, ACL 2025 Best Theme Paper] replaces a single quality scalar with four learned dimensions — professionalism, readability, reasoning, cleanliness — combined via a regression model trained to predict validation loss from proxy-model runs, doubling convergence speed at 1.3B and delivering +3.23% downstream, with gains holding to 7.2B. Perplexity-correlation methods sidestep training a new classifier entirely: correlating ~90 existing open models’ per-domain perplexity against downstream benchmark performance beats DSIR on all 8 tested benchmarks and matches DCLM’s hand-tuned bigram classifier at 160M scale, with the advantage growing with scale in a 2025 preregistered follow-up to 1.4B arXiv:2409.05816 [PAPER, ICLR 2025]; PreSelect arXiv:2503.00808 [PAPER, ICML 2025] packages the same compression-implies-transfer intuition into a lightweight fastText scorer.

Generation three replaces the static score with a signal that updates as the model trains. MATES arXiv:2406.06046 [PAPER, NeurIPS 2024] trains a small influence model that continuously re-fits to the pretraining model’s current preferences, halving FLOPs-to-target and doubling the prior static-reference-model SOTA’s gains at 410M/1B scale on C4. Group-MATES arXiv:2502.14709 [PAPER, Feb 2025] extends this to relational, group-level influence — individual-document scoring cannot capture documents cancelling or amplifying each other’s marginal contribution — reporting +3.5-9.4% relative gains over random selection across 22 tasks. TiKMiX arXiv:2508.17677 [PAPER, Aug 2025] reformulates mixture selection as a Group Influence metric re-solved as training progresses rather than fixed at the start (unlike DoReMi/RegMix); TiKMiX-D matches RegMix at 20% of its compute, and regression variant TiKMiX-M delivers +2% average across 9 benchmarks. All of these are proxy-model experiments at 400M-7B parameters — the load-bearing caveat is that dynamic influence selection has never been validated anywhere near frontier scale, let alone the scale of the actual training run.

Reject-pile recovery and agentic pipeline search

The most structurally interesting 2026 finding is that taxonomy annotation is not the ceiling because it makes an irreversible per-document keep/discard decision, and two independent 2026 papers show the discard pile is recoverable signal rather than noise. REWIRE (“Recycling the Web,” Meta FAIR/UW) takes documents a standard pipeline would discard — filtering typically removes up to ~99% of raw crawl — and guided-rewrites them into usable text instead of dropping them; 82% of REWIRE’s final synthetic mix comes from transforming lower-quality documents, and the recycled mix outperforms simply crawling 2x as much raw web data, with gains of +1.0/+1.3/+2.5pp at 1B/3B/7B across 22 tasks arXiv:2506.04689 [PAPER, COLM 2025, Jun 2025]. A June 2026 paper builds directly on Essential-Web’s taxonomy to make the same point more explicitly: documents scored low often fail only on extraction-artifact or missing-content flags rather than genuinely poor content, and a targeted recovery pass pulls performant training examples back out of the reject pile arXiv:2606.07778 [PAPER, Jun 2026]. Both papers converge on the same cheap, unglamorous insight: don’t throw away your low scores without asking why they’re low.

The most literal “data agents” answer in the corpus is DataEvolve (GAIR-NLP), a closed-loop agentic system that searches over curation strategies rather than document scores — a data observer (GPT-4o-mini) inspects sample documents per category, a strategy designer (o4-mini) proposes cleaning/rewriting prompts informed by an experience pool from prior generations, a data cleaner (gpt-oss-120b) executes the strategy, and a quality judge (gpt-5-mini) scores before/after pairs and seeds the next generation. Thirty generations across 8 academic content categories produced Darwin-CC: 504B tokens, a 25% cut from 672B raw input. At 3B/500B tokens, Darwin-CC beats DCLM by +1.71pp, FineWeb-Edu by +7.61pp, and Ultra-FineWeb by +7.84pp on average score, with knowledge-intensive tasks seeing the largest jumps (MMLU +18.64pp, MedQA +13.48pp) versus raw unfiltered data; a suboptimal-strategy ablation shows a 2.93pp gap versus the fully-evolved strategy set, meaning the evolutionary search itself — not just “use an LLM to clean data” — accounts for roughly 40% of the total gain arXiv:2603.14420 [PAPER, Mar 2026]. No total compute-cost figure for the 240-run search is disclosed in the DataEvolve paper, which is the real limitation for budgeting this at Essential-Web’s 24T-token scale rather than DataEvolve’s own 672B-token, 8-category scope arXiv:2603.14420, absence noted [PAPER, Mar 2026].

Deduplication: commodity today, unproven past 10T

Deduplication at trillion-token scale has moved from research problem to infrastructure line item. SemDeDup, still the reference method, removes ~50% of a dataset via embedding-based semantic similarity at near-zero downstream loss, with the dedup pass itself costing only ~1% of total training compute arXiv:2303.09540 [PAPER, 2023]. NVIDIA’s NeMo Curator, rewritten from Dask/RAPIDS to a Ray-based backend spanning text/image/video/audio in the Feb 2026 26.02 release docs.nvidia.com/nemo/curator/26.02 [CODE/OFFICIAL-DOCS, Feb 2026], deduplicates 1.96T tokens (RedPajama-V2 scale) in 30 minutes on 32 H100s, and 10T tokens in 4-8 hours on a single 8xH100 node — the two numbers are consistent with the all-to-all LSH bucket-collision shuffle, not raw signature computation, being the actual bottleneck. Zyda-2’s build specifically credits NeMo Curator with cutting build time from 3 weeks to 2 days and total cost of ownership 2x arXiv:2411.06068 [PAPER+OFFICIAL-BLOG, Nov 2024].

Different dedup architectures catch structurally different duplication, a nuance most 2024-era pipelines treat as interchangeable but that is not: MinHash-LSH catches whole-document near-duplication; suffix-array exact-substring dedup catches repeated boilerplate and headers within otherwise-unique documents that document-level Jaccard similarity misses — the BigScience ROOTS corpus found 21.67% of bytes duplicated at the substring level, well above what MinHash typically flags arXiv:2303.03915 [PAPER, 2023]. Olmo 3’s Dolma 3 pipeline is the most recent frontier-adjacent open pipeline to explicitly re-adopt suffix-array dedup at production scale — a 56-way sharded fuzzy suffix-array pass (duplodocus) layered on top of, not instead of, its fuzzy MinHash-style methods arXiv:2512.13961 [PAPER, Nov/Dec 2025]; Dolma’s original three-stage cascade (exact URL dedup removing ~53.2% of documents, exact document dedup a further ~14.9%, paragraph-level Bloom-filter dedup ~18.7-19.1%) is a coarse-to-fine architecture distinct from both MinHash-only and suffix-array approaches arXiv:2402.00159 [PAPER, 2024]. For a 10T-MoE build the practical read is: dedup infrastructure is solved through roughly 10T tokens on today’s GPU-native tooling, and the two dedup families (fuzzy near-duplicate, exact substring) should be run as separate, complementary stages rather than one being treated as a substitute for the other.

Synthetic rephrasing at trillion-token scale

Synthetic rephrasing is now table stakes, not a research curiosity. Nemotron-CC generates 2T tokens of synthetic diverse-QA/distill/extract-knowledge/knowledge-list text via 4 LLMs; Nemotron-CC-v2 adds 879.1B tokens (Qwen3-30B-A3B) plus 1,629.1B tokens (Mistral-Nemo-12B) more. Kimi K2 uses 10x knowledge-domain rephrasing to lift SimpleQA from 23.8% (10-epoch literal repetition) to 28.9% (10x rephrase, no repeat), plus SwallowMath-style step-by-step math rewriting arXiv:2507.20534 [PAPER, Jul 2025] — a clean, directly actionable number for anyone planning multi-epoch strategy on a >20T-token budget: rephrase scarce high-value data rather than repeating it. OLMo 2’s own microannealing ablations corroborate the mechanism at a finer grain: TinyGSM in raw code-answer form actually hurts (GSM*=25.0, below the 28.5 pre-anneal baseline) while the MIND-rewritten natural-language version scores 65.5, and doubling that rewritten data reaches 70.0 — rewriting code-formatted synthetic math into natural language is worth roughly +40 points on its own held-out metric [OLMo 2, arXiv:2501.00656, Table 12, cross-referenced via midtraining-curriculum note] [PAPER, 2024-25] (see Ch12 for the full midtraining-stage treatment).

BeyondWeb (DatologyAI) is the most-cited commercial synthetic-data result and deserves the scrutiny its baseline choice invites. DatologyAI’s original blog claim — 86.9% compute savings / 7.7x speedup, a “10x compute-equivalent” framing — is benchmarked against RedPajama v1, a 2023-era dataset already obsolete by FineWeb-Edu/DCLM standards when the claim was published datologyai.com/blog [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2024-25]. BeyondWeb itself uses harder baselines: +5.1pp vs. Cosmopedia, +2.6pp vs. Nemotron-Synth across 14 benchmarks, 7.7x/2.7x speedups respectively, and a 3B model on 180B BeyondWeb tokens outperforms an 8B model on the same token budget of Cosmopedia arXiv:2508.10975 [PAPER, Aug 2025]. The paper’s own explicit negative result is worth carrying forward: larger generator models do not necessarily produce better synthetic pretraining data than ~8B-parameter generators — corroborated independently by “Demystifying Synthetic Data in LLM Pre-training,” which finds mixing ~30% rephrased-synthetic with 70% natural web gives a 5-10x speedup to matched validation loss with no degradation at studied scales, that purely textbook-style heavily-abstracted synthetic (Cosmopedia-like) does show collapse-like patterns at high concentration, and that this single-round rephrasing regime is distinct from the iterative recursive self-training regime where “Strong Model Collapse” documents real degradation within ~5 generations arXiv:2510.01631 [PAPER, Oct 2025]; arXiv:2410.04840 [PAPER, Oct 2024]. ProX arXiv:2409.17115 [PAPER, ICML 2025] is a distinct, cheaper, more auditable alternative to free-text rewriting: a 0.3-1.7B model generates and executes fine-grained programmatic cleaning operations per document rather than rewriting prose, beating both raw and selection-only-curated data by >2% across 10 benchmarks, with domain-specific standout results on OpenWebMath refinement of +7.6% (Mistral-7B), +14.6% (Llama-2-7B), and +20.3% (CodeLlama-7B) over human-crafted rule-based cleaning.

Not every frontier lab agrees synthetic rephrasing belongs in the base model at all. Microsoft’s MAI-Thinking-1 report (June 2026) explicitly avoided all synthetic/AI-generated data in its 30T-token base pretraining run (MAI-Base-1, on 8,000 GB200s), with active filtering to strip AI-generated text from the crawl [microsoft.ai, MAI-Thinking-1 report, cross-referenced via midtraining-curriculum note] [OFFICIAL-BLOG, Jun 2026] — a live, unresolved strategic disagreement between “synthetic data is essential for data-efficiency” (Ai2, DatologyAI, Nemotron) and “avoid synthetic/AI-contaminated data at all costs in the base model” (Microsoft MAI), from a lab training at genuinely frontier scale. NVIDIA’s Nemotron Nano 2 disclosed practice sits closer to the pro-synthetic camp but at the midtraining stage specifically: taking a 14.5T-token checkpoint, continuing pretraining for 100B more tokens, and replacing 5% of those 100B tokens with synthetic “Fundamental Reasoning” SFT-style data (in place of Common Crawl) lifted MMLU-Pro from 44.24 to 56.36 (+12.1pp) and average Math from 37.92 to 39.70 [arXiv:2508.14444, cross-referenced via midtraining-curriculum note] [PAPER, Aug-Sep 2025] — a midtraining-stage result properly owned by Ch12, but directly relevant here as evidence that even a small synthetic-data injection late in training moves benchmarks a base-pretraining-stage rephrase does not obviously match.

Domain pipelines: code and math

Code and math curation have converged on the same “rules vs. model” tension visible in the general-web literature. The Stack v2 (~900B tokens, 4x v1, repo-grouped) was found insufficiently high-quality for top-tier code LLMs by OpenCoder’s own team, motivating RefineCode: 960B raw tokens across 607 languages, 130+ language-specific filter rules, two-stage dedup (file-level SHA256+LSH, then repo-level), downsampled to ~730B final tokens arXiv:2411.04905 [PAPER, Nov 2024]. Seed-Coder (ByteDance Seed) inverts the approach entirely: rather than hand-crafted rules, LLMs score and filter code data end-to-end, producing a 6T-token dataset with dramatically less human rule-engineering effort at a comparable or better quality bar per ByteDance’s own benchmarks arXiv:2506.03524 [PAPER, Jun 2025] — the same rules-vs.-model-judgment split visible in Essential-Web (taxonomy rules) vs. DataEvolve (agentic judgment) above, replayed in the code domain specifically.

Math data has a documented pipeline-brittleness problem: OpenWebMath’s Resiliparse-based HTML rendering is prone to stripping or corrupting formulas and code, a weakness FineMath (54B tokens, 5.5x more tokens than prior comparable sets) inherits by building on the same pipeline [data-curation note, SURVEY-SUMMARY + OFFICIAL-BLOG, Dec 2024]. MegaMath (371.6B tokens: 279B web + 28.1B code + 64.5B synthetic) is the largest fully-open math corpus at release, and its own build reused FineMath’s classifier as an input before further LLM-based refinement arXiv:2504.02807 [PAPER, COLM 2025] — classifiers becoming reusable, composable components across dataset projects rather than one-off artifacts is a recurring 2025-26 pattern (visible also in Ultra-FineWeb and ProX). Nemotron-CC-Math is titled as a 133B-token dataset built via a Lynx+LLM pipeline explicitly claimed to surpass MegaMath, FineMath, and OpenWebMath arXiv:2508.15096 [PAPER, Aug 2025] — some secondary summaries cite a 52B post-filter figure against the paper’s 133B title figure; treat 133B as the headline pending direct primary-source confirmation.

Published frontier mixes, tabulated

No frontier from-scratch MoE report (DeepSeek-V3, Kimi K2, Qwen3-MoE, Hunyuan-Large, Nemotron-H) claims a data mixture that differs in kind from what a compute-matched dense model would use — every disclosed mixture looks like a standard general/math/code/multilingual split regardless of architecture [data-curation note §10]. The clearest MoE-data interaction documented anywhere in the corpus is in upcycling, not from-scratch pretraining: BTS trains dense experts on specialized 200B-token continued-pretraining mixtures (a code expert at >85% code tokens, a math expert on OpenWebMath) before merging into a generalist MoE arXiv:2502.00075 [PAPER, Feb 2025] — architecturally a sparse-upcycling setup, not evidence about optimal from-scratch MoE data composition. The honest position for a 10T/200B build: no controlled, compute-matched dense-vs-MoE mixture ablation exists in the published literature, so the strongest prior is that the same curation pipeline that works for a compute-matched dense model works for the MoE, flagged explicitly as an unfilled research gap rather than a settled result either way.

Multilingual: a distinct curation axis, not a mixing afterthought

ÜberWeb is the newest and most actionable multilingual result for a 10T-scale build: across 13 languages, most “curse of multilinguality” regressions are not inherent to scaling multiple languages together but are correctable data-quality/composition deficiencies — curating English data improves non-English performance in 12 of 13 tested languages, and bespoke per-language curation (rather than one uniform multilingual pipeline) lets a 3B/8B model match uniform-mixture baselines at 4-10x lower compute on a 20T-token corpus arXiv:2602.15210 [PAPER, Feb 2026]. This is a harder-baseline, more actionable descendant of the same DatologyAI methodology behind BeyondWeb, and it inverts the intuitive assumption that multilingual curation requires uniform per-language effort throughout. FineWeb2 (20TB/5B docs, per-language adaptive pipeline, beats prior multilingual sets on 11/14 languages) and HPLT v2/v3 (8T tokens/193 languages confirmed for v2, growing to a secondary-sourced ~30T tokens for v3 — the v3 figure was not independently confirmed against a primary HPLT paper or project page, treat as CREDIBLE-HEARSAY pending direct confirmation — CC0-licensed, more permissive than FineWeb2’s ODC-By) are largely-independent, differently-sourced pipelines (CC-focused vs. CC+Internet-Archive) that a 10T-run’s multilingual mixture should plausibly draw from both rather than treating either as the sole reference arXiv:2506.20920 [PAPER, Jun 2025]; arXiv:2503.10267 [PAPER, Mar 2025, v2 figure]; [data-processing-infra note, v3 figure] [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 2025-26]. CCI4.0 is the clearest evidence that Chinese-language curation and English-language curation are increasingly not independent efforts even outside DatologyAI’s framework: its 22.5TB English subset is drawn directly from Nemotron-CC rather than built from scratch, with a from-scratch 5.2TB Chinese pipeline layered on top and 4.5B extracted chain-of-thought “thinking templates” targeting reasoning benchmarks specifically arXiv:2506.07463 [PAPER, Jun 2025].

The labeling-FLOP economics: is classifying 100T tokens affordable

The question a 10T-MoE build actually needs answered is whether taxonomy-labeling a 100T-token raw pool is a real budget line item or a rounding error. Computed directly using the standard inference-FLOPs convention (≈2N FLOPs/token) for a 0.5B labeler over 100T tokens gives 1×10²³ FLOPs; training-side FLOPs for a 200B-active-parameter MoE at ≈6·N_active·D come to 2.4×10²⁵ FLOPs at D=20T tokens and 6×10²⁵ FLOPs at D=50T tokens — a labeling-to-training ratio of 0.42% at 20T tokens and 0.17% at 50T tokens [data-processing-infra note §4d, derived] [Derived, standard 2N-inference/6N-training FLOP conventions, 2026]. Two independent cross-checks land in the same range: Essential-Web’s own disclosed 90,000 MI300X-GPU-hours to label 24T tokens arXiv:2506.14111 [PAPER, Jun 2025] scales linearly to ~375,000 GPU-hours for 100T tokens — at the same ~$2-2.50/GPU-hour wholesale-rate estimate used above (again an estimate within the research notes, not a disclosed AMD/Essential-AI figure), roughly $750k-$938k, negligible against a 100,000-GPU cluster’s own hourly burn rate [data-processing-infra note §4d, scaled + rate estimate] [Derived, 2025-26]; and a separate academic filtering-pipeline study independently reports classifier-filtering FLOP overhead at under 1% of training compute [data-processing-infra note, secondary academic source cross-referenced] [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 2024-25].

The practical verdict: “can we afford to label everything” is not a real planning question for a 10T-scale build — the answer is trivially yes, and the same math shows a 30B-parameter labeler over 100T tokens still costs only ~10-25% of training FLOPs, still affordable, just no longer a rounding error. The actual bottleneck, per Essential-Web’s own disclosed experience, is taxonomy design and distillation-quality validation — engineering and judgment time, not GPU-hours [data-processing-infra note §4d] [Derived, 2026]. A 10T-scale program has genuine headroom to spend a larger labeler (7B-32B rather than 0.5B) if taxonomy quality, not compute, turns out to be the limiting factor.

Where the real headroom is: three directions past taxonomy annotation

Taxonomy annotation (Essential-Web, GneissWeb, WebOrganizer, Meta-rater) is a solved, table-stakes layer for a 10T-scale build in 2026 — the evidence above shows ensembles reliably beat single-axis scorers and the labeling budget is not a constraint. Three directions beyond it carry the strongest evidence of genuine, currently-underexploited value:

  1. Reject-pile recovery. REWIRE and the taxonomy-guided low-tier recovery paper independently converge on the same structural insight — the discard pile is under-processed signal, not noise — with REWIRE’s recycled mix beating 2x the raw crawl volume outright. This is the cheapest of the three directions: it requires no new classifier, only a second pass over data already scored and already discarded.
  2. Dynamic, group-level influence selection. MATES → Group-MATES → TiKMiX represent a real methodological progression away from static classifier scores toward model-state-aware, group-level signal, with TiKMiX matching RegMix at 20% of its compute. The caveat is real: none of this has been validated past 7B-parameter proxy scale, so a 10T-scale build adopting it is extending published evidence, not applying it.
  3. Bespoke per-language/per-domain curation over uniform mixing. ÜberWeb’s 4-10x compute-matching result at 20T-token scale is the most directly transferable of the three, precisely because it is validated at the token scale closest to the reader’s own budget.

A fourth, more speculative candidate — agentic pipeline search (DataEvolve) — shows a genuinely large effect (Darwin-CC’s evolved strategies account for roughly 40% of the total gain over raw data) but with no disclosed compute-cost figure for the 240-run search and validation confined to 672B tokens across 8 STEM/medicine categories; the research notes’ own best-guess read (moderate confidence, not a stated conclusion of the DataEvolve paper) is to treat it as a technique for curating specific high-value sub-domains, not a whole-corpus solution at Essential-Web’s 24T-token scale [data-curation note, author inference] [DERIVED/INFERENCE, 2025-26]. Metadata conditioning (MeCo’s URL-prepending, delivering a 33%-less-data result at 1.6B/8B scale arXiv:2501.01956 [PAPER, ICML 2025], and its 2026 successor Introspective X Training’s feedback-conditioning result at 7.5-12B/18T tokens arXiv:2605.20285 [PAPER, May 2026]) is the cheapest lever of all — string concatenation — but as of July 2026 no named frontier lab has disclosed adopting it, so it remains a high-upside, unconfirmed-at-scale bet rather than a direction with the same evidentiary weight as the three above.

The 2026 reference data-factory blueprint

Layering the evidence above into a single pipeline for a 10T-total/200B-active build: an Essential-Web-style 12-field taxonomy backbone (SQL-filterable, not a single scalar), with Document Type built explicitly on WebOrganizer’s Topic×Format axes so the pipeline gets both hard-filter subsetting and RegMix-style mixture weighting rather than one or the other; ensemble rather than single-axis classifiers at every quality-scoring step (GneissWeb’s +2.73pp and the 368/1000-domain classifier-disagreement finding both argue against trusting one scorer); a reject-pile recovery pass (REWIRE-style guided rewrite) run on the taxonomy’s low-tier bucket before final discard; multi-extractor union at the HTML-extraction stage rather than a single trafilatura/resiliparse pass; FinePDFs-style dense PDF ingestion layered on top of HTML-derived web text given its 2x-median-length advantage; bespoke per-language curation for the top-N languages by token share rather than uniform multilingual mixing, per ÜberWeb; synthetic rephrasing targeted at knowledge-dense subsets specifically, held near the ~30% mixing ratio the collapse literature supports, generated by an ~8B-class model rather than the largest available teacher; two complementary dedup stages — GPU-native fuzzy MinHash (NeMo Curator-class throughput) plus suffix-array exact-substring dedup layered on top, not instead; and a labeling budget sized for a 7B-32B classifier rather than 0.5B, since the FLOP math shows headroom to spend more on taxonomy quality once compute stops being the binding constraint. Dynamic influence reweighting (TiKMiX/Group-MATES-class) is the one component to stage as a research track rather than a production dependency, given its proxy-scale-only validation — a natural candidate for the de-risking ladder Ch7 and Ch22 describe for other unproven-at-scale techniques.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

Three planning consequences follow directly. First, the curation-technology budget for a 10T-scale run should be sized around taxonomy-design and validation engineering time, not classifier compute — the labeling-FLOP math makes a larger, better labeler affordable, so the constraint is judgment, not GPU-hours. Second, the highest-confidence near-term wins (reject-pile recovery, ensemble classifiers, multi-extractor union, per-language bespoke curation) are all compatible with an Essential-Web-style taxonomy backbone and require no new training infrastructure beyond what Ch13’s dataloading pipeline already assumes; dynamic influence selection is the one technique that should be treated as an active research bet, prototyped at proxy scale before being trusted at the full run’s data-selection layer. Third, the MoE-specific data question remains genuinely open — no published source in this survey offers a compute-matched, controlled dense-vs-MoE mixture ablation — so the safest default is to inherit the dense-model curation pipeline wholesale rather than construct a bespoke MoE mixture theory with no supporting evidence, while treating that inheritance as a hypothesis a 10T-scale program is well-positioned to test rather than a settled fact to build around.

Chapter 12 · Part III — The Data

Midtraining, Mixtures, and the Endgame Curriculum

Pretraining used to end where post-training began, with nothing in between but a learning-rate decay nobody wrote a paper about. By late 2025 that gap had acquired a name, a taxonomy, two competing surveys, and — from Ai2 in particular — token-level accounting precise enough to reproduce. This chapter takes the reader through what is actually known about midtraining, long-context extension, mixture science, and the reinforcement-pretraining fringe, then commits to a staged curriculum for the 10T-total/200B-active run.

The Pipeline Gets a Third Leg: Definitions and Taxonomy

Two arXiv surveys appeared within three weeks of each other in October 2025 and converged on the same three-axis description of midtraining: a shift in data distribution toward curated, high-quality corpora; an interaction with learning-rate scheduling (decay timing and shape); and long-context extension — Mid-Training of Large Language Models: A Survey [PAPER][Oct 2025] and A Survey on LLM Mid-training [PAPER][Oct 2025]. Both credit the term itself to Phi and OpenAI usage before Ai2 formalized it in OLMo 2’s own footnote (“Abdin et al. 2024a and OpenAI 2024”) [PAPER][OLMo 2 Furious, §2.3 fn.4, Oct 2025]. A more mechanistic March-2026 follow-up, PRISM [PAPER][Mar 2026], asks why midtraining data doesn’t erase pretraining capability — a retention question rather than a mixture question — and Midtraining Bridges Pretraining and Posttraining Distributions [PAPER][Oct 2025] frames the stage as closing the KL gap between raw web text and chat-formatted instructions, not merely as a quality upsample.

The practical upshot for a 10T/200B host: midtraining is no longer an ad hoc “anneal at the end” gesture. It is a distinct, budgeted, measured stage with its own data pool, its own LR trajectory, and — per the surveys — its own open failure modes (instruction-data placement chief among them, discussed below).

The Gold-Standard Disclosures: OLMo 2 and OLMo 3’s Dolmino Mixes

No frontier-adjacent lab publishes midtraining mixture data at the resolution Ai2 does. OLMo 2 [PAPER][v3 Oct 2025, COLM 2025] splits training into Stage 1 pretraining (90-95% of FLOPs, cosine decay to 10% of peak LR, on a 3.90T-token mix: DCLM-Baseline 3.71T, StarCoder 83.0B, peS2o 58.6B, arXiv 20.8B, OpenWebMath 12.2B, Algebraic Stack 11.8B, Wikipedia 3.7B) and Stage 2 midtraining (5-10% of FLOPs, linear decay to zero, on Dolmino Mix 1124). The Dolmino source pool totals 843B tokens: an 832.6B-token high-quality subset (FastText-top-7%-filtered DCLM at 752B, decontaminated FLAN 17.0B, peS2o 58.6B, Wikipedia/Wikibooks 3.7B, curated StackExchange 1.26B) and a 10.7B-token math mix (TuluMath synthetic, Dolmino SynthMath, TinyGSM-MIND, MathCoder2 Synth Books, Metamath, CodeSearchNet, GSM8K train). From this pool they sample three fixed-size anneal mixes — 50B (7B model), 100B (13B), 300B (32B) — holding filtered-DCLM at a roughly constant ~50% share across all three by explicit design [PAPER][same, Table 13].

OLMo 3 [PAPER][Dec 2025] is the richer disclosure. Its Stage 2 midtrain mix is 100B tokens sampled from a 2.19T-token pool, with every category costed to the tenth of a percent:

[PAPER][same, Table 5]
