Methodology matters as much as the numbers: OLMo 3 runs a two-tier “distributed exploration → centralized assessment” loop — cheap microanneals (5B candidate + 5B web, 10B total) feeding periodic integration tests (full 100B-token anneals, followed by quick SFT to confirm gains survive post-training). Five integration rounds moved OlmoBaseEval from 49.7 to 50.7 to a final 53.1 [PAPER][same, Table 6] — a documented, monotonic mixture-quality trajectory, the kind of evidence trail almost no other lab discloses. A domain-tradeoff ablation quantifies the cost of upweighting math/code: a math-code-thinking-skewed mix scores Math 60.8/Code 35.6 against the balanced Round-5 mix’s Math 57.1/Code 27.7, but GenQA drops 73.1→65.9 — roughly 7 points of general-QA capability for 4-8 points of math/code [PAPER][same, Table 7].

Cross-Lab Midtraining Practice: Phi-4, SmolLM3, and the Nemotron Phase Ladders

Outside Ai2, disclosures are coarser but still numerically anchored. SmolLM3 [OFFICIAL-BLOG][Jul 2025] runs an 11.2T-token, three-stage curriculum with exact absolute-token boundaries: Stage 1 (0→8T, 85% web/12% code/3% math), Stage 2 (8T→10T, 75%/15%/10%), Stage 3 decay (10T→11.1T, 63%/24%/13%, adding OpenMathReasoning). Phi-4 [PAPER][Dec 2024] discloses a midtrain mix of 30% newly-curated long-context data / 70% recall (replay) tokens, RoPE base raised 10K→250K, and peak LR cut 10x relative to pretraining.

NVIDIA’s Nemotron family gives the cleanest cross-generation pattern. Nemotron Nano 2 [PAPER][Aug-Sep 2025] transitions Phase 1→2 at 60% of tokens and Phase 2→3 at 90%. Nemotron 3 Nano [PAPER][Dec 2025] runs a two-phase 25T-token curriculum with the switch at 94% (Phase 1: 23.5T/94% diversity-focused, Phase 2: 1.5T/6% quality-focused). Nemotron 3 Super [PAPER][Apr 2026] — also 25T tokens — switches at 80% (Phase 1: 20T/80%, Phase 2: 5T/20%), and Nemotron 3 Ultra reportedly switches near 75% (25%-share quality phase). The gradient is explicit: Nano’s quality phase is 6% of tokens, Super’s is 20%, Ultra’s is ~25% — bigger models in the same family get proportionally more midtraining, not less, which is the single most load-bearing cross-lab pattern for a 200B-active target (§ below on the mixture strawman). The same Nemotron Nano 2 report also gives a clean instruction-in-pretrain ablation: replacing 5% of a 100B continued-pretrain slice with synthetic “Fundamental Reasoning” SFT-style data lifted MMLU-Pro 44.24→56.36 (+12.1 pts) and Math 37.92→39.70 (+1.8 pts) [PAPER][same, Table 3].

[PAPER][sources as cited above] — *the SmolLM3 “~28% / 8T (71%)” figures are this chapter’s own arithmetic on the HuggingFace blog’s disclosed absolute-token stage boundaries (8T/2T/1.1T of 11.2T total), not a percentage stated verbatim in the source.

Decay-Phase Upweighting, Quantified

The purest test of midtraining’s leverage is OLMo 2’s own ablation: LR-decay-to-zero alone, no new data, on a 50B-token anneal from a 4T-token checkpoint, moves MMLU (completion-format) by +20 points and OLMES by +4.4 [PAPER][OLMo 2, Table 11, Oct 2025]. OLMo 3’s Stage-1→Stage-2 jump on its Math aggregate is even sharper: 23.5→59.8 (+36.3 points) from 100B midtrain tokens — 1.7% of the total token budget [PAPER][OLMo 3, Table 13, Dec 2025]. The per-token leverage of the decay phase is, on these two numbers, an order of magnitude higher than pretraining’s marginal token.

The mechanism behind this leverage is OLMo’s microannealing methodology: mix a candidate source 50/50 with generic web data, anneal on ~10B tokens with a proper LR decay, score on a 200-example GSM8K-derived dev set (“GSM*”) plus MMLU. OLMo 2 ran 19 microanneals totaling 130B tokens — less than three full 50B anneal runs — to derive its final math mix [PAPER][same, §4.4.2]. Concrete findings from that sweep are directly reusable: domain-specific math helps even at a 10% ratio (65/35 web/math → GSM* 63.5; 90/10 → 61.0, both far above the 28.5 pre-anneal baseline); duplicating a small math dataset up to 4x still helps, with diminishing but positive returns (1x→63.5, 2x→66.0, 4x→65.0); and rewriting code-formatted synthetic math into natural language is the single largest lever found — raw TinyGSM in code-answer form actually hurts (GSM*=25.0, below baseline), while the MIND-rewritten natural-language version scores 65.5, and doubling that rewritten data reaches 70.0 [PAPER][same, Table 12].

Mixing Science: RegMix, Mixing Laws, CLIMB, and the $100M Question

The mixture-optimization literature is mature at small scale and essentially silent at frontier scale. DoReMi [PAPER][2023] — Group-DRO minimax reweighting via a 280M proxy for an 8B target — is 2026’s standard baseline, not what any disclosed lab actually runs in production; every disclosed frontier lab (OLMo, Nemotron, SmolLM3, Qwen, Hunyuan) uses ablation/microanneal-style empirical search instead. RegMix [PAPER][ICLR 2025 Spotlight] trains 512 models at 1M params/1B tokens each, fits a LightGBM regression from mixture-proportion to loss, and applies the predicted-optimal mixture at a 1B/25B-token target — a proxy-search compute budget trivial next to the target run, and per Hunyuan’s own report, this directly inspired their production mixing method [PAPER/CREDIBLE-HEARSAY][search result, no direct fetch]. Data Mixing Laws [PAPER][ICLR 2025] compose separately-fit scaling-in-steps, scaling-in-model-size, and mixing-law-in-proportions curves to predict loss on an unseen mixture, validated on a 1B/100B-token RedPajama target. Nemotron-CLIMB [PAPER][Apr 2025] embeds and clusters an unlabeled corpus into 20 semantic clusters — solving the “Common Crawl has no domain labels” problem RegMix and DoReMi both assume away — and reports a 1B/400B-token CLIMB-optimized model beating Llama-3.2-1B by 2.0% average, with domain-targeted optimization yielding a 5% within-domain improvement; the paper discloses no scale-transfer evidence beyond 1B params. Aioli [PAPER][ICLR 2025] is the one genuinely different idea in this cluster — an online method that fits mixing-law parameters continuously from the current run’s own loss history rather than fixing a mixture upfront, beating stratified sampling by 0.27 test-perplexity points on average and by up to 12.0 points in the realistic setting where a proxy-learned mixture needs mid-run correction. That “matters most when the proxy mixture doesn’t transfer cleanly” framing is precisely the risk profile of a 10T/200B run.

The most rigorous transfer evidence available — Explaining Data Mixing Scaling Laws [PAPER][Jun 2026] and Apple’s Scaling Laws for Optimal Data Mixtures [PAPER][Jul 2025] — validates extrapolation from a 200M/8B-token proxy to a 700M/16B target, and separately from 122M/10B to 1B/30B: roughly 5-8x parameter scale-up, 2-3x token scale-up. No paper found in this corpus extrapolates a mixture law across more than one order of magnitude in parameters. This is the honest answer to the $100M question: there is no published evidence that a 1B-optimal mixture holds at 200B-active-parameter scale. The closest available signal is negative-by-implication — the Nemotron 3 family’s quality-phase token fraction changing systematically with model size (6%→20%→~25%, above) is inconsistent with a scale-invariant optimal mixture, and is the strongest circumstantial argument that mixtures do not trivially transfer. Ch5 covers the analogous problem for architecture (granularity, activation ratio) and Ch6 covers the general scaling-law transfer literature in more depth; the mixture-specific ceiling documented here is narrower and, if anything, less resolved.

Instruction Data in Pretraining and the Special-Token Landmine

Every disclosed, cleanly-ablated result in this corpus finds instruction/chat-style data in pretraining positive. Instruction Pre-Training [PAPER][Jun 2024] converts 1/5 of raw documents (40M docs) into ~200M synthetic instruction-response pairs (~10B tokens) and mixes them back with unchanged raw text. Nemotron Nano 2’s 5%-substitution ablation (above) is unambiguously positive. OLMo 3’s Table 10 ablation — full mix with thinking-traces and instruction data versus without, tokens held constant — improves every base-eval metric (Avg +1.9, Math +5.6, Code +1.1, FIM +2.7), with the paper’s own framing: “this benefit begins even before post-training” [PAPER][OLMo 3, §3.5.4, Dec 2025]. The Oct-2025 mid-training survey nonetheless calls the field’s overall verdict “unresolved” [PAPER][2510.06826, Oct 2025] — and OLMo 3 supplies the likely reason.

They tested including chat special tokens (<|im_start|>, <|im_end|>) in midtrain instruction data. The model learns to emit these tokens at inference despite never being post-trained on them, which collapses GSM8K from 49.43 to 0 and CruxEval from 32.89 to 18.91 — not because reasoning degraded, but because answer-parsing breaks on the unexpected tokens. A control using ordinary chat-template text without special tokens only drops GSM8K to 46.02, confirming the failure is vocabulary-artifact-specific, not chat-formatting-specific [PAPER][same, §3.5.4]. Their fix: strip special tokens from midtrain data entirely, use newline-based formatting, defer special tokens to SFT. Read against this finding, “instruction data in pretraining helps” is fairly robust; “chat-formatted-with-special-tokens data in pretraining” is a specific, avoidable footgun that the survey language conflates into a single unresolved question.

Contamination Policy Reality

OLMo 3’s open-sourced decontamination tool, decon (github.com/allenai/decon) [CODE][Dec 2025], runs two phases: a sparse-stride n-gram detection pass for speed, then a dense cluster-expansion pass on hits to compute a contamination score without paying full-density-matching cost everywhere. They decontaminate all splits — train, val, and test — of every eval benchmark, because some benchmarks (FLAN among them) are literally built from benchmark templates and leak via train splits, not just test splits. Figure 12 of the report shows per-benchmark contamination counts and performance deltas: most are small (0 to ±2 points), but DROP, Minerva, and SQuAD show substantial score inflation when contaminated data isn’t removed [PAPER][same, §3.5.4].

The counter-intuitive finding worth carrying forward: even where decon detected complete GSM8K leakage in some sources, removing it did not improve performance — attributed, per a public discussion of a similar Marin-32B finding, to the leaked text being in a different format than the eval harness expects, so the model doesn’t actually recognize the leaked example as the benchmark question [PAPER][same, §3.5.4]. Decontamination and eval-format-matching are more entangled than the “just decontaminate harder” instinct assumes; treat leakage removal as a hygiene and credibility requirement independent of its measured score impact, not a metric-optimization lever.

Long-Context Extension: Recipes, Ratios, and Their Context-Parallel Cost

OLMo 3 §3.6 compiles the single best cross-lab long-context token-budget comparison available:

[PAPER][OLMo 3, §3.6, Dec 2025] — a 50-100x spread across disclosed practice, with no consensus on pipeline placement either: Llama 3.1 runs LC extension before midtraining, Qwen 2.5/3 and OLMo run it after midtraining and before SFT, GLM-4.5 runs it after SFT [PAPER][same].

OLMo 3’s own Stage 3 recipe is the most granular public description available. Source pool: the Dolma 3 Longmino Pool, 639B tokens of length-bucketed olmOCR-processed scientific PDFs, bucketed from 8K-16K (144B, 22.5%) up through 1M+ (26.9B, 4.21%). Final mix: 34% long-context / 66% short-context (short-context = the Stage-2 midtrain mix), giving 50B tokens for the 7B model and 100B for the 32B model — larger model, proportionally more LC tokens, the same qualitative pattern as the Nemotron phase-size finding above [PAPER][same, §3.6.3]. The ratio itself is directly quantified at 10B-token scale: a 66% LC / 34% short-context mix costs 2.5 points of OlmoBaseEval versus equal-token short-only training; a 34% LC / 66% short mix costs only 0.8 points [PAPER][same]. That is the single most reusable exact number in this whole beat — going majority-long-context costs roughly 3x more short-context capability than staying majority-short.

Technique stack: YaRN applied only to full-attention layers, not sliding-window layers (ablated directly against full-model YaRN and against 8M-theta variants, and winning); best-fit document packing rather than naive concatenate-then-split, which under-fills sequences relative to the true document-length distribution and alone gave a “substantial” RULER improvement; intra-document attention masking; and 8-way context parallelism, all-gather-based rather than ring-based, chosen specifically because it tolerates the irregular attention masks that sliding-window plus intra-document masking produce [PAPER][same, §3.6.4]. Final model souping for this stage used a cheaper substitute than multi-seed reruns: averaging the last three checkpoints of the single LC run (steps 10000/11000/11921) [PAPER][same].

Naturally-long documents beat artificially-concatenated ones for downstream long-context performance — this is confirmed independently a year apart by Phi-4 [PAPER][Dec 2024] and OLMo 3, and is consistent with OLMo 3’s best-fit-packing finding. MiniMax-M1 [PAPER][Jun 2025] — a hybrid Lightning-Attention/softmax architecture — stages context growth 32K→1M gradually, explicitly to avoid the gradient explosions observed when scaling context aggressively in a hybrid-attention setting; at 100K generation length it uses only 25% of DeepSeek-R1’s FLOPs, a reminder that architecture choice changes the cost calculus of the LC stage independent of the mixing question (Ch5 owns the attention-architecture tradeoff itself).

The context-parallelism cost deserves stating explicitly rather than deferring entirely to Ch16: OLMo 3 needed 8-way CP for a 65K target. A 512K-1M target — the plausible ambition for a 10T/200B run given Kimi K2/DeepSeek-V3.1/Llama-3.1-class precedent — scales roughly linearly with target length, implying 64-128-way CP as a reasonable planning assumption pending direct benchmarking. That CP degree will materially depress MFU and tokens/s/GPU during Stage 3 specifically; budget a distinct, lower MFU target for the LC stage in the compute plan rather than assuming Stage-1 MFU carries through (Ch16 and Ch21 quantify the parallelism math and the resulting cost model).

Souping and Branch-Train-Merge at Frontier Scale

Ai2’s practice is the clearest disclosed example of anneal-phase souping: OLMo 2 7B trains three separate 50B anneal runs from the same Stage-1 checkpoint with different data shuffles, then weight-averages them; 13B/32B train three 100B runs plus one 300B run and average all four. Table 14’s direct ablation shows souping “consistently equals or outperforms” the best single checkpoint on aggregate metrics — but per-metric variance is real: one mix’s GSM8k score dropped 60.5→43.0 upon souping even as every other metric on that same mix improved [PAPER][OLMo 2, Table 14, Oct 2025]. Souping is a net-positive, not a metric-monotonic, operation, and any book chapter presenting it as strictly dominant should carry that caveat.

Two other souping-adjacent techniques belong in a different bucket and are worth distinguishing from Ai2’s approach explicitly, since secondary coverage often conflates them. Gemma 2’s WARP [PAPER][2406.16768, 2024] — EMA during RL fine-tuning, SLERP across RL-tuned policies, then linear interpolation back toward init — is a post-training RLHF merge technique, not a pretraining anneal-souping technique, despite the shared “souping” vocabulary. Llama 4 Maverick’s codistillation from the never-fully-released Behemoth teacher [OFFICIAL-BLOG][ai.meta.com/blog/llama-4-multimodal-intelligence, Apr 2025] is architecturally closer to Gemma 3’s teacher-distillation pretraining (256 sampled logits per token, cross-entropy against a softened teacher distribution across all four Gemma 3 sizes [PAPER][2503.19786, Mar 2025]) than to weight-averaging; no distillation-loss-weight schedule was disclosed. Net read: souping/merging is dual-track in current frontier practice — anneal-souping (Ai2) squeezes variance out of the highest-leverage, lowest-token-count tail stage, while RLHF-policy-souping (Gemma’s WARP lineage) combines reward-optimized policies in post-training. Nobody discloses souping across an entire pretraining run, only the tail stages — consistent with model-soup theory that linear mode connectivity holds best for checkpoints sharing most of their training trajectory.

A separate, distinct use of checkpoint-averaging is worth flagging for the measurement plan below: OLMo 3 cannot afford to fully anneal-to-zero at regular intervals for its 32B model, so it instead averages four checkpoints, 1,000 steps apart, as a cheap proxy for a clean mid-run evaluation signal [PAPER][OLMo 3, §3.4, citing Li et al. 2025]. This is an eval-noise-reduction trick, distinct from final-stage souping, and reusable throughout Stages 1-3.

Reinforcement Pretraining: RPT, RLP, and the MAI-1 Counter-Example

Reinforcement Pre-training reframes next-token prediction as an RL problem: the model receives a binary/sparse verifiable reward for correctly predicting the ground-truth next token via prefix-matching, with compute-vs-accuracy scaling claimed to improve consistently with more RPT compute [PAPER][RPT, 2506.08007, Jun 2025]. Its 2026 successor, RLP, is explicitly critical of RPT’s design: RPT’s sparse binary reward “often relies on proxy-model filtering of ‘easy’ tokens,” and prior demonstrations “depend on distilled checkpoints with strong prior reasoning ability, which clouds whether the method helps base models.” RLP instead uses a dense, information-theoretic reward — the increase in log-likelihood of the actual next token when conditioning on a sampled reasoning chain versus context alone — computed verifier-free at every position, not just verifiable spans [PAPER][RLP, 2510.01265, Sep 2025, rev. Mar 2026, ICLR 2026, NVIDIA authorship]. Its own evidence is Qwen3-1.7B-Base and Nemotron-Nano-12B-v2 only: +19% average on an 8-benchmark math/science suite and 42.81%→61.32% overall respectively. A March-2026 variant, LoopRPT [PAPER][Mar 2026], applies the idea to looped/recurrent-depth architectures — further signaling an active research family, not settled production practice.

Both RPT and RLP top out at ≤12B parameters, on math/science reasoning benchmarks, using an RL-style loop layered on top of standard pretraining. Neither has disclosed evidence at 100B+ active-parameter, multi-trillion-token frontier scale. RLP’s own paper functions as the closest thing to independent commentary on RPT in this corpus, and its critique — that RPT’s positive results may be an artifact of starting from already-strong distilled checkpoints rather than a base-model-general effect — is the load-bearing caveat for anyone tempted to treat RPT as validated. The most useful outside data point, though, is Microsoft’s own disclosed practice: MAI-Thinking-1 [OFFICIAL-BLOG][Jun 2026] describes RL as a separate, later hill-climbing phase layered on top of a 30T-token, human-data-only, 8,000-GPU base pretraining run (“MAI-Base-1”), with active filtering to strip AI-generated text from the crawl. This is architecturally distinct from the RPT/RLP proposal of fusing reward signal into the next-token loss during pretraining itself — a genuinely frontier-scale lab, as of mid-2026, keeps RL and pretraining as cleanly separate compute pools rather than fusing them. Treat RPT/RLP as an interesting 2026 research direction to watch, not a line item to budget into a 10T/200B curriculum.

MTP Head Timing: A Non-Question

Worth stating because it forecloses a plausible-sounding design question: DeepSeek-V3’s multi-token-prediction module is trained from scratch as an integral part of the entire 14.8T-token pretraining objective, co-optimized with the main next-token loss for the whole run, not bolted on partway through [PAPER][DeepSeek-V3]. No source in this corpus describes any lab adding an MTP head mid-run rather than from initialization — “when to add the MTP head” is not a decision point that exists in disclosed practice. Ch5 covers the MTP design choice itself (loss weight, head depth, EAGLE-style vs. DeepSeek-style); the timing question, at least, has a settled answer: from-scratch or not at all.

The Staged 25-30T-Token Curriculum Strawman

Synthesizing the above into a concrete proposal for the 10T-total/200B-active MoE. Token fractions are anchored to disclosed practice — OLMo’s 90-95%/5-10% pretrain/midtrain split, the Nemotron phase-grows-with-model-size pattern, OLMo 3’s 34/66 LC/SC ratio — but every percentage below should be read as a starting hypothesis for the run’s own microanneals to correct, not a settled answer; the mixture-transfer ceiling documented above means nothing here is validated at this parameter scale.

Total budget: 28T tokens, across four stages plus a continuous decontamination track.

Stage 1 — Pretraining (~25T tokens). Composition: ~65-70% filtered web (quality-classifier upsampling in the OLMo 3 style, top-5% resampled up to ~7x, bottom-40% discarded), ~12-15% code (FIM-transformed), ~8-10% math/STEM web, ~5-8% multilingual, ~2-4% academic/reference. LR schedule: WSD rather than pre-committed cosine, since a 25-30T-token run is likely to have its token budget revised mid-flight. Stability guardrails from step 0 (Ch7 owns the mechanics): repeated-n-gram filtering, reordered RMSNorm, QK-norm, z-loss, fixed RoPE θ from initialization to avoid a discontinuous scaling event later. Measurement: gradient-norm spike frequency, ≥500-domain Paloma-style held-out perplexity monthly, ranked-classification ladder eval weekly, continuous MFU/tokens-per-second. Abort/rebalance: 3-sigma gradient-norm excursions without recovery; MFU sustained >10% below target for 48h (infra issue, investigate before touching the mixture); single-domain Paloma perplexity diverging for 2+ consecutive evals (pipeline regression, not a mixture problem).

Stage 2 — Midtraining (2.0-2.5T tokens). This is the largest proportional departure from disclosed practice in the strawman: neither OLMo 3’s 1.7% nor Nemotron 3 Super’s 20% maps cleanly onto 200B active params, but the Nemotron family’s model-size gradient argues for erring toward Super/Ultra’s share rather than Nano’s 6%. Sub-composition (of the 2.0-2.5T budget): ~35-40% quality-filtered web replay, ~15% math (synthetic plus NL-rewritten, per the +40-GSM*-point OLMo 2 finding), ~12% code, ~10% QA/knowledge-access synthetic, ~15% reasoning/thinking traces, ~8-10% instruction data without chat special tokens, ~3-5% PDFs/academic. Methodology: microanneals (5B+5B, dozens run in parallel) feeding integration tests (200-500B-token anneals, 4-5 rounds minimum), with roughly 1-2% of total compute (~700B-1T tokens’ worth) budgeted as a distinct search line item, not absorbed silently into “midtraining.” Measurement: per-round integration-test aggregate scores on OLMo-3-style categories (not saturated legacy benchmarks — see Ch16/Ch21 for the benchmark-saturation problem), domain-tradeoff checks at each round (>5-8 points of GenQA cost per math/code gain signals over-rotation), quick-SFT probes on 2-3 candidates per round, decontamination on every new source before it enters an integration test. Abort/rebalance: single-domain tradeoff worse than OLMo 3’s observed range; quick-SFT gains not surviving post-training (investigate contamination-driven inflation before proceeding).

Stage 3 — Long-context extension (500-800B tokens). Given a 512K-1M context ambition, budget toward the Llama-3.1/DeepSeek-V3.1 end (800-840B) rather than OLMo 3/SmolLM3’s ~50-100B, since no sub-200B-token LC budget has been validated at this target length. Mix: 34% long-context / 66% short-context as the default starting ratio, per OLMo 3’s directly-quantified ablation, replicated at 10B-token scale before committing at full budget. Sourcing: genuinely long documents (scientific PDFs, books, repo-level code with real cross-file dependencies), not artificial concatenation; synthetic aggregation-task augmentation (extract salient noun-phrases, generate QA/summarization tasks over snippets) is worth replicating given it improved RULER scores in OLMo 3’s own ablation. Technique stack: RoPE-θ scaling or YaRN on full-attention layers only, best-fit packing, intra-document masking, staged growth in 2-4 sub-stages (4K→32K→128K→512K+) rather than one jump. CP cost: plan for 64-128-way context parallelism and a distinct, lower MFU target for this stage specifically. Measurement: RULER at every context bucket at each sub-stage boundary; Math/Code regression relative to end-of-Stage-2 (the single most important metric, given OLMo 3’s 5-8 point documented regression pattern); tokens/s/GPU and MFU during this stage specifically to catch CP-configuration regressions. Abort/rebalance: Math/Code regression exceeding ~8-10 points (worse than OLMo 3’s own worst-case 8.3 points at 32B) — increase short-context replay and re-run; MFU below ~50% of Stage-1 MFU — treat as infra escalation before burning further tokens.

Stage 4 — Final anneal/souping (100-300B tokens). Run 3-4 independent anneal passes from the same Stage-2 (or Stage-3, if souping post-LC) checkpoint, differing only in shuffle order, each on a 100-300B-token slice of the finalized midtrain mix with linear decay to zero. Weight-average all resulting checkpoints, budgeted explicitly as a 3-4x multiplier on anneal cost — not optional overhead, one of the best-evidenced, cheapest wins in this entire beat. Evaluate the souped checkpoint on the full eval suite before committing to it as the release candidate: do not assume every metric improves (OLMo 2’s own 17.5-point GSM8k regression on one mix, even as aggregate metrics improved, is the standing counterexample). For cheaper mid-run monitoring throughout Stages 1-3, use the 4-checkpoints/1,000-steps-apart averaging trick rather than full anneal-to-zero reruns.

Cross-cutting: decontamination. Build or adapt decon’s two-phase sparse-detection/dense-cluster-expansion design, run it against every candidate source before it enters an integration test, decontaminate all splits (not just test) of the eval suite, and treat leakage removal as a hygiene requirement independent of measured score impact — per OLMo 3’s own finding, removing detected contamination does not reliably move scores.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

Three numbers from this chapter should directly shape the run plan. First, midtraining’s per-token leverage (OLMo 3’s +36.3-point Math jump from 1.7% of total tokens) means the 2.0-2.5T-token Stage 2 budget above is not a rounding error against the 25T-token Stage 1 — it is plausibly the highest-ROI compute in the entire curriculum, and should be resourced, staffed, and measured accordingly rather than treated as pretraining’s tail. Second, the long-context stage’s documented 5-8 point Math/Code regression (worst case 8.3 points at 32B) means Stage 3 needs its own abort criteria distinct from Stage 1/2’s — a run that silently absorbs an LC-stage capability regression without a dedicated gate will ship a worse model than its own Stage-2 checkpoint. Third, and most consequentially: no mixture-optimization technique in the literature (RegMix, CLIMB, Data Mixing Laws, Apple’s transfer study) has been validated above roughly 1-8B target parameters, a gap of two-plus orders of magnitude from a 200B-active MoE. Every percentage in the strawman above is a hypothesis, not a plan — the run’s own microanneal-plus-integration-test loop, scaled up from OLMo’s proven methodology, is the actual mixture-selection mechanism, and its compute budget (Ch6’s ladder-cost discipline applies directly here) needs to be sized as seriously as the pretraining FLOPs it is meant to steer.

Chapter 13 · Part III — The Data

The Input Pipeline: Dataloading at 100k GPUs

The reader who has watched training runs stall on things nobody warned them about will ask this chapter’s question before any other: is the dataloader ever actually the bottleneck at 100,000 GPUs, and if not, why does every practitioner’s gut still flinch at the phrase “data pipeline”? The honest answer, worked from first principles and from the loader-by-loader evidence below, is that raw token throughput was never in danger — the physics make that case overwhelmingly — but three adjacent failure modes (cold-start index construction, multi-source composition imbalance, and the absence of load-time metadata-conditional sampling) are real, documented, and specific enough to budget engineering time against.

The bandwidth math nobody needs to do, but should

Start with the ceiling. A 200B-active-parameter MoE at sequence length 8k, running on a 100,000-GPU cluster, is compute-bound by the model’s own forward/backward FLOPs long before it is I/O-bound by token ingestion. Cross-checked two independent ways — a FLOPs-budget derivation from public GB200/B200 FP8 dense FLOPS figures, and a scaling argument from DeepSeek-V3’s disclosed 14.8T tokens over 2.788M H800-GPU-hours — the compute ceiling caps aggregate ingestion at roughly 75–76M tokens/s cluster-wide [derived from public GB200/B200 FP8 FLOPS and DeepSeek-V3 throughput scaling] [derived, 2026]. At 4 bytes/token — the mandatory dtype for any modern >64k-vocabulary tokenizer, since Megatron-Core’s GPTDatasetConfig.__post_init__ sets token_dtype_code = 4 if vocab_size > 65535 else 8, meaning every 2026-era frontier tokenizer (100k–256k vocab) is always in the int32/4-bytes-per-token regime on disk github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM [CODE, main branch, Jul 2026] — that ceiling implies an aggregate raw byte-ingestion rate of only ~300 MB/s across the entire cluster.

Three to four orders of magnitude below what a single NVMe drive delivers, let alone a parallel filesystem: that is the entire finding, stated as starkly as the notes state it. A single PCIe Gen5 NVMe drive sustains on the order of 14 GB/s direct to GPU memory over GPUDirect Storage [derived from GPUDirect Storage figures, notes corpus] [derived, 2026]; any shared parallel filesystem worth deploying at 100k-GPU scale — 3FS, Tectonic, DDN EXAScaler — is provisioned for multi-TB/s aggregate. Feeding 300 MB/s of pre-tokenized bytes to 100,000 GPUs is not a storage-engineering problem; it is closer to a rounding error against the provisioning headroom any of these systems already carries for checkpoint traffic. Databricks’ own engineers, writing about MosaicML Streaming, make exactly this point independently: benchmark comparisons against WebDataset and HF datasets were run on ImageNet+ResNet-50 because “for larger model training like LLMs…, this effect disappears as the models are not dataloader bottlenecked” Databricks: MosaicML StreamingDataset [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2023-2026] — a vendor conceding the thing that would otherwise be its strongest performance-marketing angle.

It is worth being precise about what this bandwidth math does not cover, because the reader’s intuition that “data at this scale must be a TB/s problem” is not wrong — it is pointed at the wrong subsystem. Checkpoint I/O for a 10T-parameter model’s optimizer-plus-weight state is genuinely TB/s-class: at the standard 14 bytes/parameter convention, the full state is ~140TB, and a naive <60s synchronous save implies ~2.3TB/s sustained write VAST Data checkpoint-bandwidth survey [OFFICIAL-BLOG, vendor, 2026]. That belongs to Ch19 — production systems dodge it via local-NVMe-first, async-drain architectures, and VAST’s own 40-run survey measures actual global checkpoint bandwidth at just 50–200 GB/s. Dataloading and checkpoint-output are architecturally distinct problems that happen to share the word “storage”; conflating them is the most common category error in how this question gets asked. This chapter is about the former.

Where dataloading genuinely can bite

Given that raw bandwidth is not the risk, the notes converge on exactly three places where the input pipeline can stall a 100k-GPU job, and all three are about orchestration and compute overhead, not bytes moved.

Cold start. Megatron-Core’s own documentation is unusually candid: for “large blends or many file prefixes,” building the document/sample/shuffle indices “can take several minutes and leave all GPUs idle while rank 0 performs CPU-only work,” and DataLoader initialization broadly “can take several minutes” because it “involves opening and memory-mapping multiple files and can significantly stress the filesystem” github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM, readme.md §“Fast DataLoader initialization” [CODE, 2026]. For a mixture with hundreds to thousands of source-file prefixes — exactly the shape of a 10T-run’s curated blend — that is minutes of 100,000 idle GPUs, priced at whatever the cluster’s hourly burn rate is. Three purpose-built mitigations exist: --dataloader-fast-cache-load (skip sync points, assuming the cache exists), --dataloader-defer-npy-index-mmap (lazy-mmap indices behind worker prefetch), and --per-dataset-sequences-path (one consolidated JSON of per-prefix metadata instead of one open() per prefix). tools/prepare_cache.py builds the index entirely offline, off the critical path of GPU-hour-billed job startup [same source, CODE]. This is standard “precompute once, cache, never pay for it live” discipline, but it must be deliberately wired in — a naive deployment at 10T-run blend complexity will hit this wall by default.

Multi-source workload imbalance. The more interesting and less obvious risk, quantified directly in ByteDance’s MegaScale-Data paper. Because attention FLOPs scale with sequence length squared, non-uniform per-rank sample-length and sample-count distributions from multi-source data create real compute imbalance even when sample counts are balanced across data-parallel ranks. In an 8-GPU VLM trial, MegaScale-Data measured max-vs-min microbatch FLOPs at 3.2× (image) and 6.9× (full sequence) purely from dataloader composition arXiv:2504.09844, “MegaScale-Data: Scaling Dataloader for Multisource Large Foundation Model Training” [PAPER, EuroSys 2026]. That imbalance is invisible to a naive “samples/sec” metric — it shows up only as pipeline-stage idle-waiting. For a 10T MoE with heterogeneous per-source document-length distributions sharded across expert- and pipeline-parallel groups, an unlucky microbatch composition is a genuine, dataloader-induced bubble, architecturally distinct from — and additive to — the EP and pipeline bubbles covered in Ch16.

On-the-fly filtering, tokenizing, and metadata sampling. The notes are unanimous: no frontier-scale system does any of these three things live, in the training hot loop. Offline pre-tokenization to a packed, fixed-width or bin-packed token format is the unanimous 2026 default — Megatron-Core, OLMo-core, MosaicML Streaming, Grain/ArrayRecord all assume pre-tokenized arrays as the on-disk format; on-the-fly tokenization shows up only in smaller-scale or fine-tuning pipelines, never in a frontier training-time hot loop. The same is true of quality filtering and metadata-conditional sampling — both happen once, upstream, at curation time, producing fixed per-category shards the dataloader consumes as opaque, already-decided inputs (Ch11’s territory for the curation side). The risk this chapter flags is not that these operations happen live — they don’t, by design — but that a team tempted to add live filtering or metadata-conditioned resampling for adaptive curricula is reaching for something no OSS loader currently does natively, and would be building genuinely new infrastructure.

Loader-by-loader against the requirements matrix

The 10T/200B run’s dataloading requirements reduce to six properties: bit-exact resume under a fixed world size, bit-exact (or at least correctness-preserving) resume across a changed world size, mixture hot-swap without a full rebuild, per-example metadata-conditional sampling, native varlen/packing metadata emission, and object-storage-native operation for a cold/archival data tier. No single OSS system satisfies all six.

The picture is consistent: Megatron-Core wins on varlen-metadata-nativity and index determinism but does not solve world-size-elastic resume or mixture hot-swap; MosaicML Streaming wins on exactly the two properties Megatron-Core lacks but says nothing about native packing metadata; nobody solves per-example metadata-conditional sampling; and MegaScale-Data — the system solving the largest number of confirmed properties simultaneously (bit-exact resume, elastic resharding, and Planner-driven mixture hot-swap) — is a bespoke, unreleased ByteDance system, not something to pip install. Its published description does not establish whether it emits native packing metadata or supports object storage, so those two cells are left undocumented rather than assumed.

Megatron-Core GPTDataset / BlendedMegatronDatasetBuilder. The storage primitive is IndexedDataset: a .bin file of raw token bytes plus a .idx file of sequence lengths, byte offsets, per-document sequence-index ranges, and (for multimodal) a per-sequence mode tag github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM [CODE, 2026]. Determinism comes from three cached index arrays built once at construction: a shuffled document index (Do_idx), a sample index mapping sample number to document position and token offset (Sa_idx), and an independent shuffle permutation on top (Sh_idx). Querying sample k is j = Sh_idx[k] → look up (i, offset) in Sa_idx → concatenate token ranges from Do_idx[i:i_next+1]. This three-level indirection gives bit-exact reproducibility under restart at fixed world size — the whole epoch’s sample order is a pure deterministic function of (seed, indexed_indices, N, S), cached once and memory-mapped by every rank [same source, CODE]. It does not, by itself, give MosaicML-style guaranteed resume across a changed world size: the global sample order is world-size-independent by construction, so re-sharding is safe, but exact mid-epoch resume requires the training loop to correctly recompute --consumed-samples under the new sharding — a training-script responsibility, not a dataset-class guarantee.

BlendedDataset draws from weighted sources via a deterministic error-feedback scheme — greedily assigning the next sample to whichever source is furthest under its target weight, not i.i.d. sampling — and bakes those weights into cached Da_idx/Sa_idx arrays at construction time. There is no code path to hot-swap blend weights mid-run without rebuilding BlendedDataset, and therefore its cache, from a new config. Multi-stage curricula in Megatron-Core are implemented as separate job phases sharing model/optimizer checkpoints, each phase getting a fresh, fixed blend, not a live re-weighting inside one continuous process github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM, blended_dataset.py [CODE, 2026] — which directly shapes how Ch12’s staged curriculum has to be implemented: discrete checkpointed phase transitions, not smooth retargeting.

Two 2025/2026-era additions are easy to miss for a reader whose mental model of Megatron-LM is frozen at the 2021 paper. object_storage_utils.py implements direct S3 (s3://) and Multi-Storage-Client (msc://) paths for .bin/.idx files with a tunable bin_chunk_nbytes and a local index-cache path — Megatron-Core can now stream pre-tokenized shards from object storage rather than requiring everything pre-staged on POSIX, relevant to a 10T-run’s cold/archival tier. And GPTDatasetConfig.inter_document_masking, when true, computes cu_seqlens via numpy.cumsum inside __getitem__ and returns it directly in the batch dict — the dataloader itself, not a separate preprocessing pass, produces the varlen metadata consumed downstream by FlashAttention-varlen and TransformerEngine kernels [same source, gpt_dataset.py lines 80–332, CODE]. A small, instructive historical war story: GitHub issue #907, a race condition where GPTDataset._build_document_sample_shuffle_indices failed to build indices on non-root nodes absent NFS — a concrete example of the “shared state assumed but not enforced across ranks” bug class that recurs through Megatron-Core’s history github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM/issues/907 [CODE/GitHub issue].

Megatron-Energon. NVIDIA’s dedicated multimodal loader — standardized WebDataset-based on-disk format plus metadata, optimized for multi-rank reads and mixing — with a savable/restorable state via get_savable_loader(), checkpointing shard position, sample-within-shard, and worker RNG state for deterministic resume after preemption github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-Energon [OFFICIAL-DOCS/CODE, 2026]. It is used only in Megatron-LM’s multimodal/mimo example paths, not the core GPT pretraining path — for a text-centric run GPTDataset remains the relevant stack unless a genuine multimodal component is present (Ch9). Its state-save design is architecturally closer to Streaming and Grain below than to Megatron-Core’s index-precompute-and-cache approach.

MosaicML StreamingDataset. Databricks’ writeup makes an unusually strong claim: elastic determinism — sample order identical regardless of GPU/node/worker count, demonstrated at 1–64 GPUs × 8 workers with bit-identical loss curves — achieved by shuffling across all samples assigned to a machine, decoupling shuffle granularity from parallelism topology Databricks: MosaicML StreamingDataset [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2023-2026]. “Begin on 8 GPUs, resume on 32, then 128” with matching loss curves is the one requirement-matrix item Megatron-Core does not solve as cleanly. Instant mid-epoch resume follows the same design — sample order is a deterministic function of small state rather than a replayable stream position. Sharded, disjoint downloads mean no machine re-downloads another’s shard — directly avoiding the N-way redundant-read cost MegaScale-Data measured below. Storage format is MDS (Mosaic Data Shard). The team states it evaluated TorchData and WebDataset first and built Streaming in-house because neither gave the “correctness guarantees, performance, and ease of use” needed — the same source supplying this chapter’s opening concession that LLM training is not dataloader-bottlenecked.

Grain (JAX) + ArrayRecord. Google’s JAX-first loader, built on ArrayRecord, layered on the Riegeli file format: indexed chunks with a metadata index at file-end enabling true random access (unlike TFRecord’s sequential-only reads), so global shuffle can shuffle indices rather than approximate with a windowed buffer developers.googleblog.com, “Building High-Performance Data Pipelines with Grain and ArrayRecord” [OFFICIAL-BLOG]. Checkpointing is get_state()/set_state() on the iterator; MaxText and other 2025/2026 JAX stacks use it for the determinism-plus-recovery combination maxtext.readthedocs.io [OFFICIAL-DOCS]. No public throughput numbers were surfaced anywhere in Google’s materials — a real documentation gap next to Mosaic’s marketing-forward disclosures.

torchdata.nodes. PyTorch’s torch.data was refocused in 2025 away from the abandoned DataPipes/DataLoaderV2 line toward StatefulDataLoader (drop-in, adds state_dict()/load_state_dict()) and torchdata.nodes, a composable-iterator DAG wrapped by a stateful Loader meta-pytorch.org/data [OFFICIAL-DOCS, 2026]. The migration guide claims nodes “performs at least as well” as the prior stack, but no quantitative benchmarks are published — a real gap for deciding whether to build on it versus reusing Megatron-Core’s stack directly.

tf.data service, Ray Data, HF streaming, DALI — mostly not the frontier-text answer. tf.data service’s disaggregation result — 31.7× throughput, 26.2× cost reduction for input-bound jobs arXiv:2210.14826, SoCC 2023 [PAPER] — is a striking anchor for “how bad naive co-located dataloading can get,” but a TensorFlow-era, vision/recsys result with no evidence of use in any 2025/2026 frontier text-LLM stack. Ray Data has confirmed production use for batch inference and RL/agentic pipelines, but MegaScale-Data explicitly benchmarks against it as a baseline it beats by up to 3.63×. HF datasets streaming solves a different problem (avoiding local materialization of data you don’t control) than the 10T run’s problem (feeding 100k GPUs from data whose layout you already control). DALI’s reader library is overwhelmingly vision/audio-centric and essentially irrelevant here.

MegaScale-Data — the closest thing to a purpose-built answer, and nobody’s open-sourced it. ByteDance’s MegaScale-Data (EuroSys 2026) is the single most directly relevant production dataloader paper in the corpus: scaling the dataloader for multisource large-foundation-model training, tested to 4,096 GPUs, benchmarked against PyTorch DataLoader, tf.data, Cachew, Ray Data, and Pecan arXiv:2504.09844 [PAPER, 2026]. Its actor-model architecture: Source Loaders hold per-source file-access state as one shared instance rather than one replicated per rank, directly fixing a redundancy that consumed over 70% of preprocessing pipeline memory in ByteDance’s own production systems before the fix; Data Constructors aggregate into batches, sharing data across CP/PP ranks to eliminate redundant copies; a Planner auto-scales based on mixing-ratio changes via offline source-cost clustering plus online moving-average-threshold-triggered rescaling. Two declarative abstractions — DGraph (dataflow graph, sample-lifecycle tracking) and ClientPlaceTree (device-mesh-aware placement) — expose mix(), distribute(), cost(), balance(), broadcast_at(). Results: 4.5× end-to-end throughput and 13.5× CPU memory reduction on multimodal VLM training, up to 3.63× iteration-time speedup versus the best baseline, context-length-dependent gains of 1.71× at 4k and 3.09× at 16k. Fault tolerance is shadow-loader hot failover with differential checkpointing; elastic resharding supports dynamic parallelism changes mid-run. This is the field’s closest answer to “what a dataloader for a 10T-MoE-scale run should look like” — and it is a bespoke, unreleased system, not something to pip install.

Sequence packing: a solved problem, and what remains open

The packing-efficiency conversation in most secondary literature treats padding waste as a live tradeoff to tune. The primary-source numbers say it is essentially a closed problem at any reasonable context length. Best-fit Packing’s two-stage algorithm — segment documents longer than context length L into ≤L chunks, then solve the resulting bin-packing problem via Best-Fit-Decreasing — achieves 0.0024% additional sequences at 2k context and 0.00063% at 8k versus pure concatenation on RefinedWeb (0.0028% on Stack/code at 2k), meaning BFD packing is compute-equivalent to naive concatenation in wasted tokens while eliminating essentially all mid-document truncation arXiv:2404.10830, “Fewer Truncations Improve Language Modeling” [PAPER, ACL 2024]. The algorithmic trick making this tractable at billions-of-documents scale: naive BFD is O(N log N) sort plus O(N log N) pack, but since L ≪ N, the sort reduces to O(N) via counting sort, and tracking bins with an O(L)-leaf tree structure gets the whole pass to O(N log L). OLMo-core’s Optimized-BFD implements this in production, applied per-source-file by default with an optional source_group_size to pack across small source files jointly, reporting a 60% runtime reduction at 1B-document scale versus unoptimized BFD olmo-core.readthedocs.io, NumpyPackedFSLDataset [OFFICIAL-DOCS, 2025/2026]. NeMo offers both first-fit-decreasing (most-optimal, risks clustering same-domain sequences non-randomly) and first-fit-shuffle (slightly less optimal, preserves randomness), defaulting to the latter docs.nvidia.com/nemo-framework [OFFICIAL-DOCS, 2024] — reported gains (fine-tuning context, Llama-7B + Dolly, not pretraining-scale) of up to 10× FLOPs efficiency and 6× wall-clock time with no convergence impact.

Downstream, the payoff from eliminating truncation is larger than intuition suggests: Best-fit Packing reports, at 7B–13B scale, 300–500B tokens, 2k–8k context, gains of +4.7% reading comprehension, +9.3% NLI, +16.8% “context following,” +15.0% HumanEval Pass@100, and up to −58.3% closed-domain hallucination [same source, PAPER]. This correctly reframes the packing question: the remaining design decisions are document-boundary masking and packing order for convergence/randomness reasons, not compute waste, which is functionally zero regardless of method.

The kernel chain that makes packing free at training time is, as of Blackwell, genuinely co-designed rather than bolted on. flash_attn_varlen_func takes cu_seqlens_q/cu_seqlens_k directly — the exact tensor Megatron-Core’s dataloader emits — with causal, sliding-window, and document-boundary mask patterns natively supported. FlexAttention generalizes to arbitrary block-mask and expression-based masks for cases the fixed enum doesn’t cover. FlashAttention-4, as of roughly March 2026, ships a Blackwell-native FlexAttention backend with CuTeDSL score/mask-modifier JIT support, and its scheduling specifically targets the load-imbalance that varlen attention creates on the SM scheduler pytorch.org/blog/flexattention-flashattention-4-fast-and-flexible [TALK/BLOG, Mar 2026] — the 2026 kernel stack treats packed/varlen training as the default case, not an afterthought (Ch15 has the full kernel inventory). This is the THD-format story in practice: total-tokens×hidden layout with cumulative-length boundary metadata riding alongside it, from dataloader emission through attention kernel with no materialization step in between.

A related but distinct idea is Apple’s Dataset Decomposition (VSL curriculum): decompose the corpus into same-length-chunk buckets each drawn from a single document — no cross-document contamination even without explicit masking — then run variable-sequence-length, variable-batch-size training with a curriculum across buckets. An 8k-context 1B model trained at the same compute cost as a 2k-context baseline, up to 6× faster to reach target accuracy on long-context benchmarks arXiv:2405.13226, “Dataset Decomposition” [PAPER, NeurIPS 2024]. Where BFD accepts cross-document packing plus masking, Dataset Decomposition avoids masking entirely by construction — a genuine alternative point in the design space, worth keeping in mind for a curriculum that intentionally staggers sequence length across phases (Ch12).

Shuffle quality at 50T tokens

The core finding across the shuffle literature: shuffle-buffer-to-dataset-size ratio, not absolute buffer size, governs quality, and that ratio’s qualitative behavior is largely scale-invariant as the dataset grows. The CorgiPile line of work finds buffer sizes of roughly 2% of dataset size achieve parity with a full shuffle, performance “noticeably dips” below that, and — the more load-bearing number for a 50T-token corpus — viable buffer sizes as low as 0.25% are achievable for datasets in the hundreds-of-terabytes range, given the right offline/online hybrid design: pre-shuffle shard order offline (cheap, once) and do only local buffer shuffling online arXiv:2309.01640, “Corgi²: A Hybrid Offline-Online Approach to Storage-Aware Data Shuffling for SGD” [PAPER, 2023]. A separate, smaller-scale genomics/DNA-LM study found no shuffle buffer at all costs roughly 4% task performance versus a buffered baseline arXiv:2510.12617 [PAPER, 2025] — cited for the qualitative “buffering unambiguously matters” signal, not as a directly transferable magnitude.

For a 50T-token run, the practical two-level pattern used by essentially every system in this chapter — Megatron’s shuffle-index, Streaming’s machine-level shuffle, Grain’s index-shuffle — is shard-order shuffle (coarse, once, cheap) plus within-shard or within-buffer shuffle (fine, per epoch). None do a literal single-pass global shuffle of 50T tokens, because none need to: the two-level scheme is provably close to full-shuffle quality at a small fraction of the I/O cost. Shuffle quality at 10T-run scale is a solved engineering pattern, not an open research question, provided the implementation does the two-level decomposition rather than approximating a single windowed buffer against the full unshuffled stream.

Pre-tokenized vs on-the-fly: the economics, worked

Offline pre-tokenization to Megatron .bin/.idx format, or the MDS/ArrayRecord equivalents, is the unanimous frontier-2026 default; every system reviewed here assumes pre-tokenized, fixed-width or packed token arrays as the on-disk format, and none tokenize on-the-fly from raw text during the training loop. The economics explain why this is not even a close call. Single-thread tokenizer benchmarks put tiktoken at “2-3× faster than HuggingFace Tokenizers on single strings,” with HF’s Rust-backed “fast” tokenizers closing much of the gap in batch mode; HF’s own docs state under 20 seconds to tokenize 1GB of text on a single CPU core, roughly 50 MB/s/core huggingface.co/docs/datasets [OFFICIAL-DOCS, 2025/2026]. At that rate and a rough 4–5 bytes/token English-text ratio, re-tokenizing a full 50T-token corpus (~200–250TB of raw text) costs on the order of 1,000–1,400 core-hours — trivially parallelizable to well under a day on a few thousand cores. Re-tokenization is essentially never the bottleneck for a curation-pipeline pivot, such as switching tokenizer or vocab mid-project; the actual cost driver for a 50T-token retokenization pass is I/O and orchestration, not CPU-bound BPE throughput. This is the same shape of finding as the classifier-cascade labeling economics: running a 0.5B taxonomy labeler over a 100T-token raw pool costs on the order of 0.2–0.4% of the training run’s own FLOPs, corroborated three independent ways — a direct FLOPs computation, Essential-Web’s disclosed 90,000 MI300X-GPU-hours to label 24T tokens scaled linearly to ~375,000 GPU-hours at 100T, and an independent filtering-pipeline study reporting sub-1% overhead arXiv:2506.14111, Essential-Web v1.0 [PAPER, 2025]. Tokenization and labeling are dollar-denominated, low-six-to-seven-figure line items against a run whose training compute runs into nine figures, not FLOPs-denominated engineering risks. One flagged gap: a circulating “51–72M tokens/sec/node” NeMo Curator tokenization figure could not be traced to a primary NVIDIA source and is excluded here as unverifiable — a documentation hole that doesn’t change the conclusion, since the core-hour derivation above already shows roughly three orders of magnitude of headroom.

The metadata-conditional sampling gap

This is the one place in the input-pipeline stack where the notes are unambiguous about an unfilled gap. Essential-Web v1.0 is the reference example of taxonomy-labeled data the reader is likely picturing: 24T tokens, every document annotated with a 12-category taxonomy (topic, format, complexity, quality) via a distilled 0.5B annotator model, explicitly designed so downstream users can apply SQL-style filters to carve out domain-specific sub-corpora — math, code, STEM, medical — at curation time arXiv:2506.14111 [PAPER, 2025]. Checking every loader reviewed here against that use case: none of Megatron-Core’s BlendedDataset, Megatron-Energon, MosaicML StreamingDataset, or Grain expose a native “filter/reweight by per-example metadata label at load or sample time” API. The pattern is identical everywhere — apply the taxonomy or quality filter once, upstream, at curation, producing separate per-category .bin files, MDS shards, or ArrayRecord files, then use the dataloader’s source-level blend-weight mechanism to control the proportion of already-materialized categories. Metadata-conditional sampling exists, but only at the granularity of “which pre-filtered source file or shard,” never “which individual example, filtered by its metadata field, at read time.”

For a 10T run wanting to dynamically reweight by fine-grained per-document taxonomy labels without a full re-materialization pass — the shape of adaptive curriculum Ch12 gestures toward — this is a genuine build-it-yourself gap, not a configuration choice hiding in some loader’s advanced options. The closest existing precedent is MegaScale-Data’s Planner, architecturally the right shape (offline source-cost clustering, online moving-average-threshold-triggered rescaling) but operating on source-level mixing ratios in the published description, not per-example predicates. Whether any frontier lab runs a genuinely online, per-example metadata-conditional resampling system internally could not be confirmed either way in the underlying research — the online-reweighting research line suggests active interest, but nothing public documents an implementation at this granularity. The practical consequence: treat this as a planned engineering deliverable, most plausibly implemented as pre-materializing a modest number of taxonomy-sliced shard sets per anticipated curriculum stage, then hot-swapping source-level blend weights across those pre-cut sets at each stage boundary, rather than assuming an off-the-shelf loader will grow the capability by the time the run starts.

War stories

Four data points calibrate where the input pipeline has actually hurt real production systems, as opposed to where it theoretically could:

  • torchtitan #650 (“[Multimodal] Adding OBELICS DataLoader”): maintainers of PyTorch’s own reference trainer flag that multimodal loading — image download, decode, resize, pad — is CPU-intensive enough to bottleneck, recommending more DataLoader workers as the first mitigation github.com/pytorch/torchtitan/issues/650 [CODE/GitHub issue] — a concrete acknowledgment that dataloader CPU cost is live for anything beyond pure-text streaming, relevant if the run carries a multimodal component (Ch9).
  • MegaScale-Data’s 3.2×/6.9× per-microbatch FLOP imbalance is a rare quantification of dataloader composition creating compute imbalance via attention’s quadratic scaling — usually discussed only qualitatively. Directly load-bearing for a 10T MoE with heterogeneous per-source document-length distributions layered on expert-parallel sharding, where an unlucky microbatch composition could stall a pipeline stage arXiv:2504.09844 [PAPER, 2026].
  • Megatron-Core issue #907, the non-root-node NFS index-race bug, is small and closed but genuinely instructive: an example of “shared filesystem state assumed but not synchronized across ranks,” a bug class that recurs across Megatron-Core’s dataset stack github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM/issues/907 [CODE/GitHub issue].
  • tf.data service’s 31.7×/26.2× disaggregation result arXiv:2210.14826 [PAPER, SoCC 2023] predates the MoE-at-scale era and comes from a non-LLM context, but is a useful anchor for “how bad naive co-located dataloading can get” — one that seems forgotten as the field’s tooling moved to PyTorch/JAX without an equivalent disaggregation benchmark being republished for text-LLM pretraining. Nobody has re-run this experiment for a 2026-era MoE workload.

Reference input-pipeline architecture that can never be the bottleneck

Synthesizing the above into a concrete architecture for a 10T/200B run on 100k GB200s:

  • Offline pre-tokenization, always. Tokenize the full corpus once to a packed fixed-width or bin-packed format (Megatron .bin/.idx, or MDS/ArrayRecord equivalents), with BFD or OBFD packing applied per curriculum stage at build time. The re-tokenization cost math above (~1,000–1,400 core-hours for a full 50T-token pass) makes it cheap enough to re-run per stage boundary or tokenizer revision without hesitation — there is no reason to ever tokenize live.
  • Native varlen metadata emission, not a separate preprocessing pass. cu_seqlens/THD-format boundary metadata should be produced by the dataloader at __getitem__ time — Megatron-Core’s inter_document_masking is the reference implementation — riding directly into FlashAttention-varlen/FlexAttention kernels with zero materialization step in between (Ch15 covers the kernel side in full).
  • Object-storage-backed cold tier, local-NVMe hot path, decoupled entirely from checkpoint I/O. Megatron-Core’s S3/msc:// support and Streaming’s sharded-disjoint-download model both point the same direction: pre-tokenized shards live in cheap object storage, get pulled once per machine, and cache locally. This subsystem shares no hardware or bandwidth budget with the checkpoint-save path in Ch19 — the ~300 MB/s ingestion requirement and the ~50–200 GB/s checkpoint-drain requirement are different orders of magnitude and should be provisioned as separate systems.
  • Cold-start elimination as a first-class deliverable. prepare_cache.py-style offline index construction, --dataloader-fast-cache-load, and consolidated per-dataset-sequences metadata should be mandatory for any blend with more than a handful of source prefixes — which a 10T-run’s curated mixture certainly has. This is the single most avoidable dataloading cost in this chapter, purely because the mitigation is “precompute it once, offline.”
  • A MegaScale-Data-shaped actor decomposition, if building bespoke. At 100,000 GPUs with heterogeneous EP/TP/PP/CP composition (Ch16), Megatron-Core’s per-rank monolithic index-consumption model is not what any validated system at this scale uses in production — the Source-Loader/Data-Constructor/Planner split, sharing per-source file state across ranks rather than replicating it, is the only architecture in this corpus demonstrated at 4,096 GPUs with the imbalance and redundancy problems already measured and fixed. Treat Megatron-Core’s dataset stack as the correctness-and-packing reference implementation to borrow logic from, not necessarily the throughput architecture to deploy unmodified at full scale.
  • Metadata-conditional sampling solved by pre-materialization, deliberately. Given no OSS loader natively supports per-example metadata filtering at load time, pre-cut a modest, curriculum-informed set of taxonomy-sliced shard groups at curation time and drive runtime adaptivity through source-level blend-weight changes across them — coarser than a true per-example predicate, but built on infrastructure that exists today.
  • Dataloader checkpoint state saved independently of model/optimizer state. Grain’s get_state()/set_state(), Energon’s get_savable_loader(), and Streaming’s native resume all point at the same principle: iteration state (shard position, worker RNG, consumed-sample count) is small and cheap to checkpoint, and should be persisted on its own cadence, decoupled from the far larger model-state checkpoint in Ch19.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

None of this chapter’s findings argue for heroic dataloader engineering. The bandwidth math is unambiguous: at ~300 MB/s aggregate raw-token demand against multi-TB/s-class storage systems already justified by checkpoint traffic, the input pipeline was never going to be the throughput bottleneck, and no amount of over-engineering on that axis buys anything. What the run does need, budgeted as real engineering line items: offline index and packing precomputation kept off the critical path of GPU-hour-billed startup; a microbatch-composition-aware scheduler or MegaScale-Data-style source disaggregation to prevent the FLOP-imbalance bubbles a naive multi-source blend will otherwise inject into the EP/PP pipeline; and an explicit build to close the metadata-conditional-sampling gap if Ch12’s curriculum plan depends on runtime-adaptive, fine-grained mixture reweighting rather than discrete, pre-cut stages. The single highest-leverage decision is architectural, not algorithmic: build on Megatron-Core’s dataset stack (mature, varlen-native, but source-blend-static and not world-size-elastic), or invest in a MegaScale-Data-shaped bespoke loader (elastic, imbalance-aware, but unreleased and therefore a from-scratch build). At 100,000 GPUs, with the heterogeneous parallelism composition Ch16 specifies, the latter is the more defensible bet — but it is a genuine systems-engineering project, not a configuration flag, and should be scoped and staffed as one from day one.

Part IVThe Machine

Chapter 14 · Part IV — The Machine

GB200 NVL72 Ground Truth and the Alternative Silicon Question

Every FLOPs-per-dollar argument anywhere in this book rests on a single contested number: what one GB200 GPU can actually do, sustained, on a sparse-MoE workload. That number turns out to be surprisingly hard to pin down even from primary sources — NVIDIA’s own reference documents, and at least two notes in the research corpus behind this book, silently substitute the wrong SKU’s peak FLOPs into a GB200-labeled calculation. This chapter reconciles that conflict, walks the only rigorous public before/after benchmark of GB200 against H100, and then asks the question the rest of this book has so far assumed away: whether 100,000 GB200-class GPUs is even the right unit of account for a run that will not start moving tokens until well into 2026 or 2027.

The Reconciled Spec Table: What “GB200 NVL72” Actually Means

GB200 NVL72 and HGX B200 are the same reticle-limited Blackwell die — 208B transistors, TSMC 4NP — configured at different power envelopes and cooling classes. NVIDIA’s own side-by-side datasheet (document #3384703, Dec 2024) puts them in one table, with a single footnote that resolves most of the confusion in downstream literature: every Tensor Core figure in the table except FP64 is a 2:4 structured-sparse number, and the dense figure relevant to ordinary training rooflines is exactly half. NVIDIA “Blackwell” datasheet #3384703 [OFFICIAL-DOCS] Dec 2024

NVIDIA “Blackwell” datasheet #3384703 [OFFICIAL-DOCS] Dec 2024; cross-validated against NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 product page and NVIDIA DGX B200 product page [OFFICIAL], fetched 2026-07; independently reconstructed by Glenn K. Lockwood, “NVIDIA B200” [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] Apr 2026.

The correction across every tensor-core precision is a clean, uniform +11.1% (5,000/4,500 = 2,500/2,250 = 10,000/9,000 = 10/9 exactly), but the vector-ALU path (FP32/FP64) only gains 6.7–8.1%. Back-calculating an implied clock from the vector path (≈1.98 GHz HGX vs ≈2.1 GHz NVL72 GB200) predicts only a ~4,770 TFLOPS dense-FP8 GB200 figure if the same ~6% gap applied uniformly to tensor cores — not the actual 5,000. NVIDIA publishes no official clock speed for either SKU, and the gap between the tensor-core ratio and the vector-ALU ratio is unexplained in any source located for this corpus; the likely mechanism is a more aggressive sustained-clock floor specifically under Tensor-Core-saturated load at 1,200W, but this is inference, not a cited fact. [reconciliation, this corpus] 2026-07

This distinction matters practically because two notes in this book’s own research corpus got it wrong in exactly the way a careless reader would: one imported the JAX Scaling Book’s undifferentiated “B200” FLOPs table — unambiguously the HGX dense figure (2.3e15 BF16, 4.5e15 FP8, 9.0e15 FP4, matching HGX within rounding) — into a GB200 NVL72-specific cluster analysis (see Ch16’s critical-batch and EP-viability-threshold derivations, corrected there); the second computed a GB200 MFU of 46.6% for DeepSeek-V3-scale training using the HGX 2,250-TFLOPS BF16 denominator, when the correct GB200 denominator of 2,500 gives 41.9% (see Ch15/Ch16 for the corrected kernel-level MFU discussion). The scaling book’s own text is internally inconsistent on this point — its main FLOPs table uses HGX-class numbers while a worked example three sections later reasons from an 80-TFLOPS FP32 figure that is unambiguously the GB200 number, without ever flagging the SKU switch. Any GB200 FLOPs figure in this book uses the corrected 5,000/2,500/10,000 TFLOPS dense FP8/BF16/FP4 figures above; any number tracing to an un-disambiguated “B200” citation should be treated as suspect. jax-ml/scaling-book, gpus.md [PRIMARY]; reconciliation per this corpus, 2026-07

A third SKU, B100 (700W, a drop-in HGX H100-baseboard-compatible part), exists specifically as a compatibility bridge and is generally excluded from benchmarking literature because of reported yield/throttling problems that kept it from hitting claimed specs — worth knowing only so a stray “B100 TFLOPS” search result is not conflated with either number above. glennklockwood.com [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] Apr 2026

MFU Over Software Releases: The SemiAnalysis H100-vs-GB200 Benchmark

The single most load-bearing empirical document for this chapter is SemiAnalysis’s “H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks — Power, TCO, and Reliability Analysis, Software Improvement Over Time” (Dylan Patel & Daniel Nishball, Aug 20, 2025), built on NVIDIA’s own DGXC benchmarking-team scripts run on NVIDIA’s internal H100 EOS reference cluster, using NeMo Megatron-LM — explicitly not native PyTorch/FSDP2, a methodological gap the authors themselves flag since many end users do not run NeMo Megatron-LM in production. SemiAnalysis, “H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks” [SA-PAID] p.2-3, Aug 2025

The document contains zero theoretical-peak TFLOPS figures for either chip anywhere in its 27 pages — its entire analysis runs in measured tokens/s/GPU, %MFU (without disclosing the FLOPs-peak denominator), $/M-tokens, and J/token. Anyone pairing this article’s MFU% with a peak-FLOPs figure from elsewhere must import that denominator independently; this book uses the corrected 5,000/2,500 TFLOPS GB200 dense FP8/BF16 figures established above wherever one is needed alongside this source’s numbers. [SemiAnalysis, “H100 vs GB200 NVL72”] [SA-PAID] Aug 2025

Capex/TCO baseline. H100 server ≈$190k (all-in ≈$250k); GB200 NVL72 rack ≈$3.1M (all-in ≈$3.9M). Across buyer tiers, GB200’s all-in capex-per-GPU runs 1.6–1.7x H100’s, and combined TCO is ~1.6x — meaning GB200 needs ≥1.6x the effective throughput just to break even on perf/TCO, before reliability is factored in at all. [SemiAnalysis, “H100 vs GB200 NVL72”] [SA-PAID] p.3-4, Aug 2025

Software-only MFU gains on fixed hardware are large and fast. On a static 128×H100 cluster training GPT-3 175B (TP=4, seq 2,048, global batch 500k tokens), BF16 MFU rose 34%→54% and FP8 MFU rose 29.5%→39.5% between Jan and Dec 2024, from CUDA-stack/NCCL software improvements alone — no hardware change. This translated to FP8 pretraining cost falling from 72¢ to 54.2¢ per million tokens ($218k→$162k to replicate GPT-3’s original run) and energy from 3.63 to 2.46 J/token. [SemiAnalysis, “H100 vs GB200 NVL72”] [SA-PAID] p.9-13, Aug 2025

Dense-model weak scaling is close to flat, but MoE is where GB200 actually earns its premium. Llama3-405B FP8 MFU held ~43% and BF16 ~54% across 576–2,304 H100 GPUs; Llama3-70B FP8 MFU instead degraded with scale (38.1%→35.5% from 64 to 2,048 GPUs) while BF16 stayed comparatively flat (54.5%→53.7%) — a reproducible signal that small-scale FP8 MFU should not be extrapolated naively to 100k-GPU regimes. DeepSeek-670B (MoE) landed at just 16.6% BF16 MFU on H100, versus 54.5% for dense Llama3-405B, attributed to massive dispatch/combine communication for token routing (comm volume scales with top-k — routing to 4 experts instead of 1 roughly quadruples traffic). On GB200, DeepSeek-670B throughput went from a barely-compelling 1.3x H100’s tokens/s/GPU in May 2025 to 2.5x by July 2025 (a 1.5x perf/TCO advantage), with NVIDIA’s Dec-2025 roadmap projecting 4.7x. The mechanism is structural: GB200’s 72-GPU NVLink domain makes 64-GPU all-to-all collectives — the exact primitive MoE dispatch/combine needs — run 18x faster than on H100. Llama4 Maverick 400B MoE (17B active) showed an even sharper GB200 advantage in FP8: 2.4x H100’s throughput, a 50% perf/TCO win ($0.061 vs $0.093/M tokens). [SemiAnalysis, “H100 vs GB200 NVL72”] [SA-PAID] p.15-20, Aug 2025

This is the chapter’s central mechanistic finding for a 10T/200B MoE program: GB200’s economic case over H100 is overwhelmingly a sparse-MoE, all-to-all-bound story, not a generic compute-density story. A dense-model buyer gets a much weaker case for GB200 over plain HGX B200 — SemiAnalysis’s own closing verdict on the dense case is blunt: “It appears the vanilla B200 is better performance TCO than GB200 NVL72 for pretraining normal sized models. As model sizes scale or reinforcement learning comes in, GB200 NVL72 will take a lead again.” [SemiAnalysis, “H100 vs GB200 NVL72”] [SA-PAID] p.24-26, Aug 2025

Independently, NVIDIA’s own published Megatron-Bridge performance tables (dense-FP8-peak-normalized, using the corrected 5,000-TFLOPS GB200 denominator) show the same dense-vs-MoE split from a different angle: dense Llama models land in a tight 32–39% MFU band across H100/B200/GB200, while MoE models on the identical hardware land at 9–18%.

NVIDIA, Megatron-Bridge Performance Summary [OFFICIAL-DOCS], accessed Jul 2026, TFLOP/GPU tables normalized against corrected dense-FP8 peaks

The GB200 36.8% Llama3.1-405B figure cross-validates against NVIDIA’s own separately-stated claim of “sustained FP8 training MFU on NVL72: 38–46% of peak” — though this specific range could not be traced to any retrievable primary source (not the SemiAnalysis PDF, not NVIDIA’s own GB200 developer blog) in this corpus’s reconciliation pass, and should be treated as directionally consistent rather than independently confirmed. [OFFICIAL-BLOG claim, unverified] n.d.; see NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 spec reconciliation [reconciliation, this corpus] 2026-07

A separate, from-first-principles reconstruction of DeepSeek-V3’s actual delivered MFU — using the paper’s own 2.664M H800-GPU-hour pretraining figure, 14.8T tokens, 37B active parameters, and the standard 6·N·D FLOPs approximation against H800-SXM’s 1,979-TFLOPS FP8 peak — gives ~17.3% MFU, consistent with the NVIDIA-table pattern above. A widely-circulating “58% MFU for DeepSeek-V3” figure does not survive this reconstruction and appears to be a misreading; it should not be treated as fact in any downstream synthesis. DeepSeek-AI, arXiv:2412.19437 [PAPER] Dec 2024, MFU reconstruction per this corpus

Reliability: NVL72 in the Field

GB200 NVL72’s mean time between interruption (MTBI) is measurably worse than H100’s — 1,000–3,000 GPU-days versus 2,000–5,000 GPU-days — compounded by a much larger blast radius per event: a switch-tray or backplane fault takes down the entire 72-GPU rack, not an 8-GPU node. The mechanical root cause is concrete: each rack’s four copper backplane cartridges connect via Paladin HD connectors rated for only 200 mating cycles before the gold plating must be discarded, and root-causing a backplane signal-integrity fault (“XID 149”) can take hours because the ambiguity spans three possible connector interfaces. The repair-time ladder is steep: compute-tray swap 1–4 hours (job continues on other trays); switch-tray swap 1–4 hours but requires a full-rack drain; backplane cartridge swap 8–24 hours. Operators run 64-of-72 GPUs live with 8 held as hot spares, but the Slurm --segment flag that pins 64-of-72 provides no protection against switch-tray or backplane events, which drain the whole rack regardless — genuine resilience requires provisioning entire spare racks, not just spare GPUs within a rack. [SemiAnalysis, “H100 vs GB200 NVL72”] [SA-PAID] p.20-23, Aug 2025

Pricing in this reliability tax materially changes the GB200 economic case: Llama4 Maverick’s headline 50%-cheaper-per-token GB200 advantage falls to 20% cheaper once the 8-of-72 hot-spare GPUs are charged as opportunity cost, or 30% cheaper if only fully-idle spare racks are charged. [SemiAnalysis, “H100 vs GB200 NVL72”] [SA-PAID] p.24-25, Aug 2025 A separately-documented firmware saga corroborates the operational maturity gap: GB200 NVL72’s early NVLink backplane cabling/sync issues, which affected AWS’s, GCP’s, and Meta’s initial NVL36x2 deployments broadly, were traced to a firmware bug (not hardware) but took 6–7 months to resolve — engineering-sample units shipped November 2024, with a stable firmware v1.3 not released until roughly spring/summer 2025. Top providers (CoreWeave, Oracle) have since moved to rack-level SLAs at 99% uptime rather than component/node-level SLAs, which effectively requires provisioning hot-spare racks as standard practice. SemiAnalysis, “ClusterMAX 2.0” [SA-PAID/ANALYSIS] Nov 2025

For calibration on what “good” reliability looks like at scale even on mature Hopper-class hardware: Meta’s 54-day, 16–24k-GPU Llama3-405B run recorded 466 job interruptions (419 hardware failures), an implied MTBF of ~2,111 H100-days — over 5x better than the sub-400-GPU-day MTBF “gold/silver”-tier neoclouds reportedly deliver on smaller clusters. [SemiAnalysis, “ClusterMAX 2.0”] [SA-PAID] Nov 2025 As of the Aug 2025 benchmark’s own writing, no large-scale frontier training run had yet completed on GB200 NVL72 at all — a dated claim this corpus could not confirm or refute for mid-2026, but worth carrying forward as a live open question: as of late 2025 the largest publicly-verified GB-generation MLPerf submission was ~5,120 GPUs (v5.1, Nov 2025); by MLPerf v6.0 (June 2026, discussed below) that ceiling moved to 8,192 GPUs, but even the newer figure is a ten-minute benchmark, not a months-long pretrain — a materially lower reliability bar than sustaining a 10T-parameter run across 100,000 GPUs for months. [SemiAnalysis, “H100 vs GB200 NVL72”] [SA-PAID] p.20-23, Aug 2025; MLPerf figures per this corpus’s MLPerf-benchmarks note

Power, Cooling, and the Rack Envelope

NVIDIA’s own GB200 NVL72 SuperPOD reference architecture defines one Scalable Unit as 8 racks / 576 GPUs at a stated 1.2 MW TDP — implying ≈150 kW/rack on average, though the document never states this per-rack figure directly, only the SU-level total. NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 SuperPOD RA [DOC], updated Nov 2025 Real deployed racks diverge from this reference by a wide margin: Meta’s Catalina platform, its OCP contribution built on the same NVL72 GPU domain, draws only 93.5 kW/rack at its highest disclosed power tier — genuinely lower than NVIDIA’s own implied figure, the opposite of the “hyperscalers push power density higher” intuition, reflecting Meta’s different rack composition (OCP-form-factor NICs instead of PCIe CEM cards, a doubled 1:1 Grace-CPU-to-GPU ratio that forces the 72-GPU domain across two physical racks rather than one). Hot Chips 2025, Arnold & Bowman, “Meta’s Catalina Platform (NVL72)” [TALK] Aug 24, 2025 Cooling on the GB200 RA is stated only as “hybrid direct-liquid and air cooling” without a quantified split; Meta discloses more granularity — purpose-built Facility Liquid Cooling for liquid-native datacenters plus an “Air Assisted Liquid Cooling” retrofit path for air-cooled facilities, governed by a per-rack Rack Management Controller with a four-tier leak-detection response. [Hot Chips 2025] [TALK] Aug 2025

A genuine planning trap sits in NVIDIA’s own storage-bandwidth guidance: the top-tier (“Enhanced”) per-GPU storage-bandwidth figure in the GB200 RA is ≈217 GBps read per 1,000 GPUs, roughly half the H100 RA’s top-tier (“Best”) ≈488 GBps per 1,000 GPUs — NVIDIA’s own reference sizing dropped materially between generations even though GB200-class checkpoints and datasets are unambiguously larger. This should be read as a signal that NVIDIA’s published sizing assumes dense/lower-active-parameter H100-and-GB200-era workloads, not validated for a 200B-active/10T-total design point whose checkpoint payload scales with total, not active, parameter count (see Ch19 for the full checkpoint-math treatment). [docs.nvidia.com H100 and GB200 SuperPOD RAs, Table 6] [DOC], both updated Nov 2025; derived calculation per this corpus
