Finally, NVIDIA’s own compute-fabric reference tables have a hard, disclosed ceiling: the GB200 RA fully tables a non-blocking two-tier InfiniBand fabric only to 16 Scalable Units / 9,216 GPUs, beyond which the document adds an undertabled third “core” tier and directs the reader to “contact your NVIDIA representative.” Every hyperscaler’s 100k+-GPU fabric — OCI’s 131,072-GPU Supercluster, Meta’s DSF, Azure’s MRC, AWS’s EFA “10P10U,” Google’s Jupiter — is proprietary in-house engineering built past that point, not an extension of a published NVIDIA reference design (see Ch18 for the full fabric-by-fabric treatment). No single vendor-published reference architecture covers the full 100,000-GPU scale this book’s hypothetical run targets. docs.nvidia.com GB200 SuperPOD RA, Table 3 [DOC], updated Nov 2025

Rubin Timing: Build Now or Wait

The build-now-vs-wait-for-Rubin question turns on one architectural fact that is easy to miss in FLOPs-headline comparisons: NVLink6 doubles link count relative to NVLink5, not just per-lane rate.

ml-engineering repo, network/README.md [CODE] 07/2026

Because per-lane rate stays fixed at 25 GBps/lane between NVLink5 and NVLink6, the entire generational jump comes from doubling link count — and because PCIe stays at generation 6 across both Blackwell and Rubin, the NVLink-over-PCIe advantage roughly doubles from ~7x (Hopper, Blackwell) to ~15x (Rubin). For an EP-heavy 10T/200B MoE design, this is directly relevant, not a generic bandwidth footnote: NVLink-domain all-to-all bandwidth is the dominant lever on MoE dispatch/combine MFU (per the DeepSeek-670B 18x-faster-collective finding above), and Rubin doubles the raw headroom on exactly that primitive relative to Blackwell. [ml-engineering repo, network/README.md] [CODE] 07/2026

But Rubin’s own spec sheet is still moving as of its most recent public reveal, in directions that complicate a simple “wait for the bigger number” argument. Rubin GPU: 35 PFLOPS dense FP4 (3.5x GB200), but BF16/TF32 tensor-core width only grows 1.6x over Blackwell — a signal that NVIDIA expects training and inference to keep migrating to FP8/FP4, not that BF16 workloads get a proportionate speedup. HBM4 target is 22 TB/s at 10.8 GT/s pin speed, but memory suppliers are reportedly struggling to hit that above-JEDEC-spec ask — Samsung’s own ISSCC 2026 disclosure shows only ~50% front-end yield on the 1c DRAM node through 2025, and the informed prior is initial shipments land closer to ~20 TB/s. TDP was raised late in the design cycle from 1,800W to 2,300W (Max-P), and VR NVL72 rack TDP reaches 180–220 kW versus GB200/GB300’s 120–140 kW, with NVIDIA’s roadmap explicitly targeting 1 MW/rack within a couple of years. SemiAnalysis, “Vera Rubin – Extreme Co-Design” [SA-PAID] Feb 2026; [SemiAnalysis, “ISSCC 2026 Roundup”] [SA-PAID] Apr 2026

NVIDIA’s own GTC 2026 recap resolves a real ambiguity about whether Rubin forces a co-packaged-optics transition on the reader’s cluster design: it does not, at least not yet. Rubin NVL72 and Rubin Ultra NVL72/144/288 stay entirely copper for scale-up; only Rubin Ultra NVL576 (8 racks bridged) and the following Feynman NVL1152 generation introduce CPO, and only rack-to-rack, not within a rack — copper remains NVIDIA’s medium of choice as long as physically possible, because pushing NVLink SerDes rates further is itself described as “a challenging feat” requiring unproven bidirectional echo cancellation. SemiAnalysis, “Nvidia GTC 2026 recap” [SA-PAID] Mar 2026

The broader supply-side signal cuts against an aggressive wait-for-Rubin strategy: AI workloads (accelerator + host CPU + networking) are modeled to consume just under 60% of TSMC N3 wafer output in 2026, rising to 86% in 2027, with effective N3 utilization projected to exceed 100% of nameplate capacity in H2 2026 — every major accelerator family converges onto the same constrained N3 process, and HBM (already ~3x the wafer-per-bit cost of commodity DRAM, rising to 4x with HBM4) competes directly with server DRAM for the same fab capacity. SemiAnalysis, “The Great AI Silicon Shortage” [SA-PAID] Mar 2026 Read together: GB200 is field-validated, if imperfectly, and its dense-FP8/BF16 peak, HBM capacity, and rack power envelope are settled numbers; Rubin’s headline FP4/HBM4/NVLink6 numbers are directionally compelling — especially for a genuinely EP-heavy MoE, where the NVLink6 link-count doubling matters more than any other single Rubin spec — but its TDP, realized HBM4 bandwidth, and CPO scope were still moving as of its most recent disclosure, and it sits behind the same N3/HBM4 supply constraint squeezing every other 2026–2027 accelerator family. A program with a 2026 procurement deadline is choosing GB200/GB300 not because it is unambiguously superior on paper, but because it is the only option with settled specs and a maturing reliability track record; a program with genuine flexibility to slip into 2027 has a real, quantified reason — not just a hype-cycle reason — to wait for Rubin specifically because of the EP all-to-all doubling. See Ch16 for how this delta would reshape the parallelism math if the build timeline allows it.

The Alternative Silicon Verdicts

AWS Trainium2/3 (Project Rainier). Anthropic’s disclosed Trainium2 chip count roughly doubled in six months — from “nearly half a million” (Oct 2025) to a present-tense “currently over one million Trainium2 chips” (Apr 20, 2026) — with the same announcement confirming an internal Trainium4 designation already inside the AWS-Anthropic contract. Anthropic/Amazon official announcement [OFFICIAL-BLOG] Apr 20, 2026 Trainium2 is 96 GiB HBM3 @ 2.9 TB/s, 1,299 FP8/667 BF16 TFLOPS/chip — a 3.85x raw-FLOPs gap versus GB200 per chip, with SemiAnalysis’s blunt framing that “400k Trainium2 is less raw FLOPs than a 100k GB200 cluster.” The 64-chip Trn2 UltraServer’s 3D torus is asymmetric — the Z-axis carries only 64 GB/s versus 128 GB/s on X/Y, a topological wart invisible in spec-sheet comparisons that a TPU’s symmetric ICI mesh does not share. SemiAnalysis, “Amazon’s AI Self Sufficiency” [ANALYSIS] ~2025 Software maturity is the real open question: NKI only reached Beta→Stable in April 2026 — roughly 16 months after Trainium2’s late-2024 GA — with AWS’s own launch blog admitting hand-tuned kernel development requires expertise “few teams can afford”; PyTorch/XLA is being fully retired the same window for a ground-up native-PyTorch/TorchNeuron rewrite; and the NeuronX Collective Communication Library’s all-to-all gap — the exact primitive MoE dispatch/combine needs — has reportedly been outstanding since roughly 2022. Vendor-disclosed MFU brackets the gap directly: Trainium3 Day-0 PyTorch-native/torch.compile hits only 20–30% BF16 MFU on MoE workloads, rising to ~40% (DeepSeek-670B-class) with hand-written NKI kernels. SemiAnalysis, “AWS Trainium3 Deep Dive” [ANALYSIS] Dec 2025 No public Trainium2/3 MFU number exists for a DeepSeek/Kimi-scale MoE pretraining run — the same disclosure gap this book finds on every non-Nvidia platform.

Google TPU v7 (Ironwood) / TPU 8t. Sundar Pichai stated on the record that Google trains and serves its own frontier models on Ironwood, but no Google source names a specific Gemini generation with citable specificity — even SemiAnalysis’s own deep-dive, while confirming “Gemini 3 was trained entirely on TPUs,” does not specify which generation. Sundar Pichai, X [OFFICIAL X POST] Nov 6, 2025 Google bifurcated training and inference silicon outright five months later: TPU 8t (training, 9,600 chips/pod, 3x Ironwood’s processing power) versus TPU 8i (inference, 1,152-chip pods, 3x on-chip SRAM), announced at Cloud Next, April 22, 2026 — a fast reversal of the “one chip trains and serves” framing Ironwood used at GA five months earlier. Google Cloud Next [OFFICIAL-BLOG/TALK] Apr 22, 2026 SemiAnalysis’s MFU/TCO breakeven math is the sharpest quantified case for TPU economics available: at Nvidia’s ~30% assumed training MFU baseline, Google needs only ~15% MFU (internal) or ~19% (Anthropic’s GCP-priced floor) to match GB300’s cost-per-effective-FLOP, and Anthropic’s realized ~40% MFU implies ~52% lower TCO per effective PFLOP once GCP’s external margin is included. SemiAnalysis, “TPUv7: Google Takes a Swing at the King” [ANALYSIS] Nov 2025 Apple pretrains its cloud foundation model on the latest-generation TPU while serving its largest cloud model on Nvidia GPUs hosted inside Google Cloud — a clean illustration that training-chip and inference-chip choices diverge even within one model family. Apple Machine Learning Research [OFFICIAL-BLOG] Jun 2026 TPU is the most credible non-Nvidia software-maturity story here (MaxText/Pathways, a decade of Google-internal hardening), but it shares the same MLPerf-absence gap as every other non-Nvidia platform (below).

Huawei Ascend / CloudMatrix 384. Ascend 910C is a dual-die package, 128 GB HBM2e @ 6.4 TB/s, built on SMIC 7nm(N+2) — but the binding constraint is national-level HBM supply, not chip design: CXMT’s domestic HBM output caps China at roughly 250,000–300,000 Ascend-910C-equivalent chips/year, well short of Huawei’s own 600,000-unit 2026 target, as the TSMC-fabricated die bank that has cushioned production runs dry within the 2026 window. SemiAnalysis, “Huawei Ascend Production Ramp” [ANALYSIS/CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 2025-2026 The single most concrete named-lab data point on Ascend’s frontier-pretraining readiness remains DeepSeek’s reported R2 experience: urged to shift training to Ascend, the attempt reportedly hit unstable performance, slow interconnect, and immature CANN tooling, and DeepSeek reverted to Nvidia for training while keeping Ascend for inference only. Tom’s Hardware/TrendForce [REPORTED] Aug 2025 The counter-datapoint is Huawei’s own team: PanGu Ultra MoE, 718B parameters trained on 6,000 Ascend NPUs, reached 30.0% MFU after optimization (up from 18.9% at baseline), via simulation-driven architecture co-design tuned specifically to Ascend’s NPU characteristics. arXiv:2505.04519 [PAPER] May 2025 A June 2026 update shows a Huawei-led consortium completing full-parameter post-training of a 1.6T-parameter DeepSeek-V4-Pro on 1,000+ Ascend 910C chips across 1,500+ iterations with zero interruptions — genuine evidence of sustained large-cluster reliability — but the reporting is explicit this “does not demonstrate the chips can pre-train a frontier model from scratch,” and no source discloses where V4-Pro actually pretrained. Tom’s Hardware/Gigazine/Let’s Data Science [REPORTED] Jun 2026 The honest 2026 verdict: post-training-scale reliability on Ascend is now credibly demonstrated by an external-to-Huawei consortium; full pretraining-from-scratch at frontier scale remains undemonstrated outside Huawei’s own PanGu team.

AMD MI355X/MI400. MLPerf Training v6.0 (June 2026) was AMD’s strongest public showing yet and the first built on Primus, its unified training framework, debuting a production MXFP4 recipe: MI355X came within ~5–6% of B200 on Llama3.1-8B pretraining (FP4) and within ~5% on Llama2-70B LoRA fine-tuning, a 3.46x generational leap over MI300X on the LoRA benchmark. AMD did not submit the new, frontier-relevant DeepSeek-V3-671B MoE benchmark that round. AMD/ROCm blogs [OFFICIAL-BLOG] Jun 2026 The OpenAI-AMD deal (6 GW total, first 1 GW of MI450 from H2 2026) never disambiguates training from inference, and given MI450 deployment doesn’t begin until H2 2026, any AMD-trained frontier model is at best mid-training as of this writing — no publicly disclosed frontier-scale pretraining run exists on AMD silicon as of July 2026. OpenAI/AMD official [OFFICIAL-BLOG] Oct 2025

Is 100k GB200 the Right Assumption?

The sharpest answer available in this corpus is a benchmark absence, not a spec-sheet comparison. When MLPerf Training v6.0 (June 16, 2026) added the industry’s first third-party-audited, standardized large-MoE training benchmark — 671B parameters, DeepSeek-V3-shaped — Nvidia was the only platform to submit a result, in its own words “the only platform to submit on every test.” No AMD, Google/TPU, or AWS/Trainium entry exists for this benchmark, even though AMD submitted to other v6.0 categories that same round and Google is nominally a v6.0 participant. CoreWeave’s winning submission trained DeepSeek-V3 671B to target quality on 8,192 GB300 NVL72 GPUs in 2.02 minutes, with per-GPU throughput rising from 1,298 to 1,648 TFLOPS/GPU in the three months between MLPerf rounds — pure software maturation, no hardware change, and a concrete illustration of how much MFU headroom a mature Nvidia software stack still finds generation-over-generation. MLCommons/Nvidia/CoreWeave [BENCHMARK] Jun 16, 2026 Every other non-Nvidia performance number in this chapter — Trainium’s disclosed FLOPS, TPU’s SemiAnalysis-estimated MFU, Ascend’s PanGu MFU — is vendor-reported or single-lab, not independently reproduced under MLCommons audit rules.

This does not mean the alternatives do not work: Anthropic’s >1M-Trainium2-chip and Google’s Gemini-on-TPU deployments are real, at-scale, production counter-evidence, and PanGu Ultra MoE is a genuine, if narrow, existence proof for Ascend. But as of July 2026, Nvidia is the only platform with a third-party-audited large-MoE training result at all, a materially sharper and more falsifiable claim than the generic “GPUs still dominate” framing. The reader’s answer to “is 100k GB200 the right assumption” should be conditional: yes, as the only platform with an audited data point at this workload class, with three qualifications. First, by the time an actual procurement lands, the relevant conversation has already substantially shifted from GB200 to GB300 — GB300 is 1.6x GB200’s speed at identical scale on the MLPerf DeepSeek-V3 benchmark, and most 2026-vintage TCO modeling in this corpus already uses GB300 as its reference SKU. Second, GB200’s own reliability envelope was still described as immature as recently as August 2025, and no source confirms whether a genuinely months-long, 100,000-GPU-scale frontier pretraining run has yet survived on GB200/GB300-class hardware as of mid-2026. Third, Trainium’s scale trajectory (roughly doubling every six months) is real enough that a program with genuine multi-vendor flexibility should not treat single-sourcing Nvidia as risk-free — it is simply the option with an audited number behind it.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

  • Use the corrected dense-FP8 peak. 5,000 TFLOPS/GPU (GB200 NVL72), not the frequently-conflated 4,500-TFLOPS HGX B200 figure, as the denominator for every MFU and critical-batch calculation in this book; see Ch16 for the corrected downstream thresholds.
  • Plan MFU around the MoE band, not the dense band. Vendor-published, apples-to-apples data (NVIDIA’s own Megatron-Bridge tables) puts MoE MFU at 9–18% versus 32–39% for dense, on identical hardware — and a genuinely 2%-active 10T/200B design is sparser than any measured data point in this corpus (DeepSeek-V3 at 5.5% active gets 18%), so budget toward the low end, not the middle, absent a specific reason to expect better.
  • Price the reliability tax into TCO, not just into contingency. GB200’s headline perf/TCO advantage over H100 for MoE workloads (up to 50% cheaper) compresses to 20–30% once hot-spare-rack opportunity cost is charged; budget rack-level, not node-level, SLAs and provision accordingly (see Ch19).
  • Treat NVLink6/Rubin as a genuine, quantified alternative if the timeline allows it — the link-count doubling (18→36 links) is specifically valuable for an EP-heavy MoE’s all-to-all traffic, not a generic speed bump — but recognize that Rubin’s TDP, realized HBM4 bandwidth, and CPO scope were all still moving targets as of its most recent public disclosure, and that the same N3/HBM4 supply constraint squeezing GB200/GB300 procurement squeezes Rubin too.
  • Do not budget on an audited non-Nvidia alternative as a hedge. As of MLPerf Training v6.0 (June 2026), no platform besides Nvidia has submitted an independently-audited large-MoE training result; every alternative-silicon number in a vendor’s own favor should be read as the vendor’s best case, not a validated floor.

Chapter 15 · Part IV — The Machine

Kernels for Blackwell: FlashAttention-4, Grouped GEMM, and the Fusion Inventory

Every rooflines-and-MFU argument in this book (Ch16, Ch21) presumes a kernel layer that actually delivers the FLOPs a spec sheet promises. That layer turned over completely between Hopper and Blackwell — not incrementally, but architecturally: Blackwell moved matrix-multiply issuance from warp-scope to single-thread CTA-scope, introduced a new on-chip memory space that must be managed by hand, and shifted the attention kernel’s binding constraint from tensor-core FLOPs to shared-memory bandwidth and exponential-unit throughput. As of July 2026 the software response to that shift is real, fast-moving, and unevenly finished: FlashAttention-4 exists and is fast, but its FP8 path is forward-only; DeepGEMM already speaks Blackwell’s native microscaling format while attention kernels do not; and the single hardest unsolved problem in the entire stack — grouped GEMM at the small-M shapes a 10T-total/200B-active MoE actually produces — has a documented 10x gap between naive and hand-tuned code, with the best-known public technique still absent from every major library’s default path. This chapter inventories that layer precisely enough to plan a build-or-fund list against it.

The Blackwell Programming Model: tcgen05, TMEM, and 2-CTA MMA

The single biggest conceptual change for a kernel author moving from Hopper to Blackwell is where matrix-multiply issuance lives. Hopper’s WGMMA instruction is warpgroup-scoped — CuTe’s own layout algebra expresses this as ThrID = Layout<_128>, 128 cooperating threads jointly issuing one WGMMA — so a kernel author reasons about pieces of the MMA result living in each thread’s registers. Blackwell’s tcgen05.mma collapses that to ThrID = Layout<_1>: a single thread issues the instruction on behalf of the entire CTA. SemiAnalysis, “Dissecting NVIDIA Blackwell Tensor Cores” [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 2025–2026, corroborated by NVIDIA CUTLASS tcgen05 programming docs [OFFICIAL-DOCS] 2026. The practical consequence is that threads no longer implicitly own accumulator fragments in registers; the accumulator (and optionally operand A) now lives in Tensor Memory (TMEM), a new 256KB-per-SM on-chip space wired directly to the tensor cores that software must explicitly allocate and manage — there is no implicit register-file fallback the way there was on Hopper and earlier. NVIDIA CUTLASS tcgen05_programming.html [OFFICIAL-DOCS] 2026

The second load-bearing primitive is 2-CTA (2-SM) cooperation, CUTLASS’s “cluster launch” mechanism: two adjacent CTAs in the same cluster jointly execute one tcgen05.mma via cta_group::2 in PTX (2CTA in SASS), with only the leader CTA issuing the instruction while both CTAs’ TMEM and SMEM remain addressable within the pair. Microbenchmarks show near-perfect weak scaling — doubling compute resources roughly doubles throughput — and for SMEM-bound shapes with N<128, splitting operand-B SMEM bandwidth demand across two SMs delivers over 2x speedup, not just 2x compute. SemiAnalysis, “Dissecting NVIDIA Blackwell Tensor Cores” [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 2025–2026 A companion primitive, TMA multicast, lets a single Tensor Memory Accelerator load push data into multiple CTAs’ shared memory simultaneously via a hardware CTA bitmask, coalesced by the L2 Request Coalescer to approach the ideal 1/cluster_size L2-bytes-per-SMEM-byte ratio. [same source] [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 2025–2026 And because clusters are guaranteed co-scheduled within a GPC, Blackwell adds Cluster Launch Control (CLC): dynamic, persistent-kernel work-stealing across SMs, so an SM that finishes its assigned tile early can grab another SM’s remaining work rather than idling. [SemiAnalysis; NVIDIA CUTLASS docs] [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY][OFFICIAL-DOCS] 2025–2026

CLC is worth flagging as more than a footnote. The identical “persistent kernel plus atomic-counter-driven work-stealing” pattern recurs independently in three unrelated places in this chapter — FA4’s tile scheduler, the state-of-the-art NVFP4 grouped-GEMM worklog below, and Blackwell’s own hardware-level CLC feature — which is convergent evidence that irregular-workload load balancing (varlen attention, small-M MoE) is becoming a first-class, hardware-assisted primitive on this generation of silicon rather than a software workaround layered on top of a fixed hardware model, the way Hopper-era kernels solved the same problem with clever static tile-size heuristics. [synthesis across primary sources cited below] [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 2026

One further architectural wrinkle matters for anyone assuming a single Blackwell code path: consumer/workstation Blackwell (SM120/SM12x) has a tensor-core programming model closer to Ampere’s mma.sync than to datacenter SM100’s tcgen05, forcing Triton, CUTLASS, FA4, and FlashMLA to each maintain three genuinely different code paths — Ampere-style, Hopper WGMMA, Blackwell tcgen05 — rather than one path with minor per-generation tweaks. A 10T/200B design living entirely on GB200/SM100 datacenter parts is insulated from this, but it explains why consumer-Blackwell kernel maturity (flagged below) lags so far behind datacenter Blackwell despite sharing an architecture name. [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 2026

FlashAttention-4: Redesigning Around Asymmetric Scaling

FlashAttention-4 (Zadouri, Hoehnerbach, Shah, Liu, Thakkar, Dao, “Algorithm and Kernel Pipelining Co-Design for Asymmetric Hardware Scaling”) is not FA3 ported to Blackwell; it is a redesign forced by one hardware fact. Going from H100 to B200, BF16 tensor-core throughput scales 2.25x (1 to 2.25 PFLOPS dense on the HGX B200 SKU), while shared-memory bandwidth and the count of Special Function Units executing softmax’s exp stay essentially flat. arXiv:2603.05451 [PAPER] Mar 2026 The paper’s own per-SM roofline (tile M=N=D=128) makes the shift concrete: in the forward pass, tensor cores need roughly 1,024 cycles and the exponential unit is also roughly 1,024 cycles — a genuine co-bottleneck — against 768 cycles of SMEM traffic. In the backward pass, which needs five MMAs instead of two, SMEM traffic balloons to roughly 3,328 cycles against 2,560 cycles of MMA compute, making shared-memory bandwidth the dominant backward-pass bottleneck by 25–60% depending on configuration. arXiv:2603.05451 [PAPER] Mar 2026; roofline independently corroborated by Colfax Research, “FlashAttention-4” technical summary [BLOG] 2026

FA4’s forward pipeline uses distinctly more warp roles than FA3’s ping-pong scheme: a load warp driving TMA transfers, an MMA warp issuing async tcgen05 matmuls, two 4-warp softmax warpgroups deliberately synchronized so they never compute exponentials simultaneously (spreading the SFU-plus-emulated-FMA exp load across time), a dedicated correction warpgroup applying rescaling off the critical path, and one to two epilogue warps — with one tile’s MMA overlapping the adjacent tile’s softmax. Modal, “Reverse-Engineering FlashAttention-4” [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] Sept 2025; confirmed by arXiv:2603.05451 [PAPER] Mar 2026

Two specific micro-optimizations are worth naming because they are genuinely novel, not incremental. First, FA4 decomposes 2^x via Cody-Waite range reduction and approximates the fractional part with a degree-3 polynomial in Horner form (coefficients 1.0 + 0.6951x + 0.2276x² + 0.0771x³, fit via Sollya for minimal relative error), running this software path on the underused FMA datapath in parallel with the hardware MUFU.EX2 instruction — splitting the softmax exponential workload across both rather than letting one displace the other. Second, conditional softmax rescaling: standard FlashAttention rescales the running output accumulator every time a new K-block potentially raises the row max; FA4 defers correction until the new max exceeds the old one by more than a tunable threshold τ (the paper cites τ ≈ log₂(256) = 8.0 in one configuration), reported to cut rescale operations by roughly 10x. arXiv:2603.05451 [PAPER] Mar 2026, the rescaling mechanism independently corroborated by SemiAnalysis’s live coverage of Tri Dao’s Hot Chips 2025 talk [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] Aug 2025

The performance headline is consistent across primary and secondary sources: 1,605–1,613 TFLOPS/s BF16, 71% of B200’s ~2,250 TFLOPS BF16 dense peak (the HGX B200 SKU — see the SKU caveat below), 1.1–1.3x faster than cuDNN 9.13, and 2.1–2.7x faster than Triton, with the largest wins at 4K+ sequence lengths. arXiv:2603.05451 [PAPER] Mar 2026 One conflation worth flagging for accuracy: a commonly repeated “~1.2 PFLOPS/s on B200” figure is actually FA3’s FP8 number on H100 (arXiv:2407.08608, July 2024), not an FA4/B200 figure — FA4’s own reported BF16 peak is ~1.6 PFLOPS, not 1.2. arXiv:2407.08608 [PAPER] Jul 2024 vs arXiv:2603.05451 [PAPER] Mar 2026

The “71% of B200’s peak” figure itself carries the same SKU trap this book flags repeatedly (Ch8, Ch14, Ch16): the FA4 paper’s denominator is the HGX B200 dense BF16 peak of 2,250 TFLOPS, not GB200 NVL72’s dense BF16 peak of 2,500 TFLOPS established as authoritative in this corpus’s reconciliation. [notes/30-gb200-nvl72-peak-flops-reconciliation.md, this corpus] Against the GB200 NVL72 denominator, FA4’s measured 1,605–1,613 TFLOPS/s would read as 64.2–64.5% of peak rather than 71% — the kernel’s absolute throughput is unchanged, but any reader porting “71%” into a GB200-denominated roofline calculation elsewhere in this book should use the corrected figure. Note that this specific percentage is this chapter’s own arithmetic, not a figure stated in any note: the reconciliation note performs the analogous HGX-to-GB200 correction explicitly only for the Megatron-Core MFU figure below, and does not itself flag FA4’s “71%” headline as SKU-ambiguous. The division (1,605–1,613 ÷ 2,500) is straightforward given both inputs are independently sourced, but it is a derived recomputation applied here by analogy, not a quoted “64.2–64.5%” from the corpus.

pytorch.org/blog/flexattention-flashattention-4 [OFFICIAL-BLOG] 2026; arXiv:2603.05451 [PAPER] Mar 2026; Hazy Research blog, ThunderKittens 2.0 [OFFICIAL-BLOG] Feb 2026

FA4’s Backward Pass and the July 2026 Repo-Verified Feature Matrix

Because papers lag shipped code, the state of FA4 as actually usable in a training stack is best established by reading the repository directly rather than the paper. A direct inspection of Dao-AILab/flash-attention (flash_attn/cute/, HEAD 0816ef1, pulled 2026-07-13) against the March 2026 paper shows real, dated progress. github.com/Dao-AILab/flash-attention [CODE] verified 2026-07-13

Backward pass: fully present as of the paper, a 5-MMA chain (recompute S, compute dP, dV, dS, then dQ/dK) bottlenecked by SMEM bandwidth rather than compute, with 2-CTA mode as the main backward-specific optimization and a deterministic mode reaching 85–90% of nondeterministic throughput via semaphore-locked, shortest-processing-time-first accumulation scheduling. arXiv:2603.05451 [PAPER] Mar 2026 This is a genuinely fast maturation: Modal’s engineers, reverse-engineering the kernel in September 2025, wrote that “a backwards version of this kernel is not available, but is expected imminently” — meaning FA4 went from no backward pass at all to a full, deterministic-capable backward in roughly five to six months. Modal, “Reverse-Engineering FlashAttention-4” [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] Sept 2025 vs arXiv:2603.05451 [PAPER] Mar 2026

The July 2026 repository confirms varlen (cu_seqlens_q/k, seqused_q/k, a SeqlenInfo dataclass precomputing tile offsets once per batch element — this is the THD/packed-sequence path Ch13’s dataloading architecture depends on), full GQA via pack_gqa.py, causal/local/sliding-window and arbitrary score/mask modifiers unified through a _resolve_causal_local_window() helper, first-class block-sparsity (block_sparsity.py), and dedicated sparse-MLA forward and backward kernels for DeepSeek V4’s attention variant. The sparse-MLA backward landed via release fa4-v4.0.0.beta19, published 2026-06-24 — roughly two months after DeepSeek-V4’s April 2026 release, and about three weeks before this chapter’s research date. Head-dim-256 gets its own hand-tuned 2-CTA forward/backward path rather than falling through the generic kernel. github.com/Dao-AILab/flash-attention [CODE] verified 2026-07-13; release dates via GitHub Releases API [CODE] 2026-07-13

Two gaps remain load-bearing for a training-time precision decision. FP8 is explicitly forward-only: the code raises NotImplementedError("FA4 CuTe FP8 backward is not supported yet (forward-only).") whenever is_fp8 and requires_grad, and further asserts FP8 is SM100-only — there is no FP8 attention training path on Blackwell in FA4 CuTe as of July 2026. And a repository-wide grep for “mxfp8”, “nvfp4”, or “block_scale” across flash_attn/cute/*.py returns zero hits: FA4’s FP8 support is plain per-head-batch descale scalars (q_descale/k_descale/v_descale), not Blackwell’s native 32-element microscaling format. github.com/Dao-AILab/flash-attention [CODE] verified 2026-07-13 This is a real asymmetry within the same 2026 Blackwell kernel stack: DeepGEMM (below) already speaks native UE8M0 block-scaling for MoE GEMM, while FA4 attention does not — the MoE-GEMM and attention halves of a Blackwell-native precision stack are on different format-maturity curves, a fact that only becomes visible by diffing the two codebases directly rather than reading either project’s documentation in isolation. Release cadence is roughly weekly (beta8 through beta21, 2026-04-08 through 2026-07-08 — thirteen releases in thirteen weeks), meaning any “current status” claim in this chapter should be read as dated precisely and likely to be stale within weeks of publication. GitHub Releases API [CODE] verified 2026-07-13 The repository also depends on quack.compile_utils, meaning FA4 and Quack (Dao-AILab’s separate CuTe-DSL library for memory-bound ops — §The Fusion Inventory below) share build infrastructure and their release cadences correlate.

cuDNN SDPA: Closing the Gap From Behind

cuDNN was first to ship any performant Blackwell attention kernel, ahead of both FA3-for-Blackwell (which never existed — WGMMA does not exist on SM100) and FA4, during a 2025 window the field describes as having “a lot of flux in attention implementations for new Blackwell hardware.” [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 2026 By FA4’s own account, NVIDIA and the FA4 authors collaborated starting with cuDNN 9.13/9.14 to fold FA4’s techniques back into cuDNN, and “the latest cuDNN 9.19.1.2 now offers similar performance to FA4.” arXiv:2603.05451 [PAPER] Mar 2026 cuDNN’s own 9.19-line release notes document a unified SDPA-forward backend descriptor, GPT-OSS-style attention-sink support for forward and backward on all SM80+ GPUs, and — the detail that should give a frontier-run planner pause — a fix for MXFP8 SDPA-backward NaN/mismatch bugs specifically on Blackwell. NVIDIA cuDNN release notes [OFFICIAL-DOCS] 2026

The net kernel-selection calculus for 2026: cuDNN and FA4 are close enough in raw BF16 dense/causal throughput (FA4 still leads by roughly 10–30% forward) that the practical decision is more about integration convenience — cuDNN ships in every NGC container, FA4 CuTe is a separate, fast-moving pip package on a weekly release cycle — than about a large performance gap. But the recency of the MXFP8 backward correctness fix implies that code path is young and under-exercised at production scale; treating “cuDNN supports MXFP8 attention” as a settled checkbox for a $2B run’s precision recipe would be premature.

Triton on Blackwell: Why Gluon Exists

Triton can emit tcgen05 instructions — generic tcgen05.cp lowering and matrix descriptors landed by early 2026 — but multiple independent sources converge on the same conclusion: matching hand-tuned or CuTe-DSL-quality Blackwell attention codegen from Triton’s high-level, automatically-pipelined abstractions failed. Blackwell’s single-thread MMA issuance (with its leader-election requirement), multi-barrier pipelining, and TMEM allocation-with-contention-handling are exactly the low-level, hard-to-discover patterns a block-level compiler struggles to infer automatically. patricktoulme.substack.com, “CuTile on Blackwell: NVIDIA’s Compiler Moat Is Already Built” [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 2026

The Triton team’s response was Gluon: a lower-level, Triton-adjacent frontend where the programmer manually allocates SMEM buffers, places barriers, and issues async-copy and tcgen05-MMA instructions explicitly — trading Triton’s automatic pipelining for CuTe-DSL-like explicit control while staying inside the Triton/MLIR toolchain. Gluon tutorials, including a dedicated 06-tcgen05.py walkthrough and a documented 2-CTA blocked-scaled-matmul example, live directly in the Triton repository, and a third-party worked example (“Inside a Blackwell 2-CTA GEMM: A Gluon Kernel Tour”) demonstrates the pattern is reproducible outside NVIDIA and Dao-AILab, not an internal-only workaround. triton-lang/triton, Gluon tutorials [CODE]; tanmaypatil.com Blackwell 2-CTA Gluon GEMM post [BLOG] 2026 The severity here is worth stating plainly: this was not an incremental compiler improvement but an entirely new, lower-level frontend spun up specifically because high-level Triton abstractions could not close the gap — a stronger statement about tile-based-compiler limits on Blackwell than “Triton is a bit behind cuDNN.”

GPU MODE’s own lecture catalog corroborates this framing from the authors’ side: Lecture 104, “Gluon: Tile-Based GPU Programming with Low-Level Control” (Peter Bell, Mario Lezcano, Keren Zhou), is the primary talk from Gluon’s own authors. github.com/gpu-mode/lectures [CODE] 2026 It is worth a direct correction here to a common assumption: GPU MODE’s roughly 106-lecture catalog as of mid-2026 has almost no Blackwell/DeepGEMM-specific frontier-lab kernel disclosures — Tri Dao’s FA4 talk was delivered at Hot Chips 2025, not GPU MODE, and no dedicated DeepGEMM lecture exists by title search. GPU MODE is the stronger source for kernel-authoring pedagogy — CuTe, Triton, Gluon fundamentals — while frontier-lab kernel disclosures themselves cluster at Hot Chips and in papers and repos directly. The pedagogically load-bearing lectures for a reader building this stack are Lecture 57 (“CuTe,” Cris Cecka, the conceptual on-ramp), Lecture 86 (“Introduction to CuTeDSL,” Vicki Wang, explicitly framed as onboarding for an NVIDIA NVFP4/Blackwell kernel-writing competition), Lecture 103 (“Fundamentals of CuTe Layout Algebra,” Jack Carlisle and Jay Shah — Shah is a Colfax co-author of the FA3 and CUTLASS-Blackwell tutorial material cited above), Lecture 84 (“Numerics and AI,” Paulius Micikevicius, NVIDIA’s FP8/Transformer Engine architect), and Lecture 23 (“Tensor Cores,” Vijay Thakkar and Pradeep Ramani, both NVIDIA CUTLASS-team). github.com/gpu-mode/lectures [CODE] 2026

CuTe-DSL Adoption, ThunderKittens, and the Tile-Language Field

CUTLASS 4.x’s CuTe-DSL — a Python authoring surface for the primitives above — is now the primary kernel-authoring layer for Blackwell at NVIDIA and Dao-AILab: FlashAttention-4 itself is written in CuTe-DSL rather than CUTLASS C++, DeepGEMM explicitly builds on CUTLASS/CuTe concepts, and Dao-AILab’s Quack library reimplements RMSNorm, softmax, LayerNorm, and cross-entropy purely in CuTe-DSL, claiming 33–50% faster than torch.compile plus Liger on H100 at roughly 3TB/s memory throughput — near speed-of-light for memory-bound kernels. github.com/Dao-AILab/quack [CODE] Jul 2025 Adoption goes further than expert tooling: PyTorch and NVIDIA shipped a fourth TorchInductor autotuning GEMM backend built on CuTe-DSL in 2026, alongside Triton, CUTLASS C++, and cuBLAS, beating Triton by up to 1.73x for BF16, 1.78x for MXFP8, and 1.6x for NVFP4 on B200 in decode-shaped (small-M) GEMMs, and covering grouped_mm directly. pytorch.org/blog/gemms-torchinductor-cutedsl-backend [OFFICIAL-BLOG] 2026 The underappreciated implication: a plain torch.compile(mode="max-autotune") call on current PyTorch can already silently select a CuTe-DSL-compiled kernel over both Triton and hand-written CUTLASS C++ for an ordinary matmul on B200, without the user ever touching CuTe-DSL syntax. Status, per NVIDIA’s own docs: public beta, targeted to graduate by end of summer 2026 — any “production-ready” framing should be treated as premature by NVIDIA’s own stated timeline as of this writing. NVIDIA CUTLASS docs [OFFICIAL-DOCS] 2026

Stanford’s ThunderKittens, an embedded-C++ tile-primitive DSL, shipped Blackwell support in March 2025 and a major 2.0 rewrite in February 2026 adding MXFP8/NVFP4 support, Cluster Launch Control scheduling, and explicit TMEM control; GEMM kernels are reported to “surpass or match cuBLAS” on B200 across BF16/MXFP8/NVFP4, with attention forward and backward running near cuDNN speed, “up to 2x faster than FA3 on H100” — note this last figure compares against FA3-on-H100, not FA4-on-B200. Hazy Research blog, ThunderKittens 2.0 [OFFICIAL-BLOG] Feb 2026 The 2.0 release’s engineering post-mortems are unusually concrete and worth citing for their specificity: removing unnecessary memory-fence instructions recovered ~20 TFLOP/s in GEMM kernels; discovering that tcgen05.cp pipelines implicitly with tcgen05.mma with no explicit sync needed recovered ~500 TFLOP/s (~10%) for NVFP4; and replacing manual leader-thread-selection loops with PTX’s elect.sync instruction removed serialization and improved small-GEMM throughput by up to 10%. [Hazy Research blog] [OFFICIAL-BLOG] Feb 2026 These are exactly the kind of micro-optimizations invisible in a paper abstract that determine whether a hand-written kernel hits 80% or 95% of a competitor’s throughput.

Two further tile-language efforts round out the field and are easy to conflate. cuTile, NVIDIA’s own tile-based Python DSL shipped in CUDA 13.1/13.2, has its tileiras compiler backend supporting only Blackwell and Ampere/Ada as of mid-2026 — Hopper support is explicitly deferred to “coming versions.” Tilus (NVIDIA/tilus), a separate Triton-inspired language emphasizing explicit SMEM and register control closer to Gluon’s philosophy than Triton’s, spans both Hopper and Blackwell today with step-by-step tutorials reaching cuBLAS-level performance. NVIDIA cuTile blog [OFFICIAL-BLOG]; github.com/NVIDIA/tilus [CODE] 2026 The practical consequence for a shop that must span an H100/H200 fleet alongside a GB200 fleet simultaneously: cuTile currently cannot, Tilus and CuTe-DSL both can. On the JAX side, Pallas’s Mosaic-GPU backend documents Blackwell matmul support — warp specialization, tiled epilogues, collective 2-CTA matmul, persistent kernels with CLC — meaning JAX is not architecturally blocked from the same primitives CUTLASS/CuTe-DSL expose. No independent benchmark comparing Mosaic-GPU-authored Blackwell kernels against CUTLASS/CuTe-DSL or Triton/Gluon equivalents was found in this corpus; treat Mosaic-GPU’s relative maturity as a genuine open question for a JAX-based stack (see Ch17). JAX Pallas Blackwell matmul docs [OFFICIAL-DOCS] 2026

Grouped GEMM at MoE Expert Shapes: The Small-M Problem

This is the single most consequential kernel gap for a 200B-active MoE design, and it is worth stating the mechanism precisely. A grouped GEMM for MoE shares K (hidden size) and N (expert intermediate size) across experts but gives each expert a different, data-dependent M — the number of tokens actually routed to it. At the expert-parallelism degree a 10T-total model needs, per-GPU per-expert M can be small, and small-M GEMMs are architecturally penalized on Blackwell specifically: TMEM occupancy caps at one CTA per SM once the accumulator plus scale-factor tensors fill the 512-column TMEM partition, so a naive per-expert-kernel-launch approach burns enormous time in launch and synchronization overhead relative to actual compute.

A detailed public worklog on NVFP4 grouped-GEMM optimization on B200 is unusually concrete about the resulting headroom. A reference/naive implementation runs at 238µs geomean across four representative expert shapes; a hand-optimized version using a persistent kernel with a single flattened global work queue (atomic-counter-based tile scheduling across all experts, replacing per-expert launches), 4-stage warp-specialized pipelining (up from 2), TMA cluster multicast for operand-A reuse (up to 4x traffic reduction on memory-bound shapes), and problem-aware L2 cache-eviction hints reaches 23.8µs geomean — roughly 10x — with individual shapes improving 7.4x to 13.9x. Even this optimized kernel only achieves ~50% average / ~80% steady-state tensor-core utilization and 12.4% SM occupancy (the TMEM-fits-one-CTA constraint again), and the author explicitly notes the best-known public submissions (~16µs geomean) go further via cooperative cta_group::2 MMA — 2-CTA-shared TMEM, amortizing per-CTA overhead — and an M-major epilogue output layout enabling 256-bit vectorized stores, techniques the author’s own kernel does not implement. mufeezamjad.com, NVFP4 grouped-GEMM worklog [BLOG][CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 2026 The competition-versus-production-library provenance of that ~16µs figure is itself unresolved — the source’s language (“faster submissions”) implies a competitive kernel-optimization context rather than a shipped default, meaning a training team may need to reimplement the technique rather than pip install it.

The takeaway for a 10T/200B design is unambiguous: off-the-shelf grouped GEMM at production MoE expert-parallelism degrees on Blackwell is closer to “a documented 10x of headroom exists between naive and hand-tuned” than “solved,” and the state-of-the-art technique — 2-CTA cooperative MMA specifically for grouped GEMM — is not in any public library’s default path as of mid-2026.

DeepGEMM (deepseek-ai/DeepGEMM, inspected directly) supports SM90 and SM100; SM90 grouped and dense GEMM is NT-layout only, while SM100 supports all four layouts (NT/TN/NN/TT) and requires LHS scaling factors in packed UE8M0 format — DeepGEMM already speaks Blackwell’s native block-scaling format for FP8, the asymmetry with FA4 attention noted above. Two grouped-GEMM variants ship: a contiguous layout (tokens sorted by expert, M-axis grouped) for training-style large-batch MoE, and a masked layout for inference-decode-style variable-length CUDA-graph use. Cited peak is 1,550 TFLOPS on H800 (Apr 2025); a Blackwell-native peak figure for DeepGEMM itself was not found in this corpus’s sources — a third-party guide’s claim of “another 1.5–2x on top of H200” is not DeepGEMM’s own published number and should be treated as an estimate, not a confirmed figure. The 2026-era changelog adds “Mega MoE” (overlapped NVLink communication fused with grouped GEMM — the mechanism behind DeepSeek-V4’s wave-based EP overlap discussed in Ch4), an FP4 indexer kernel, and FP8×FP4 mixed-precision GEMM. github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepGEMM [CODE] verified 2026-07-13

CUTLASS ships dedicated grouped-GEMM examples (75_blackwell_grouped_gemm, 75_blackwell_grouped_gemm_block_scaled.cu) claiming up to 5x throughput over H200-FP16 using Blackwell FP4. But this maturity is uneven across Blackwell SKUs in a way worth flagging for anyone tempted to treat “CUTLASS supports it” as a checkbox: consumer/workstation Blackwell (SM120/SM121, RTX Pro 6000, DGX Spark) has documented correctness bugs — a flashinfer-ai issue reports CUTLASS Grouped Block-Scaled GEMM producing invalid output on SM120 NVFP4 MoE, and a vLLM issue confirms CUTLASS 4.5 lacks a CollectiveBuilder specialization for tensor/token-scaled FP8 grouped GEMM on SM_120/SM_121 entirely. This is a distraction for a datacenter-only GB200 design (SM100 throughout), but it is evidence CUTLASS’s grouped-GEMM code is newer and less battle-tested than its dense-GEMM code even in places it nominally “supports” a target. github.com/flashinfer-ai/flashinfer issues #2723, #3628 [CODE/ISSUE] 2026

Transformer Engine, per Megatron-Core’s own 2026-Q2 roadmap, lists “GroupedGEMM and Gradient Accumulation Fusion,” “GroupGEMM + SwiGLU + quantize fused MLP,” and “grouped GEMM BF16/MXFP8 support with CUDA Graph compatibility” as completed items for the June 2026 (26.06) Megatron-Core release, alongside FP8/MXFP8 grouped-GEMM support and FP8 weights with BF16 optimizer states — confirming TE’s grouped-GEMM plus fused-MLP path is production-integrated into the reference training framework as a pluggable backend alongside DeepGEMM. github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM issue #4815 [OFFICIAL-DOCS/CODE] 2026

github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepGEMM [CODE]; github.com/flashinfer-ai/flashinfer issues [CODE/ISSUE]; github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM issue #4815 [OFFICIAL-DOCS/CODE]; mufeezamjad.com [BLOG] — all 2026

Megatron-Core’s own MoE decomposition — route, permute, dispatch (all-to-all across EP ranks), grouped GEMM, combine, unpermute — surfaces a related, sobering number about where time actually goes in MoE training. The DeepEP token dispatcher fuses permute into dispatch and unpermute into combine as single fused kernels, and a new HybridEP dispatcher targets GB200 specifically. Megatron-Core MoE user guide [OFFICIAL-DOCS] 2026 But even after this optimization, the Megatron-Core MoE technical report states that routing and permutation overhead adds ~9% to layer execution time, and — the more consequential figure — GEMMs occupy under 50% of execution time for DeepSeek-V3-scale MoE training, versus roughly 70% for a dense Llama-3-405B run: for current-generation MoE stacks, roughly half of step time is router, permute, communication, and other non-FLOP-bearing kernel overhead, not the matmuls themselves. arXiv:2603.07685 [PAPER] Mar 2026 The same report gives 1,233 and 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU sustained on GB300 and GB200 respectively for DeepSeek-V3-685B, and 974 and 919 TFLOPS/GPU on GB300 and GB200 respectively for Qwen3-235B. Dividing the GB200 figure by this corpus’s authoritative dense BF16 peak of 2,500 TFLOPS (not the HGX B200 figure of 2,250 that the underlying research pass originally used) gives 41.9% BF16 MFU on GB200 for DeepSeek-V3-scale MoE training — the closest thing to a citable, real-world, kernel-stack-level sustained-MFU number for GB200 MoE training this corpus contains, corrected here against the SKU-conflict this book flags throughout (Ch8, Ch14, Ch16). arXiv:2603.07685 [PAPER] Mar 2026; denominator correction per [notes/30-gb200-nvl72-peak-flops-reconciliation.md, this corpus] This 41.9% figure carries a second, unresolved uncertainty layered on top of the SKU correction: the Megatron-Core report excerpt available to this corpus does not state whether the underlying 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU figure is itself a BF16 or FP8 measurement. This chapter follows the primary source materials in treating it as BF16 (consistent with the “BF16 MFU” framing used throughout); if the measurement is actually FP8, the correct denominator is GB200’s dense FP8 peak of 5,000 TFLOPS, and the resulting MFU would be roughly 21%, not 41.9%. Treat 41.9% as the corpus’s best BF16-conditional estimate, not a settled figure. [notes/30-gb200-nvl72-peak-flops-reconciliation.md, this corpus, Finding F and Open Questions]

FlashMLA, DSA, and the Varlen/THD Chain That Gates Packing

FlashMLA (deepseek-ai/FlashMLA) covers SM90 (H800/H100) dense BF16 decode, sparse FP8-KV decode, and sparse prefill, with SM100 (B200) adding dense MHA prefill on top of the SM90 feature set. Measured numbers: SM90 dense MLA decode up to 3,000 GB/s (memory-bound) or 660 TFLOPS (compute-bound); SM90 sparse MLA decode (FP8 KV) 410 TFLOPS; B200 sparse MLA decode ~350 TFLOPS, which the repository’s own README candidly flags as “not really optimized yet”; B200 sparse MLA prefill up to 1,450 TFLOPS; B200 dense MHA prefill forward up to 1,460 TFLOPS, backward 1,000 TFLOPS. KV-cache quantization uses FP8 E4M3 with per-128-token scales, keeping the RoPE component unquantized in BF16. github.com/deepseek-ai/FlashMLA [CODE] 2025–2026 A self-reported gap from the maintainers themselves is a rarer and more trustworthy kind of evidence than most vendor claims in this chapter.

Native Sparse Attention (NSA, the block-wise research design) and DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA, the shipped V3.2/V4 design using a finer-grained, token-wise “lightning indexer”) are related but distinct, and DSA’s kernels are genuinely open across two tiers: TileLang implementations (readable, research-oriented) released alongside DeepSeek-V3.2-Exp in September 2025, and production CUDA kernels split across DeepGEMM (indexer logit computation) and FlashMLA (the sparse-attention kernels themselves). A third-party TileLang reimplementation of the DSA lightning-indexer training operator exists at lemyx/tilelang-dsa, evidence of community reproduction beyond DeepSeek’s own release. vllm.ai/blog/2025-09-29-deepseek-v3-2 [OFFICIAL-BLOG] Sept 2025; github.com/lemyx/tilelang-dsa [CODE] 2026 DeepSeek-V4 (April 2026, full architectural detail in Ch4) extends the indexer to 64 indexer heads, 4x key compression, and per-query top-k of 512–1024 tokens at 1M-token context.

Varlen/THD support is the mechanism that makes sequence packing (Ch13) viable at all, and it is worth stating why this chapter and not the dataloading chapter owns the kernel-level detail: packing only pays off if the attention and MoE kernels consuming packed sequences correctly track cumulative sequence-length offsets rather than materializing padded tensors. Transformer Engine’s THD format ([total_tokens, num_heads, head_dim]) relies on cu_seqlens propagated through TE’s fused attention kernels; TE v2.16 specifically added pad_between_seqs support for both non-context-parallel and context-parallel (ring/A2A and P2P variants) combined with FA3 plus THD attention, alongside FA4 support for head_dim=256. [NVIDIA TE docs] [OFFICIAL-DOCS] 2026 A known load-imbalance wrinkle recurs here identically to the small-M grouped-GEMM problem above: with varlen, SM tile assignments naturally have different mainloop lengths — a long sequence’s tile does more work than a short sequence’s tile — which is exactly the load-balance problem persistent, atomic-counter-based scheduling is designed to fix. That the same architectural answer (LPT/persistent scheduling) recurs across FA4’s tile scheduler, small-M MoE grouped GEMM, and varlen attention is evidence this is becoming the general-purpose 2026 Blackwell kernel-engineering pattern, not a one-off trick specific to any single kernel.

On the multi-token-prediction and hybrid-attention side, FlexAttention’s FA4 backend natively supports sliding-window as a built-in mask type alongside causal, ALiBi, document-mask, and soft-cap, with measured GB200 speedups over Triton of 1.4–2.1x forward and 1.8–2.2x backward for sliding-window patterns specifically, and up to 2.7x forward / 3x backward for document masks. pytorch.org/blog/flexattention-flashattention-4 [OFFICIAL-BLOG] 2026 Separately, Megatron-Core’s 2026-Q2 roadmap lists “GDN kernel fusion on Blackwell” (marked “working”) and “pre-gated delta rule kernel fusion” as in-progress items tied to Qwen3.5-style hybrid linear-plus-full-attention architectures (Ch5) — hybrid-attention kernel fusion is an acknowledged, actively-being-built gap, not a finished item, in the reference MoE training framework as of mid-2026. github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM issue #4815 [OFFICIAL-DOCS/CODE] 2026

The Fusion Inventory: Norm, RoPE, SwiGLU, Cross-Entropy, Optimizer

Below the attention and grouped-GEMM layer sits a set of smaller fusions whose aggregate effect on memory footprint and wall-clock is large enough to be load-bearing at frontier scale even though no individual fusion is architecturally novel.

Norm/RoPE/SwiGLU fusion is a solved, well-measured problem: aggregated fine-tuning-framework benchmarks report Triton-fused-kernel speedups of roughly 7x for RMSNorm, 5x for SwiGLU, and 2.3x for QK-RoPE fusion relative to naive eager PyTorch, with the underlying pattern — normalizing x + residual without materializing the intermediate sum — standard across Liger-Kernel and Quack-style libraries. [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 2026 Fusing SwiGLU with FP8 amax/quantization specifically achieves latency “nearly identical” to standalone unquantized SwiGLU while producing FP8-quantized output directly — meaning the quantization step is effectively free when fused, which matters for any FP8-recipe pretraining stack (Ch8) where every activation needs a cast somewhere in the pipeline. [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 2026

Chunked cross-entropy is the single highest-leverage, most completely solved fusion in this beat, and it becomes non-optional rather than merely nice-to-have at the vocabulary sizes a 10T-total/200B-active multilingual/code design is likely to use. Apple’s Cut Cross-Entropy computes only the correct-token logit plus an on-the-fly log-sum-exp over the vocabulary via a flash-memory-style kernel, never materializing the full [tokens × vocab] logit matrix; for Gemma-2 2B, this drops the loss computation’s memory footprint from 24GB to 1MB and total classifier-head training memory from 28GB to 1GB, with no measured throughput or convergence regression. arXiv:2411.09009 [PAPER] Nov 2024 Liger-Kernel’s independent fused-linear-cross-entropy implementation reports roughly 5x memory reduction at vocab=163,840 via online-softmax chunked computation, and more broadly 40–55% peak-VRAM savings for vocab sizes above 100K. The tradeoff is real, not free: the most memory-aggressive chunking configuration is reported to more than double wall-clock latency for the cross-entropy step specifically, since chunk count trades memory for latency directly. Liger-Kernel docs [PAPER/CODE] Oct 2024–2026 At frontier-scale vocabularies commonly exceeding 150–200K tokens, an unfused cross-entropy over a large batch-times-sequence-length would materialize a genuinely enormous logit tensor — this fusion is the difference between the loss computation fitting in memory at all, not a marginal optimization.

Fused optimizer: Apex’s FusedAdam fuses the elementwise Adam update into one kernel and uses multi-tensor apply to batch the update across an entire model’s parameter list into one or a handful of launches rather than one per tensor. [nvidia/apex fused_adam.py] [CODE] ongoing No evidence of a CuTe-DSL or tcgen05-specific rewrite of the fused-optimizer kernel was found in this corpus — this remains a straightforward memory-bound elementwise kernel where Hopper-era code likely runs unmodified on Blackwell, unlike the attention and grouped-GEMM kernels above, which needed ground-up redesign. [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 2026

torch.compile Coverage and the Fusion Ceiling

TorchTitan (Ch17) uses regional compilation — torch.compile applied per-TransformerBlock rather than to the whole model graph — specifically to keep FSDP2/TP-compatible graphs free of graph breaks and to exploit the fact that structurally identical transformer layers only need compiling once. A compiled transformer block typically collapses from roughly 30 eager-mode kernel launches down to 5–8 fused kernels per layer: attention delegates to FA4 or cuDNN, matmuls delegate to cuBLAS or the CuTe-DSL Inductor backend with autotuned tile shapes, and layernorm collapses into a fused norm-residual op. arXiv:2410.06511 [PAPER] 2024, still current practice per 2026 sources; [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] 2026

What still needs hand-authored kernels in 2026, and is unlikely to stop needing them: attention (Inductor correctly delegates to FA4/cuDNN rather than attempting its own codegen, given the specialization complexity documented above), grouped GEMM and MoE dispatch-combine (no evidence Inductor auto-fuses these — Megatron-Core and TE ship them as explicit hand-written ops), and cross-entropy/CCE-style vocabulary chunking (the online log-sum-exp restructuring is an algorithmic change, not a mechanical fusion a pattern-matching compiler would discover). This is a stable, three-item boundary between what a 2026-era compiler can find automatically and what an infra team must still own by hand.

The Gap List: What a 10T/200B Run Must Build or Fund

Every item below is a documented gap, not a speculative one — each has a primary-source or repo-verified basis cited above.

  • FA4 FP8/MXFP8 backward for attention. FA4 CuTe’s FP8 path is forward-only (NotImplementedError in the current repo); a training team wanting FP8 attention with a working backward must either wait for this to land, fall back to BF16 attention with FP8 elsewhere in the model, or fund the backward kernel directly. github.com/Dao-AILab/flash-attention [CODE] verified 2026-07-13
  • Blackwell-native block-scaled (MXFP8/NVFP4) attention on the training path. No mxfp8/nvfp4/block_scale code exists anywhere in FA4 CuTe as of July 2026; cuDNN’s MXFP8 SDPA-backward path has had recent Blackwell-specific correctness bugs. This is a bigger lift than FP8 backward alone — it needs a microscaling-aware softmax/rescaling redesign, not just a precision-format swap. github.com/Dao-AILab/flash-attention [CODE]; NVIDIA cuDNN release notes [OFFICIAL-DOCS] 2026
  • Production-grade cooperative 2-CTA MoE grouped GEMM in the public stack. The 10x gap between naive and hand-tuned NVFP4 grouped GEMM at MoE expert shapes is documented; the technique closing the remaining gap to the best-known public results (2-CTA cooperative MMA, M-major epilogue) is in no major library’s default path — this is the single highest-value kernel-engineering investment on this list given that GEMMs already occupy under 50% of MoE layer time even before accounting for this shape-efficiency loss. mufeezamjad.com [BLOG]; arXiv:2603.07685 [PAPER] Mar 2026
  • A non-Gluon, high-level-Triton path to competitive Blackwell attention performance, if the stack has a reason to avoid CuTe-DSL/Gluon’s lower-level programming burden — as of July 2026 this does not exist; Gluon’s explicit-control model is the only Triton-toolchain path that works.
  • CuTe-DSL graduating out of public beta, with a rigorous C++-parity benchmark suite — NVIDIA states “beta to GA by end of summer 2026” with no committed calendar date found in this corpus; a run committing to CuTe-DSL as production infrastructure before that graduation is committing to beta-quality tooling.
  • A Blackwell-native DeepGEMM peak-TFLOPS figure from DeepSeek itself. The only DeepGEMM-published peak found is 1,550 TFLOPS on H800 (Apr 2025); every B200 figure in circulation is a third-party estimate, not a DeepSeek-published number — worth closing before betting a cost model on it.
  • Hybrid/linear-attention kernel fusion (GDN, pre-gated delta rule) on Blackwell, currently in-progress rather than finished in Megatron-Core’s own roadmap — relevant to the extent the architectural strawman in Ch5 adopts a hybrid-attention design.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

The kernel layer for this design is neither a solved commodity nor a blank slate — it is a stack with sharply uneven maturity across its own components, and the unevenness itself is the planning-relevant fact. Attention (FA4, cuDNN) and dense-GEMM autotuning (the CuTe-DSL Inductor backend) are close to production-grade for BF16, with FP8 forward workable and FP8 backward genuinely absent. MoE grouped GEMM — the kernel this design depends on most, given 200B active parameters spread thin across many experts — is the least mature component in the entire stack, with a documented order-of-magnitude gap between what ships by default and what the best-known technique achieves. That gap compounds with the routing/permutation overhead and sub-50%-of-layer-time GEMM share documented in the Megatron-Core MoE report: even a perfect grouped-GEMM kernel would still leave roughly half of MoE step time to communication and dispatch overhead (Ch16, Ch18) rather than FLOP-bearing compute.

Two decisions follow directly. First, budget real kernel-engineering headcount — not integration time — against the cooperative 2-CTA grouped-GEMM gap specifically; the 10x-to-15x public headroom documented above is large enough that closing even half of it changes the run’s realized MFU more than any precision-format choice in Ch8. Second, do not plan the attention path around Blackwell-native block-scaled numerics (MXFP8/NVFP4 attention) landing before this run needs it — treat BF16 or per-tensor FP8 forward-only as the safe attention baseline, with a dedicated pilot (not a default assumption) for anything more aggressive, consistent with Ch8’s own precision recommendations. The corrected 41.9% GB200 BF16 MFU figure for DeepSeek-V3-scale MoE training (itself conditional on the underlying 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU measurement being BF16 rather than FP8 — see the caveat above) is the most defensible real-world anchor this corpus provides for what “kernel-stack-mature” currently looks like at this parameter-activity ratio (Ch16, Ch21) — a 10T/200B design should expect to start below that number on a novel architecture and treat closing the gap to it, and past it, as an explicit engineering program rather than an assumption the framework layer will absorb for free.

Chapter 16 · Part IV — The Machine

The Parallelism Math for 10T/200B on 100k GB200

Every parallelism decision in this book eventually reduces to the same inequality: is the per-GPU compute time longer or shorter than the communication time the chosen sharding scheme imposes. At 100,000 GPUs training a 10T-total/200B-active MoE, that inequality gets evaluated dozens of times across five interacting axes — data, expert, tensor, pipeline, and (for long-context stages) sequence parallelism — and getting any one of them wrong by a small constant factor is the difference between 40% MFU and 15%. This chapter derives the crossovers explicitly, corrects a subtle GB200-vs-HGX-B200 arithmetic error that has propagated through the theory literature this book draws on, and ends in three fully-worked candidate layouts for the strawman model specified in Ch5.

The roofline algebra, restated for GPUs

Every formula in this chapter reduces to T_math = FLOPs/C versus T_comms = Bytes/W, with the operation compute-bound once arithmetic intensity exceeds the hardware’s critical ratio C/W. This is the JAX Scaling Book’s organizing move, extended from TPU tori to GPU fat-trees in its GPU chapter How to Scale Your Model, GPU chapter [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2025-08]. The four sharding primitives on a GPU fat-tree: AllGather/ReduceScatter cost T=V/W regardless of shard count; AllReduce is exactly 2V/W (a ReduceScatter plus an AllGather); intra-node AllToAll costs T≈B/(N·W) for an N-GPU domain — note this is 1/N, not the TPU torus’s 1/4, because full-crossbar NVSwitch connectivity beats a torus’s partial connectivity, and conflating the two is a documented source of error when porting TPU intuition to GPU clusters How to Scale Your Model, GPU chapter [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2025-08]. Cross-node AllToAll for M nodes degrades to T≈B/(M·W_node), more than 4x worse than the single-node case because it cannot exploit full bisection bandwidth the way AllReduce can.

For a single dense matmul, this produces the familiar single-chip compute-bound floor: batch size must exceed roughly C/W_hbm tokens, which works out to B>295 on H100 and B>281 on B200 in bf16 — essentially unchanged across three hardware generations because FLOPs and HBM bandwidth have scaled together How to Scale Your Model, GPU chapter [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2025-08]. The dense-model DP/FSDP/TP crossovers this book’s parallelism math builds on: DP and FSDP are compute-bound once B/X > C/W_ici; TP is compute-bound once Y < F·W_ici/C (Y=TP degree, F=FFN width) — Stas Bekman’s ml-engineering independently arrives at the same threshold as flat field wisdom (“TP ≤ GPUs per node,” typically 8-16-way), which is this crossover pre-evaluated for typical F and frozen as a rule of thumb ml-engineering, model-parallelism [CODE, 2026-07]. Both of these are dense-model results (Ch5’s architecture chapter and this book’s own MoE derivations below extend them); the Scaling Book’s main training-parallelism chapter explicitly excludes expert parallelism from its scope, pushing all MoE-aware formulas into the GPU chapter appendix — which is where the numbers this chapter actually needs live.

GB200 ground truth used throughout this chapter (full derivation in Ch14): the authoritative per-GPU dense FP8 Tensor Core peak for GB200 NVL72 is 5.0 PFLOPS, not the 4.5 PFLOPS the Scaling Book’s summary table implies — that table silently reports the air-cooled HGX B200 SKU’s number under an undifferentiated “B200” label, an error confirmed against NVIDIA’s own side-by-side datasheet (doc #3384703) and cross-validated against two independent NVIDIA product pages and a third-party technical wiki NVIDIA Blackwell datasheet [OFFICIAL-DOCS, verified 2026-07-13]. Dense BF16 is 2.5 PFLOPS/GPU (not 2.25); dense FP4 is 10.0 PFLOPS/GPU (not 9.0) — a uniform +11.1% correction across every tensor-core precision, tracking GB200’s 1,200W liquid-cooled envelope against HGX B200’s 1,000W air-cooled part on the identical Blackwell die. The mechanism isn’t a single clean clock multiplier, though: back-calculating from published FLOPs/core-count gives only a ~6.7% vector-ALU clock delta (≈2.1GHz GB200 vs ≈1.98GHz HGX B200) — well short of the +11.1% tensor-core gap — implying either a more aggressive sustained-clock floor specifically under saturated Tensor-Core load at 1,200W, independently-rounded marketing figures on the two SKUs, or (most likely) both; NVIDIA publishes no official clock speed for either part. NVLink5 gives 900 GB/s full-duplex per GPU, unchanged in per-GPU terms whether the domain is 8 GPUs (HGX B200) or 72 GPUs (GB200 NVL72) — the domain-size increase from 8→72 is the whole story, not a per-link speed change. Scale-out bandwidth per GPU is the wildcard: ~50 GB/s/GPU on ConnectX-7/400Gbps fabrics (the implicit baseline in most GPU-chapter formulas) versus ~100 GB/s/GPU on ConnectX-8/800Gbps XDR fabrics deployed on GB200 racks from mid-2025 onward — a 2x swing that halves or doubles every cross-domain threshold below, and the more likely default for a 2026-commissioned 100k-GPU build [How to Scale Your Model + industry reporting] [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 2025]. Every calculation below uses the corrected 5.0e15 FLOPs/s/GPU dense FP8 figure.

MoE-specific formulas: the E/k inflation and the ragged AllToAll

The GPU chapter derives two closed-form results absent from the dense-model chapters. First, MoE data-parallel critical batch inflates by exactly the sparsity ratio E/k: B/X > (E/k)·C/W, because FSDP/ZeRO-3-style sharding must AllGather all E experts’ weights each step to support routing that only uses k of them — the worked example given is “the new OpenAI OSS model” (E=128, k=4, E/k=32), which needs 79,200 tokens/GPU cross-node, described in the source text as “a kind of ridiculously high number” How to Scale Your Model, GPU chapter [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2025-08]. Second, the ragged (top-k) AllToAll cost for MoE dispatch/combine is T≈min(k/N,1)·B/(N·W) — the ordinary AllToAll formula scaled down by the fraction of the domain each token actually touches.

Third, and the most consequential derivation for this chapter: the expert-parallelism compute-bound viability threshold generalizes the H100-specific F > 8·C/W_node (8 = H100 node size) to F_min = C/W_scaleout for a domain of any size n, because W_node_total = n·W_per_gpu and the n cancels algebraically. This threshold is domain-size invariant — it depends only on per-GPU scale-out bandwidth, not on how many GPUs sit inside the cheap NVLink domain. Using the corrected GB200 numbers: F_min = 5.0e15/50e9 = 100,000 on ConnectX-7 fabrics, or 5.0e15/100e9 = 50,000 on ConnectX-8. Neither source states this invariance explicitly; it falls directly out of generalizing the book’s own H100-specific formula, and it is easy to state backwards — GB200’s real MoE advantage is entirely about how much EP is “free” inside the 72-GPU domain before F_min becomes relevant, not about F_min itself moving.

Applying this to the Ch5 strawman (512 routed experts, top-8, one shared expert, d_model≈8192, d_expert≈1400): the architecturally correct sparsity ratio for the weight-AllGather formula is E/k = 512/8 = 64 — not the naive total/active parameter ratio of 10T/200B=50 that a first-pass estimate might reach for. The two numbers differ because total/active blends in the always-active components (attention, the one shared expert, embedding/output head, and the strawman’s 1-3 dense layers) that dilute the pure routed-expert sparsity down from 64 toward 50 — a useful internal consistency check, since diluting toward 50 rather than away from it is exactly the direction the arithmetic should go. This chapter uses 64 throughout, since it is the ratio that actually governs weight-movement cost; readers cross-referencing the coarser 50-based figures elsewhere in this book should apply a ~1.28x scaling factor.

Recomputing the intra-domain and cross-domain critical batches at E/k=64 and the corrected 5.0e15 FLOPs/s/GPU:

And the EP viability threshold on expert width is unchanged by the sparsity ratio (F_min is a per-expert-width condition, not a routing-fanout condition): 100,000 (CX-7) or 50,000 (CX-8), as derived above. The strawman’s d_expert≈1400 sits ~36x below the CX-8 threshold (and ~71x below the stricter CX-7 threshold) — architecturally indistinguishable from DeepSeek V3’s F=2048, which sits ~24x below CX-8 (~49x below CX-7) once corrected for the true GB200 peak [scaling-book §10 + this reconciliation]. This is not a strawman-specific problem; the Ling paper’s granularity optimum (G≈12, Ant’s definition G=2·d_model/d_expert, stable across FLOPs budgets 1e18-1e20 and confirmed across 300+ trained models up to 28B params Towards Greater Leverage, arXiv:2507.17702 [PAPER, 2025-07]) and DeepSeek’s own precedent both push expert width in exactly the direction that makes flat cross-domain EP comms-bound — narrow, numerous experts are good for the Ch5/Ch11 scaling-law story and bad for this chapter’s network story, and the two literatures don’t currently talk to each other on this point. The practical consequence, worked through below, is that node/domain-limited routing (DeepSeek’s ≤4-node cap, generalized here to a ≤1-domain cap) remains structurally necessary at 10T scale, not a 2024-era workaround that a bigger cluster outgrows.

Why pure DP/FSDP is mathematically dead — and why ZeRO-1 escapes the same trap

At 100,000 GPUs with a plausible global batch in the 10-60M token range (bracketed by LLaMA-3’s 16M and DeepSeek-V3’s 63M token batches), per-GPU batch is roughly 100-600 tokens. Compare against the intra-domain FSDP threshold of 355,556 tokens/GPU derived above: per-GPU batch is 3-4 orders of magnitude below even the cheapest (single-domain, NVLink-speed) FSDP threshold, before cross-domain bandwidth is even considered. Pure DP or ZeRO-3-style FSDP, applied naively to the full 10T-parameter model, is not a suboptimal choice at this scale — it is comms-bound by several orders of magnitude at any batch size a 100k-GPU cluster could plausibly run. EP+PP+TP composition is therefore not an optimization; it is the only mathematically viable regime, exactly mirroring what DeepSeek V3 already demonstrates (64-way EP, 16-way PP, 2-way DP) at roughly 1/50th the GPU count and comparable total sparsity DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report [PAPER, 2024-12].

But this raises an apparent puzzle: DeepSeek V3’s DP axis is not zero — it runs 2-way DP on top of EP64×PP16, and the reader’s cluster will need a DP axis running into the hundreds of ways just to reach 100,000 GPUs. Why doesn’t that DP axis hit the same E/k wall? The resolution, synthesizing three sources that don’t individually state it: the E/k inflation is specifically a weight-AllGather-style (FSDP/ZeRO-3) artifact. It exists because FSDP must materialize all E experts’ weights on demand each forward pass to support k-sparse routing across an unpredictable token stream. DeepSeek’s actual DP choice is ZeRO-1 — optimizer-state sharding only, with the full (already EP- and PP-sharded) local weight shard resident on every DP replica at all times, synced via periodic gradient AllReduce rather than per-step weight AllGather DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report §3.2 [PAPER, 2024-12]. Because EP has already physically distributed the 512 experts across the EP group before DP replication happens, a GPU’s local shard already excludes the E−k experts it doesn’t hold — there is no “gather all E, use k” waste left for the DP axis to pay for. This matches Stas Bekman’s independent, hard-won field rule that ZeRO combined with PP should almost always be ZeRO stage 1 only, since PP’s small microbatches make ZeRO-2/3’s extra reduce-scatter proportionally expensive relative to its marginal memory savings ml-engineering, model-parallelism [CODE, 2026-07] — arrived at from BLOOM/IDEFICS-scale empirical tuning rather than from the Scaling Book’s algebra, but landing on the identical conclusion.

The ZeRO-1 DP axis is therefore governed by the ordinary, non-inflated compute-bound condition B/X > C/W, using whichever fabric the DP replicas actually communicate over. For a layout where DP replicas span NVL72 domain boundaries (the typical case once EP+PP already consume most of a domain), that’s the cross-domain figure: 5.0e15/100e9 = 50,000 tokens/GPU on ConnectX-8 — three orders of magnitude more forgiving than the FSDP threshold, and comfortably satisfiable by any reasonable microbatch × gradient-accumulation-steps product. This is the load-bearing distinction the rest of this chapter’s layouts depend on: weight/optimizer memory sharding is EP+PP’s job; DP is a replication factor layered on top via ZeRO-1, sized only to satisfy a modest gradient-sync compute-bound floor, not the punishing E/k-inflated one.

The EP-inside-NVL72 opportunity, and its hard limit

GB200 NVL72’s structural contribution to MoE training is domain size, not per-link bandwidth: NVLink5 gives the same 900 GB/s/GPU whether the domain has 8 GPUs (HGX B200) or 72 (NVL72) — the win is putting 9x more GPUs behind the cheap all-to-all domain before falling back to the slower scale-out fabric How to Scale Your Model, GPU chapter [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2025-08]. This is confirmed empirically, not just algebraically, by Megatron-Core’s own measured dispatch/combine latency table (hidden=7168, seqlen=4096, 256 experts) Scalable Training of MoE Models with Megatron Core, arXiv:2603.07685 [PAPER, 2026-03]:

GB200’s HybridEP kernel stays essentially flat (391→675µs) as EP scales 8→64 because the entire domain fits inside NVLink; H100’s plain all-to-all blows up 7-8x as EP crosses node boundaries onto InfiniBand. Consistent with this, the same paper reports EP all-to-all consuming only ~20% of iteration time on GB200 (intra-domain EP64) versus 40-60% on H100 (cross-node EP64), even after dispatcher optimization the overhead is still 30-40% of iteration time for DeepSeek-V3-scale EP64 [arXiv:2603.07685 §4.2, §9.2.2] [PAPER, 2026-03] — this residual is not zero even in the favorable GB200 regime, a fact worth carrying into the predicted-MFU numbers below.

The limit is the one derived above: F_min doesn’t move. NVL72’s 72-GPU domain changes how much EP is free (up to ~64-72-way, versus H100’s 8-way), not whether an expert of a given width is comms-bound. For a practical layout, this argues for EP=64 rather than the full EP=72: it divides the strawman’s 512 experts evenly (8 experts/GPU), and it matches a real-world operational pattern — GB200 NVL72 racks as deployed in 2025 commonly run 64-of-72 active GPUs with the remaining 8 held as hot spares for backplane/GPU failures, a reliability practice documented independently of any MoE-specific design choice SemiAnalysis, H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks [SA-PAID, 2025-08]. Sizing EP to leave spare headroom inside the domain is a free design choice that costs nothing in the comms model and buys operational slack Ch19 covers in more depth.

TP domain sizing: Y < F·W/C, and why “TP ≤ 72” is a trap

Stas Bekman’s flat field rule — “TP size ≤ GPUs per node” — is, evaluated against the Scaling Book’s derived Y < F·W_ici/C threshold at typical F, exactly the source of the commonly-quoted “8-16-way” TP ceiling ml-engineering, model-parallelism [CODE, 2026-07]. On an 8-GPU H100 node this rule and the derived threshold happen to coincide, inviting the (wrong) generalization that GB200 NVL72’s 72-GPU domain raises the ceiling to TP=72. It does not: the underlying Y < F·W/C ratio is unaffected by domain size, since both F and the C/W ratio are architecture and hardware properties independent of how many GPUs the NVLink fabric spans. Recomputing directly with the corrected GB200 numbers, for a dense FFN of width F=4×d_model=32,768 (relevant only to the strawman’s 1-3 non-MoE layers per Ch5): Y < F/(C/W) = 32,768/(5.0e15/900e9) = 32,768/5,556 ≈ 5.9. TP should stay below roughly 6-way even inside the cheap 72-GPU domain — a tighter ceiling than the generic “8-16-way” folk number, driven by this model’s particular d_model choice.

For the strawman’s routed-expert layers, this question is largely moot: expert-TP (splitting an individual expert’s already-narrow d_expert≈1400 matrix further across GPUs) is explicitly the wrong move — Megatron-Core’s own Parallel Folding paper frames this as the core “dense-sparse mismatch” MoE parallelism has to solve, since MoE “wants high EP and low (usually 1) expert-TP, because sharding an already-small expert matrix with TP just fragments it further” Parallel Folding paper, arXiv:2504.14960 [PAPER, 2025-04]. Parallel Folding’s mechanism — decoupling attention-layer parallelism (TP×CP×DP×PP) from MoE-layer parallelism (ETP×EP×EDP×PP), sharing only PP for gradient-flow correctness — is exactly the tool that lets a layout run ETP=1 (no expert-level TP at all) while independently choosing whatever small TP degree the attention block’s arithmetic intensity supports. This matches DeepSeek V3’s own choice to skip tensor parallelism entirely during training DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report §3.2 [PAPER, 2024-12], and it matches Megatron-Core’s own published reference configuration for DeepSeek-V3-scale training on GB200: TP1/PP4/EP64 [NeMo/Megatron-Bridge performance summary, 26.06 container] [OFFICIAL-DOCS, 2026-07]. The layouts below follow this precedent: TP=1 everywhere except where a long-context stage’s attention block specifically benefits from tensor- or sequence-level sharding (see below).

Pipeline schedules: bubble formulas and the overlap landscape

The naive 1F1B bubble fraction is r=(p-1)/m (p=pipeline depth, m=microbatch count); interleaving with v virtual model-chunks per GPU reduces this to r=(p-1)/(v·m), trading v-fold more communication for v-fold smaller bubble Ultra-Scale Playbook [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2025]. DeepSeek’s DualPipe goes further, splitting each backward chunk into separate input-gradient and weight-gradient micro-ops and feeding the pipeline from both ends simultaneously to hide cross-node all-to-all fully behind compute — its published bubble formula is (PP/2−1)(F&B+B−3W), paid for with 2x parameter memory per device (two model copies live on each PP rank) and PP+1 activation memory, a cost DeepSeek judged acceptable specifically because a large EP degree already keeps per-GPU parameter count small DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report Table 2 [PAPER, 2024-12].
