Megatron-Core did not adopt DualPipe’s code directly; instead combined_1f1b.py implements a “conceptually DualPipe-like” merged-forward-backward 1F1B overlap on top of the standard schedule, achieving a comparable bubble/memory profile without the 2x parameter-memory tax — a still-open GitHub thread on this exact design (issue #1524) asks whether the VPP-based version requires more inter-node communication than true DualPipe under equivalent bubble rates, unresolved as of the issue’s last comment github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM [CODE/ISSUE, 2026].

For a 90-100-layer model (Ch5’s strawman), Megatron-Core’s Flexible Asymmetric VPP — which allows arbitrary per-virtual-stage layer type and count, rather than the traditional uniform-layer-count constraint — is directly relevant: DeepSeek V3’s own PP=16/VPP=2 layout assigns embedding+3 dense layers to rank 0 (cost-matched to 2 MoE layers), 2 MoE layers per VPP stage to ranks 1-13, the MTP module alone to rank 14, and the loss layer alone to rank 15 Scalable Training of MoE Models with Megatron Core, Table 10 [PAPER, 2026-03]. The same tool lets a 96-100-layer, MTP-augmented strawman balance genuinely uneven per-stage costs (dense layers, MoE layers, and 2-3 MTP modules per Ch5’s depth-2-or-3 recommendation) rather than forcing a naive uniform split.

Activation recomputation economics

Full activation checkpointing trades a well-quantified throughput tax for a large memory win: for a Llama-3.1-8B-shaped block at seqlen=32768, bs=1, activations without checkpointing run 224 GiB versus 31 GiB with it — a 7.2x reduction — for a throughput cost of roughly 20-25%, “up to 30-40% per newer papers” ml-engineering, training/performance [CODE, 2026-07]. Selective recomputation (recomputing only cheap-to-recompute, expensive-to-store ops like attention softmax/dropout rather than the full block) claws back most of this overhead while retaining most of the memory win, and is the approach Megatron’s own sequence-parallel + selective-recompute combination validated at 530B-parameter, 2,240-A100 scale (54.2% MFU versus a 42.1% full-recompute baseline, +29% relative) Reducing Activation Recomputation in Large Transformer Models, arXiv:2205.05198 [PAPER, 2022]. At 200B-active-parameter scale with PP-imposed small microbatches and a long-context extension stage reaching 32-128K tokens (Ch5’s curriculum), some form of recomputation is close to mandatory rather than optional — but the exact tax for this specific block structure (MoE FFN plus MLA-and-sparse-attention plus MTP heads) is not directly measured anywhere in this book’s source corpus; the 20-40% range above is a dense-model anchor to budget against, not a validated MoE-specific number, and should be treated as the single largest source of uncertainty in the predicted-MFU figures below.

Sequence and context parallelism for long-context stages

Attention FLOPs only dominate the per-layer FLOPs budget once context length exceeds roughly 8×d_model How to Scale Your Model, Transformer Math [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2025-02]; at d_model≈8192 that crossover sits at T>65,536 tokens — squarely inside Ch5’s planned context-extension stage (32-128K tokens), meaning sequence/context parallelism stops being optional exactly where the curriculum says the model needs to go. Among the six cataloged SP implementations, DeepSpeed-Ulysses’s comms volume scales down with parallelism degree (4Nh/P) while Megatron-LM’s native sequence parallelism stays flat (4Nh) regardless of P ml-engineering, model-parallelism [CODE, 2026-07]. Ulysses’s core primitive — an all-to-all that redistributes the sequence dimension across GPUs and gathers back the full sequence per local attention head — is architecturally the same shape as MoE’s own dispatch/combine all-to-all, making it the natural SP pairing for a ZeRO+EP-based stack rather than Megatron-style SP, which would add fixed, non-shrinking traffic on top of EP’s own all-to-all rather than sharing the same comms budget logic. Ring-attention variants (Liu et al.’s ring-passed K/V, Colossal-AI’s ring self-attention, DistFlashAttn’s load-balanced variant) remain live alternatives with less production precedent at this scale.

Stas’s composition rules as a field-experience cross-check

The Scaling Book’s crossovers are first-principles derivations; ml-engineering’s decision tree is compressed field experience from BLOOM-176B and IDEFICS-80B, and the two should agree once evaluated at the same constants ml-engineering, model-parallelism [CODE, 2026-07]. Walking the tree for a 10T-total model: it categorically does not fit on a single node (ruling out the “try ZeRO-with-replicas first” branch, which assumes the model fits within one node’s memory), leaving the multi-node branches — “fast inter-node connectivity: PP+TP+DP” or, if still memory-constrained, “DP+PP+TP+ZeRO-1.” Both land on exactly the EP(-substituting-for-much-of-TP)+PP+ZeRO-1 composition this chapter derives from the roofline algebra independently, a genuine convergence between a from-first-principles theory source and a from-scratch-trainings-run practitioner source that neither document states explicitly.

Three worked candidate layouts

Layout 1 — the anti-pattern, shown to make the “pure DP is dead” argument concrete. EP=8 (single H100-style node-equivalent, deliberately under-using the 72-GPU domain), DP≈12,288 to reach 100k GPUs, PP and TP minimal. At E/k=64 and any realistic per-domain bandwidth, the FSDP-style critical batch this configuration needs is in the millions-to-tens-of-millions of tokens per GPU — even the most permissive cross-domain figure derived above (3.2M tokens/GPU at ConnectX-8) times a DP degree in the thousands implies a global batch requirement several orders of magnitude beyond anything in the LLaMA-3-to-DeepSeek-V3 range. This configuration does not train at a usable MFU; it is included to show numerically why “just use more DP” fails, not as a real candidate.

Layout 2 — DeepSeek-style scaled up, the bulk-training (4-8K context) recommendation. EP=64 (fits inside one NVL72 domain, 8 of the strawman’s 512 experts per GPU, leaving 8-GPU hot-spare headroom), PP=8 physical stages with VPP=4 (Flexible Asymmetric VPP, 32 effective pipeline slots for ~96 layers ≈ 3 layers/slot), TP=1, DP=192 via ZeRO-1. Total: 64×8×192 = 98,304 GPUs (≈1,365 NVL72 domains), leaving headroom below the 100k budget for evaluation, checkpoint-coordination, and spare capacity. Interleaved-bubble check: keeping r=(p-1)/(v·m) under 10% with p=8, v=4 requires m≥18 microbatches per flush — comfortably achievable. ZeRO-1 compute-bound check on the DP axis: 50,000 tokens/GPU (ConnectX-8) is easily cleared by 18-24 microbatches of a few thousand tokens each. Predicted global batch, order of magnitude: 10-15M tokens, bracketed by LLaMA-3’s 16M and DeepSeek-V3’s 63M. Predicted MFU: the closest measured empirical anchor is Megatron-Core’s own DeepSeek-V3-685B benchmark on GB200 at PP4×VPP4×EP64/256 GPUs — 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU sustained MXFP8, which is 21.0% FP8 MFU against the corrected 5.0 PFLOPS denominator (857 TFLOPS/GPU BF16, 34.3% BF16 MFU against the corrected 2.5 PFLOPS denominator) arXiv:2603.07685 Table 11 + [GB200 FLOPs reconciliation] [PAPER, 2026-03]. The strawman’s narrower experts (d_expert≈1400 versus DeepSeek’s 2048, roughly 30% narrower) push arithmetic intensity down further, and 512 experts versus 256 adds routing/permutation overhead on top of the ~9% Megatron-Core already measures post-fusion; both effects push predicted MFU below the anchor. This is an extrapolation, not a measurement: a defensible planning range is 15-19% FP8 MFU (≈30-38% BF16-equivalent), with the DeepSeek-V3-on-GB200 figure as the optimistic bound and the activation-recomputation tax (20-40%, above) layered on separately as a further, not-yet-included, throughput cost.

Layout 3 — long-context extension stage (32-128K tokens), Ch5’s curriculum tail. Starting from Layout 2’s EP64×PP8 unit, insert Ulysses-style context parallelism CP=4 and shrink DP correspondingly: EP64×PP8×CP4×DP48 = 98,304 GPUs (same total). At 128K context, the T>8D crossover derived above means attention FLOPs are solidly dominant, making CP load-bearing rather than optional. Two countervailing effects on predicted MFU relative to Layout 2: Megatron-Core’s own long-context measurements show DeepSeek-V3-style (dense-attention-heavy) architectures losing MFU at 256K context (88% of short-context MFU on H100) while SDPA-kernel-efficient architectures like Qwen3-235B-A22B actually gain MFU at long context (129% of short-context) [arXiv:2603.07685 §6.5] [PAPER, 2026-03]. The strawman’s DSA-style trainable sparse attention (Ch5) — O(Lk) rather than O(L²) — argues for a milder version of the Qwen3 pattern rather than the DeepSeek-V3 pattern, since sparse attention’s cost doesn’t scale with L the way dense attention’s does. Net predicted MFU: roughly 85-95% of Layout 2’s figure, i.e., in the low-to-mid-teens percent FP8 — but this combines two extrapolations (Layout 2’s own uncertainty, plus the DSA-tempers-long-context-cost argument) and should be treated as the most speculative number in this chapter.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

The arithmetic in this chapter yields four load-bearing conclusions for the run plan. First, EP degree should be sized to the NVL72 domain (64-way, leaving hot-spare headroom) and never allowed to flatten across domains at this expert width — the strawman’s d_expert≈1400 sits tens of times below the compute-bound EP viability threshold on any realistic scale-out fabric, so domain-limited (or DeepSeek-style node-capped) routing is a permanent architectural constraint, not a scale-dependent one that a bigger cluster escapes. Second, ZeRO-1 — not ZeRO-3/FSDP — is the correct DP-axis primitive precisely because EP has already done the expensive weight-sharding; treating this as a free choice rather than a structural necessity is the single most common way a first-pass parallelism plan would waste an order of magnitude of communication budget. Third, tensor parallelism belongs almost nowhere in this layout: not on the expert matrices (already too narrow), and only marginally (Y<6) on the strawman’s few dense layers — Parallel Folding is the mechanism that makes “TP=1 for MoE, small TP for attention if at all” a composable choice rather than an all-or-nothing one. Fourth, the predicted 15-19% FP8 MFU for the bulk-training layout is a real number with a real empirical anchor (Megatron-Core’s measured GB200/DeepSeek-V3 benchmark), but it sits below the more optimistic MFU figures sometimes quoted for GB200 elsewhere in the frontier-lab literature — Ch21’s money-math chapter should treat this chapter’s bottom-up derivation as a conservative floor to reconcile against, not a number to average away.

Chapter 17 · Part IV — The Machine

Frameworks: Megatron, TorchTitan, MaxText, and the Field

A 10T-total/200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200 GPUs will be built on top of somebody else’s parallelism engine, patched in dozens of places, and the choice of whose engine determines which bugs the team inherits. This chapter reads the field at the level of dispatcher classes, open GitHub issues, and merged pull requests rather than marketing claims, because at this scale the deciding factor is rarely “does it support MoE” (everything now does) and almost always “what breaks when EP, communication overlap, uneven pipeline layouts, FSDP, and CPU offload are all turned on simultaneously.”

Megatron-Core: the reference stack, read at the commit level

Megatron-Core is the library a 10T/200B build will most likely fork, and as of July 2026 it has a primary source detailed enough to make that decision on evidence: NVIDIA’s “Scalable Training of Mixture-of-Experts Models with Megatron Core” (arXiv:2603.07685, March 2026), benchmarked against Megatron-Core v0.16. It reports 1,233 TFLOPS/GPU on GB300 and 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU on GB200 for DeepSeek-V3-685B, and 974/919 TFLOPS/GPU for Qwen3-235B, under force-balanced routing PAPER, 03/2026. The architecture matters more than the headline numbers, and it’s organized around a sequence of “walls” — parallelism, communication, memory, compute-efficiency — each solved by a shipped mechanism, not a roadmap item.

Parallel Folding is the load-bearing idea. Attention wants high TP/CP; MoE wants high EP and low expert-TP, since sharding an already-small expert matrix with TP just fragments it further. Traditional frameworks force EP ≤ DP by nesting EP inside the data-parallel group. MoE Parallel Folding (arXiv:2504.14960, April 2025 — shipped code, not a proposal) decouples this: attention gets its own topology (TP × CP × DP × PP), MoE layers get a separate one (ETP × EP × EDP × PP), sharing only PP for gradient-flow correctness [PAPER §3.2.4, 03/2026]. Worked example: attention at TP=4/CP=2/DP=8/PP=4 (256 GPUs) traditionally caps EP at DP=8; under folding EP reaches 64 — 8x higher at the identical GPU count [PAPER §3.3.3, 03/2026]. A sharper example: CP=8 plus EP=8 needs only 8 GPUs under folding versus 64 under the traditional constraint, since CP and EP can share the same GPU group. This is process-group management, and it’s what makes EP64 on a 256-GPU DeepSeek-V3 config arithmetically possible. The companion paper’s own pre-2603.07685 numbers — 49.3% MFU on Mixtral-8x22B, 39.0% on Qwen2-57B-A14B, validated to 1,024 GPUs — are the honest floor before dispatchers, CUDA-Graph coverage, and FP8 are layered on [PAPER, arXiv:2504.14960, 04/2025].

Three dispatcher backends, one class hierarchy. token_dispatcher.py (1,859 lines) confirms the paper’s description at the class level: a unified MoEFlexTokenDispatcher delegates to _HybridEPManager, _DeepepManager, or _NCCLEPManager [CODE, 07/2026]. DeepEP (Hopper, cross-node, MIT-licensed) and HybridEP (NVIDIA’s GB200/GB300 NVL72-specific kernel) are the two production backends beyond raw NCCL all-to-all, both wired through fused_a2a.py. The surprising code-level fact: fused_a2a.py imports from deep_ep import HybridEPBuffer — HybridEP, despite being “developed by NVIDIA” in the paper’s prose, is upstreamed into DeepSeek’s own DeepEP repository on a hybrid-ep branch rather than shipped as a separate NVIDIA package [CODE + PAPER, 03/2026] — a narrow but concrete NVIDIA/DeepSeek infra collaboration signal absent from either org’s public messaging.

The communication numbers this zoo solves for are large: DeepSeek-V3’s 58 MoE layers × 2 ops/layer gives 116 dispatch/combine operations per forward pass, doubling on backward [PAPER §2.2.1, 4.2.1]. Table 7 is the sharpest available statement of why NVL72 topology matters for EP: at EP=64, GB200 HybridEP dispatch latency is 675µs versus 930µs for plain all-to-all, while the same comparison on H100 (crossing nodes) is 4,626µs versus 9,164µs — GB200 HybridEP stays flat across EP 8→64 because EP64 fits inside the NVL72 domain, while H100 all-to-all blows up 4-8x crossing node boundaries [PAPER Table 7]. Unoptimized, EP all-to-all consumes 20-60% of training time depending on whether EP stays intra-NVLink (~20% GB200) or crosses nodes (40-60% H100) [PAPER §4.2]. Overlap is handled by a 1F1B all-to-all overlap scheme — “conceptually a DualPipe-like bidirectional schedule built on top of standard 1F1B” — in combined_1f1b.py, via --overlap-moe-expert-parallel-comm [PAPER §4.2.3]. Tellingly, a feature request for native DualPipeV support (issue #1524, closed Dec 2025) was resolved by pointing to this scheme rather than porting DeepSeek’s DualPipeV verbatim, and an unresolved sub-thread asks whether the VPP-based approach needs more inter-node communication than true DualPipe at equal bubble rate — an open, unanswered doubt inside Megatron-Core’s own community [CODE/ISSUE, #1524].

Dropless MoE and CUDA Graphs — the least-publicized section. Dropless MoE has per-expert token counts known only on-device; host-initiated kernels need a device→host sync to read shapes, blocking CUDA Graph capture. Three mechanisms close this gap, none widely discussed elsewhere. Device-initiated Grouped GEMM (cuBLASLt with device-array shapes, CUDA 13.1+; cuteDSL with SwiGLU/FP8 fused into the epilogue) plus a sync-free HybridEP dispatch mode. ECHO (Elastic Cloning for Hot Experts, PR #2368, merged Feb 2026): a bin-packing planner identifies overloaded experts per forward pass, clones their weights onto underutilized ranks via sync-free comm, redirects overflow tokens, and reduces clone gradients back to the home expert on backward — architecturally distinct from DeepSeek’s EPLB (inference-time replica redistribution); ECHO is training-time and gradient-aware [PAPER §4.3.7]. Paged Stashing (PR #2690): replaces per-layer worst-case CUDA-Graph buffers (O(layers×worst_case), often an order of magnitude above actual usage) with one shared tmp buffer plus a paged buffer storing only actual per-layer token counts, cutting memory to O(worst_case+actual_total), overlapped on dedicated streams [PAPER §4.3.7]. Both PRs are merged into dev, but the 2026-Q2 roadmap still lists “sync-free, full-iteration CUDA-Graph MoE training” as long-term — full integration is not yet default as of July 2026 [ROADMAP, issue #4815].

Memory and compute-efficiency. At PP4×VPP4×EP64 on 256 GPUs, BF16, DeepSeek-V3 measures 199.5 GB/GPU (36.4 weights+grads, 32.1 optimizer, 131.0 activations); the full optimization stack (FP8, fine-grained recompute, memory-efficient permutation, offloading) claims to bring this under 80 GB [PAPER Table 3, §4.1.1, §11]. GEMMs account for ~70% of layer time in dense Llama-3-405B but under 50% in DeepSeek-V3 — MoE structurally halves GEMM’s share of the timeline before communication is even counted [PAPER §1.2].

Distributed optimizer, FSDP, checkpointing. The distributed optimizer shards MoE optimizer state only among replicas of the same expert via EDP, avoiding cross-EP-group duplication. Megatron-FSDP is “the practical fully sharded data parallel path in Megatron Bridge today” per NVIDIA docs, using a Dual DeviceMesh design for EP compatibility [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 07/2026]; “FSDP + A2A overlap” is still roadmapped, i.e. not yet mature. Distributed checkpointing uses a ShardedTensor abstraction enabling fully-parallel save and any-to-any reshard on load — save at TP=2/EP=4, load at TP=4/EP=8, no offline conversion — backed by Zarr or PyTorch Distributed [PAPER §7.4]. Two features worth flagging: Flexible Asymmetric VPP (arbitrary per-stage layer type/count — DeepSeek-V3’s PP=16/VPP=2 layout individually places dense, MoE, MTP, and loss layers) [PAPER Table 10, §7.5]; and LatentMoE (compresses routed-expert computation through a shared low-rank projection around dispatch, reducing both all-to-all volume and per-expert weight size — already production-deployed in Nemotron-3 Super/Ultra) [PAPER §7.3]. LatentMoE compresses the dispatch payload itself, a distinct axis from anything in the DeepSeek or MAI-1 disclosures, and easy to miss buried in a systems-paper appendix.

Ecosystem restructuring. As of July 2026: Megatron-Core (the engine, a library not a trainer), Megatron-Bridge (submodule dependency, HF↔Megatron checkpoint conversion, explicitly “a refactor of the previous NeMo training stack that adopts a PyTorch-native training loop”) [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 07/2026], and NeMo AutoModel (DTensor-based, bypasses Megatron-Core entirely). NeMo 2.0 is deprecated as of the 25.11 release. Current Megatron-Bridge release is 0.5.0 (06/22/2026); NeMo RL runs Megatron-Core inside Ray workers and also backs veRL and Slime via the same interop layer. The net read: Megatron-Core is the engine to build on directly — it’s what “Megatron-LM” colloquially means now — while Megatron-Bridge is the right layer only for HF interop; at 100k-GPU scale the team will be patching Megatron-Core directly regardless (open issue #4590, “Reduce downstream Megatron patching for RL use cases,” is explicitly about how much even NVIDIA’s own RL frameworks have to patch it).

What actually breaks first, from open issues, not vibes. The paper’s own benchmarks top out at EP64/1,024 GPUs (H100) and 256 GPUs (GB200/GB300) — the live tracker surfaces a sharper ceiling signal:

  1. NCCL collective-order deadlocks in EP overlap with uneven pipeline layouts. #5749 (open): a 128-rank deadlock after 92-852 steps, EP-disabled the only workaround (2.2x step-time cost). #1810 (open) root-causes it: a single shared CUDA event synchronizing both compute and comm streams lets different ranks enqueue the same two collectives in different orders, violating NCCL’s same-order invariant — a currently open correctness bug (silent hang, not wrong numbers) in the flagship --overlap-moe-expert-parallel-comm feature, triggered exactly by the EP + comm-overlap + uneven-VPP combination a heterogeneous dense/MoE/MTP 10T model would use [CODE/ISSUE, #5749, #1810].
  2. Hardware protocol limits becoming training-time constraints. #3999 (fixed): HybridEP’s token-count computation used flattened microbatch size, pushing RDMA queue-pair depth past InfiniBand’s 65,535 hardware limit at 8,192-seqlen×8-microbatch, aborting all ranks — fixed, but the class of bug will recur at 10T-scale batch sizes.
  3. Fault-tolerance/checkpoint-integrity gaps compound at scale. #5281 (open): local replica-based checkpointing can silently restore a corrupt replica (deterministic NaN 3 iterations after load); with no validation and no automatic fallback to the global checkpoint, ft_launcher enters an infinite restart loop (44 identical ~90s cycles observed). At 100k-GPU scale, where local checkpointing is the only practical fast-restart mechanism, this is a real availability risk (see Ch19).
  4. Megatron-FSDP + optimizer CPU offload checkpoint hangs. #4910 (open): save hangs during optimizer-state preprocessing specifically under optimizer_cpu_offload=True — a gap between two headline memory-saving features the paper itself recommends composing.
  5. Two closed-but-instructive issues (#1707, checkpoint-validation OOM at scale; #1053, Zarr losing distributed-optimizer state) confirm checkpointing-at-scale is a recurring soft spot, not a one-off.

Raw throughput does not look like a near-term ceiling — the dispatch/compute/combine pipeline scales cleanly through EP64 and thousands of GPUs when the stack cooperates. The bottleneck at 100k-GPU/10T-parameter scale is the intersection of features (EP + comm-overlap + uneven-VPP + FSDP + CPU-offload + fault-tolerance, all active together), consistent with the paper’s own thesis that “addressing walls in isolation leads to suboptimal solutions” [PAPER §4].

The Megatron-Bridge 26.06-container performance-summary page publishes exact TP/PP/CP/VP/EP configs across eight model families and four hardware generations; a representative slice:

[Megatron-Bridge perf-summary] [OFFICIAL-BLOG/DOCS] 07/2026

TorchTitan: the PyTorch-native challenger, honestly assessed

TorchTitan has grown far past its October 2024 paper (2410.06511, Llama-3-only). The current tree natively supports DeepSeek-V3 (real MLA — kv_lora_rank=512, qk_nope_head_dim=128, matching the public architecture — and a _671b() config registering the real 256 experts), gpt-oss, Qwen3/Qwen3.5, and Llama3/4 [CODE, 07/2026]. Its bet, unchanged since 2024, is that DTensor/DeviceMesh are mature enough to build FSDP2/TP/CP/EP/PP on top of, inside roughly 7-9K lines versus Megatron-LM’s 93-269K [PAPER, 10/2024] — auditability and PyTorch-nativeness, not comprehensiveness. The MoE stack is newer and moving fast rather than battle-tested.

Five dispatcher backends. LocalTokenDispatcher (EP=1), AllToAllTokenDispatcher (plain collectives), TorchAOTokenDispatcher (16/32-element alignment for FP8/MXFP8 grouped-GEMM), DeepEPTokenDispatcher (native DeepEP v2), and HybridEPTokenDispatcher (GB200/NVLink72-specific, with a non-blocking dispatch mode trading bounded token-drop risk for CPU-sync-free dispatch) [CODE, 07/2026]. A sixth, MinimalAsyncEPTokenDispatcher, is explicitly scoped EP-with-SP=1-only per its own docstring. A dedicated HybridEPTokenDispatcher living in core code (not experiments) is a concrete signal the PyTorch team is targeting exactly the NVL72 topology a 100k-GPU GB200 build would use.

MXFP8 real-training numbers. The richest datapoint in this space: PyTorch+Nebius, DeepSeek-V3-671B on 256×B200, TP=2/PP=2/EP=32: BF16 baseline 651 tok/s → BF16+DeepEP 859 tok/s (+32%) → MXFP8 grouped-GEMM+DeepEP 918 tok/s (+41% over baseline), loss convergence matching BF16 over 1,500 steps [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 03/2026, upd. 05/2026]. MXFP8 was applied only to grouped GEMMs, not dense linears, “because torch.compile does not yet support MXFP8 linear layers when combined with tensor parallelism” — 41% is not the ceiling.

Real frontier-adjacent usage. Arcee AI’s Trinity Large (400B total/13B active, top-4 of 256+1 experts, 17T tokens) trained on 2,048 B300 GPUs on a modified torchtitan (arXiv:2602.17004, Feb 2026) [PAPER] — the single best “production grade” data point, unusually candid about failure modes: the team fell back from MXFP8 to BF16 for stability and needed six concurrent interventions (a new load-balancing scheme “SMEBU,” z-loss, sequence-wise aux loss, more dense layers, intra-doc attention masking, BF16 fallback) to eliminate router-collapse loss spikes. AMD/TensorWave ran DeepSeek-V3-671B on 1,024 MI325X via a modified torchtitan plus Primus-Turbo kernels, 96-97% scaling efficiency 256→1024 GPUs, 2.77x cumulative speedup [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 12/2025]. Every public benchmark tops out in the 1K-2K GPU range.

Fault tolerance is thin. torchft (DiLoCo/semi-sync via a Rust “Lighthouse” quorum service) is a torchtitan experiment, its own README calling it “an ongoing development effort, subject to change.” Most recent validation (AMD/ROCm, Feb 2026) is 32-GPU-only, no throughput-overhead numbers published. Its architectural ceiling matters independent of scale: torchft tolerates losing an entire HSDP replica group, not single-GPU failures inside a 3D/4D-parallel job — the more relevant primitive for a 100k-GPU EP+PP+FSDP job is NCCL-level process-group reconfiguration, which torchft doesn’t address (see Ch19).

GraphTrainer — the compiler-driven bet, explicitly still experimental. experiments/graph_trainer/ captures an entire training step as one flat FX graph, running activation checkpointing, CUDAGraph, CPU offload, and comm/compute overlap as composable graph passes rather than competing eager-mode hooks. Its own MANIFESTO.md: “each combination [of FSDP2 + AC + compile + CUDAGraph] needs its own workaround, and workarounds for one pair can break another” [CODE, 07/2026]. Sub-components include SimpleFSDP (arXiv 2411.00284, FSDP collectives as traceable graph nodes), AutoParallel (GSPMD-style automatic sharding search), and a quotable GB200 hard number: NUMA-aware CPU offload documents ~350 GB/s NUMA-local versus ~120 GB/s cross-NUMA D2H/H2D over NVLink-C2C. The July-2026 composability table is the honesty check: TP/CP/DCP/CUDAGraph/EP done; Float8/MXFP8 training, EP+AC, EP+PP, graph-based PP, and micro-batch overlap all still in-progress [CODE, 07/2026]. GraphTrainer is explicitly barred from leaking into core torchtitan by repo policy.

Corroborating context: Ed Yang (PyTorch core/compiler) gave an unusually candid August-2025 assessment that DTensor lacks a “jagged sharding” concept needed for imbalanced-routing EP, and torch.compile is “not SPMD-first” [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, high-trust, 08/2025] — the code confirms this directly, with EP dispatch/combine dropping out of DTensor into plain local tensors at the GroupedExperts boundary, an explicit comment noting the shape “cannot be easily expressed as DTensors.”

Bottom line: torchtitan’s parallelism composition and MoE/quantization engineering look genuinely state-of-the-art and GB200-aware, but the operational/reliability layer at 50-100x today’s largest demonstrated scale is unproven in public, and Megatron-LM (plus NVIDIA’s internal tooling) remains the safer bet purely on “has anyone run this at anything approaching 100k GPUs” grounds — a caveat that, fairly, applies to every open framework.

MaxText and AI-Hypercomputer: compiler-first, and honest about not being GB200-ready

MaxText is Google’s reference framework — pure Python/JAX + XLA + Pallas — betting the opposite of Megatron: “trusts the underlying compiler to handle the complexities of hardware optimization… only for the most performance-critical operations… does MaxText use custom kernels” [OFFICIAL-DOC, 06/2026]. It ships native configs for DeepSeek V3/V3.1/V3.2, a DeepSeek-V4-shaped config, Kimi-K2 (1T, 384 experts), Qwen3.5-397B-A17B (GatedDeltaNet every 4 layers), and Qwen3-480B-A35B across roughly 85 model YAMLs [CODE, 06/2026].

The GB200 gap is the single most consequential, checkable finding here. accelerator_to_spec_map.py — defining every valid compile-topology for AOT compilation — has roughly 200 TPU entries and exactly one GPU entry: "a3" (H100, single-host only), zero A4/A4X/GB200/B200 entries anywhere [CODE, 06/2026]. GPU attention doesn’t use MaxText’s own Pallas kernel at all — docs state “Flash does not work on GPUs” in MaxText, so GPU training depends on NVIDIA Transformer Engine’s cuDNN kernels instead, an entirely separate code path. The only GPU benchmarks shipped are Llama2-7B on 1-16x A3 nodes; no MoE-at-scale GPU numbers exist. A sharding.md doc misdescribes GB200 NVL72 as “72 hosts or 576 chips” (canonical is 72 GPUs across 18 hosts) — unedited aspirational text, not a validated recipe. Google Cloud markets A4X (GB200 NVL72) GA with “4x LLM training perf vs A3” [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2025], but that optimization hasn’t been upstreamed into MaxText’s config files; JAX itself only added Blackwell support in the April 2026 release [NVIDIA JAX release notes, 04/2026] — the compiler layer for GB200 is roughly three months mature as of this writing. MaxText-as-shipped is not the tool for a 100k-GB200 cluster today; its proven scale story (9,216-chip Ironwood pods, a 50,944-chip v5e job) is entirely TPU/Pathways-native.

The compiler-driven architecture is still worth studying for design principles. The 3-layer sharding system (physical mesh → logical axes → per-tensor rules, with XLA deriving collectives from hints) and arithmetic-intensity-first culture generalize: EP’s arithmetic intensity reduces to simply the MLP dimension, independent of batch size — EP communication cost is set by model width, not batch, exactly why EP-heavy MoE training is comms-bound differently than dense-model FSDP [OFFICIAL-DOC+CODE, 06/2026]. Five pre-built mesh configs specifically target large-MoE cases, including ep-as-cp/cp-as-ep (repurposing the otherwise-idle expert axis for context-parallel duty outside MoE FFN blocks — a “free lunch” a hand-coded Megatron-style stack would need bespoke code to replicate) and pipeline-large-moe, built explicitly for “extremely large-scale MoE jobs (such as DeepSeek models).” A “Sharding Dump” regression harness with golden JSON files per model/topology protects these configs against silent regressions — engineering culture worth borrowing regardless of framework choice.

MaxText’s MoE kernel story is two-speed: Megablox (mature, Pallas-native grouped-matmul) versus Tokamax Ragged Dot (the officially “recommended” dropless path, but BF16 support is still “in progress” — only FP8 is complete) [OFFICIAL-DOC, 06/2026]. Anyone benchmarking MaxText MoE in BF16 today is effectively still on Megablox.

The goodput culture is the part most worth stealing regardless of vendor: 93% goodput for Gemini 2.5 across multiple 8,960-chip TPU v5p pods spanning data centers, 97% for Gemini 1.0 on TPU v4 [PAPER, arXiv:2606.15870, 06/2026, citing Gemini technical reports]. A July 2026 demo killed a TPU mid-run and recovered a 48-chip job in under two minutes total — 13s detection, 50s Kubernetes reschedule, 5.39s checkpoint restore, 12.7s to first post-recovery step — losing only 88 steps [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 07/2026]. That granularity in a public elastic-recovery breakdown is rare (see Ch19).

The rest of the field

InternEvo’s successor, XTuner V1, makes a claim worth stress-testing. InternEvo (arXiv:2401.09149) used simulator-searched 4D parallelism for up to 4.8x MFU on 7-65B dense models. Its successor, InternLM/xtuner, inverts industry consensus: claiming pure FSDP with zero expert parallelism up to 200B-scale MoE, only intra-node EP at 600B — “the first framework where FSDP throughput beats traditional 3D-parallel (TP+PP+EP) schemes for MoE above 200B parameters” — with no absolute tokens/s or MFU given, only an unlabeled chart, and no backing paper as of 07/2026. This directly contradicts the assumption underneath DeepSeek’s DualPipe+DeepEP, Megatron-Core’s Parallel Folding, and MegaScale-MoE’s SP+EP hybrid, all of which treat non-trivial EP as structurally necessary at fine-grained, multi-hundred-billion-parameter scale. If it replicates independently it’s consequential; if it’s an artifact of Ascend interconnect specifics or a weak baseline, it’s a cautionary tale — treat as credible-hearsay, not a design input.

veScale has pivoted to FSDP-specific work. ByteDance/Volcengine’s volcengine/veScale is explicitly “an internal ByteDance library, of which this repo open-sources a small piece.” 2026 energy has moved to veScale-FSDP (arXiv:2602.22437, 5-66% higher throughput, 16-30% lower memory than existing FSDP systems, scaling to tens of thousands of GPUs) and a sibling multimodal project, VeOmni.

PanGu Ultra MoE trains on MindSpeed (Huawei’s Megatron-derived platform), not the separately branded MindSpore — the corpus contains no independent MindSpore training-framework detail beyond this, a real gap worth flagging rather than papering over. The paper (arXiv:2505.04519) discloses 718B total/39B active on 6,000+ Ascend 910B NPUs at TP=8/PP=16/VPP=2/EP=4 — notably small EP leaning on deep PP, the inverse of the DeepSeek/Megatron playbook. Its most reusable contribution is a bottom-up simulator sweeping ~10,000 configs before committing GPU time (88.9-90.1% simulated-vs-measured accuracy) — the most rigorous public “simulate before you burn GPU-hours” methodology outside internal lab tooling, and a direct template for a 10T/200B config search. Bottom line: 30.0% MFU/1.46M tok/s at 6K NPUs. A conflation trap worth flagging: secondary sources routinely cite “>52% MFU” for “Pangu Ultra” as the MoE number — that figure is the 135B dense sibling model; the MoE model gets 30.0%.

AMD’s Primus is the real Megatron-compatible AMD stack — a three-layer system (Primus-LM orchestrating Megatron-LM/Megatron-Bridge/TorchTitan backends, Primus-Turbo kernels, Primus-SaFE cluster management) with CLI 1.0 shipping November 2025 and AMD’s own docs describing it as “battle-tested… with hundreds of GPUs” — capping validated scale at hundreds, not tens-of-thousands. The Zyphra/IBM/AMD ZAYA1 paper (arXiv:2511.17127, Dec 2025), a modified Megatron-LM fork integrated with Primus on 2,048 MI300X GPUs (8.3B-total MoE, three orders of magnitude below the reader’s target), is the richest fresh AMD systems source: MI300X HBM bandwidth saturates at 4.330 TB/s versus H100’s 3.036 TB/s, and xGMI requires all GPUs in a node to participate in a collective to reach full bandwidth (B_per-GPU = (n-1)·B_link) — a structural difference from NVSwitch, where partial-node collectives aren’t bandwidth-starved. MLPerf v6.0 (June 2026) shows MI355X at parity-to-1.16x versus B200 single-node but falling to 0.96x at 8-node scale — the AMD advantage compresses as scale-out grows, a much smaller gap than SemiAnalysis found in December 2024 but the same direction. None of this makes AMD a candidate for a GB200-based build; it’s the right comparison set for vendor-risk hedging.

DeepSpeed is not dead but is not where frontier pretraining happens — and it received a substantive commit the day before this corpus was compiled: “Add AutoEP + AutoTP parallel folding” (#8064), merged 2026-07-12, letting EP groups span TP lanes and dense-DP ranks without EP nested inside DP [CODE, verified via git log]. Conceptually adjacent to, though independently engineered from, Megatron-Core’s Parallel Folding — two teams converging on the same abstraction in the same window. AutoEP notably borrows TorchTitan’s grouped-GEMM kernels rather than building its own. The synthesized verdict across this corpus: Megatron-Core/TorchTitan own frontier-scale pretraining; DeepSpeed’s remaining edge is ZeRO-3 CPU/NVMe offload for memory-constrained fine-tuning plus HF-ecosystem AutoTP/AutoEP convenience. No 2025-2026 frontier pretraining disclosure in this corpus — DeepSeek, MegaScale-MoE, PanGu Ultra MoE, ZAYA1 — uses DeepSpeed as its core engine.

The MoE-systems research the frameworks are absorbing

Megatron-Core and torchtitan’s dispatcher zoos are absorbing a wave of MoE-systems papers, several already shipped at scale exceeding either open framework’s public demonstrations. COMET (arXiv:2502.19811, ByteDance+SJTU, MLSys 2025) attacks a problem distinct from DeepEP’s: instead of optimizing the all-to-all kernel itself, it does thread-block-level decomposition of the tensors between MoE sub-operations, splitting GPU thread blocks into concurrent “communication” and “computation” blocks — 1.96x single-layer, 1.71x end-to-end speedup, hiding 86.5% of communication latency versus 68.6% (Tutel) and 29.2% (FasterMoE). It runs in ByteDance’s ten-thousand-GPU production clusters since early 2025, open-sourced inside bytedance/flux — a materially different mechanism from DeepEP that could in principle layer on top of either NVSHMEM-based EP, though no public source discusses combining them, and Flux/COMET have no Blackwell backend as of their last commit (August 2025), verified via GitHub API — a real, checkable gap for anyone assuming ByteDance’s stack is GB200-ready.

MegaScale-MoE (arXiv:2505.11432, ByteDance/PKU, EuroSys ’26) is the fullest disclosed production MoE systems paper after DeepSeek’s own. It inverts standard practice: sequence parallelism instead of TP for the attention path (~25% of TP’s communication latency intra-node), and for the FFN path, when top-k exceeds the EP world size, replaces standard all-to-all dispatch with allgather+local-scatter+reduce-scatter — a different trick from both DeepEP and HybridEP. Measured: 352B MoE, 1,440 H800, 1.41M tok/s, 1.88x over Megatron-LM; a Mixtral-8x7B ablation across H800/H20/A100 shows a consistent 1.58x MFU gain, indicating the mechanism generalizes across GPU generations. Production use confirmed training a 200B-total/20B-active MoE across 10,000+ GPUs for months.

Triton-distributed (ByteDance-Seed, arXiv:2504.19442) is the compiler-level answer to Flux/COMET’s hand-written kernels — compiling comm-overlap kernels from a tile-centric Triton DSL, up to 44.97x versus raw NCCL/RCCL on microbenchmarks, and it’s the only one of these libraries with working AMD ROCm backends today, independently confirmed by AMD’s own ROCm blog. For any build hedging vendors or anticipating Blackwell gaps in ByteDance’s Hopper-era libraries, this is the more portable bet.

Newer academic ideas worth flagging as the visible edge of what comes after EPLB, none yet adopted in a frontier lab’s public stack: ScMoE (arXiv:2404.05019, decouples communication from layer-sequential ordering, 1.49x train/1.82x inference, already production-adopted by Meituan’s LongCat-Flash — one of the few academic MoE-systems ideas that has actually shipped); FlowMoE (arXiv:2510.00207, NeurIPS 2025, unified pipeline scheduler with joint expert-relayout/routing optimization, 1.13-1.82x over prior schedulers); and two 2026 EPLB-successors, UltraEP (arXiv:2606.04101) and LAER-MoE (arXiv:2602.11686), both targeting dynamic training-time expert re-layout on rack-scale nodes but not cleanly quantified in the underlying research pass — low-confidence entries worth a follow-up if expert-rebalancing becomes critical-path.

Fork-vs-build guidance

One recommendation, stated plainly: fork Megatron-Core, do not build from a clean-sheet PyTorch-native or JAX stack, and do not treat the fork as configuration-only — budget for sustained upstream patching.

Megatron-Core is the only framework here with (a) a primary-source paper detailing its MoE stack down to the dispatcher class and CUDA-Graph memory layout, (b) validated throughput on the exact target hardware generation for the exact model class, and (c) an open issue tracker letting a team pre-identify correctness bugs — not performance bugs — before committing capital. TorchTitan’s GB200-aware MoE/quantization engineering is genuinely comparable or better in places, but every public validation point tops out at 1-2K GPUs and its fault-tolerance layer is validated only at 32 GPUs with a replica-group failure model that doesn’t match a 100k-GPU job’s failure granularity. MaxText’s compiler-driven sharding and goodput culture are worth studying for principles, but the framework has zero production GB200 support at the code level — not a close call. AMD’s Primus and PanGu Ultra MoE’s MindSpeed are real, working systems built for non-NVIDIA silicon (ROCm/MI-series, Ascend NPUs respectively); Baidu’s PaddlePaddle-Fleet stack is actually NVIDIA-native (ERNIE 4.5’s disclosed 47%-MFU run used 2,016 H800s [PAPER]) but validated three orders of magnitude below the reader’s target GPU count — none of the three is a candidate for a 100k-GPU GB200 build. DeepSpeed has structurally exited frontier pretraining.

The concrete modification list to scope before committing to a Megatron-Core fork:

  • Fix or work around the EP+comm-overlap+uneven-VPP deadlock (#5749/#1810) before it’s hit at scale — patch the shared-CUDA-event synchronization for a consistent same-order invariant, or restrict initial de-risking runs (Ch7) to uniform VPP layouts until the upstream fix lands.
  • Validate the local-checkpoint corruption path (#5281) end-to-end before relying on ft_launcher’s fast-restart mechanism; add a checksum step on local-checkpoint restore with automatic fallback to the last-known-good global checkpoint.
  • Test Megatron-FSDP + optimizer CPU offload checkpointing under real load (#4910) rather than assuming the combination works because both features are individually documented as production-ready.
  • Budget engineering time to mature ECHO and Paged Stashing beyond their current merged-but-not-default state; plan de-risking runs both with and without full CUDA-Graph capture, since sync-free full-iteration graphs remain roadmapped, not default.
  • Evaluate HybridEP against DeepEP directly on target GB200/GB300 hardware early — HybridEP’s Table 7 latency advantage is specifically a GB200 NVL72 story; DeepEP alone, tuned for Hopper, won’t capture it.
  • Treat Megatron-Bridge as checkpoint-interop and recipe-scaffolding only, not the training loop, given #4590’s own acknowledgment that downstream frameworks still patch Megatron-Core directly.
  • Consider layering COMET/Flux-style overlap or Triton-distributed on top of the chosen EP dispatcher as a research track, not a day-one dependency, given the unresolved Blackwell-backend gap in ByteDance’s Hopper-era libraries — Triton-distributed’s compiler-first, cross-vendor approach is the safer bet if pursued.
  • Prototype LatentMoE (already production in Nemotron-3 Super/Ultra) as a dispatch-payload-compression lever independent of the EP-degree decisions in Ch16, since it addresses a different point in the design space than either large-EP (DeepSeek/Megatron) or small-EP-plus-deep-PP (PanGu Ultra MoE).

Implications for the 10T/200B run

Frameworks are not a solved problem at this scale in mid-2026 — they are a set of specific, named, currently-open correctness bugs sitting exactly at the intersection of the features a 10T/200B-active MoE will use simultaneously (EP, communication overlap, uneven pipeline layouts, FSDP, CPU offload, fault tolerance). Megatron-Core is the right base precisely because its maturity means these gaps are visible and filed, several already partially fixed, rather than undiscovered. A team should treat the framework decision as settled early and redirect the saved decision-cycles toward the modification list above — particularly the EP-comm-overlap deadlock and the local-checkpoint corruption path, both silent-failure modes far more expensive to discover mid-run at 100,000 GPUs than in a pre-flight validation pass (Ch20). The dropless-MoE CUDA-Graph story and the HybridEP-versus-DeepEP choice are the two levers most likely to move measured MFU on the actual target hardware, and both are new enough — merged within months of this writing — that no public source yet demonstrates them composed together at anything near 100k-GPU scale. That gap, more than any single missing feature, is the honest state of frameworks for a run of this size in July 2026.

Chapter 18 · Part IV — The Machine

Networking at 100k: NVL72 Domains, Fabrics, MRC, and Collectives

Every other chapter in this book’s infrastructure arc treats the network as a budget line: a bandwidth number to divide FLOPs by, a latency term to hide behind overlap. This chapter is about where those numbers actually come from, and about the uncomfortable fact that the network fabric a 10T/200B-active MoE run depends on above roughly 10,000 GPUs does not exist as a single vendor-published design — it has to be assembled from five hyperscalers’ independently-converged, mostly-proprietary engineering, cross-checked against one open academic-industrial standard still finding its silicon and one very new open transport spec that only became public in May 2026.

The NVL72 Domain: Mechanics and What It Changes

The atomic scale-up unit for the entire GB200/GB300 generation is now settled industry-wide: 72 Blackwell GPUs, 1.8 TB/s NVLink5 per GPU (18 links × 100 GB/s bidirectional each), roughly 130 TB/s of aggregate switched bandwidth through nine NVSwitch trays — arithmetically consistent as 72 × 1.8 TB/s [14-networking-scale.md, OFFICIAL-BLOG, NVIDIA docs 2025–2026]. Physically this is 5,184 copper cables forming a backplane compute trays “bite into” on insertion, using Amphenol Paladin HD 224G connectors rated at 900 GB/s unidirectional per pair [14-networking-scale.md, CREDIBLE-HEARSAY/OFFICIAL, vendor blogs 2025]. Every cloud that has published a GB200 reference design — Azure’s ND GB200 v6, GCP’s A4X, AWS’s P6e-GB200 UltraServer, NVIDIA’s own SuperPOD RA — converges on the identical 72-GPU/1.8-TB/s-per-GPU/≈28.8-Tbps-scale-out-per-rack figure, making NVL72 the first genuinely standardized scale-up unit of the accelerator era rather than something each cloud independently re-derives [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §9, DOC, multiple vendors 2025].

That standardization is not yet matched by operational maturity. SemiAnalysis’s field reporting from mid-2025 states plainly that the NVLink copper backplane, even after extensive burn-in, “is not that reliable,” that diagnostic tooling lags the hardware, and that no large-scale synchronous training run had yet completed on NVL72 at a leading lab or CSP [14-networking-scale.md §2, CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, SemiAnalysis Aug 2025]. NVIDIA’s own DGX GB200 release notes corroborate active firmware work here — recent updates address “NVLink Reduction” accounting and “Head-of-queue Lifetime Limit” interactions when NVLink Recovery is enabled, now on by default, implying it was not reliably on earlier [14-networking-scale.md §2, OFFICIAL-BLOG, NVIDIA 2025–2026]. Separately, GB200/GB300 backplane and cable NVLink sync issues were traced to a firmware bug (not hardware) that took six to seven months to resolve — early-ship units shipped November 2024, stable firmware v1.3 only landing spring/summer 2025, affecting AWS’s, GCP’s, and Meta’s early NVL36x2 deployments broadly [22-local-semianalysis-htmls.md §ClusterMAX, CREDIBLE-HEARSAY]. For a build committing capital to a specific NVL72 delivery window, this is a live risk, not settled history [14-networking-scale.md Open Questions §1].

The domain’s real significance for a 10T/200B MoE is architectural, not just electrical. With 72 GPUs sharing one non-blocking NVLink fabric, expert-parallel (EP) dispatch and combine can execute entirely inside NVLink for EP degrees up to 72, rather than crossing into RDMA/scale-out territory the way EP traffic had to at EP≤8 in the H100-node era. Meta’s NCCLX paper documents a host-kernel coordinated mode in which the MoE all-to-all lets the CUDA kernel perform the NVLink copy in parallel with CPU-driven RDMA whenever there’s no data dependency between the two hops — EP dispatch inside NVL72 and DP/PP traffic on RDMA can genuinely overlap rather than serialize [14-networking-scale.md §2, PAPER, Meta NCCLX Jan 2026]. DeepSeek’s DeepEP embodies the identical idea one layer down: an asymmetric-domain forwarding kernel routes tokens IB→matching-index-GPU, then NVLink→target expert, overlapping the two hops on roughly 20 SMs [14-networking-scale.md §2, CODE/PAPER-adjacent, DeepEP + arXiv:2606.05951]. Tensor-parallel degree inherits the same headroom — TP can now size up to 72 without leaving the NVLink domain, versus TP≤8 previously — reshaping the classic 3D-parallelism layout: EP and TP both live inside NVL72, while PP and DP get pushed onto the RDMA scale-out fabric (full composition math is Ch16’s territory; this chapter establishes only the fabric boundary that math has to respect).

Scale-Out at 100k: The Production Fleet and the Reference-Architecture Gap

NVIDIA’s own published reference architectures quietly stop being non-blocking beyond roughly 10,000 GPUs. The GB200 NVL72 SuperPOD RA’s compute InfiniBand fabric tables out cleanly to 16 Scalable Units — 9,216 GPUs — but it is a three-tier leaf/spine/core fat-tree (32 leaf + 24 spine per SU), not a two-tier design: the core tier appears as soon as a second SU joins the fabric (18 core switches at 2 SU/1,152 GPUs), scaling to 512 leaf / 384 spine / 144 core at the full 16-SU/9,216-GPU configuration. The separate storage/Ethernet fabric adds its own additional “super-spine” tier beyond 4 SUs to preserve non-blocking storage access as the SuperPOD scales toward 16 SUs, and beyond 16 SU the document says only “contact your NVIDIA representative” [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §1–2, DOC, NVIDIA Nov 2025]. Every fabric operating past that line — OCI’s 131,072-GPU Supercluster, Meta’s 100K+ clusters, Azure Fairwater, AWS’s Rainier — runs on a proprietary hyperscaler invention (MRC/SRv6, Disaggregated Scheduled Fabric, EFA “10P10U,” Oracle Acceleron RoCE), not an NVIDIA-blessed blueprint [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md TL;DR #1]. This is arguably the single most load-bearing fact in this chapter: there is no vendor-published reference architecture covering the full cluster size a 10T/200B run needs. The rack/SU/compute-fabric layer is exhaustively documented; the 10K→100K+ scale-out layer must be assembled in-house or licensed from whichever cloud hosts the build.

Three production papers anchor what “assembled in-house” looks like at hyperscale, and all three converge independently on the same handful of design moves.

Meta’s RoCE fabric (SIGCOMM’24, Gangidi et al.) is the canonical scale-out reference: 24,000-GPU backend clusters at publication, a two-stage Clos “AI Zone” topology, and routing evolving through four stages — naive ECMP, path-pinning, Enhanced-ECMP with queue-pair (QP) scaling, centralized Traffic Engineering — because flow entropy in AI training traffic is pathologically low versus general cloud traffic [14-networking-scale.md §3, PAPER, Aug 2024]. QP scaling to 16 gave up to 40% AllReduce improvement over QP=1; Traffic Engineering gave a further 5–10% but was not adopted at datacenter scale due to fragility under simultaneous link failures, leaving Enhanced-ECMP-with-QP-scaling as production default. The paper’s most counterintuitive finding: Meta turned DCQCN off entirely for 400G deployments after firmware bugs, running over a year on PFC-only flow control with “stable performance” — contradicting the assumption that DCQCN is mandatory RoCE hygiene at scale [14-networking-scale.md §3, Surprises]. That fabric did not stop at 24K: a September 2025 @Scale disclosure states Meta’s production RoCE backend scaled from 4,000 to 24,000 to 129,000 GPUs in under two years [45-conference-archive-mining.md TL;DR #1, OFFICIAL-BLOG, Sept 2025] — evidence that Ethernet/RoCE does not cap out near 32K–64K GPUs before something like MRC becomes mandatory.

Alibaba’s HPN 7.0 (SIGCOMM’24, Qian et al.) bets on a two-tier dual-plane Clos instead of three tiers, fitting 15,360 GPUs in one non-oversubscribed Pod, purpose-built to avoid hash polarization [14-networking-scale.md §3, PAPER, Aug 2024]. Its failure telemetry is the most granular production reliability data in this corpus: 0.057%/month NIC-to-ToR link failure rate, 0.051%/month ToR critical-failure rate, 5,000–60,000 link-flapping events per day cluster-wide, translating to only 1–2 crashed jobs per month at 15K-GPU-Pod scale thanks to non-stacked dual-ToR (each NIC connects to two independent ToRs with no sync link, avoiding the >40% of Alibaba’s critical failures traced to stacked-dual-ToR sync bugs) [14-networking-scale.md §3, Hard numbers]. A single-ToR failure costs only 6.25% throughput degradation rather than a full halt, and multi-chip chassis switches showed 32.6× more operational instability and 3.77× more critical hardware failures than single-chip 51.2 Tbps switches — a concrete reason the industry converged on single-ASIC switches for AI fabrics [14-networking-scale.md §3, Surprises].

xAI’s Colossus 2 is the counter-example on disclosure: confirmed live at gigawatt scale as of January 2026, two roughly 110,000-GPU clusters (one GB200, one GB300) on NVIDIA Spectrum-X Ethernet, targeting ~2 GW / ~555K GPUs across Memphis — with zero peer-reviewed or arXiv paper behind it, unlike Meta, Alibaba, and OpenAI [14-networking-scale.md §3, CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, Jan 2026]. Its numbers should be read as trade-press triangulation, not verified disclosure.

Three further cross-checks matter for the reader’s own build. Azure’s Fairwater fabric collapses a three-tier Clos into two by splitting each NIC’s 800 Gbps port into 8×100 Gbps sub-ports fanned across parallel planes (worked example: 512 Tier-0 switches × 256 NICs = 131,072 NICs on two tiers); Fairwater 2 Atlanta goes further, splitting an entire compute tray’s 3,200G into 32 planes to reach 524,288 GPUs on a two-layer network — at the cost that GPUs on the same tray can no longer talk over the scale-out network and must route through PXN over NVLink, with MRC handling the scale-out layer [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §5, OFFICIAL-BLOG, Nov 2025; 22-local-semianalysis-htmls.md §Microsoft/OpenAI]. OCI’s 131,072-GPU Supercluster publishes an exact tiered latency budget — Tier-0 ≤256 GPUs at ≤2 µs, Tier-1 ≤2,048 at ≤5 µs, Tier-2 ≤131,072 at ≤8 µs [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §7, OFFICIAL-BLOG, Oct 2025]. And Meta’s DSF, disclosed at Hot Chips 2025, publishes an exact Layer-2 non-blocking ceiling of 18,432 GPUs at 800G per zone, four zones composing one building-scale domain [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §8, TALK, Aug 2025].

The condensed cross-architecture picture:

[51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §9, DOC/OFFICIAL-BLOG, multiple vendors 2025]

The through-line across these designs is a shared bet: static/deterministic source routing over dynamic control-plane routing, multi-plane topologies trading a third Clos tier for parallel lower-radix planes, and endpoint-driven congestion and failure handling — no two hyperscalers arrived here by sharing a document; each reinvented the same ideas independently [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §10]. The RA paper trail also encodes an assumption a 10T-parameter MoE run will violate: every topology here is rail-optimized against a DP/PP-dominant traffic pattern, corroborated by Alibaba’s table showing PP Send/Recv (6 MB) is the lowest-volume collective in their GPT-3-175B config, versus 5.5 GB for DP AllReduce [14-networking-scale.md §2, PAPER]. A 10T-MoE run with EP spanning multiple SUs pushes proportionally more spine/core-tier traffic than these switch-count ratios (32 leaf : 24 spine per SU) were sized to assume [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §10]. The RA tables are correct topology inventories; their implicit sizing rationale needs re-deriving for an EP-heavy workload, not inheriting.

The Transport Layer: MRC, UEC, Falcon, and the RDMA Fork

The primary source for OpenAI’s MRC (Multipath Reliable Connection) is not a conference talk — it is a joint arXiv paper, “Resilient AI Supercomputer Networking using MRC and SRv6” (arXiv:2605.04333, submitted May 5, 2026), with 50 co-authors spanning OpenAI, Microsoft, AMD, Broadcom, and NVIDIA, plus a formal OCP 1.0 specification and reference implementation repository [14-networking-scale.md §1, PAPER, May 2026]. The disclosure venue matters for citation hygiene: MRC’s real public debut post-dates OCP Global Summit 2025 (which instead saw ESUN, a related but distinct scale-up-Ethernet workstream, launch) — no OCP Summit 2025 talk on MRC specifically exists [14-networking-scale.md §1, Surprises].

MRC is a minimal extension to RoCEv2 Reliable Connection semantics, not a clean-slate hardware transport like Falcon. It restricts the verb surface to WRITE and WRITE-IMM only — no READ, SEND, or ATOMIC, RNR explicitly unsupported — trading generality for cross-vendor implementation simplicity [14-networking-scale.md §1, PAPER]. Out-of-order placement is native: every packet carries the RDMA virtual address and rkey so the NIC places arriving packets directly into memory regardless of order, making spraying-without-resequencing tractable at wire speed. PFC is disabled entirely; MRC runs Ethernet lossy/best-effort, pushing all reliability onto the endpoint NIC — a break from both Meta’s PFC-based deployment and Alibaba’s deep-buffer-switch approach [14-networking-scale.md §1, PAPER]. Spraying works via 32-bit entropy values (EVs) striped across the UDP source port and IPv6 flow label; each queue pair maintains 128–256 EVs, round-robins per packet, and balances them across network planes, with each EV deterministically mapped via SRv6 micro-segment IDs (uSIDs) to one static path — genuinely static source routing, not adaptive/hash-based ECMP [14-networking-scale.md §1, PAPER]. Reliability offload — SACK retransmission, “packet trimming” to distinguish congestion-drops from failure-drops, background path probing — lives entirely on the NIC; failure detection runs in tens of microseconds, full EV-set remapping completes in a few seconds [14-networking-scale.md §1, PAPER].

MRC is already in production on OpenAI’s largest GB200 clusters, including OCI’s Abilene, TX site and Microsoft’s Fairwater supercomputers, across ConnectX-8 (800 Gb/s), AMD Pollara/Vulcano (400 Gb/s), and Broadcom Thor Ultra NICs simultaneously. NCCL testing at 42,000-GPU scale measured 92 GB/s send/recv and peak link bandwidth of roughly 770 Gb/s (96% of theoretical), with a stated theoretical target of 131,072 GPUs; two named production clusters give latency of 5.09 µs same-rack and 6.54 µs cross-tier [14-networking-scale.md §1, Hard numbers/PAPER]. The strongest argument for why MRC needed to exist as a new open spec rather than adopting UEC 1.0 wholesale is heterogeneity: Meta, Alibaba, and xAI each run single-vendor NIC fleets, while OpenAI/Microsoft need ConnectX-8, two distinct AMD NIC ASICs, and Broadcom Thor Ultra to interoperate on one wire protocol [14-networking-scale.md §1, Surprises]. NVIDIA’s own framing positions MRC as an alternative transport alongside Spectrum-X’s native “Adaptive RDMA,” hedging by supporting both on the same ConnectX SuperNIC silicon [14-networking-scale.md §1, OFFICIAL-BLOG].

MRC’s relationship to the wider standardization effort is partial convergence, not adoption. UEC 1.0 (released June 11, 2025, 560+ pages) sprays packets using real-time congestion telemetry rather than hop-by-hop hashing, moving away from PFC toward receiver-driven congestion management [14-networking-scale.md §5, OFFICIAL-BLOG/spec, June 2025] — architecturally close to MRC’s endpoint-driven, lossy-tolerant philosophy, but dynamic/telemetry-driven where MRC is static/SRv6-source-routed; MRC’s paper cites UET work as prior art it partially diverges from, trading sophistication for determinism and simpler cross-vendor implementation [14-networking-scale.md §1, PAPER]. Google’s Falcon is the opposite design point: a genuine clean-slate hardware transport, opened at OCP Summit October 2023 (older than MRC), formalized in a SIGCOMM 2025 paper — 200 Gbps/120 Mops/sec first implementation, up to 8× lower completion time and 65% higher goodput than ConnectX-7 RoCE under congestion, ~3 µs one-way latency, only 5–6% die area / 3–4% power to integrate, drawing on a decade of internal Google transport work (Carousel, Snap, Swift, PLB) [14-networking-scale.md §5, PAPER, SIGCOMM 2025]. It is the closest thing to a true UEC-native hardware transport shipping today, but has no public confirmation at 100K+-GPU training scale the way MRC does.

AWS’s EFA/SRD completes the picture at Anthropic’s Project Rainier — roughly 500,000 Trainium2 chips, Indiana campus, 455 MW across seven buildings [14-networking-scale.md §5, OFFICIAL-BLOG/CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, late 2025]. EFAv3 delivers up to 800 Gb/s/chip on Trn2 (deliberately reduced on Trn2-Ultra to prioritize NeuronLink), packet latency improved from 9 µs to 6.5 µs [14-networking-scale.md §5]. AWS’s internal codename for its EFA Gen3 fabric, surfaced only through a named-engineer interview, is “10P10U”: tens of petabytes/second of UltraCluster bisection bandwidth plus 10 µs worst-case any-to-any latency, with active rerouting rather than ring-based multipathing [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §4, TALK/CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, SDxCentral, Ron Diamant, Nov 2025]. One unresolved inconsistency: Diamant states NeuronLink delivers “two terabytes per second at approximately one microsecond per hop,” while AWS’s own Neuron docs state 1.28 TB/s per chip — a discrepancy this research could not reconcile [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §4, Surprises].

Collectives: NCCL’s 2.23–2.28 Arc and the SHARP Reality Check

NCCL’s evolution across five point releases through mid-2026 tracks the transition to NVL72-scale domains directly. Version 2.23 introduced PAT (Parallel Aggregated Trees), giving AllGather/ReduceScatter logarithmic-latency scaling versus Ring’s linear scaling and working on arbitrary rank counts [14-networking-scale.md §7, OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2025]. Version 2.26 optimized PAT so up to 16 warps run 16 parallel trees concurrently. Version 2.27 is most relevant to NVL72: symmetric memory, up to 9× lower latency for small messages but scoped to a single NVLink domain (up to NVL72 on GB200/GB300) — a scale-up optimization correlating positively with EP traffic already being NVL72-bounded [45-conference-archive-mining.md §e; 14-networking-scale.md §7]. Secondary sources disagree on the exact multiple (7.6× vs 9×), preserved here rather than resolved. The same release extended SHARP to AllGather/ReduceScatter (cutting SM consumption from 16 to 6) and added communicator-shrink to exclude failed GPUs mid-training without a full restart [14-networking-scale.md §7]. Version 2.28 added GIN (GPU-Initiated Networking), letting CUDA kernels directly trigger network transfers, matching NVSHMEM’s device-initiated performance within roughly 1–2% [14-networking-scale.md §7, PAPER, arXiv:2606.05951, June 2026].

Meta’s NCCLX/CTran paper (arXiv:2510.20171, Jan 2026, built for Llama4) is the richest primary source on production 100K+-GPU collectives engineering here. Its central break from stock NCCL is a zero-copy, SM-free transport: instead of routing data through a pre-registered FIFO buffer on both ends, CTran lets the NIC DMA engine RDMA directly between user buffers with zero device-to-device copy and no kernel involvement [14-networking-scale.md §7, PAPER, Jan 2026]. The countervailing risk — zero-copy hands the entire message to the network at once, poorly matched to Meta’s non-DCQCN strategy — is addressed with Dynamic Queue Pair Load Balancing tuned higher for cross-DC links, cutting switch buffer buildup by an order of magnitude. Measured gains: up to 12% steady-step latency reduction, up to 11× faster cluster init at 96K-GPU scale, and — the single most concrete number for modeling straggler/overlap budgets — cross-hop latency multipliers of 7× cross-AI-Zone and 30× cross-DC-building relative to same-rack [14-networking-scale.md §7, Hard numbers]. Microsoft’s MSCCL++ (ASPLOS March 2026, arXiv:2504.09014) sits below NCCL as a portable primitive/DSL, claiming up to 3.8× speedup over NCCL/RCCL/MSCCL and already adopted by AMD’s RCCL — a de facto cross-vendor standard [14-networking-scale.md §7, PAPER].

SHARP’s actual production footprint is thinner than marketing suggests. NVIDIA’s Quantum-X800 delivers 14.4 TFLOPS of in-network SHARP compute per switch, and NCCL 2.27’s SHARP extension is real and shipped — but no primary source confirms SHARP in continuous use at a specific 100K+-GPU frontier run; available material is capability description, not a field report [14-networking-scale.md §7]. There is also a measurement trap for the community’s most widely-recommended benchmarking tool: Stas Bekman’s all_reduce_bench.py, validated against nccl-tests, computes bandwidth using the same busbw_coeff = 2*(ranks-1)/ranks correction — a formula assuming a ring algorithm’s data-movement ratio [40-stas-network-comms-debug.md §1, CODE, 07/2026]. When SHARP/NVLS is active, correct reduction is an N+1-sends model, not 2N; the ring-assuming formula systematically under-counts true bandwidth on any NVLS-active fabric — an open, unfixed nccl-tests issue (#153), so any NVLS-active number from this script should be read as a pessimistic lower bound [40-stas-network-comms-debug.md §1, Surprises; Open Questions]. Separately, SHARP correctness cannot be assumed safe merely because health-check tooling passes — a documented incident found an NVSwitch ALU silently corrupting AllReduce results under NVLS while DCGMI reported healthy throughout [14-networking-scale.md §8; 22-local-semianalysis-htmls.md §Multi-datacenter]. Independent numerical verification of in-network compute paths is a distinct discipline from link-failure health-checking.

MoE Bandwidth Budgeting: EP All-to-All Against DP/PP AllReduce

The traffic pattern a 10T-total/200B-active MoE generates is structurally different from what every reference architecture above was sized against. NVIDIA’s own GB200 RA rail-optimization principle — traffic per rail stays one hop from every other SU node, cross-rail traffic traverses the spine — assumes ring/tree gradient AllReduce and rail-aligned tensor/pipeline collectives, structurally predictable and mostly rail-local [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §10]. EP dispatch and combine are not rail-local: every GPU in an EP group potentially exchanges tokens with every other GPU on every MoE layer, an inherently all-to-all pattern. The good news is that EP degree up to 72 stays entirely inside the non-blocking NVL72 domain — an all-to-all pattern that would be catastrophic on a rail-optimized scale-out fabric is comparatively cheap confined to a switched 130 TB/s domain. The risk is EP degree spanning multiple SUs, pushing more spine/core-tier traffic than the leaf:spine ratios (32:24 per SU) were provisioned to assume [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §10].

External validation for “all-to-all needs a switched fabric, not a torus” comes from AWS’s own roadmap. Trainium2, deployed at Project Rainier, uses a pure 3D-torus mesh (4×4×4 = 64 chips, no switches) for NeuronLink scale-up; Trainium3 abandons this for an all-to-all switched fabric, with AWS stating explicitly that torus topologies “are not optimized for frontier MoE models which require all-to-all collectives,” with oversubscription degrading badly as EP message sizes grow from 16 KB to 1 MB [22-local-semianalysis-htmls.md §Trainium3, CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, Dec 2025]. AWS discloses a concrete decision rule: its 64-chip switched topology suffices for MoE models with 2–3T total parameters, while models above roughly 4T benefit from a larger 144-chip domain — independent, cross-vendor confirmation that EP bandwidth-boundedness shapes hardware roadmaps directly, not a detail that falls out of generic provisioning [22-local-semianalysis-htmls.md §Trainium3].

The practical budgeting question is whether EP all-to-all traffic and DP/PP AllReduce genuinely overlap rather than compete for the same wires. Inside NVL72, both Meta’s NCCLX paper and DeepSeek’s DeepEP kernel answer yes by construction — EP rides NVLink, DP/PP AllReduce rides RDMA, independent unless a specific layout forces cross-traffic. NVIDIA’s Megatron-Core MoE “Parallel Folding” technique matters directly: decoupling attention-layer and MoE-layer parallelism configs breaks the traditional EP≤DP constraint, freeing the layout to keep EP inside NVL72 while DP spans further out [45-conference-archive-mining.md §e, PAPER, arXiv:2603.07685, Mar 2026]. The same report discloses per-GPU throughput for a real 685B/37B-active MoE — 1,233 TFLOPS/GPU on GB300, 1,048 on GB200 — a citable, framework-vendor MFU-class anchor (full derivation is Ch21’s territory) [45-conference-archive-mining.md Hard numbers, Mar 2026]. ByteDance’s MegaScale-MoE adds a caution: on a 352B-parameter MoE across 1,440 Hopper GPUs it achieves 1.65–1.88× speedup over Megatron-LM through per-layer-type overlap, but MFU still declines from 32.48% to 27.89% as GPU count scales — even a purpose-built MoE communication system doesn’t solve weak-scaling MFU decay, only shifts the curve up [45-conference-archive-mining.md §f, PAPER, arXiv:2505.11432, May 2025].

Batching discipline generalizes directly to MoE communication design: Stas’s all_reduce_latency_comp.py shows one 4 GB reduction dramatically outperforms 1,000× 4 MB reductions, arguing for batching expert-gradient or router-logit reductions rather than firing one collective per expert [40-stas-network-comms-debug.md §1, CODE]. NCCL_DEBUG_SUBSYS=COLL call-counting — grepping NCCL INFO AllToAll/AllGather counts per iteration — is an underused verification method for confirming actual all-to-all volume matches theoretical per-layer prediction [40-stas-network-comms-debug.md §3].

Multi-Datacenter Training: Where the Physics Bites

Synchronous, single-building-or-single-campus training remains the dominant frontier paradigm as of mid-2026 — OpenAI/Microsoft, Google, and Anthropic all report synchronous gradient descent for flagship runs [14-networking-scale.md §8]. Google is the earliest confirmed instance of crossing a DC boundary at all: Gemini 1.0’s technical report and Google Cloud CEO Thomas Kurian’s statements confirm Gemini Ultra was trained “across multiple sites, and multiple clusters within those sites,” though whether this means true multi-campus WAN training or multi-building-within-one-campus is undisclosed [14-networking-scale.md §8, OFFICIAL-BLOG/CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 2024]. SemiAnalysis’s “Multi-Datacenter Training” analysis is the most detailed public treatment: roughly 5 Pbit/s of inter-campus bandwidth within a region and roughly 1 Pbit/s between regions for a 100T+-parameter-class model, at which line rate exchanging a 400 TB weight payload takes only 0.64 seconds [14-networking-scale.md §8, CREDIBLE-HEARSAY]. The hard physical floor is speed-of-light-in-fiber latency — a US east-west coast RTT floor of 43.2 ms that improves with neither better silicon nor cleverer software, only fewer syncs or shorter distances [14-networking-scale.md §8, Surprises]. DWDM capacity is not the bottleneck — 400ZR gives 25.5 Tbps per fiber pair, long-haul systems up to 121.6 Tbps [14-networking-scale.md §8]. The proposed mitigation generalizes the NVL72→AI-Zone→cross-DC hop hierarchy one level further: hierarchical synchronous gradient descent, syncing every step within a building-scale domain and less often across regions.

Silicon and fiber infrastructure for this already exist. Broadcom’s Jericho4 — a 51.2 Tbps fabric router, shipping since August 2025 — is explicitly positioned for distributed-AI-across-datacenters: “lossless RoCE across 100km+,” UEC-compliant, HBM-based packet buffering claimed at 160× on-chip SRAM [14-networking-scale.md §6, OFFICIAL-BLOG, Aug 2025]. Microsoft’s AI WAN backbone connecting Atlanta and Wisconsin uses over 120,000 new fiber miles [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §5, OFFICIAL-BLOG, Nov 2025], and Meta names deploying “some of the largest transoceanic fiber cable systems in the world” as AI-training infrastructure [45-conference-archive-mining.md §d, OFFICIAL-BLOG, Sept 2025]. NVIDIA’s InfiniBand MetroX (40 km extension) is essentially unused for this purpose — even InfiniBand-committed Microsoft uses Ethernet ZR/ZR+ optics between datacenters [22-local-semianalysis-htmls.md §Multi-datacenter].

The asynchronous alternative is maturing but remains unproven at frontier quality bars for pretraining specifically. Streaming DiLoCo (arXiv:2501.18512, Jan 2025) claims data-parallel-comparable quality at roughly 400× less cross-island bandwidth through scheduled partial sync and 4-bit outer-gradient quantization [14-networking-scale.md §8, PAPER]. Decoupled DiLoCo (DeepMind, ~April 2026) combines DiLoCo with Pathways’ heterogeneous-speed orchestration, reporting 88% goodput under high simulated hardware-failure rates [14-networking-scale.md §8, PAPER, secondary-sourced]. The cleanest revealed-preference signal against fully decentralized pretraining comes from the team most invested in proving it works: Prime Intellect’s INTELLECT-3 (106B MoE, ~90.8% AIME24) was trained on centralized GPUs despite the company’s decentralized-training mission [14-networking-scale.md §8, Surprises]. Decentralization already works for RL/post-training, but synchronous, single-campus training with hierarchical sync as the multi-site extension remains dominant for pretraining, and no source confirms any 2026 lab has crossed true multi-campus synchronous pretraining [14-networking-scale.md Open Questions §6].

Failure Math: Optics, Flaps, and the Mitigation Stack

The most granular public disclosure of the actual failure chain behind a link flap comes from OCI: a 400G Ethernet link’s FEC tolerates at most three sequential uncorrectable 5,140-bit code blocks before going down, and three uncorrectable errors inside a 20-nanosecond window trigger a link-down event. The optical layer recovers within milliseconds, but full link bring-up — PMD, PMA, FEC, PCS, MAC, Datalink, and IP layers each independently re-running link-training — stretches into a 10–15 second outage, and each flap triggers a full BGP/OSPF network-wide reconvergence with real risk of transient routing microloops [51-cluster-reference-architectures.md §7, OFFICIAL-BLOG, Oct 2025]. OCI’s “link debounce” mitigation prevents a momentary PMA-layer blip from cascading into the full reconvergence tax. Alibaba’s HPN telemetry gives the aggregate picture this has to survive: 5,000–60,000 link-flapping events per day cluster-wide at 15K-GPU-Pod scale, with non-stacked dual-ToR absorbing that down to only 1–2 crashed jobs per month [14-networking-scale.md §3].

Co-packaged optics reliability sits on contested ground between vendor claims and independent validation. Broadcom states TH5-Bailly (second-gen CPO) testing at Meta demonstrated 1 million link-hours without a single flap and a claimed 2.5M-hour MTBF, versus “significantly higher failure rates” for pluggable FR4 optics [14-networking-scale.md §6, OFFICIAL-BLOG, vendor-sourced, Oct 2025]. A related Meta/Broadcom dataset presented at ECOC 2025 frames the same testing differently: 1,049k 400G-port-device-hours across 15 Bailly switches with zero failures, extending to 15M port-device-hours with zero uncorrectable codewords through the first 4M, yielding an MTBF comparison of 2.6M device-hours for CPO versus 0.5–1M for pluggables [22-local-semianalysis-htmls.md §CPO book, Jan 2026]. Both point the same direction but are not identical calculations, and both are explicitly caveated as 15 switches over roughly eleven months in a lab, not thousands over years in the field. No independent, non-Broadcom-commissioned link-flap study at true 100K+-link production scale exists for either technology [14-networking-scale.md Open Questions §4]. CPO economics are also more modest than marketing implies at the scale-out layer: for a 200,000-GPU GB300 NVL72 three-layer cluster drawing 435 MW, optical transceivers account for only 17 MW, and moving fully to CPO cuts transceiver power 84% but total cluster power only 2–4% and total cost only 3–7% — the real CPO opportunity is scale-up (removing NVLink copper’s ~2-meter reach ceiling), not scale-out [22-local-semianalysis-htmls.md §CPO book, Jan 2026].

Synthesizing Alibaba’s link-failure telemetry, Meta’s transport choices, and MRC’s detection/reroute timings yields a coherent industry-wide mitigation stack for 100K+-GPU training: multipath spraying at the transport layer so no single link failure blocks a flow (MRC’s EVs, Falcon’s multipath connections, UEC’s telemetry-driven spraying); multi-plane physical topology so a switch or plane failure degrades rather than halts a job (MRC’s multi-plane Clos, Alibaba’s dual-plane); dual-ToR/dual-NIC-port at the host, the most rigorously quantified layer (Alibaba’s 6.25% degradation versus full halt); receiver-driven congestion admission in the collective library rather than transport-layer control alone (Meta’s DCQCN-off choice, MRC’s SACK-plus-trimming); straggler detection as a discipline distinct from link-failure handling (silent, gradual, not caught by health-check tooling — Ch19’s fuller territory, though flapping links are a documented contributor); and independent numerical verification of in-network compute paths, since SHARP/NVLS correctness cannot be assumed safe just because DCGMI passes [14-networking-scale.md §9].

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

The fabric decision for a 100,000-GB200 build is a stack of choices, and the evidence here points toward specific defaults rather than open questions at every layer. Inside the rack, NVL72 is now a de facto industry standard the build should simply inherit — but backplane reliability should be treated as still-hardening rather than closed, and the firmware-driven six-to-seven-month NVLink sync saga of 2024–2025 argues for real schedule slack around any delivery ramp rather than assumed day-one stability. Above the rack, there is no single reference architecture to adopt wholesale: NVIDIA’s own tables stop at 9,216 GPUs, and the 10K→100K+ layer must be built from the hyperscaler playbook synthesized here — static/deterministic source routing, multi-plane two-tier topologies, endpoint-driven failure handling, non-stacked dual-ToR redundancy — whether via MRC directly, an internal equivalent, or a cloud partner whose fabric already implements these patterns at scale.

On transport specifically, MRC’s production deployment at 42K-GPU-tested/131,072-GPU-target scale, its multi-vendor NIC interoperability, and its explicit heterogeneous-fleet design make it the most de-risked open option today for a build that will not be single-vendor NIC-homogeneous by construction; UEC 1.0 is more architecturally ambitious but a year behind in confirmed production deployment at this scale.
