The EP/TP-inside-NVL72, DP/PP-outside-it boundary established here should feed directly into Ch16’s parallelism math as a hard constraint — AWS’s Trainium3 torus-to-switched-fabric pivot is independent confirmation that all-to-all EP traffic breaks any topology not built to carry it. NCCL 2.27+’s symmetric memory and SHARP extensions are worth adopting for their NVL72-scoped latency wins, but SHARP correctness should be independently, continuously verified rather than trusted on DCGMI’s word alone, and any all_reduce_bench.py-style benchmarking pipeline needs its busbw formula corrected for NVLS before the numbers are trusted as anything but a lower bound. Multi-datacenter training is not a near-term requirement at 100,000 GPUs — that scale fits comfortably within a single gigawatt-class campus using hierarchical-sync patterns already proven at building scale — but Jericho4-class inter-campus infrastructure exists today should the program need to federate for a subsequent, larger run.

Part VThe Run

Chapter 19 · Part V — The Run

Fault Tolerance, Checkpointing, and Goodput

A 10T-total/200B-active MoE on 100,000 GB200-class GPUs will not run for six months without interruption; it will run through several interruptions per hour, and the run’s economic case rests on how cheaply those interruptions can be absorbed. This chapter builds the failure-rate ground truth from independent field datasets, works the checkpoint-state math for the strawman spec, and synthesizes the layered reliability architecture — hardware acceptance through algorithmic mitigation — a well-engineered version of this run would need.

The Failure-Rate Ground Truth

The single richest public dataset remains Meta’s Llama 3 405B run: 466 total job interruptions over 54 days on up to 16,384 H100 GPUs — 47 planned, 419 unexpected — yet the team still held >90% effective training time [PAPER, arXiv:2407.21783, Jul 2024]. The unexpected-interruption breakdown is the base rate everything below is measured against: GPU faults 30.1%, HBM3 memory 17.2%, software bugs 12.9%, network switch/cable 8.4%, unplanned host maintenance 7.6%, SRAM 4.5%, host/system processor 4.1%, NCCL watchdog timeouts 1.7%, NIC failures 1.7%, SDC 1.4% — roughly 78% hardware, only 3 of 419 requiring manual intervention [PAPER, arXiv:2407.21783, Jul 2024]. Checkpointing wrote 1MB–4GB per-GPU shards to Meta’s Tectonic filesystem, 7,500 SSD servers delivering 2TB/s sustained and 7TB/s peak aggregate throughput — a number that matters directly for the bandwidth analysis below [PAPER, arXiv:2407.21783, Jul 2024].

MegaScale (ByteDance/Peking, NSDI’24) is the next maturity-curve point: a multi-week 100B+-parameter run on 12,288 GPUs recorded over 100 failure-and-recovery events with the loss curve converging through all of them, >90% auto-diagnosed, mean detection+diagnosis under 10 minutes, recovery within 15 [PAPER, arXiv:2402.15627, Feb 2024]. Its successor ByteRobust reports at genuinely frontier scale: across three months, 778,135 jobs, a fleet exceeding 200,000 GPUs, 43,264 explicit failures (78.1%, dominated by CUDA/CPU errors) and 5,948 implicit failures (job hangs), checkpoint-blocking-time down to 0.01–0.04s, and an Effective Training Time Ratio (ETTR) of up to 97% for a three-month dense run on 9,600 GPUs versus 91% for a one-month MoE run [PAPER, arXiv:2509.16293, Sep 2025] — a dense-vs-MoE gap that recurs below.

The most directly relevant public source for this beat is “Training LLMs with Fault Tolerant HSDP on 100,000 GPUs” (FT-HSDP), which appears — from its “AI-Zone” building-level replica terminology — to be a Meta-style architecture, though the paper does not confirm authorship [PAPER, arXiv:2602.00277, Feb 2026]. It reports 12 replicas across 4 datacenter buildings, each ~8,192 GPUs, and gives this chapter’s single most load-bearing number: at 100,000-GPU scale, a failure occurs every 18 minutes. A naive synchronous-restart baseline spends 10 of every 18 minutes recovering, for only 44% effective training time; their peer-replica recovery scheme — HTTP fetch of optimizer state at ~60ms/GB from a peer’s CPU memory, a 60-second hang-detection timeout, root-cause analysis under 5 seconds at 97.8% accuracy across 1,500 injected-failure tests — cuts total stall to ~3 minutes and pushes effective training time to 80% running with 11 of 12 healthy replicas [PAPER, arXiv:2602.00277, Feb 2026]. Their 32K-GPU production reference (2.3 interruptions/1,000 servers/day; top causes GPU HBM3 22.9%, PCIe 18.0%, NCCL watchdog 9.0%) is structurally the same top-3 taxonomy as Llama 3’s, differently weighted.

A smaller academic study checks scale-dependence itself: a 63-node, 504-GPU B200 cluster over 55 days logged 17 failure events, found “no single Prometheus metric is consistently distinctive across failure types” across 751 monitored metrics, and documented “performance characteristics observed at 2–4-node scale do not predict behavior at 60 nodes” — a storage I/O bottleneck invisible below 60 nodes cost over 8 hours to fix [PAPER, arXiv:2605.09370, 2026], a warning against extrapolating small-cluster burn-in without dedicated mid-scale validation.

GB200 NVL72: A Step Change in Blast Radius, Not Just a Faster Chip

The GB200 generation changes the reliability calculus in a way a pure FLOPs/TCO comparison misses entirely. SemiAnalysis’s field-derived mean-time-between-interruption (MTBI) ranges put H100 clusters at 2,000–5,000 GPU-days and GB200 NVL72 clusters at 1,000–3,000 GPU-days — GB200 racks fail roughly 2x more often per GPU-day than H100 servers even at the most-advanced-operator end. Blast radius compounds this: an H100/H200/B200 HGX server failure takes down 8 GPUs, but a GB200 NVL72 rack’s 18 compute trays, 9 NVSwitch trays, and 4 copper backplane cartridges share a common NVLink domain, so a switch-tray or backplane-cartridge swap drains the entire 72-GPU rack — a 9x larger, structural blast radius. Root-cause diagnosis compounds further: identifying which component is faulting “can take upwards of a couple hours” with current tooling, and the swap sequence — compute tray (1–4h, no drain), switch tray (1–4h, full drain), backplane (8–24h) — is an order of magnitude more complicated than an 8-GPU Hopper HGX board swap. XID 149 is the near-universal early-warning signature of backplane signal-integrity problems, and the gold-plated Paladin HD connectors are rated for at most 200 mating cycles before discard. Whole-rack diagnosis also requires three-way coordination between the customer (compute-tray access only), the hyperscaler (SSH to the switch tray), and NVIDIA support — friction SemiAnalysis states is why no large-scale frontier run had completed on GB200 NVL72 as of mid-2025, with advanced labs confined to inference and small experiments precisely because wide-EP MoE inference pays off the NVLink domain fastest without needing the mega-training reliability problem solved first [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, SemiAnalysis, “H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks,” Aug 2025].

The economics fold together cleanly:

[CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, SemiAnalysis, Aug 2025]

The report’s own blunt conclusion — “the vanilla B200 is better performance-TCO than GB200 NVL72 for pretraining normal-sized models. As model sizes scale or reinforcement learning comes in, GB200 NVL72 will take a lead again” — bears directly on a 10T-total/200B-active design, since its expert-parallel communication footprint (Ch16) is exactly the regime where the 64-GPU all-to-all runs 18x faster on GB200 NVL72 than on 64 H100 GPUs, the single biggest lever that can make the reliability tax worth paying. Whether this run sits on the “NVL72 wins” or “loses” side of that line is a threshold this report leaves for the reader to locate against their final EP width (Ch16).

Deriving the Number: Interruptions per Hour at 100,000 GB200 GPUs

Converting MTBI (GPU-days) to a fleet-level rate at N=100,000 GPUs by dividing MTBI/N gives:

[Derived from CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, SemiAnalysis, Aug 2025]

Three structurally independent methods converge on the same band. Llama 3’s own observed rate, linearly scaled to 100,000 GPUs, gives 1.97 interruptions/hour (~47/day) — landing almost exactly on the H100 “worst-case” estimate, plausible since the Llama 3 cluster predates years of subsequent reliability hardening [PAPER, arXiv:2407.21783, Jul 2024; extrapolation this report’s own]. FT-HSDP’s directly measured “failure every 18 minutes” at 100K GPUs (3.33/hour) sits squarely inside the GB200 band — notable because that paper does not specify GPU generation, suggesting the 100K-GPU regime itself, independent of hardware, converges toward multiple interruptions per hour at that scale [PAPER, arXiv:2602.00277, Feb 2026]. The planning number this report carries forward: expect 1.5–4 unplanned interruptions per hour (35–100/day) on a 100,000-GB200 fleet, strongly dependent on operator sophistication and whether the fleet has completed its first 3–6 months of tribal-knowledge accumulation.

Silent Data Corruption: From Ad Hoc Tooling to a Board-Level Problem

Silent data corruption is qualitatively different from the crash-and-restart failure modes above, because nothing crashes. The lineage starts with Google’s “Cores that don’t count” (HotOS’21), establishing that mercurial/defective cores produce silent corrupt execution errors at rates far above classical soft-error expectations, chip-specific rather than fleet-wide [PAPER, HotOS 2021]. Meta’s July 2025 disclosure is the clearest statement of what is deployed today: Fleetscanner (targeted micro-benchmarks during maintenance, full-fleet coverage every 45–60 days), Ripple (lighter-weight tests alongside live workloads), and Hardware Sentinel (test-agnostic, inspecting application exceptions in kernel space, claimed 41% better detection than testing-based approaches) [OFFICIAL-BLOG, engineering.fb.com, Jul 2025]. Meta states historical soft-error rates of ~1-per-million devices have been superseded by SDC rates of ~1-per-thousand devices — ~1000x higher — with over 66% of training interruptions tracing to hardware failures in SRAM/HBM/processing-grid/network-switch components; Google states SDC events occur weekly-to-biweekly during Gemini training, while Meta attributed 6 of 419 unexpected Llama-3-405B interruptions (1.4%) to SDC [OFFICIAL-BLOG, engineering.fb.com, Jul 2025; CREDIBLE-HEARSAY via arXiv:2502.12340, Feb 2025].

The methodologically strongest public study, “Understanding Silent Data Corruption in LLM Training” (ACL 2025), makes the case that loss-monitoring alone cannot be the whole defense: one consistently-corrupting node produced training loss curves indistinguishable from healthy nodes while its gradients diverged up to 5.1% (noise-to-signal ratio) from ground truth, weights measurably drifting despite flat loss. Checksummed-matmul ABFT caught corruption on only 1 of several known-bad nodes tested; the proposed viable mitigation — a shadow data-parallel replica comparing gradients during all-reduce, at ~2x compute cost — is the only method shown to reliably catch what loss-monitoring misses, and corrupted-node models degraded to near-0% accuracy on downstream tasks despite normal pretraining loss [PAPER, arXiv:2502.12340, Feb 2025].

A genuinely non-obvious result for anyone choosing precision on throughput/memory grounds alone: LLM-PRISM’s 7,664-run fault-injection study found SDC resilience ranks FP8 ≫ BF16 ≫ FP16, because FP8’s narrow dynamic range forces faults to either saturate harmlessly or trigger a detectable NaN — binarizing outcomes — while BF16/FP16 permit large-but-finite silent corruption NaN-checking never catches, with perplexity deviations up to 400–600% [PAPER, arXiv:2604.10390, 2026]. Going to FP8 (Ch8) is, incidentally, also an SDC mitigation. A companion gate-level fault-injection campaign (3M+ simulator-hours) finds corruption is dominated not by NaN/Inf (1.01%) but by silent nullification (50.68%) and non-special bit-flips (48.31%), with “warp-aligned spatial periodicity” — GPU SDC is structured, not uniform-random [PAPER, arXiv:2605.04213, 2026].

OCP’s “Silent Data Corruption in AI” whitepaper (Sept 2025), co-authored by NVIDIA, Meta, Google, AMD, Intel, ARM, and Microsoft under a new Server Component Resilience Workstream, is a rare case of cross-vendor coordination on training reliability specifically, signaling the problem crossed a severity threshold no single lab’s tooling could address alone [OFFICIAL-BLOG, opencompute.org, Sep 2025]. The most concrete hardware response is Google’s Ironwood (TPU v7), the first TPU generation to move SDC defense into silicon: a Functional Built-In Self-Test in the MXU for manufacturing/burn-in, plus a compiler-transparent Hardware Replay Unit in the VPU that re-executes vector-instruction bundles on idle SIMD lanes at “zero performance overhead,” reported to catch defects that evaded all prior screening (Ch14) [PAPER, arXiv:2606.15870, Jun 2026]. No equivalent hardware SDC mitigation has been disclosed for Blackwell/GB200 in the sources surveyed here — an absence-of-evidence observation, not a confirmed denial, but an open gap against Ironwood’s silicon-level answer regardless.

Worth naming the failure class none of the above catches: a correctness bug with no corruption signature. HuggingFace’s SmolLM3 team restarted an 11T-token run after burning 1T tokens (46,080 GPU-hours) because every GPU in a tensor-parallel group shared the same random seed — passed loss-curve inspection, caught only by downstream eval regression [LOGBOOK, HuggingFace Smol Training Playbook, Oct 2025]. Checkpointing protects against hardware failure and instrumented SDC; it does nothing for this class, Ch20’s territory.

Checkpoint Math for the 10T/200B Strawman

Base Adam-style optimizer state for the 10T-total-parameter design: BF16 parameters at 10×10¹² × 2 bytes = 20TB; FP32 master weights (mixed-precision training) at 10×10¹² × 4 bytes = 40TB; Adam’s first and second moments, both FP32, at 10×10¹² × 4 bytes × 2 = 80TB. Total: 140TB.

Muon (Ch7) changes this materially: it replaces Adam’s two per-parameter state tensors with a single momentum buffer and no second-moment estimate, applied to the >95% of parameters that are 2D matrices — FFN/expert/attention weights, excluding embeddings/LayerNorm/biases, which stay on Adam [PAPER-derived, multiple 2025-2026 Muon papers]. FP32 momentum replaces Adam’s 80TB with 40TB (new total ~100TB, 29% reduction); BF16 momentum drops it to 20TB (~80TB total, 43% reduction). Published 8-bit quantized Muon variants report 74–86% optimizer-state reduction versus Adam-32/Muon-32 in small-model ablations, extrapolating — with real caution about scale-dependent quantization error, explicitly unvalidated at 10T scale — toward an 11–21TB footprint and ~70–80TB total, roughly half the Adam baseline [PAPER, arXiv:2509.23106, Sep 2025].

[Derived from the 10T-total/200B-active spec; Muon reduction mechanics from multiple 2025-2026 papers; 8-bit figures from arXiv:2509.23106, Sep 2025]

The device→host “stage 1” write is not the bottleneck at any realistic scale. At 100,000 GPUs, 140TB divided across the fleet is a 1.4GB/GPU local shard; copying that over GB200’s NVLink-C2C (900GB/s Grace↔Blackwell, up to ~450GB/s per GPU) completes in ~3ms, and even over PCIe Gen5 x16 (~64GB/s) it takes ~22ms — matching ByteRobust’s 10–40ms blocking stall and FT-HSDP’s ~60ms peer-memory fetch per GB [PAPER, arXiv:2509.16293, Sep 2025; PAPER, arXiv:2602.00277, Feb 2026].

The real bottleneck is draining to a durable, off-node tier. A synchronous full-fleet drain of 140TB to object storage inside a 60-second budget requires ~2.33TB/s aggregate sustained write bandwidth (~1.67TB/s Muon-100TB, ~1.33TB/s Muon-80TB). Meta’s Tectonic fleet built for the much smaller Llama 3 405B checkpoint sustained 2TB/s and peaked at 7TB/s across 7,500 dedicated flash servers [PAPER, arXiv:2407.21783, Jul 2024] — a 10T-MoE, 100,000-GPU deployment draining in under 60s would need a comparably sized dedicated storage fleet just for checkpoint traffic, layered on top of whatever the dataloader needs (Ch13). This is precisely why every 2025-2026 production system surveyed here — Gemini, MaxText, ByteRobust, FT-HSDP — converges on the same architecture: local/peer DRAM replication absorbs the common-case single-to-few-node failure at sub-100ms recovery latency, and the slow durable tier refreshes at a much longer cadence (tens of minutes) purely as a correlated-failure backstop.

Young’s formula, modernized, confirms the implied cadence: t* = √(2δM), δ the checkpoint-write stall, M the MTBF. Plugging in δ≈1–5s (async local/peer snapshot) and M≈1,080s (FT-HSDP’s 18-minute failure interval) gives t* ≈ 80 seconds — recommending checkpointing every 1–2 minutes at 100K-GPU MTBF, exactly the cadence ByteRobust and FT-HSDP independently converged on empirically [PAPER, arXiv:2509.16293, Sep 2025; PAPER, arXiv:2602.00277, Feb 2026]. If δ is instead the synchronous full drain to durable storage — tens of minutes to an hour at this scale — the formula correctly recommends a much longer interval, exactly why nobody drains to object store every step.

The Hierarchical Checkpoint Architecture

Google’s in-memory-replica architecture from the Gemini SOSP’23 paper remains the conceptual root of every subsequent system: rather than periodic dumps to persistent storage, keep redundant in-memory copies of model state across replicas and recover directly from an intact peer on failure, because “conventional periodic checkpointing to persistent cluster storage” could not sustain goodput at Gemini’s scale [PAPER, SOSP 2023]. This architecture’s goodput claims remain the still-unbeaten reference point: Gemini 1.0 on TPU v4 went from 85% to 97% goodput on a single supercomputer, and Gemini 2.5 sustained 93% doing synchronous data-parallel training across multiple 8,960-chip TPU v5p pods in multiple datacenters — a materially harder problem, still comfortably above 90% [OFFICIAL-BLOG/PAPER, Gemini 1.0/2.5 tech reports, cited directly in arXiv:2606.15870, Jun 2026]. Every GPU-side system below is, in effect, re-deriving a GPU-cluster analogue of this design.

The concrete stack, layer by layer:

  • torch.distributed.checkpoint (DCP), PyTorch-native, async_save=True overlapping serialization with training; NeMo-reported single-checkpoint save times ~1s (Megatron Core distributed optimizer) or ~3s (Apex distributed fused Adam) [OFFICIAL-DOCS, docs.pytorch.org / docs.nvidia.com].
  • ByteCheckpoint, disaggregating checkpoint data/metadata storage from parallelism strategy for online resharding across Megatron-LM/DDP/FSDP; 54.20x average reduction in checkpoint-induced stalls, up to 9.96x faster saves, 8.80x faster loads vs prior open-source baselines [PAPER, arXiv:2407.20143, Jul 2024, rev. Apr 2025].
  • Orbax (JAX-native, Google), replica-parallel multi-slice saving, up to 2.39x speedup across 16 slices; an independent 2026 benchmark shows 405B-model saves at 173.0s vs 595.0s for PyTorch DCP (3.4x) [PAPER, arXiv:2605.23066, 2026]; its emergency-checkpointing feature restores from a single replica in chunks to manage HBM pressure — a direct descendant of Gemini’s design.
  • MaxText / Google Cloud: three explicit tiers — ramdisk local RAM, peer replica in a different slice/superblock, Cloud Storage — combining “speed of ramdisk, resilience of in-cluster replication, durability of GCS” [OFFICIAL-DOCS, cloud.google.com, MaxText docs, 2025-2026]; TierCheck generalizes the same idea academically to four tiers (HBM → host memory → NVMe → object store) [PAPER, arXiv:2605.17821, 2026].
  • ByteRobust’s “Hot-Update” in-place recovery, 11.04x faster than full requeue (454s→46s at 128×16 GPUs), 0.01–0.04s checkpoint-blocking via hierarchical host-memory-plus-SSD tiering, in production across 200,000+ GPUs [PAPER, arXiv:2509.16293, Sep 2025].

NVIDIA’s resiliency stack (nvidia-resiliency-ext, “NVRx”) is the open-source infrastructure most directly usable underneath a Megatron build: per-rank heartbeat monitored by a “rank monitor” launched by ft_launcher supporting in-job restart without SLURM reallocation, plus in-process restarting, async/local checkpointing, and straggler detection — plumbing, light on published overhead numbers [CODE, github.com/NVIDIA/nvidia-resiliency-ext].

At 140TB (Adam) or 80–100TB (Muon), the layering above is non-optional: the local/peer tier absorbs the 1.5–4-interruptions-per-hour common case at sub-100ms-to-low-seconds recovery, and the durable tier — comparable in scale to Meta’s Llama-3-era Tectonic buildout — exists purely as a correlated-failure backstop refreshed every 10–30 minutes, not the steady-state recovery path.

Recovery Engineering: Warm Pools, Stragglers, and Semi-Sync Fault Tolerance

Straggler detection. MegaScale’s CUDA Event Monitor — a heatmap of unsynchronized per-op timings — found roughly 0.5% of machines meaningfully slower than the fleet, worth ~0.7% MFU when removed [PAPER, arXiv:2402.15627, Feb 2024]; a 2026 follow-on, Guard, benchmarks against it and DCGM/SuperBench, though its quantitative improvement was not extractable in this research pass [PAPER, arXiv:2605.17879, 2026 — open question]. Mycroft traces dependency graphs through collective communication to localize the causally first faulty rank rather than the rank that merely timed out first [PAPER, arXiv:2509.03018]. NCCL Flight Recorder + fr_trace — a ring buffer of collective start/end/enqueue times and stack traces per rank, aligned across ranks by sequence ID — exists because collective desync, not raw slowness, is almost always the actual root cause behind a raw watchdog timeout [CODE/OFFICIAL-BLOG, pytorch.org/blog, PyTorch 2.5-2.6 era].

torchft implements per-step fault tolerance for semi-synchronous algorithms (LocalSGD, DiLoCo, Streaming DiLoCo) via a Lighthouse global coordinator and per-replica-group Manager, Gloo for cross-group control-plane traffic and NCCL within a group. On 300 L40S GPUs with TorchTitan and Llama-3-1B, it degrades gracefully but not indefinitely under chaos testing: step-commit efficiency 82.3% at 1 injected failure per 60 seconds, collapsing to 30.2% at 1 per ~15 seconds; dead-replica recovery is under 1 second via Gloo, and a 40-step DiLoCo sync interval sustains ~4,000 tok/s versus ~1,200 tok/s for an HSDP2 baseline under identical conditions [OFFICIAL-BLOG, pytorch.org/blog, mid-2025]. SemiAnalysis’s own hands-on testing corroborates the cost differently: cross-replica-group allreduce under torchft must fall back to Gloo-over-CPU/TCP instead of NCCL-over-RDMA, measured at over 10% throughput overhead [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, SemiAnalysis, Apr 2026]. ElasWave, a 2026-era successor, targets torchft’s throughput ceiling via multi-dimensional scheduling across graph/dataflow/DVFS/RNG state; on a 96-NPU testbed it reports 1.35x throughput over ReCycle and 1.60x over torchft with sub-second communicator recovery [PAPER, arXiv:2510.00606, Oct 2025] — a research prototype, not near 100K-GPU scale.

Two other production fault-tolerance frameworks, benchmarked head-to-head by SemiAnalysis against torchft, are direct procurement options rather than research demos:

AWS’s figure is both AWS-claimed and confirmed hands-on by SemiAnalysis on a 4-node H200 cluster, internally proven on Amazon’s Nova model training before its December 2025 launch; Clockwork TorchPass is the only one of the three with no measured performance overhead, its cost pushed entirely into idle spare-node capacity — closer in spirit to the rack-level hot-sparing NVIDIA bakes into GB200 NVL72 (Ch14) [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, SemiAnalysis, Apr 2026].

Hot-spare ratios and elastic vs. fixed world size. The only concrete industry number is Lenovo’s recommended minimum 2% spare-to-production ratio for NVL72; NVIDIA’s rack design holds back 8 of 72 GPUs (~11%), conflating true idle spares with GPUs standing by on pre-emptible workloads, and SemiAnalysis separately notes top-tier providers reserve 2–6% of nodes as hot spares [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, SemiAnalysis, Aug 2025 & Apr 2026; OFFICIAL-DOCS, Lenovo NVL72 documentation, 2025-2026]. No public source validates a spare ratio tuned to a 100K-GPU fleet’s own MTBI curve. Every production-scale system surveyed — Llama 3, MegaScale/ByteRobust, FT-HSDP, Gemini, Alibaba HPN — runs a fixed logical world size with hot-spare or warm-pool replacement, not elastic membership. torchft/DiLoCo and ElasWave demonstrate genuine elastic resharding, but the largest publicly documented run for either is 300 GPUs (torchft/Crusoe) or 96 NPUs (ElasWave) — three to four orders of magnitude below 100K-GPU. Warm-spare-pool-with-fixed-topology, not elastic membership, is the frontier-lab pattern as of mid-2026.

TPU/Pathways Resilience Culture: The Contrast Case

Google’s TPU resilience architecture is structurally different because it is built around Optical Circuit Switches (OCS), present from TPU v4 onward. TPUs are grouped into “cubes” of 4×4×4=64 chips, each cube’s 96 optical links routing out to OCSes rather than fixed copper, making the 3D-torus wraparound links software-reconfigurable — a failed chip or cube is routed around, not hardware-swapped, MEMS-mirror reconfiguration happening in milliseconds. This buys a scheduling benefit beyond fault tolerance: without OCS a scheduler must find contiguous idle chips; with it, any two cubes anywhere can be selected, which is why Ironwood is provisioned at 9,216 nodes rather than a clean power of 2, running four 2,048-chip slice jobs simultaneously with some nodes down, backed by 16 spare cubes. The paper is explicit about the real bottleneck: “without OCSes, host availability must be >99.9% to achieve high slice goodput” — OCS relaxes an otherwise punishing host-uptime requirement, Ironwood running 2,304 CPU hosts, each serving 4 TPUs. OCS also changes deployment economics: each 64-chip cube enters production as soon as it and its cables are tested, whereas pre-OCS (TPU v3) the entire 1,024-chip system had to be fully cabled and tested first [PAPER, arXiv:2606.15870, Jun 2026].

The paper frames Google’s dependable-computing philosophy as inherited from distributed-systems practice for long-running batch jobs, not classical HPC checkpoint-restart culture, resting on five pillars: enhanced node quality, error checking, checkpoint/restore, strict deterministic repeatability, and modular isolation. Headline goodput, from Google’s own tech reports: Gemini 1.0 on TPU v4 achieved 97% (up from 85% pre-optimization) on a single supercomputer; Gemini 2.5 achieved 93% at multi-pod, multi-datacenter scale — slightly lower, plausibly the harder synchronization problem of more pods/regions even as per-pod hardware improved. The paper’s summary: “O(10,000) supercomputer size in a single-tier network with enhanced resilience allows training of long-running giant models across multiple supercomputers in multiple regions at >90% goodput simply by using data parallelism” [PAPER, arXiv:2606.15870, Jun 2026].

Google has also productized goodput as a first-class metric via the open-sourced ml-goodput-measurement library, instrumenting “goodput” and “badput” with async export to Cloud Monitoring/TensorBoard [OFFICIAL-BLOG/CODE, github.com/google/cloud_tpu_goodput]. No equivalent cross-lab-adopted library exists on the GPU side — every GPU-cluster goodput number here comes from a paper’s self-reported ETTR, not a shared standard, a gap worth noting for Ch20.

Goodput Accounting: What the Number Actually Means

ByteRobust’s Effective Training Time Ratio is the closest GPU-side analogue to Google’s goodput metric, and its own numbers already show the structural penalty MoE pays relative to dense: 97% ETTR for a three-month dense run on 9,600 GPUs, versus 91% for a one-month MoE run [PAPER, arXiv:2509.16293, Sep 2025]. SemiAnalysis’s April 2026 “Grand Unifying Theory of Goodput” gives the most rigorous public decomposition of what drives that gap: Goodput Expense is modeled under three fault-recovery regimes — G_chkpt-cold (provider-managed cold spare, hours to days), G_chkpt-hot (the 2–6%-of-nodes hot-spare pool top-tier providers reserve), and G_tolerant (the job survives entirely, well understood for stateless inference, “less well understood in training”). Its central point: cluster-level MTBF shrinks as cluster size grows, so larger jobs suffer disproportionately more goodput loss than small ones at identical per-GPU reliability — the same dynamic behind the interruption-rate derivation above [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, SemiAnalysis, Apr 2026].

Three worked scenarios quantify the magnitude, holding $/GPU-hr constant across provider tiers:

[CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, SemiAnalysis, “How Much Do GPU Clusters Really Cost?,” Apr 2026]

The pattern is unambiguous: goodput expense is a rounding error for small, naturally fault-tolerant workloads and a first-order cost driver — 6% to 21% of TCO — for exactly the large, synchronous, collective-bound pretraining class this report’s reader is planning. Silver-tier’s 20.91% expense in the Large LLM Pretrain scenario is driven by a 60%-worse assumed MTBF, 1-hour versus 15-minute failure-ID/repair, and slower job-init from weaker storage — a quantified argument that provider/tier selection is not a commodity decision for a 10T-MoE program, independent of headline $/GPU-hr.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

Layered reliability spec.

  • Layer 0 — Hardware acceptance. Stricter-than-vendor-default ODM/OEM acceptance testing on every GB200 NVL72 rack before fleet entry; budget for a first-3-to-6-month tribal-knowledge tax on backplane/switch-tray diagnosis, and staff a dedicated NVL72 hardware-diagnostics team before the run starts.
  • Layer 1 — Fabric/topology. Dual-plane, multi-ToR-redundant Clos (Ch18); NVLink-domain-aware scheduling that never splits a job across a partial NVL72 rack; whole-spare-rack provisioning on top of the in-rack 8-of-72 hot-spare GPUs.
  • Layer 2 — Transport/collective layer. NCCL Flight Recorder plus fr_trace-style desync debugging as a standing capability; dependency-tracing fault localization (Mycroft-style); continuous straggler-detection heatmapping, not just at admission.
  • Layer 3 — Framework/checkpoint layer. Hierarchical, tiered checkpointing — HBM local write in single-digit milliseconds, peer/replica DRAM copy at sub-100ms recovery, periodic drain to a dedicated flash/object tier sized for 1–2TB/s+ sustained write, refreshed every 10–30 minutes as a correlated-failure backstop. At 140TB (Adam) / 80–100TB (Muon), this tier is a multi-thousand-SSD-server capex line comparable to Meta’s Llama-3-era Tectonic fleet.
  • Layer 4 — Fault-tolerance/recovery orchestration. Fixed-world-size warm-standby-pool architecture, the only pattern validated above 10K-GPU scale; peer-replica HTTP/RDMA fetch rather than always-from-persistent-storage; automated root-cause triage targeting FT-HSDP’s demonstrated bar of sub-5-second, >95%-accuracy attribution.
  • Layer 5 — Algorithmic/numerical layer. FP8 doubles as SDC mitigation by collapsing silent-degradation failure modes into binary recover/crash outcomes; a periodic shadow-replica gradient-comparison pass for SDC detection, since loss/NaN monitoring alone is insufficient; Muon or a quantized variant shrinks the checkpoint problem, its interaction with SDC-style gradient corruption an open question at this scale.

Expected goodput number. Triangulating Gemini 1.0/2.5 (93–97%, single-to-multi-pod TPU), ByteRobust production (91–97% dense vs MoE), the SemiAnalysis MTBI-derived range (77.8%–99.6% by operator sophistication and GPU generation), and FT-HSDP’s demonstrated 80% (versus 44% naive-synchronous) at 100K GPUs: a well-engineered 100K-GB200, 10T-MoE run should target 85–93% goodput, the wide range reflecting MoE’s heavier communication overhead versus dense, GB200’s larger blast radius and lower MTBI versus H100, and the fact that this exact scale/hardware combination has no direct precedent.

Top 5 residual risks, ranked:

  1. GB200 NVL72 backplane/switch-tray reliability may not mature fast enough. No frontier lab had completed a mega-training run on GB200 NVL72 as of the most recent primary data (Aug 2025); the 72-GPU blast radius and 200-mating-cycle connector wear-out are architectural, not firmware-fixable. If unimproved by procurement time, plan around HGX B200/B300 for portions that don’t need the scale-up domain, reserving NVL72 for wide-EP-MoE-communication-bound work where its 18x all-to-all advantage pays for the tax.
  2. SDC at 10T-parameter scale is unvalidated territory. Every quantitative SDC study is calibrated on ≤670B-parameter, ≤16K-GPU regimes; whether SDC’s rate and downstream gradient impact scale linearly, worse, or better at 10T parameters and 100K GPUs is unknown, and a shadow-replica detection scheme costs roughly 2x compute if deployed at full scale.
  3. Checkpoint-tier storage capex may be underestimated. The ~2.3TB/s-for-under-60s-drain calculation implies a storage fleet on the order of Meta’s entire Tectonic buildout, dedicated just to checkpoint traffic; if unbudgeted it becomes a late-discovered bottleneck — the 504-GPU study’s 8-plus-hour storage-I/O surprise at 60 nodes previews the same mistake at far higher cost.
  4. No validated elastic-pretraining path exists at frontier scale, so the fallback under sustained failure is a fixed-world-size warm-pool architecture that, per FT-HSDP’s own numbers, still costs ~3 minutes of stall per failure when well-engineered — at 1.5–4 interruptions/hour, potentially 4.5–12 minutes/hour lost to recovery alone, before detection overhead.
  5. Muon/quantized-optimizer memory-savings claims are extrapolated, not scale-validated. The 74–86% optimizer-state-reduction figures for 8-bit Muon come from ablations orders of magnitude below 10T total parameters; whether quantization error interacts badly with MoE routing sensitivity, or with gradient-comparison SDC-detection schemes it could mask or amplify, is unstudied.

Chapter 20 · Part V — The Run

Operations: War Stories, Debugging Doctrine, and Running the Run

Every chapter before this one has been about what to build. This one is about what happens after you turn it on: the incident that starts at 3am, the throughput regression nobody can explain, the eval score that looks a little off three weeks later. The published record of how large training runs are actually operated — as opposed to how they are architected — is thin, unevenly distributed, and aging in a specific way: the genre of the day-by-day training diary has migrated almost entirely out of the frontier commercial labs and into the open/academic tier, which means the best operational ground truth available for a 10T/200B run on 100,000 GB200s comes from labs one to three orders of magnitude smaller. Read correctly, that ground truth is still the most useful thing in this book for the humans who will be paged.

The Logbook Genre: From OPT’s Chronicles to the 2025-2026 Open-Tier Diaries

The reference artifact for this entire chapter is Meta’s OPT-175B logbook: 148 pages of raw notes plus four dated progress updates covering roughly 90 restarts over 33 days on 1,024 A100s OPT-175B chronicles, facebookresearch/metaseq [LOGBOOK] (11/2021–1/2022). The team’s own history splits cleanly into two eras separated by one decision. The “11.xx lineage” (through Nov 5–11, 2021) was numerics-driven firefighting — grad-clip tightened 2.5→1.5→1.0, weight decay and Adam β2 hot-swapped mid-run, GeLU swapped for ReLU on suspicion of its cubic term interacting badly with FP16, a discovery that learned positional embeddings had never actually trained because sinusoidal initialization froze them in place. None of it stabilized the run. On Nov 11 the team invoked a pre-declared “plan B,” abandoned six months of from-scratch architectural choices, and copied Megatron/GPT-3’s published 175B settings as closely as possible. From experiment 12.00 onward, “the only restarts we’ve had to make were all related to hardware issues” — the cleanest data point in the corpus for when to stop tuning and start copying a known-working recipe. The subsequent “40+ restarts in two weeks” period around Thanksgiving 2021 was pure hardware attrition against an inadequate buffer pool (12–18 spare nodes, hours-long manual cloud-console replacement, “~2 machines go down every day” as the team’s own baseline), compounded by a 1.6TB checkpoint that hung nondeterministically on download. The “Omicron SEV” — a cloud provider accidentally deleting the entire cluster while replenishing the buffer pool — pushed the team to build automated health-check/recovery tooling that immediately paid off: eight subsequent hardware failures over the Christmas break were auto-recovered with reduced on-call burden, with “14 consecutive days with zero human intervention” explicitly set as the metric for retiring the on-call role.

BLOOM-176B’s chronicles.md is the most granular public training diary that exists: 694 lines of minute-by-minute SLURM log entries plus a 1,394-line pre-launch “prequel” BLOOM chronicles.md, bigscience-workshop/bigscience [LOGBOOK] (2022). Three episodes are worth carrying forward as case studies in their own right. First, a grad-clip/tensor-parallel-sync bug: DeepSpeed’s BF16Optimizer wasn’t clipping gradients on TP ranks >1, so layer-norm weights that are supposed to be identical across ranks silently drifted apart — invisible in the training loss (each rank just learned its own private copy) but catastrophic at checkpoint-merge time. Tracing and fixing it took from March 24 to March 30, 2022, across at least three restarts and a temporary all-reduce sync band-aid that itself only recovered ~60% of the drifted parameters on the first pass. Second, a three-act eval-hang saga spanning April 30 to May 18: a DeepSpeed pipeline-engine race condition that hung training specifically at eval time, recurring three separate times (costing 18h, then 6h, then 11h) before Daniel Hesslow traced it, via a fleet-wide py-spy dump, to a known PyTorch DataLoader worker-thread lock bug (pytorch/pytorch#25158) — never fixed upstream, only worked around by setting num_workers=0 on the validation loader. Third, a pre-launch scale-dependent hang: models under 100B parameters trained fine, but crossing that threshold caused random GPUs to stall in all_reduce; the empirical fix, CUDA_LAUNCH_BLOCKING=1 (forcing fully synchronous CUDA), eliminated the hang with zero measured throughput penalty, and the team shipped the entire 176B production run with it permanently enabled, never finding the true root cause BLOOM chronicles-prequel.md [LOGBOOK] (undated, ~2/2022). That result directly contradicts the reflexive assumption that synchronous CUDA always costs performance, and Stas Bekman — who was on that team — has carried the same anecdote into his own current-day debugging doctrine (below).

PaLM’s spike-handling protocol is the field’s most-cited historical citation, and both OPT and BLOOM’s own practices descend from it directly: roughly 20 spikes over the full 540B run, handled by restarting from a checkpoint ~100 steps before the spike and skipping 200–500 data batches PaLM: Scaling Language Modeling with Pathways [PAPER] (2022). The detail most secondary sources drop is PaLM’s own ablation: replaying the exact spike-triggering batches from a different earlier checkpoint did not reproduce the spike — evidence that spikes are a joint function of optimizer state and data, not a property of either alone (cross-reference Ch7’s optimizer-stability treatment for the mechanistic side of this).

No frontier commercial lab — DeepSeek, post-Llama-3 Meta, OpenAI, Google, Anthropic, xAI — has published anything resembling a day-by-day diary since roughly 2022, though the underlying research corpus flags this as a plausible-but-unconfirmed absence rather than a proven negative: no equivalent chronicle was located for any of these labs’ recent model families, but that could reflect a search-coverage gap as easily as genuine non-disclosure. DeepSeek’s entire public stability disclosure across V3/V3.2/V4 is a one-line “zero loss spikes” claim (see Ch7). The genre now survives almost exclusively in the open/academic tier, and its 2025–2026 instances show the same texture at radically different reliability baselines. HuggingFace’s “Smol Training Playbook” documents SmolLM3 (3B params, 11T tokens, 384 H100s) restarting its entire main run after burning 1T tokens (46,080 GPU-hours) because every GPU in a tensor-parallel group had been initialized with the identical random seed rather than seed + tp_rank — a bug invisible in the loss curve, caught only via a downstream eval regression against the model’s own smaller predecessor Smol Training Playbook [LOGBOOK] (10/2025). Total ablation/debugging compute for that project (161,280 GPU-hours) exceeded the main run’s own 276,480 GPU-hours by more than half — a ratio worth internalizing when budgeting a 10T run’s total compute envelope, not just its “final” FLOPs. Marin’s 32B retrospective is 2025’s closest analogue to the OPT/BLOOM chronicle style, and it is the best evidence in the corpus that architectural intervention, not more monitoring, is sometimes the only fix: three separate numerics-level interventions at ~80,000 steps (tighter grad-clip, a clip-update-norm filter, a 2σ skip-bad-steps filter) all failed to stop recurring spikes; the actual fix was a mid-flight swap of the attention backbone to a QK-Norm architecture, warm-started from the old checkpoint, which imposed a one-time loss penalty that recovered within ~10B tokens Marin 32B retrospective [LOGBOOK] (2025). The same retrospective documents a failed mid-run Muon swap that produced temporary loss relief before the model began performing what the team describes as gradient ascent — a cautionary counter-example for anyone assuming DeepSeek’s from-scratch AdamW→Muon architectural choice (Ch7) implies a live optimizer swap is equally safe. And Ai2’s OLMo Hybrid run (512 B200s, 6.19 days, 3T tokens, Dec 25–31, 2025) reports 97% uptime (99% excluding scheduled development windows) with a median incident-recovery time of 3 minutes 42 seconds under a “fail fast” philosophy of automated GPU health-checking and quarantine-and-restart rather than degraded continuation Open model, open metrics [OFFICIAL-BLOG] (~12/2025–1/2026) — a striking, dated, production before/after against OPT’s 2021 reality of hours-long manual node replacement and a 12–18-node buffer pool.

The Incident Taxonomy: Five Classes, and What 2026 Automated Away

Reading ten-plus logbooks side by side — OPT, BLOOM, PaLM, GPT-NeoX-20B, Pythia, OLMo 2/Hybrid, SmolLM3, Marin, INTELLECT-1/3 — five incident classes recur, and they have aged in sharply different ways.

The delta on hardware fault-tolerance is the single largest reliability-engineering improvement visible across this whole corpus: OPT’s 2021 reality was a 12–18-node buffer pool and hours of manual cloud-console replacement; a production run in December 2025–January 2026 achieves sub-4-minute median automated recovery (see Ch19 for the checkpoint and replication mechanics underneath this). The class that has not moved is (d) — silent, non-crashing correctness bugs. None of BLOOM’s grad-clip bug, SmolLM3’s seed bug, or OPT’s unexplained dependency-bump crashed training or crossed any monitored threshold; all were caught by a human noticing a downstream eval regression, a checkpoint-merge-time concern, or plain curiosity about an unexplained throughput bump, and all required multi-day forensic work — checkpoint tensor diffing, cross-node py-spy comparison, dependency bisection. There is no evidence anywhere in this corpus that 2026 tooling has compressed the detection-to-diagnosis time for this class at all, and INTELLECT-3’s newly emerging training/inference policy-mismatch crashes in async RL — where inference and training policies silently diverge in token probabilities and only manifest as a crash “multiple days into the experiments” INTELLECT-3 Technical Report [PAPER] (12/2025) — suggest the class is growing, not shrinking, as systems get more complex. The organizational lesson for class (b): build automated spike suppression as the default cheap first response, but pre-agree an explicit escalation trigger (“if spikes recur after N automated interventions across M checkpoints, convene an architecture review”) before the run starts, because a team without that pre-agreement will burn calendar time re-litigating the decision live, exactly as Marin’s saga did across three numbered phases.

Bare-Metal War Stories: Bring-Up Gauntlets and Bandwidth-Constrained Labs

Imbue’s public bring-up of a 4,088-H100/511-node cluster is the fullest documented “how do you actually stand up a non-blocking fabric” gauntlet available From bare metal to a 70B model [OFFICIAL-BLOG] (6/2024). The quantified failure taxonomy is directly transferable: ~10% of machines failed to boot on the first attempt (mostly physical — miswired ethernet, dead PSUs, missing cabling); a baseline ~3%/week breakage rate once running, but explicitly not uniform — a small population of “malcontent” machines break repeatedly, which reframes fleet-reliability planning away from a Poisson-failure model and toward “identify and quarantine the bad 5%”; ~25% of hosts initially ran PCIe at x4 instead of x16; the initial InfiniBand fabric was misdesigned as eight disjoint networks with no common routing paths and had to be completely rewired. Imbue’s most operationally useful artifact is a lookup table mapping MFU signature to root cause: immediate <10% of expected throughput means a dead switch or a host stuck at PCIe x4; stable ~30% MFU means GDR misconfigured on a single host; stable 60–80% MFU means degraded IB links forcing NVLink fallback or CPU throttling; periodic ~10x dips every ~15 minutes mean other CPU-heavy workloads sharing a host or a dataloader bottleneck; gradual throughput decay across a run means unsynchronized Python garbage collection stalling a synchronous FSDP step across all workers. This table is worth reproducing wholesale as a diagnostic reference for any 100k-GPU on-call rotation, since it converts a throughput number alone into a short list of hypotheses before anyone touches py-spy.

Character.AI’s story is the inverse of every other lab surveyed in this beat: rather than assume adequate bandwidth and worry about failures, their largest pretraining cluster ran at “only one-quarter the bandwidth of state-of-the-art systems” blog.character.ai/squinch [OFFICIAL-BLOG] (2024), which directly motivated Squinch, a custom 6-bit block-wise gradient-compression scheme built specifically to make FSDP viable on a quarter-bandwidth fabric. For a 100k-GB200 build this is not the binding constraint (see Ch18), but the underlying discipline — profile the actual constraint you have, not the one the literature assumes — is the transferable lesson. Zyphra’s public “training cookbook” is candid that it discloses no GPU counts, no cluster topology, and no MFU numbers; its real content is a reusable annealing/LR-schedule playbook (Ch12 covers this in the curriculum context) built by a roughly seven-person team running Zamba/Zamba2 on 128 H100s over 30–50 days Zyphra Training Cookbook [OFFICIAL-BLOG/CODE] (2024) — a reminder that headline infrastructure disclosure and operationally useful disclosure are frequently two different documents from the same lab.

Yi Tay’s account of building Reka with fewer than five people on pretraining is the sharpest cross-cloud reliability claim in the corpus: a UL2-20B run at Google once completed a full month with zero infrastructure failures purely by accident, whereas “if this were in GPU land, it would have failed within the first few days for sure” — a claim he attributes explicitly to Google’s hardware-operations maturity rather than any fundamental TPU-vs-GPU silicon advantage yitay.net [OFFICIAL-BLOG] (3/2024). Databricks’ one disclosed DBRX war story reinforces the same lesson from the opposite direction: a misconfigured network driver caused a 5x slowdown on a 3,072-H100 cluster, caught only via monitoring granular enough to flag a non-crashing regression rather than a node death Introducing DBRX [OFFICIAL-BLOG] (3/2024) — the same MFU-signature-not-crash-signature diagnostic problem Imbue’s table is built to solve. Snowflake’s Arctic Cookbook quantifies a related, easy-to-underestimate cost center at MoE scale: before kernel-level optimization (RoPE fusion +5–8%, SwiGLU fusion +8%, a fused MoE-gating kernel 2.2x faster for top-2 routing), the baseline all-to-all communication overhead consumed more than 50% of step time on their 480B-total/17B-active model Snowflake Arctic Cookbook Series [OFFICIAL-BLOG] (2024) — a number worth holding up against Ch16’s parallelism math for the actual 10T/200B EP design. Apple’s AXLearn repository, finally, is the one place in this entire research corpus with a directly measured, apples-to-apples cross-silicon step-time table sitting in a code comment above a 7B model’s mesh_rules block — TPU v4, TPU v5e, TPU v5p, and H100 (AWS p5) timings for one fixed config github.com/apple/axlearn [CODE] (accessed 7/2026) — a level of disclosure no tech report or conference talk anywhere else in this fleet’s research matches, and a reminder that some of the most load-bearing operational facts live in engineering repos, not papers.

The Debugging Doctrine: Bekman’s Toolbox at 100k-GPU Scale

Stas Bekman’s two open books — the-art-of-debugging and ml-engineering, both actively maintained through the July 2026 commit window this research was gathered in — are the closest thing to a codified operational doctrine that this corpus contains, drawn from first-hand experience leading BLOOM-176B and IDEFICS-80B and current work at Snowflake AI Research on ArcticTraining. The core methodology is a five-step loop — reproduce reliably, shrink the payload, localize, get a real signal, change one thing — built on two compounding needs: debug cycles must be fast and the payload must be small, because slow cycles over large payloads cause an engineer to lose track of which hypothesis was actually tested the-art-of-debugging [CODE] (2023–2026). A companion instruction aimed explicitly at AI debugging agents — “observe before guessing”: obtain a stack dump, traceback, syscall trace, or tensor stat before proposing a root cause, never speculate from an error string alone — is worth adopting as literal on-call policy independent of who or what is doing the debugging.

The fleet-scale hang-forensics tool is py-spy, scaled through four explicit transports: single-process attach, multi-process fan-out on one node via pgrep chains, multi-node via SLURM srun (--gres=gpu:0 is load-bearing — omit it and the monitoring srun deadlocks waiting for the GPU allocation held by the job it’s trying to inspect), and pdsh/ds_ssh as SLURM-independent alternatives ml-engineering [CODE] (07/2026). Bekman’s own largest documented worked example caps at 40 nodes; there is no evidence in either repo of testing beyond that. This is a genuine, acknowledged blind spot relative to a 100k-GPU/12,500-node fleet. The srun --nodes=N --tasks-per-node=1 fan-out pattern is architecturally O(N) and SLURM is designed to handle multi-thousand-node launch trees, so the mechanism itself is unlikely to be the bottleneck — but aggregation and triage become the real problem: 12,500 trace-%N.out files are not human-grep-able the way 40-node examples are, and no first-party tooling exists anywhere in this corpus for automatically clustering stack traces by “same top frame” to spot the diagnostic signature of a genuine collective-comm deadlock (N-1 ranks stuck in the same NCCL call, one rank elsewhere). Building that aggregation layer — a py-spy-diff/histogram tool operating on tens of thousands of per-node dumps, not a human running grep — is squarely build-in-house territory for a 10T-MoE operation and belongs on the Ch22 gap list. A second, subtler trap sits inside the tool itself: two ranks stuck at different iterations of the same for loop report the identical line number in a py-spy dump, since it’s literally the same source line, making a genuine desync look like a uniform hang unless the loop is manually unrolled first (next(d_iter) called explicitly per iteration) so each step gets a distinct line. This is a real, non-obvious false negative in the exact “dump every rank, diff by line” diagnostic the doctrine itself prescribes as primary.

ml-engineering’s debug/pytorch.md (3,849 lines, now merged with the-art-of-debugging’s equivalent content as of a July 2026 consolidation commit) documents a catalog of silent, non-obvious performance and correctness bugs worth treating as a pre-flight checklist rather than tribal knowledge rediscovered under fire. Since SLURM 22.05, srun no longer inherits --cpus-per-task from sbatch/salloc — it must be re-passed explicitly or via SRUN_CPUS_PER_TASK, or DataLoader workers silently starve down to a single CPU core per task, a regression invisible to any GPU-side monitoring and testable with a one-line len(os.sched_getaffinity(0)) check. Separately, and orchestrator-agnostic: enabling Hyper-Threading on AWS p4 nodes made NCCL all-reduce throughput 4x slower, the opposite of Bekman’s experience on other clusters — the lesson generalizes to “test HT on vs. off empirically per instance-type/NIC-driver combination, never assume a default is correct” ml-engineering, orchestration/slurm/performance.md [CODE] (07/2026). A third catalog entry with real 10T-MoE relevance: all_gather_object (Python-pickle-based, CPU-mediated) is both a documented PyTorch memory leak and 23x slower than all_reduce for the common “is everyone done” flag-synchronization pattern used in fault-tolerance signaling — a double reason to ban it from any hot-path status-sync code (see Ch19). A fourth, purely empirical finding worth flagging for a coherent-memory GB200/GB300 fleet: nvidia-smi/NVML cannot report GPU memory usage at all on DGX Spark-class coherent-memory hardware, and by default Linux silently lets CPU allocations borrow unused GPU HBM in one direction only — undocumented behavior Bekman discovered empirically and reported to NVIDIA without resolution as of this writing, fixable only by forcing CDMM mode via a modprobe parameter and reboot.

Three purpose-built pre-flight test scripts recur across Bekman’s SLURM chapters and are worth lifting verbatim into any 100k-GPU admission-control gate rather than treating as reactive debug tools: a CUDA-availability check (torch.cuda.is_available() fanned out and reporting only failures), a “Broken NCCL” test (a minimal two-node NCCL barrier() wrapped in try/except with serialized per-rank output), and a GPU memory allocation check (attempt to allocate just under the card’s full capacity, catching allocation failures per-GPU before a real job starts). The explicit process note attached to all three — “report the faulty nodes so they get replaced,” since excluding a bad node from your own job is a workaround, not a fix — is a cheap organizational discipline worth codifying, especially given the SemiAnalysis-sourced burn-in data both repos cite: new accelerator batches show a 3–10% failure rate at delivery absent adequate burn-in, and accelerators account for roughly 95% of Bekman’s own observed hardware failures.

Scheduler and Ops Layer: SLURM, Kubernetes, and the FairShare Gap

Bekman’s stated opinion is unambiguous: “IMHO kubernetes is the wrong environment for doing training work,” citing loss of per-user Unix accountability, missing POSIX primitives, and higher operational headcount cost, while acknowledging SLURM-on-Kubernetes wrapper projects (SchedMD’s Slinky, Nebius’s Soperator, CoreWeave’s unreleased SUNK) as the emerging compromise for orgs forced onto K8s-based fleets ml-engineering [CODE] (07/2026). Most of the SLURM-specific operational patterns transfer conceptually even where the implementation does not: kill-switch/save-switch file-polling maps onto a K8s-native distributed KV-store flag; SIGUSR1-based graceful-preemption exit (with the crucial nuance that a B:-prefixed signal must route to the sbatch shell and be forwarded to the launcher’s children, since torchrun/accelerate/deepspeed don’t propagate SIGUSR1 by default) maps onto Kubernetes’ preStop hook plus terminationGracePeriodSeconds; automated benign-drain recovery and rolling maintenance reboots that respect running-job boundaries map onto a node-health-controller pattern. One SLURM pattern is strictly obsolete on K8s in a good way — SLURM’s crontab emulation (sbatch --begin=now+1hour self-resubmission, chosen specifically because neither SLURM nor most training clusters ship native cron) is fully subsumed by K8s’s first-class CronJob resource.

The one genuine capability gap is FairShare. SLURM’s sshare-reported score — above 0.5 meaning under-allocation-usage (higher scheduling priority, lower preemption risk), below 0.5 meaning the opposite, decaying over time, with relative rank mattering more than the absolute number — has no drop-in Kubernetes equivalent. Kueue and Volcano offer static priority classes, not a decaying-usage-based scheduling-priority algorithm; a multi-team 100k-GPU K8s fleet needs to source or build this capability explicitly, not assume it comes free with the orchestrator switch. This is worth flagging directly for Ch22, since it is one of the few items in this entire operational survey that is a genuine build-vs-buy gap rather than a reimplementation exercise ml-engineering, orchestration/slurm/users.md [CODE] (07/2026). On the hardware-diagnostic layer beneath either scheduler, nothing changes: Xid-error/DCGM RMA playbooks (dcgmi diag -r 2/3/4 to catch silent data corruption, GPU-UUID tracking to detect “same broken node again”) are vendor-specific, not orchestrator-specific, and Kubernetes’ own memory.oom.group=1 default (≥1.28) — which kills an entire pod’s process group on a single CPU-OOM’d process rather than just the offending rank, catastrophic for interactive debugging where the goal is recovering one crashed rank without losing the whole job’s logs — needs the singleProcessOOMKill: true kubelet flag (1.32+) set explicitly on any K8s-orchestrated 10T-MoE deployment ml-engineering, compute/accelerator/nvidia/debug.md + orchestration/kubernetes/README.md [CODE] (07/2026).

War-Room Design, On-Call, and the Six-Month Operational Readiness Checklist

Two independent teams working on completely unrelated infrastructure — Meta’s OPT team on cloud-provider hardware, BigScience’s BLOOM team on the Jean Zay HPC — converged on the identical solution to an identical constraint: neither SLURM cluster gave any concept of Unix-group job ownership, so a colleague’s hung, unattended job could only be killed by its owner or a sysadmin holding sudo, and both teams independently built a sentinel-file-triggered graceful-exit kill switch as the fix. That convergence is itself the strongest evidence in this corpus that this is a predictable, generalizable organizational failure mode for any large shared cluster — one to design for on day one rather than rediscover mid-incident. Both teams also made the cost of downtime an explicit, stated operational motivator rather than an implicit assumption: OPT’s on-call rotation of named volunteers paired with shadowing engineers was framed around “each hour the experiment stagnates or goes down costs us $$” OPT-175B chronicles, 56_percent_update.md [LOGBOOK] (12/16/2021). And a specific, recurring pattern across both logbooks — major incidents clustering around holidays and weekends when senior engineers are hardest to reach (OPT’s Thanksgiving and Christmas incidents, BLOOM’s weekend throughput hunt and overnight eval hang) — argues for treating holiday-period coverage as a first-class rotation-design input, not an afterthought, a lesson OLMo Hybrid’s own Dec 25–31, 2025 production run structurally echoed three years later.

Synthesizing the logbook practices that demonstrably worked, the debugging doctrine’s pre-flight discipline, and the ops-layer transfer analysis above into a readiness checklist for a six-month, 100,000-GPU run:

  • Indexed, queryable per-step telemetry from step 1 — BLOOM’s raw per-iteration scalar set (tokens consumed, LR, batch size, loss, grad norm, TFLOPs, skipped/NaN iteration counts) is still the right minimum schema; at 10T-token scale it must live in a time-series store, not a text log a human tails, because BLOOM’s own workflow of manually running perl one-liners over dumped stack traces is infeasible past a few hundred nodes.
  • Archive before/after checkpoints around every detected spike, not just the rolling checkpoint — BLOOM’s practice of preserving global_step31200/global_step31259 around its one spike cost negligible storage and would be the first artifact any root-cause investigation needs.
  • Automated hardware-fault recovery as a hard SLA (sub-5-minute), not a research target — the technology exists and is production-validated (ByteRobust, FT-HSDP, OLMo Hybrid); re-creating OPT’s or BLOOM’s manual-heroics era for this incident class is not acceptable at this scale.
  • A pre-authorized, bounded escalation path for numerics instability — automated spike suppression as the default first response, with an explicit, pre-agreed trigger (“N automated interventions across M checkpoints without convergence → architecture review”) so the org doesn’t re-litigate the decision live under a live incident, as Marin’s team was forced to.
  • A standing, cheap “does this look right” eval cadence faster than the full benchmark suite, specifically to shorten the detection lag on data-pipeline and silent-correctness-bug classes, which this entire corpus shows are still caught by a human noticing an anomaly, sometimes weeks later.
  • A byte-level checkpoint-diffing tool built and tested before it’s needed, not reinvented under fire — BLOOM’s custom weight-dump-and-diff instrumentation is the template.
  • The three orchestrator-agnostic pre-flight scripts (CUDA-available, NCCL-barrier, GPU-memory-headroom) wired into node admission control, gating every job allocation, not run reactively after a suspected bad node.
  • Imbue’s MFU-signature-to-root-cause table posted in the war room, since it converts a throughput number alone into a short, ranked hypothesis list before anyone opens a debugger.
  • A purpose-built aggregated stack-dump collector for hang forensics beyond ~100 nodes, since no lab in this corpus has open-sourced one and Bekman’s own tooling caps at 40 — build this before the first unexplained 12,500-node hang, not during it.
  • A kill-switch mechanism that works independent of who holds cluster-admin privilege, and a named on-call rotation with real cross-timezone and holiday-period backup coverage, with cost-of-downtime stated explicitly as the motivating metric.
  • A dated internal incident diary maintained as a living artifact from day one — every team in this corpus that kept one used it as its own best debugging tool during the run and its best postmortem material afterward; a 10T-parameter run producing no equivalent artifact discards this genre’s single highest-leverage lesson.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

The operational floor this chapter establishes is that hardware fault-tolerance — the incident class every 2021–2022 logbook treats as the dominant daily burden — is now a solved engineering problem at sub-5-minute recovery, and a 100k-GPU program that fails to hit that bar has under-invested relative to 2025–2026 production baselines (Ch19 carries the checkpoint and replication mechanics that make this possible). The unsolved floor is everywhere else: silent correctness bugs still require a human, days later, noticing something subtly off, at exactly the rate they did in 2021; numerics instability has an automated first line of defense but a hard ceiling that Marin’s 2025 experience shows can only be crossed by a pre-authorized architecture review, not more clipping; and fleet-scale hang forensics has no public precedent past 40 nodes, meaning the aggregation tooling a 100,000-GPU on-call rotation actually needs does not exist off the shelf and belongs on the build list in Ch22 alongside the FairShare-equivalent scheduling gap. None of this is exotic engineering — every individual piece (kill switches, pre-flight gates, spike-escalation triggers, an incident diary) is cheap and well-understood in isolation. What the logbook record shows, repeatedly and across labs with no contact with one another, is that the organizations who did this work before the run started paid a fraction of the cost of the organizations who improvised it during an active incident.

Chapter 21 · Part V — The Run

Benchmarks and the Money Math

Every other chapter in this book reasons from first principles — rooflines, formulas, architectural argument. This one exists because first principles alone cannot tell you what a training run actually costs: that requires audited throughput numbers from clusters that exist, not clusters that could exist. The public record for that is thinner than the industry’s marketing implies — one benchmark suite, a handful of vendor-published recipe tables, and a small set of primary-source cost analyses — but it is enough to build a defensible number. This chapter builds one, shows every step, and ends with the table a reader would need to defend simultaneously to a CFO asking “how much” and a chief scientist asking “why that MFU.”

MLPerf Training as the only audited ground truth

MLPerf Training’s large-language-model benchmark has been rebased twice in the window this book cares about. The v3.0 round (June 2023) debuted GPT-3-175B, with NVIDIA and CoreWeave setting the marquee record at 10.9 minutes on 3,584 H100 GPUs. v4.0 (June 2024) tripled submission scale to 11,616 H100 GPUs and cut time-to-train to 3.4 minutes. v4.1 (November 2024) brought the first Blackwell appearance, but only as a 64-GPU air-cooled HGX B200 preview entry — not the rack-scale GB200 NVL72 — reporting roughly 2x per-GPU gains over H100 on GPT-3-175B and 2.2x on Llama-2-70B-LoRA [OFFICIAL-BLOG][NVIDIA blog, Jun 2024; Blocks and Files, Nov 2024]. The pivotal change came in v5.0 (announced May 2025): GPT-3-175B was retired in favor of Llama-3.1-405B pretraining as the flagship large-model benchmark — AllenAI C4-en data, 8,192-token context (4x GPT-3’s), a fixed log-perplexity target of 5.6 rather than a fixed token count, the Mixtral-8x22B 32k-vocab tokenizer, NeMo Framework as reference implementation, median of three runs [OFFICIAL-BLOG][MLCommons, “MLCommons MLPerf Training Expands with Llama 3.1 405B,” May 2025]. v5.1 (November 2025) added GB300 NVL72 (Blackwell Ultra), the first NVFP4 MLPerf Training submission (with FP8 “healing” on final iterations to hit the accuracy target), and the first 800 Gb/s NIC platform (Quantum-X800 InfiniBand) to appear in the benchmark [OFFICIAL-BLOG][NVIDIA dev blog, Nov 2025].

One methodological wrinkle matters for everything that follows: because the benchmark trains to a fixed perplexity target rather than a fixed token count, tokens processed is not identical across submissions, and faster-converging recipes reach the target with fewer tokens. Any MFU back-calculated from an MLPerf wall-clock time inherits this ambiguity — it is a throughput number, not a pure FLOPs-utilization number, unless the submitter separately discloses tokens processed.

[OFFICIAL-BLOG][MLCommons; NVIDIA dev blog; CoreWeave blog — see Ch21 sources]

GB200 NVL72 submissions: a reproducible weak-scaling curve

The largest verified GB200 Llama-3.1-405B submission is CoreWeave + NVIDIA + IBM on the “Carina” cluster (39 racks, IBM-built): 2,496 GB200 GPUs, 27.33 minutes, v5.0, described as “34x larger than the only other cloud provider GB200 submission” [OFFICIAL-BLOG][CoreWeave blog, Jun 2025]. CoreWeave published the full scaling ladder, which is rare enough to be worth reproducing — most vendor scaling claims arrive as a single ratio, not a curve an outside reader can recompute:

efficiency(N) = ideal_time(N) / actual_time(N),  ideal_time(N) = T(512) × (512/N)

This reproduces CoreWeave’s stated “91% scaling efficiency from 512 to 2,496 GPUs” almost exactly [OFFICIAL-BLOG][CoreWeave blog, Jun 2025][CALCULATED] — a useful confirmation that the underlying numbers are internally consistent, and a genuine data point (not a marketing ratio) for what weak-scaling efficiency looks like on real NVL72 hardware in the 512–2,500 GPU range. At the same round, NVIDIA’s own 512-GPU Blackwell entry hit 121.09 minutes against Hopper’s 269.12 minutes at the same GPU count — a 2.2x generational gain — and NVIDIA separately states GB200 NVL72 delivers “up to 1,960 TFLOPS of training throughput” per GPU on this benchmark at 512-GPU scale [OFFICIAL-BLOG][NVIDIA dev blog, Jun 2025]. Dividing 1,960 by the dense-FP8 peak established below (5,000 TFLOPS/GPU) gives an implied 39.2% MFU for this specific submission — already a useful anchor before the more systematic derivation in the next section.

Software-only gains between rounds were large: the same GB200 hardware class went from 27.33 minutes (2,496 GPUs, May 2025) to 18.79 minutes (2,560 GPUs, Nov 2025) — a 45% time reduction in six months with almost no change in GPU count [OFFICIAL-BLOG][NVIDIA dev blog, Nov 2025]. This mirrors the H100-generation software-maturation curve documented independently by SemiAnalysis (below) and is the single best evidence in this corpus that a “bet-your-run” MFU number measured on day one of a new SKU understates what the same hardware will deliver a year later. v5.1’s max GB300 submission reached 5,120+ GPUs at 10 minutes — a new record — though the specific “85% scaling efficiency, 512→5,120” figure NVIDIA quotes for that round could not be independently re-derived from a full intermediate ladder in the source notes; treat it as vendor-reported, not re-verified [OFFICIAL-BLOG][NVIDIA dev blog, Nov 2025]. Lambda’s own v5.1 submission directly compared GB300 vs GB200 vs 64×B200 on Llama-2-70B-LoRA: 1.26 min (GB300) vs 1.598 min (best GB200, Oracle) vs 2.019 min (64×B200) — a 27% GB300-over-GB200 speedup, decomposed by Lambda as roughly 1.12x hardware, 1.13x software-stack, 1.13x FP4-vs-FP8 precision (these three factors multiply to ~1.43x, not the stated 1.27x — Lambda’s own attribution is reported here as stated, without reconciling the arithmetic) [OFFICIAL-BLOG][Lambda blog, Nov 2025].

Two absences round out the picture. AMD closed materially in v5.1: MI355X progressed from MI300X’s 27.95-minute Llama-2-70B-LoRA result through MI325X’s 21.05 minutes to 10.18 minutes, landing within 3–6% of NVIDIA’s B200/B300 on the same benchmark and within 5–6% on Llama-3.1-8B pretraining — the closest AMD has come to NVIDIA parity on a from-scratch MLPerf Training submission [OFFICIAL-BLOG][ROCm blog; AMD blog, Nov 2025]. AMD has never submitted a Llama-3.1-405B result. Google has never submitted a TPU result on Llama-3.1-405B through v5.1 — its competitive perf/$ claims against Blackwell (2.1–2.5x vs v5e/v5p, >50% MFU improvement from Trillium “host-offloading”) are self-reported MaxText benchmarks, not MLPerf-audited numbers, and no on-the-record explanation for the absence exists in the public record [OFFICIAL-BLOG][Google Cloud blog; cross-referenced via MLCommons/HPCwire coverage, 2025].

Deriving MFU: the vendor-published recipe tables, and a SKU trap worth naming

The most load-bearing MFU evidence in this beat is not MLPerf at all — it is NVIDIA’s own Megatron-Bridge performance-summary documentation, which publishes “Model TFLOP/sec/GPU” (actual forward+backward FLOPs, directly comparable to peak hardware FLOPs) across DGX-H100, DGX-B200, and DGX-GB200 for a battery of recipes including dense Llama-3/3.1 and MoE models (DeepSeek-V3, Qwen3-30B-A3B, Qwen3-235B-A22B) [OFFICIAL-DOCS][NVIDIA, docs.nvidia.com/nemo/megatron-bridge, accessed Jul 2026]. Computing MFU from this table requires the correct dense-FP8 peak denominator per SKU, and this is where a genuine trap lives in the public literature: NVIDIA’s own GB200 NVL72 product page and datasheet report 2:4 structured-sparse FLOPs without always labeling them as such, and a separate SKU — HGX B200 (air-cooled, 1,000W max TDP) — is routinely conflated with GB200 NVL72 (liquid-cooled, 1,200W max TDP) even though they are the same die at different power envelopes and genuinely different dense-FP8 peaks: 4,500 TFLOPS/GPU for HGX B200, 5,000 TFLOPS/GPU for GB200 NVL72 — a clean, uniform +11.1% across every tensor-core precision (5000/4500 = 2500/2250 = 10000/9000 = 10/9 exactly), confirmed against three independent NVIDIA properties plus a third-party technical wiki [OFFICIAL-DOCS][NVIDIA Blackwell datasheet #3384703, Dec 2024; NVIDIA GB200 NVL72 and DGX B200 product pages, verified 2026-07; Glenn K. Lockwood, glennklockwood.com, Apr 2026]. The trap is not hypothetical: at least one instance was found in this corpus of a downstream derivation using the HGX B200 figure (2,250 TFLOPS dense BF16) as the denominator for a GB200-labeled MFU calculation — turning a Megatron-Core-reported 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU sustained figure for DeepSeek-V3-685B-scale training into a claimed 46.6% BF16 MFU on GB200, when the corrected GB200-specific denominator (2,500 TFLOPS dense BF16) yields 41.9% — a ~10% relative overstatement from a single wrong SKU assumption (and if the underlying figure is actually FP8 rather than BF16, the corrected number falls further, to 21.0% against the 5,000 TFLOPS FP8 denominator) [PAPER][Megatron-Core MoE technical report, arXiv:2603.07685, Mar 2026][CALCULATED]. Every MFU number in this chapter uses the correct 5,000 TFLOPS/GPU dense-FP8 (2,500 TFLOPS dense-BF16) GB200 NVL72 peak; a reader importing a “B200” FLOPs figure from elsewhere in this book’s source material should confirm which SKU it actually describes before trusting it — see Ch16’s kernel-level MFU derivations for a second instance of the same hazard.

With that denominator fixed, NVIDIA’s own best-tuned recipes produce the single clearest empirical fact in this beat:

[OFFICIAL-DOCS][NVIDIA Megatron-Bridge performance-summary docs, accessed Jul 2026][CALCULATED]

Dense models cluster tightly at 32–39% MFU across three hardware generations; MoE models fall to 9–18% — roughly half to a third, and the gap holds regardless of accelerator generation, which rules out “the chip isn’t mature yet” as the explanation. The mechanism (interpretation, not a directly sourced claim) is arithmetic intensity: per-token compute in a top-k MoE layer scales with top_k × expert_hidden_dim, while per-token expert-parallel all-to-all traffic (dispatch + combine) scales with d_model × bytes_per_element, largely independent of expert count or width — so compute-to-communication ratio falls as tokens fan out across more, smaller experts, even holding total active parameters fixed. This is the single most load-bearing empirical fact for sizing a 2%-active 10T/200B MoE run, because DeepSeek-V3 (5.5% active) and Qwen3-235B-A22B (9.4% active) are both denser-active than the strawman in Ch5 — extrapolating the trend, a 2%-active model should land below both, not between them.

SemiAnalysis’s independently measured production economics corroborate the same gap from a different methodology entirely: DeepSeek-670B (MoE) achieved only 16.6% BF16 MFU on H100, against 54.5% for dense Llama3-405B on the identical hardware and framework family — attributed to massive dispatch/combine traffic and to MoE communication volume scaling with top_k (routing to 4 experts instead of 1 roughly quadruples comm volume) [SA-PAID][SemiAnalysis, “H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks,” Aug 2025]. On GB200 specifically, the same document tracked DeepSeek-670B’s MFU trajectory improving fast: GB200 delivered only 1.3x H100’s tok/s/GPU in May 2025 (not compelling — GB200 was actually 7% more expensive per token at that point, $0.684 vs $0.626/M tokens), reaching 2.5x by July 2025 ($0.307 vs $0.468/M tokens, a 1.5x perf/TCO advantage), with NVIDIA’s own software roadmap projecting 4.7x throughput and ~42% BF16 MFU by December 2025. The mechanism is GB200’s 72-GPU NVLink scale-up domain: 64-GPU all-to-all collectives run 18x faster than the same collective on an 8-GPU-domain H100 cluster — this is the entire reason GB200 wins specifically for MoE and is comparatively unremarkable for dense models [SA-PAID][SemiAnalysis, Aug 2025]. Full DeepSeek-670B pretrain cost on GB200: $4.5M as of July 2025, projected to fall to $2.5M by December 2025 as software matures.

DeepSeek-V3: reconstructing MFU from the paper’s own numbers, and debunking a myth

DeepSeek-V3’s technical report states 14.8T pretraining tokens, 37B active/671B total parameters, and 2.664M H800-GPU-hours specifically for the pretraining phase (on a 2,048-GPU cluster) [PAPER][DeepSeek-AI, arXiv:2412.19437, Dec 2024]. Using the standard FLOPs ≈ 6 × N_active × D_tokens approximation:

FLOPs_needed   = 6 × 37e9 × 14.8e12 = 3.286e24 FLOPs
FLOPs_available = 2,664,000 GPU-hrs × 3,600 s/hr × 1.979e15 FLOP/s  (H800 SXM FP8 dense peak)
               = 1.898e25 FLOPs
MFU = 3.286e24 / 1.898e25 = 17.3%

This lands within a point of the SemiAnalysis-measured 16.6% figure for DeepSeek-670B on H100 above, despite being a wholly independent reconstruction from a different primary source — real convergent evidence that MoE MFU in the mid-teens is the correct anchor for a ~5%-active model on Hopper-class hardware, not an artifact of one methodology. A widely circulating “~23%” figure for DeepSeek-V3 implicitly uses the PCIe H800 variant’s lower ~1.5 PFLOPS peak rather than the SXM 1.979 PFLOPS figure the cluster’s NVLink-domain topology almost certainly used; a separately-circulating “58% MFU” claim found in casual write-ups does not survive reconstruction from the paper’s own stated GPU-hours and token count and should be treated as a misreading, not a fact — worth flagging explicitly since it would badly distort any DeepSeek-V3-anchored Hopper-class MFU baseline if laundered forward [CALCULATED][CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, various, undated]. This chapter’s 17.3% uses the standard 6×N_active×D cross-model approximation for direct comparability against the dense models in the table above; Ch4’s own reconstruction, built from DeepSeek’s disclosed 250-GFLOPs/token hardware-reflections figure rather than the 6ND shortcut, lands slightly higher at FP8-normalized ≈19.5% (BF16-normalized ≈39.0%). Both are mid-teens-to-twenties FP8-MFU reconstructions, an order of magnitude from the debunked 58% figure; read 17.3% here as the standardized-methodology number used for cross-model comparison and Ch4’s 19.5% as the more precise single-model figure, not as competing claims.

Hopper-era dense baselines and the software-maturation curve

Dense-model MFU references anchor the optimistic end of the sensitivity bands used below. MegaScale (ByteDance, 175B dense, 12,288 Ampere-class GPUs) reported 55.2% MFU [PAPER][arXiv:2402.15627, NSDI’24, Feb 2024]. Llama-3-405B’s own paper states 38–43% overall BF16 MFU, with a Meta engineer separately tweeting 43% peak at 8K-GPU scale [PAPER][Meta, arXiv:2407.21783, Jul 2024]. SemiAnalysis’s own re-benchmark of the identical Llama-3-405B recipe roughly a year later, on NVIDIA’s own DGXC scripts across 576–2,304 H100 GPUs, found FP8 MFU ~43%, BF16 MFU ~54% — meaning software maturation pushed real-world MFU well above the as-shipped figure within about a year, at $1.95/M tokens and $29.1M total cost for the full 15T-token pretrain [SA-PAID][SemiAnalysis, Aug 2025].
