GPT-3-175B on a fixed 128×H100 cluster tracked the same curve even more starkly: BF16 MFU rose 34%→54% and FP8 29.5%→39.5% purely from software changes between January and December 2024, cutting FP8 cost per million tokens from 72¢ to 54.2¢ — no hardware change at all [SA-PAID][SemiAnalysis, Aug 2025]. One scale-sensitivity wrinkle worth carrying into any 100k-GPU extrapolation: Llama3-70B’s FP8 MFU degrades with scale (38.1% at 64 GPUs → 35.5% at 2,048 GPUs) while BF16 stays comparatively flat (54.5%→53.7%) — small-scale FP8 benchmarks should not be extrapolated naively to frontier cluster sizes.

[Sources per row, tags as cited above]

GB200’s reliability tax on realized economics

Raw MFU and its dollar translation are not the whole story: GB200 NVL72 is measurably less reliable per rack than H100, not more. Mean time between interruption runs 1,000–3,000 GPU-days on GB200 versus 2,000–5,000 GPU-days on H100, and the failure blast radius is 72 GPUs (a whole rack drained for a switch-tray or backplane-cartridge swap) against 8 GPUs on an H100/B200 HGX server; backplane cartridge swaps take 8–24 hours, and the Paladin HD copper connectors carry only ~200 mating cycles before replacement [SA-PAID][SemiAnalysis, Aug 2025 — full failure taxonomy and reliability-adjusted goodput math is Ch19’s territory]. Priced into a real workload, this materially erodes the headline perf/TCO advantage: SemiAnalysis’s own worked example for Llama4-Maverick-400B-MoE-style training found GB200’s raw 50%-cheaper-per-token advantage over H100 shrinks to 20–30% cheaper once the opportunity cost of 8-of-72 hot-spare GPUs and dedicated spare racks is charged against the run, and their explicit verdict — “the vanilla B200 is better performance-TCO than GB200 NVL72 for pretraining normal-sized models; as model sizes scale or reinforcement learning comes in, GB200 NVL72 will take a lead again” — is a load-bearing caveat for the reader’s specific case: a 10T/200B MoE at 100k-GPU scale is exactly the regime where NVL72’s reliability tax is worth paying, because it is precisely the scale-up domain that makes expert parallelism tractable at all (Ch16). GB200’s all-in capex-per-GPU runs 1.6–1.7x H100’s across buyer tiers, and total TCO ~1.6x — meaning GB200 needs at least that much aggregate throughput advantage just to break even before reliability is considered, a threshold SemiAnalysis dates as first crossed around July 2025 [SA-PAID][SemiAnalysis, Aug 2025].

What Epoch AI’s cross-checks add

Epoch AI’s public compute-estimation methodology gives useful outside calibration on how conservative or aggressive an assumed MFU should be: their historical compilation spans single-GPU CNNs at 30–75%, GPT-3’s original run at just 25%, GSPMD at 62%, Google LaMDA at 56.5%, with a stated default assumption of 0.3 for LLMs when utilization is unreported [PAPER/METHODOLOGY][Epoch AI, “Estimating training compute of deep learning models,” ongoing]. Their frontier-run FLOP estimates for the current generation — GPT-5 pretrain ~3e25 FLOP, Grok 4 ~5e26 FLOP (their largest confirmed run as of the most recent count), broad 2026 frontier runs in the 1e26–1e27 FLOP range — sit one to two orders of magnitude below the strawman’s own compute budget (computed below), consistent with a 10T/200B design being a genuinely frontier-scale undertaking rather than an incremental one [OFFICIAL-BLOG/SUBSTACK][Epoch AI, 2025–2026, medium confidence on the aggregated range]. Separately, and more structurally relevant to why this book plans around a 100,000-GPU instantaneous cluster rather than a smaller cluster run longer: Epoch AI’s data-insight on training-run duration finds frontier LLM training time has grown ~1.4x/year since 2020 across 38 frontier LLMs, and argues runs longer than roughly 8.6–9 months become economically suboptimal — missing out on hardware and algorithmic improvements a later, shorter run could capture — projecting the industry hits this ceiling around May 2027 (wide confidence interval, Aug 2025–Sep 2029) [OFFICIAL-BLOG][Epoch AI, 2025/2026]. Since training-run duration growth has historically contributed roughly a third of total compute-scaling progress since 2018, hitting that ceiling forces future scaling to come disproportionately from simultaneous GPU count rather than run length — a direct structural argument for the 100k-GPU target this book assumes throughout, independent of any MFU or cost argument. Worth noting for calendar-planning purposes: Epoch AI separately finds that a single final pretraining run’s GPU-hour cost systematically understates total program cost, since experimentation, ablations, and failed runs consume the majority of total compute budget in practice [OFFICIAL-BLOG][Epoch AI, Gradient Updates].

The money table

FLOPs per token, following the Ch5 strawman (N_active = 200B) and folding in the DeepSeek-V3-style single-MTP-module overhead (Ch7), conservatively estimated at +2% for a 60–120-layer model (MTP overhead scales roughly as 1/n_layers, so this shrinks further for a deeper design):

FLOPs/token ≈ 6 × N_active × (1 + MTP_overhead)
            ≈ 6 × 200e9 × 1.02
            ≈ 1.224e12 FLOPs/token

This omits the attention quadratic term (≈ 2 × 2 × n_layers × d_model × seq_len per token, forward+backward), a few percent of the total at 4–8K training sequence lengths but growing materially during any long-context curriculum stage (Ch12) — add it explicitly when modeling that phase.

Total FLOPs: 25T tokens → 1.224e12 × 25e12 = 3.06e25 FLOPs; 50T tokens → 6.12e25 FLOPs.

Cluster peak, 100,000 GB200 GPUs at the reconciled 5,000 TFLOPS/GPU dense-FP8 peak: 100,000 × 5e15 = 5e20 FLOP/s.

Two MFU bands frame the sensitivity: an “optimistic/dense-analog” band (25/35/45%) matching what NVIDIA’s own tables show for dense models, and a “MoE-realistic” band (10/15/20%) anchored to the actually-measured 9–18% MoE MFU above, pushed to the low end because 200B/10T = 2% active is sparser than any measured data point (DeepSeek-V3 at 5.5% active gets 18% on GB200; Qwen3-235B-A22B at 9.4% active gets 13.2%). The table below shows both days-to-token-target and dollar cost, bracketed across a $2.40–$5.00/GPU-hr range spanning a 25th-percentile-neocloud spot price ($2.40) through an illustrative GB300-equivalent-pricing upper bound ($5.00) [SA-PAID][SemiAnalysis, “How Much Do GPU Clusters Really Cost”]. Dating note: the source corpus itself disagrees on this report’s publication date — one research pass logs it as an Aug-2025 pricing snapshot (the origin of the $2.40 figure used here), while a later pass that read the actual archived PDF directly dates the document Apr 20, 2026, with matching goodput-expense figures (6.14%/10.53%/20.91%, used below). This chapter follows the primary-source Apr-2026 dating for citation purposes throughout but flags the conflict rather than silently resolving it, since the $2.40 pricing figure’s own within-document date could not be independently re-confirmed.

daily_$ = 100,000 GPUs × 24 hr × $/GPU-hr    (=$5.76M/day at $2.40/hr, $12.0M/day at $5.00/hr)
cost    = days(MFU, tokens) × daily_$

[CALCULATED, this chapter, consistent with the independently-published anchor figures in the source notes to within rounding]

Calendar-vs-compute adjustment. These are pure-compute floors; the calendar schedule is longer once goodput is accounted for. Two independent goodput analyses in the source corpus give complementary views. SemiAnalysis’s ClusterMAX “Goodput Expense” framework (based on a 5,184×GB300 NVL72 large-pretrain scenario, not identical scale/hardware to this book’s 100k-GB200 target, but the best available quantified real-world figure) gives Gold-tier 6.14%, Hyperscaler-tier 10.53%, Silver-tier 20.91% goodput expense — equivalently, multipliers of 1.065x, 1.118x, and 1.264x on pure-compute days [SA-PAID][SemiAnalysis, “How Much Do GPU Clusters Really Cost,” Apr 2026]:

Ch19’s independent, more granular reliability analysis — built from MTBI field data, a 100K-GPU HSDP production paper, and Google’s own Gemini goodput disclosures — targets 85–93% goodput for a well-engineered 100k-GB200, 10T-MoE run specifically (equivalently, 1.08x–1.18x calendar multipliers), which brackets the ClusterMAX Gold/Hyperscaler figures closely and treats Silver-tier’s 79%-effective outcome as below the bar a serious 100k-GPU operator should accept. The two methodologies converge: at realistic goodput, expect roughly 8–20% calendar overhead on top of the pure-compute floor, not the 2–3x sometimes assumed from early GB200-generation horror stories.

Implications for the 10T/200B run

The pure-FLOPs floor at 100k GB200 GPUs is genuinely not the binding constraint: even at the pessimistic 10% MoE-realistic MFU, 50T tokens costs 14.2 pure-compute days and $82–170M; at a more plausible 15–20% MFU once EP kernels and routing mature (Ch15–Ch17), that falls to 7–9.4 days and $41–113M. Three numbers should anchor any budget conversation. First, the MFU band to plan against is 10–20%, not the 32–39% dense figure a naive reading of vendor marketing would suggest — the sparsity penalty is real, measured across three hardware generations and multiple independent methodologies, and a 2%-active design should be modeled toward the low end of that band until EP all-to-all bandwidth (Ch16, Ch18) is proven out at 100k-GPU scale. Second, software maturation is worth a full MFU tier over the run’s lifetime — the GB200 generation improved 45% in wall-clock terms in six months on identical hardware, and DeepSeek-670B’s GB200 economics improved from marginally-worse-than-H100 to a 2.5x-and-climbing advantage in the same window, so the de-risking ladder (Ch7) should assume day-one MFU numbers are a floor, not a target. Third, the reliability tax is not a rounding error: GB200’s larger blast radius and lower MTBI relative to H100 erode a naive cost advantage by roughly a third in SemiAnalysis’s own worked example, and that erosion is the direct reason Ch19’s checkpoint and fault-tolerance architecture is not optional infrastructure but a load-bearing part of the money math itself. A defensible number for this specific run — the one to bring to both the CFO and the chief scientist — is: 7–10 calendar days and $55–115M in pure GPU-hour cost for a 50T-token run at a 15% blended MFU with Gold-tier goodput, with the explicit caveat that this is the training-compute line item alone, not the total program cost, which Epoch AI’s own findings suggest experimentation and failed runs will multiply by a factor this chapter does not attempt to bound.

Part VIThe Ledger

Chapter 22 · Part VI — The Ledger

The Gap List: What to Fix for 10T/200B on 100k GB200

Every system examined in this book has been validated somewhere between one and three orders of magnitude below the scale this chapter assumes. DeepSeek-V3 proved the FP8-blockwise-plus-DualPipe recipe at 2,048 GPUs; Kimi K2 proved MuonClip at 1.04T total parameters; Meta proved 90%+ goodput at 16,384 GPUs; OpenAI’s MRC has a stated theoretical target of 131,072 GPUs but production evidence at 75,000. None of that is a validated recipe for 10T total / 200B active on 100,000 GB200s — it is the nearest available evidence, and the distance between “nearest available evidence” and “the plan” is this chapter. What follows is organized by the seven systems a team actually has to stand up, in each case naming the gap, why it binds, the evidence pointer, whether to build or adopt, a rough effort class, and — the operative question for a team that has to start now — how many months of lead time it needs before the first training step.

The Training Codebase

Megatron-Core is the only framework with a primary-source MoE-at-scale paper current as of this writing — Scalable Training of Mixture-of-Experts Models with Megatron Core [PAPER, 03/2026] reports 1,233 TFLOPS/GPU (GB300) and 1,048 TFLOPS/GPU (GB200) for a DeepSeek-V3-685B-class model, and 974/919 TFLOPS/GPU for Qwen3-235B, both under force-balanced routing — and Ch17’s framework survey concludes it should be the base for a build at this scale. The base is not the gap. The gap is the modification list that stands between “Megatron-Core runs DeepSeek-V3-685B at reasonable MFU” and “Megatron-Core runs a 10T/200B model at 100,000 GPUs without EP dispatch eating the run.”

The single largest binding item is EP all-to-all overlap correctness and efficiency. Unoptimized EP all-to-all consumes 20–60% of total training time depending on whether the expert-parallel group stays inside an NVLink domain (~20%, DeepSeek-V3-shaped EP64 on GB200 NVL72) or crosses nodes (40–60%, the same config on H100 across 8 nodes) Scalable Training of Mixture-of-Experts Models with Megatron Core [PAPER, 03/2026]. At 100,000 GPUs a 10T/200B model’s EP group will by construction span far more than one NVL72 domain (Ch16 derives the parallelism layout in full), which puts the run in the expensive regime by default unless the dispatch kernel is genuinely tuned for the topology. Megatron-Core now ships two production dispatcher backends — DeepEP (Hopper-native, cross-node, MIT-licensed) and HybridEP (NVIDIA’s own kernel, tuned for GB200/GB300 Multi-Node NVLink, claimed comparable-or-higher bandwidth at lower SM usage) — confirmed directly in fused_a2a.py in the Megatron-LM repository [CODE, github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM, 2026]. Adopt both and validate the crossover point for the reader’s specific expert width and EP degree; do not assume either backend’s published numbers, which were measured at DeepSeek-V3/Qwen3 scale, transfer linearly to a 50x-sparser, 200B-active model.

MoE Parallel Folding — the architectural fix that decouples attention parallelism (TP×CP×DP×PP) from expert parallelism (ETP×EP×EDP×PP), breaking the old EP ≤ DP constraint — is already merged into megatron/core’s process-group management MoE Parallel Folding [PAPER, 04/2025]; this is load-bearing infrastructure, not a research proposal, and should be adopted rather than rebuilt. Two more recent, less-publicized production fixes close the remaining gap to full CUDA-Graph coverage for dropless MoE: ECHO (Elastic Cloning for Hot Experts, dynamically clones overloaded experts to spare slots via bin-packing, merged February 2026) and Paged Stashing (replaces per-layer worst-case activation buffers with one shared buffer plus paged storage, cutting CUDA-Graph memory from O(layers×worst-case) to O(worst-case+actual-total)) [CODE, github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM, PR #2368/#2690]. Both are already merged into dev — adopt rather than rebuild — but the framework’s own Q2-2026 roadmap still lists “sync-free, full-iteration CUDA-Graph MoE training” under long-term priorities [ROADMAP, github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM/issues/4815], meaning full end-to-end integration is not yet default/stable as of this writing; budget validation time before treating either as a turnkey flag.

Precision is a build item, not an adopt item, if the goal is MXFP8 across the full model. TorchTitan’s own 671B-class, 256×B200 reproduction got +41.0% throughput (651→918 tok/s) combining MXFP8 grouped-GEMM with DeepEP overlap — but explicitly could not apply MXFP8 to the dense (non-expert) linear layers because of a torch.compile+tensor-parallel incompatibility, so the 41% figure is expert-GEMM-only pytorch.org/blog, “Enabling up to 41% faster pre-training… MXFP8 and DeepEP” [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 03/2026, updated 05/2026]. Getting MXFP8 across 100% of layers — the version of the recipe Ch8 recommends betting the run on for GB200 — requires closing that torch.compile+TP gap, which is genuine kernel-integration work, not a config flag. Router and gate logits stay FP32; this is the one item on the list that costs nothing and is unambiguous — a naive --fp8-format hybrid MoE config with an unprotected router landed at val loss 7.71 (PPL 2222) vs a BF16 baseline’s 7.25 (PPL 1410) on Mixtral-8x7B, a 57% perplexity blowup traced directly to router precision [CODE, github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM/issues/1152]. Full precision recipe and the FP8/MXFP8/NVFP4 verdict are Ch8’s territory; here the point is narrower: the recipe Ch8 recommends is not yet a drop-in flag in the framework the team will actually run.

The optimizer is the largest open validation gap in the entire codebase list, not primarily an engineering one. Distributed Newton-Schulz for Muon is now solved-but-nontrivial engineering — NVIDIA’s Megatron-Core “Emerging-Optimizers” library ships three sharding modes (duplicated/distributed/blockwise NS) and measures Muon within 1–3% of AdamW throughput on GB300 NVL72 for Kimi-K2-shaped and Qwen3-30B-A3B-shaped configs [developer.nvidia.com, Megatron Core Emerging-Optimizers] [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2026] — but no optimizer, Muon or AdamW, has been publicly validated on a >100B-active-parameter MoE. DeepSeek-V4-Pro (49B active), Kimi K2 (32.6B active), and GLM-4.6 (32B active) are the largest confirmed data points; Llama 4 Behemoth targeted 288B active and never shipped. A 200B-active design is genuinely unprecedented optimizer territory, and this gap does not close by adopting a library — it closes by running the de-risking ladder Ch7 specifies and the next section of this chapter restates.

Checkpointing at this parameter count is a systems-build item with no off-the-shelf equivalent inside Megatron-Core today. NVIDIA’s own resiliency extension (nvrx) provides fault-tolerance and async/local checkpointing primitives but is, in its own documentation’s words, “infrastructure plumbing more than a benchmarked research contribution” [CODE, github.com/NVIDIA/nvidia-resiliency-ext] — it is not, out of the box, ByteRobust-grade in-memory peer replication with the measured 0.01–0.04s blocking stall ByteDance reports at 9,600–16,384-GPU scale ByteRobust [PAPER, 09/2025]. This item is covered in full under the Reliability Layer below, since it is fundamentally a checkpointing/fault-tolerance build, but it belongs on the codebase gap list too because it has to be built into the training loop, not bolted on afterward.

Finally, the framework’s own open issue tracker is a pre-flight test list, not background noise. As of this research, NCCL collective-order deadlocks in EP all-to-all overlap on uneven pipeline layouts are open (#5749, #1810), HybridEP RDMA queue-pair depth overflow at large batch×seqlen was found and fixed (#3999), and fault-tolerance/local-checkpoint corruption causing infinite restart loops under ft_launcher is open (#5281) [CODE, github.com/NVIDIA/Megatron-LM, verified 07/2026]. These are the concrete failure modes a 100,000-GPU shakedown run is most likely to hit first, and each is a targeted regression test to write before committing the schedule, not a surprise to discover during it.

The Scaling-Laws Program

Ch6 and Ch7 lay out the ladder recipe and its cost; the gap this section names is organizational, not technical — the ladder has to be budgeted and run before the architecture, optimizer, and precision recipe are frozen, and every source in this corpus that discloses numbers treats it as a large, non-optional line item rather than an afterthought. HuggingFace’s SmolLM3 ablations cost 161,280 GPU-hours against a final run of roughly 276,000 GPU-hours — about 58% [SmolLM3 ablations, HuggingFace] [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 2025]. Budgeting 30–60% of final-run compute for law-fitting and ablation is the empirical norm at labs that publish, not an edge case.

Three specific technical gaps sit inside that budget. First, muP/muTransfer is solid for width transfer and unreliable alone for depth, batch size, and training-horizon transfer, and is genuinely ambiguous for MoE fan-in (total vs. active parameters) — a live disagreement, with a Qwen-affiliated paper arguing muTransfer underperforms a directly-fitted LR(N,D) law once QK-Norm is present [arXiv:2601.05049] [PAPER, 01/2026] against Essential AI’s claim that muP transfer held for both LR and weight decay at 3.7B [arXiv:2505.02222] [PAPER]. A team building a 10T/200B host needs its own resolution of this disagreement at its own architecture before committing, not an import of either paper’s conclusion. Second, critical batch size is not well-predicted by the classical gradient-noise-scale estimator at LLM scale — Ai2’s “Critical Batch Size Revisited” found the noise-scale proxy off by orders of magnitude for OLMo 7B, and CBS instead has to be measured directly via branched short runs [arXiv:2505.23971] [PAPER, 05/2025]; skipping this and trusting a formula is a specific, named way to mis-budget the batch-size schedule. Third, MoE-specific scaling is now its own joint law rather than a dense law with a sparsity correction bolted on: the Ling team’s Efficiency Leverage law (300+ models to 28B) and the “comprehensive MoE law” (446 experiments) both find granularity and shared-expert ratio have a non-monotonic optimum that is roughly scale-independent, while activation ratio and efficiency leverage follow clean power laws that get sparser as N grows arXiv:2507.17702 [PAPER, 07/2025]; arXiv:2509.23678 [PAPER]. Ch5’s strawman spec already builds on this; the gap is running the granularity/shared-expert/activation-ratio sweep at the reader’s own d_model and expert width rather than importing Ant Group’s fitted optimum wholesale, since both source papers stop several orders of magnitude of compute short of a 10T/200B run (Ch5, Open Questions).

Two further items are cautions rather than build items, but belong on the checklist because they are easy to get wrong under schedule pressure. Precision-aware scaling laws disagree with each other on the numeric optimum — Kumar et al. put compute-optimal precision at roughly 7–8 bits, a competing Chinese-lab law (“Capybara”) puts the cost-optimal range at 4–8 bits with a critical-data-size effect beyond which more tokens actively hurt a low-precision model [arXiv:2411.04330] [PAPER, 11/2024]; [arXiv:2501.02423] [PAPER] — and both are fit at ≤1.7B params / ≤26B tokens, two-plus orders of magnitude below the target run; treat the qualitative direction as trustworthy and the specific bit-width coefficients as not (Ch8 reaches the same conclusion independently). Downstream-task scaling laws are unreliable far more often than pretraining-loss laws — an EMNLP-2025 meta-analysis found only 39% of published downstream scaling-law fits extrapolate reliably, and the Gemstones suite (4,000+ checkpoints, 10T+ cumulative tokens) shows scaling-law prescriptions are highly sensitive to which checkpoints and LR schedule they’re fit on [arXiv:2507.00885] [PAPER]; [arXiv:2502.06857] [PAPER]. A law fit once on pretraining loss and trusted for downstream capability commitments is a specific, documented failure mode, not a hypothetical one.

The sequencing implication is unambiguous: this program gates the architecture spec (Ch5), the optimizer choice (Ch7), and the precision recipe (Ch8), all of which need to be frozen well before the main run’s procurement and scheduling commitments are made. It has to start first, not run in parallel with codebase or cluster work.

The Data Factory

Ch10, Ch11, and Ch12 own the deep evidence on the crawl/curation/annotation/mixture stack; the gap-list question here is narrower — what has to be built, not what the corpus already establishes about the state of the art. Three items recur as genuine build gaps rather than adopt-and-configure items. First, the annotation/taxonomy layer that makes runtime curriculum control possible: Essential-Web v1.0 demonstrates the pattern (24T tokens, every document annotated with a 12-category taxonomy via a distilled 0.5B annotator model claimed within 3% agreement of a 32B-instruct judge) Essential-Web v1.0 [PAPER, 2025], but this is a pattern to reproduce against the reader’s own corpus and taxonomy, not a shared artifact to import — the annotator model itself has to be trained and validated on the specific document mix the run will draw from. Second, and this is the sharpest genuinely-unfilled gap found anywhere in the input-adjacent literature: no mainstream OSS dataloader — not Megatron-Core’s BlendedDataset, not Megatron-Energon, not MosaicML Streaming, not Grain — exposes a native filter/reweight-by-per-example-metadata API at load time. Every system applies the taxonomy filter once, upstream, producing separate per-category shard files, then blends at the source level (Ch11, Findings §17). For a 10T-run wanting genuinely adaptive, fine-grained-taxonomy-conditioned curricula rather than coarse pre-materialized-category blending, this is a build-it-yourself system, and the closest published architecture — ByteDance’s MegaScale-Data Planner — auto-scales mixing ratios at source granularity, not per-example predicates MegaScale-Data [PAPER, EuroSys 2026], so even the best available reference implementation would need extending, not just adopting. Third, the midtraining/anneal-phase mixture staging Ch12 specifies as a curriculum needs its filtered, weighted shard sets materialized and validated well ahead of the phase transition — this is a data-factory deliverable with a hard dependency on the curriculum plan being frozen, which in turn depends on the scaling-laws program above.

The remaining data-factory items — own-crawler infrastructure, CC-decline mitigation, licensing economics, dedup/classifier pipelines — are Ch10/Ch11’s core content and are adopt-grade rather than novel-build items once a curation stack is chosen; the binding constraint the reader should track from this chapter’s vantage point is lead time. Building or substantially extending a crawler, plus the legal/compliance review that accompanies it, is a 12-month-plus item independent of any GPU procurement timeline, and it has to start in parallel with the scaling-laws program, not after architecture is frozen, because the token-budget math (Ch10’s stock-vs-30-50T-budget accounting) constrains what architecture and training-token count are even feasible.

The Input Pipeline

Ch13 owns the full loader-requirements matrix; the headline finding worth restating here is that raw token ingestion is nowhere near the binding constraint. The compute ceiling for a 200B-active MoE caps aggregate ingestion at roughly 75–76M tokens/s cluster-wide, which at 4 bytes/token (int32, any modern >64K-vocab tokenizer) is only ~300MB/s aggregate — three to four orders of magnitude below what a single NVMe drive delivers (Ch11, Hard Numbers). The gap list here is therefore not “make the dataloader faster”; it is a short set of specific, evidenced failure modes that a naive deployment will hit.

Sequence packing is a solved problem and should simply be adopted: Best-fit Packing achieves 0.00063–0.0028% additional sequences versus naive concatenation at 2k–8k context — functionally zero waste — and OLMo-core’s Optimized-BFD gets packing time to O(N log L) with a 60% runtime reduction versus naive BFD at 1B-document scale Best-fit Packing [PAPER, ACL 2024]; [olmo-core.readthedocs.io] [OFFICIAL-DOCS]. Intra-document masking via cu_seqlens/varlen is likewise a first-class, adopt-grade code path: Megatron-Core’s GPTDatasetConfig.inter_document_masking emits cu_seqlens directly from the dataloader, and FlashAttention-4’s Blackwell-native backend is co-designed with exactly this load pattern in mind (Ch11 Findings §14; Ch15 covers the kernel side in full).

The genuine gap is multi-source workload imbalance, and it is quantified, not speculative: ByteDance’s MegaScale-Data measured per-microbatch FLOP imbalance up to 3.2× (image) and 6.9× (full sequence) across 8 ranks purely from non-uniform per-rank sample-length distribution in a multi-source job, invisible to a naive samples/sec metric MegaScale-Data [PAPER]. A 10T-run with a heterogeneous source mix (web, code, math, multilingual, long-context) and expert-parallel sharding is exactly the configuration this failure mode targets — an unlucky microbatch composition can stall an entire pipeline stage while other ranks idle, and no mainstream OSS loader (Megatron-Core, Streaming, Grain) implements the actor-model redesign MegaScale-Data uses to fix it. This is a build gap inherited from the data-factory section above, since fixing it requires either adopting and extending MegaScale-Data’s unreleased architecture or building an equivalent source-loader/data-constructor disaggregation.

Two smaller, cheap-to-fix items round out the list. Dataloader cold start can genuinely stall a 100,000-GPU job: Megatron-Core’s own documentation flags that index-map construction for large blends “can take several minutes and leave all GPUs idle,” mitigated by tools/prepare_cache.py and the --dataloader-fast-cache-load/--dataloader-defer-npy-index-mmap flags (Ch11 Findings §1) — cheap, adopt-grade, but easy to miss if nobody runs the offline cache build ahead of a billing-metered job start. And object-storage support is already native: Megatron-Core’s object_storage_utils.py supports direct S3/msc:// streaming for .bin/.idx shards, relevant for a 10T-run’s cold/archival tier (Ch11 Findings §1) — adopt, do not rebuild.

Cluster and Network

Ch14 and Ch18 carry the fabric and topology evidence in full; the gap this chapter names is that GB200 NVL72’s reliability and diagnostic maturity are, as of the most recent primary field data available in this corpus, not yet where a synchronous mega-training run needs them to be. SemiAnalysis’s field report found GB200 NVL72’s mean-time-between-interruption running 1,000–3,000 GPU-days versus H100’s 2,000–5,000 — GB200 racks fail roughly twice as often per GPU-day as H100 servers, even at the most-advanced-operator end — and the failure blast radius is the entire 72-GPU rack for a switch-tray or backplane-cartridge swap, versus 8 GPUs for an HGX server, because the NVLink domain is shared across the whole rack (Ch14; SemiAnalysis, “H100 vs GB200 NVL72 Training Benchmarks” [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY, 08/2025]). Diagnostic tooling — Nvloom, multi-node dcgmi diag, ubergemm — is explicitly described by operators as “behind and sub-optimal” for backplane-specific fault localization, and the Paladin HD copper connectors are rated for at most 200 mating cycles before requiring discard, a hard physical limit on how many times a given pairing can be serviced (same source). As of that report, no large-scale synchronous training run had completed on GB200 NVL72 at any frontier lab or CSP.

The direct consequence for a 100,000-GB200 procurement is that stricter-than-vendor-default ODM/OEM acceptance testing on every rack before it enters the fleet is not optional hygiene — it is SemiAnalysis’s own top recommendation, and it is the single highest-leverage gap-closing action available before the run starts, because every other reliability number in this section assumes the fleet has already survived burn-in. Budget for a first-3-to-6-month “tribal knowledge” tax on backplane/switch-tray fault diagnosis and staff a dedicated NVL72 hardware-diagnostics team ahead of the run, not reactively during it (Ch15’s Layer 0 makes the same recommendation from the reliability side).

Transport and topology, by contrast, are closer to adopt-grade than the hardware layer. MRC — the OpenAI/Microsoft/AMD/Broadcom/NVIDIA joint spec extending RoCEv2 with per-packet entropy-value spraying over static SRv6 source routing — is already in production on GB200 clusters including OCI Abilene and Microsoft’s Fairwater supercomputers, measured at 42,000-GPU NCCL scale (92GB/s send/recv, ~770Gb/s peak link bandwidth, 96% of theoretical) with a stated theoretical target of 131,072 GPUs Resilient AI Supercomputer Networking using MRC and SRv6 [PAPER, 05/2026]. NVIDIA’s own framing positions MRC as an alternative alongside Spectrum-X’s native adaptive RDMA on the same ConnectX SuperNIC/Spectrum-X silicon — both are adopt-grade choices for a fleet this size, and the decision is a procurement/vendor-fit question more than an engineering-risk one (Ch18 covers the transport comparison in full). NVL72-aware scheduling is likewise adopt-grade but non-optional: NVIDIA’s own public guidance states outright that “operating GB200 NVL72 clusters at scale requires new workload scheduling algorithms to treat NVLink domains as hard boundaries for jobs,” and the SLURM topology/block plugin co-developed with SchedMD (SLURM 23.11+) is the shipping answer [developer.nvidia.com] [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2025-2026] — a job that fragments across a partial NVL72 rack is a self-inflicted reliability and performance problem, and the fix is a scheduler configuration, not a research question.

Two structural topology choices carry direct evidence and should be treated as design requirements rather than options. Alibaba’s HPN 7.0 paper found single-ToR-switch failure to be the single largest network-reliability risk to an LLM training job, motivating a non-stacked dual-ToR design that cut single-link-failure degradation from a full job halt to 6.25% throughput loss [SIGCOMM’24 Qian et al.] [PAPER, 08/2024] — a finding independently consistent with MRC’s own multi-plane Clos design philosophy. And because backplane/switch-tray maintenance drains an entire 72-GPU NVL72 rack rather than a single server, the in-rack 8-of-72 hot-spare provisioning NVIDIA’s own rack design bakes in is necessary but not sufficient — operators need whole spare racks on top of it (Ch14; Ch15 §3). Finally, in-network reduction correctness needs independent verification rather than trust in standard health checks: a documented case had DCGMI diagnostics reporting healthy while an NVSwitch ALU silently corrupted AllReduce results (Ch14 §8, citing SemiAnalysis’s multi-datacenter-training analysis) — SHARP and other in-network compute paths should be canary-checked, not assumed correct because the surrounding hardware passed a standard diagnostic.

The Reliability Layer

Ch19 develops the full failure-rate tables, checkpoint math, and layered spec; this section names the specific build items a 10T/200B/100k-GB200 run cannot get by adopting off-the-shelf tooling alone. The scale of the problem is set by two numbers. First, the expected interruption rate: triangulating Llama 3’s own observed rate linearly scaled to 100,000 GPUs (~2/hour), SemiAnalysis’s field-derived MTBI ranges for H100 and GB200, and a 2026 production paper on 100K-GPU HSDP training that reports a failure every 18 minutes directly, the best single planning number is 1.5–4 unplanned interruptions per hour, 35–100 per day (Ch19 §4). Second, the checkpoint size: Adam-style state for 10T parameters is 140TB (20TB BF16 weights + 40TB FP32 master + 80TB Adam m/v), and a naive <60-second synchronous drain of that state implies roughly 2.3TB/s sustained aggregate write bandwidth — comparable in scale to Meta’s entire 7,500-server Tectonic fleet, built for a far smaller checkpoint (Ch11 §9; Ch19 §6).

No production system in this corpus actually tries to hit that 2.3TB/s number, and that convergence is itself the build target. VAST Data’s survey of 40 real production runs (85,000+ checkpoints, 18 clusters, 45B–1T+ parameter models) found actual global checkpoint-storage bandwidth demand is only 50–200GB/s, “well below 1TB/s even for trillion-parameter models,” because production systems write to local/peer NVMe first and drain to durable storage asynchronously over minutes rather than seconds [blog.vastdata.com] [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 2026]. ByteRobust achieves 0.01–0.04s per-step checkpoint blocking time via in-memory peer replication with backups placed outside a rank’s own parallel group, sustaining 99.11–99.71% MFU versus 39.84–43.07% for synchronous-remote-filesystem checkpointing [arXiv:2509.16293] [PAPER, 09/2025]. AWS’s December 2025 “checkpointless training” launch for SageMaker HyperPod goes further, recovering directly from peer GPU memory over EFA with no filesystem round-trip in the common case, cutting recovery from 15–30 minutes to under 2 minutes and reportedly used to train Amazon Nova [aws.amazon.com/blogs/aws] [OFFICIAL-BLOG, 12/2025]. None of this exists natively, at this level of sophistication, inside the framework the reader will actually run (the Training Codebase section above makes the same point from the other direction) — building a ByteRobust/AWS-checkpointless-grade peer-replication layer, with the local-first-then-async-drain architecture and cross-parallel-group replica placement both systems converge on independently, is the largest single build item in this entire chapter.

Silent data corruption is the second major gap, and it is unvalidated at this scale in a specific, documented way: every quantitative SDC study in this corpus (LLM-PRISM, “Understanding Silent Data Corruption in LLM Training,” the OCP whitepaper) is calibrated on ≤670B-parameter, ≤16K-GPU regimes, and the core finding — that loss/NaN monitoring alone is insufficient because a consistently-corrupting node can produce loss curves indistinguishable from healthy nodes while gradients diverge by several percent — was established at that smaller scale Understanding Silent Data Corruption in LLM Training [PAPER, 02/2025]. The only production-viable mitigation demonstrated is a shadow-replica gradient comparison during all-reduce, at roughly 2x compute cost for the shadow — a real, unavoidable trade-off decision the reader has to make without a directly-applicable precedent at 10T scale (Ch19 §2, §10). One genuinely useful, non-obvious mitigation costs nothing extra: FP8 training doubles as SDC insurance, because FP8’s narrow dynamic range forces faults to either saturate harmlessly or trigger a detectable NaN, essentially binarizing corruption outcomes, whereas BF16/FP16 permit large-but-finite silent corruption that NaN-checking never catches — a controlled fault-injection study found perplexity deviations up to 400–600% under BF16/FP16 that never triggered a crash [LLM-PRISM] [PAPER, 2026]. This is one more reason, independent of the throughput case in Ch8, to commit to FP8-or-lower as the default GEMM precision.

Finally, no validated elastic-pretraining path exists at frontier scale, and this constrains the fault-tolerance architecture the reader can actually build. Every production system surveyed — Llama 3, MegaScale/ByteRobust, FT-HSDP, Gemini — runs a fixed logical world size with hot-spare/warm-pool replacement, not elastic membership; torchft/DiLoCo and its 2026 successor ElasWave both work, but the largest publicly documented deployment is 300 GPUs (torchft) or 96 NPUs (ElasWave) — three to four orders of magnitude below the target scale [github.com/meta-pytorch/torchft] [CODE]; [arXiv:2510.00606] [PAPER, 10/2025]. The practical implication is that the reader should plan a fixed-world-size, warm-standby architecture — the FT-HSDP paper’s own demonstrated numbers (failure every 18 minutes at 100K GPUs, a naive synchronous restart leaving only 44% effective training time, its own scheme cutting the stall to ~3 minutes and reaching 80% effective training time with 11-of-12 healthy replicas) are the best available target to engineer against, not an elastic-membership system that has never run at more than a few hundred devices [arXiv:2602.00277] [PAPER, 02/2026].

Operations and Organization

Ch20 develops the incident taxonomy, debugging doctrine, pre-flight gauntlets, and war-room/on-call design in full; this chapter’s role is to state the organizational consequence of everything above. Three items above are, in practice, org decisions before they are engineering ones. The scaling-laws program’s 30–60%-of-final-run compute budget has to be approved as an org commitment 12-plus months out, not discovered as a cost overrun once ablations are already running. The dedicated NVL72 hardware-diagnostics team named under Cluster and Network is a staffing decision with the same lead time as the hardware procurement itself — a team assembled after racks arrive is already behind the “couple of hours to localize a fault, 8–24 hours to swap a backplane” timeline SemiAnalysis documents (Ch14; Ch15 §10). And the SDC shadow-replica decision under the Reliability Layer is a standing capacity commitment (roughly 2x compute for the shadow, if deployed continuously), which means it has to be sized into the cluster’s total GPU-hour budget rather than treated as a free software add-on.

The concrete pre-flight test list this chapter can add to Ch20’s gauntlet is the set of already-observed, named failure modes surfaced across the notes above: the open Megatron-LM EP-overlap deadlock and checkpoint-corruption issues (#5749, #1810, #5281), the documented NVSwitch-ALU silent-corruption case despite healthy DCGMI diagnostics, and the ByteDance straggler-accumulation case where progressive slow-chip contamination over roughly 11,500 steps dropped MFU from 40% to 30% — a 25% relative loss — entirely undetected by link-failure tooling (Ch14 §8, citing SemiAnalysis). Each of these is a specific, reproducible scenario a shakedown run should deliberately inject and verify recovery against before the real run’s clock starts, rather than a category of risk to acknowledge in the abstract.

The Master Checklist

The items above resolve into five lead-time bands. This is the version to pin to the wall.

T-14 to T-12 months — must start now, gates everything downstream:

  • Approve and staff the scaling-laws ladder as an org-level compute commitment (30–60% of final-run budget).
  • Begin or substantially extend own-crawler infrastructure and its legal/compliance review.
  • Assemble the dedicated NVL72 hardware-diagnostics team ahead of rack delivery.
  • Begin the distributed-Muon->100B-active de-risking ladder (Ch7); this and the scaling-laws program share critical path.
  • Begin design and staffing for the hierarchical peer-replication checkpoint stack (largest single build item in the chapter).

T-12 to T-9 months — architecture- and framework-dependent, needs the ladder’s early results:

  • Run muP-transfer completeness ablations (depth, batch, MoE fan-in) at target architecture.
  • Run direct critical-batch-size measurement via branched runs.
  • Sweep granularity/shared-expert-ratio/activation-ratio at target d_model and expert width (feeds Ch5 spec freeze).
  • Build the full-model MXFP8 kernel integration (torch.compile+TP gap).
  • Build the metadata-conditional load-time sampling layer, if adaptive curriculum is in scope.
  • Stand up strict rack-level acceptance testing as a standing process, not a one-time gate.
  • Decide and size the SDC shadow-replica detection scope.
  • Engineer the fixed-world-size warm-standby fault-tolerance architecture to the FT-HSDP demonstrated bar.

T-9 to T-6 months — integration and topology:

  • Tune EP dispatch kernels (DeepEP/HybridEP) for the target EP degree and expert width.
  • Build multi-source workload-imbalance mitigation for the dataloader.
  • Finalize transport choice (MRC vs. Spectrum-X adaptive RDMA) and dual-ToR/multi-plane topology.
  • Provision whole-rack spare capacity on top of in-rack hot spares.
  • Materialize midtraining/anneal mixture shard sets against the frozen curriculum plan.
  • Run the largest feasible scaling-law confirmation run.

T-6 to T-3 months — configuration and pre-flight:

  • Enable NVL72-domain-aware scheduling (SLURM topology plugin).
  • Adopt sequence packing and intra-document masking (cheap, should already be done, verify it is).
  • Build SHARP/in-network-reduction canary verification.
  • Inject and verify recovery against the named failure-mode scenarios (EP-overlap deadlocks, checkpoint-corruption loops, silent NVSwitch corruption, straggler accumulation).
  • Configure dataloader cold-start mitigations and object-storage native shard streaming.

Ongoing / day-0 and beyond:

  • Router/gate-logit FP32 discipline — non-negotiable, zero cost.
  • FP8-as-default-GEMM-precision, both for throughput and as SDC mitigation.
  • Continuous straggler detection and root-cause automation.
  • Target 85–93% goodput as the standing operational bar (Ch19 §10); anything below that on a sustained basis is a signal to re-open one of the gaps above, not to accept as the cost of scale.

Chapter 23 · Part VI — The Ledger

The Source Atlas: Annotated Bibliography and Surprise Index

Every claim in this book traces back to one of forty-two research notes, and every one of those notes traces back to a primary artifact — a paper, a repository, a technical report, a podcast transcript, a blog post written by a researcher who happened to say more than their employer’s PR team would have liked. This closing chapter is that trail made explicit: an index into the 42-file, roughly 332,000-word corpus this book draws on, an annotated bibliography organized by the topics Chapters 1–22 cover, a ranked catalogue of the corpus’s most surprising individual findings, and an honest accounting of what the research fleet could not pin down. Treat it as the book’s footnotes, collected in one place and made navigable, rather than as new argument.

Reading This Atlas

The provenance tags follow Chapter 1’s convention exactly: [PAPER], [OFFICIAL-BLOG], [CODE], [BENCHMARK], [TALK], [DOC], [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY], [RUMOR], with dates preserved from the underlying notes wherever the notes give one. A ★ marks the 29 items this research judges most likely to be new to a reader who has already worked through the DeepSeek V3/V4 reports, the MAI-1 paper, and the standard scaling-law canon — items buried a citation-hop deep, published in the last few months of this book’s research window, or sitting in a genre (a personal wiki, a maintainer’s scratch notes, an unpublished thesis) that doesn’t show up in a normal literature search. The Surprise Index that follows the bibliography is deliberately terse — one sentence, one pointer — because the underlying notes already argue each point at length; the pointer is the invitation to go read the argument in full.

The Corpus: Forty-Two Notes

The research fleet worked in two rounds. Round one (notes 01–23) covers the load-bearing frameworks, the DeepSeek and Chinese-lab reference stacks, the architecture and scaling-law literature, precision, networking, fault tolerance, benchmarks, and a first pass through Stas Bekman’s ml-engineering book. Round two (notes 30–53) fills the gaps round one’s own critique pass surfaced: a dedicated GB200-FLOPS reconciliation, multimodal integration, Baidu/PaddlePaddle, the Llama 4 Behemoth failure, Nemotron 3’s LatentMoE, three further Bekman-repo deep-dives, the companion the-art-of-debugging book, open-lab war stories, conference-archive mining, MoE systems papers, data-processing infrastructure, theory blogs, insider journalism, podcast mining, cluster reference architectures, non-Nvidia silicon, and historical training postmortems.

Annotated Bibliography

Epistemics, the Closed Labs, and the Provenance Discipline (Ch1–2)

  • Gangidi et al., “RDMA over Ethernet for Distributed AI Training at Meta Scale”, SIGCOMM 2024. [PAPER] The canonical Meta RoCE paper; superseded in scale (not in mechanism) by the @Scale 2025 recap below.
  • ★ Meta Engineering, “@Scale: Networking 2025” recap, engineering.fb.com, Sept 2025. [OFFICIAL-BLOG] Discloses the RoCE fabric’s growth to 129,000 GPUs — larger than the SIGCOMM’24 figure most readers have anchored on, and not yet reflected in most secondary coverage.
  • OpenAI et al., “Resilient AI Supercomputer Networking using MRC and SRv6”, arXiv:2605.04333, May 2026. [PAPER] The actual MRC primary source — not an OCP Summit talk, a 50-author engineering-blog-grade disclosure of OpenAI’s production fabric.
  • Epoch AI, notable_ai_models.csv, pulled July 12, 2026. [EPOCH-ESTIMATE] The field’s most methodologically transparent compute estimator — and, as of this pull, blind to Gemini 3 Pro, Claude Opus 4.x, and GPT-5.1.
  • Epoch AI, “Final training runs account for a minority of R&D compute spending”, fetched July 13, 2026. [EPOCH-ESTIMATE/ANALYSIS] The ~9.6%–22.6% final-run-share finding that reframes every “training cost” headline in the corpus.
  • Dwarkesh Podcast, “Jeff Dean and Noam Shazeer”, Feb 12, 2025. [SPOKEN-CLAIM] Confirms synchronous multi-metro pretraining is already routine at Google, gated purely by step-time budget.
  • SemiAnalysis, “Meta Superintelligence – Leadership, Compute, Talent, and Data”, Dylan Patel et al., July 11, 2025. [OFFICIAL-BLOG/PAID-NEWSLETTER] ★ Contains the Llama 4 Behemoth failure post-mortem; the fleet’s initial passes cited its title without reading the body, which turned out to be sitting in the fleet’s own local archive, misfiled among untitled HTML captures.

The Open Frontier: China’s Labs and DeepSeek (Ch3–4)

  • DeepSeek-AI, “DeepSeek-V3 Technical Report”, arXiv:2412.19437, Dec 2024. [PAPER] The reference stack this entire corpus benchmarks against.
  • DeepSeek-AI, “Insights into DeepSeek-V3: Scaling Challenges and Reflections on Hardware for AI Architectures”, arXiv:2505.09343, May 2025. [PAPER] ★ A wish list to NVIDIA/AMD that several vendors substantially granted within a year — the single most-skipped DeepSeek paper by readers who stop at the V3 report.
  • DeepSeek-AI, “DeepSeek-V4: Towards Highly Efficient Million-Token Context Intelligence”, arXiv:2606.19348, June 2026. [PAPER] Hybrid CSA/HCA attention, Muon, mHC — none of what the pre-release rumor mill predicted.
  • Moonshot AI, “Kimi K2: Open Agentic Intelligence”, arXiv:2507.20534, July 2025. [PAPER] The most infrastructurally transparent Chinese-lab report in the corpus.
  • Moonshot AI, checkpoint-engine, GitHub. [CODE] ★ Broadcasts a full 1T-parameter weight update across thousands of GPUs in roughly 20 seconds — a concrete number for an RL weight-sync problem most labs mention only qualitatively.
  • Ant Group/Ling Team, “Every Activation Boosted: Scaling General Reasoner to 1 Trillion Open Language Foundation”, arXiv:2510.22115, Oct 2025, and “Towards Greater Leverage”, arXiv:2507.17702, July 2025. [PAPER] ★ The richest infra disclosure in the whole Chinese-lab beat, including a full MFU waterfall (16.9%→32.8%) with each technique isolated.
  • MiniMax, “Why did M2 end up as a full attention model?”, Nov 2025. [OFFICIAL-BLOG] A rare, quantified, named postmortem on abandoning an architectural bet (linear attention) after shipping it.
  • ERNIE Team, Baidu, “ERNIE 4.5 Technical Report”, June 29, 2025. [PAPER] The one frontier Chinese lab not building on the Megatron-LM lineage; 47% MFU on 2,016 H800s via PaddlePaddle.
  • LMSYS, “Squeezing 1TB Model Rollout into a Single H200”, Jan 2026. [OFFICIAL-BLOG] Independent reproduction of Kimi K2 Thinking’s INT4 QAT within roughly two months of disclosure.

MoE Architecture, Scaling Laws, and Optimizer Theory (Ch5–7)

  • Elango et al., “LatentMoE: Toward Optimal Accuracy per FLOP and Parameter in Mixture of Experts”, arXiv:2601.18089, Jan 2026. [PAPER] ★ A full roofline derivation with named GB200 constants, sitting one citation-hop from the Megatron-Core MoE report and unread by any prior pass.
  • NVIDIA, “Nemotron 3 Nano/Super/Ultra” technical reports, Dec 2025–June 2026. [PAPER] ★ LatentMoE in actual production, plus two undiagnosed training-divergence incidents disclosed with MaxVio/residual-norm precursor signals — a genuinely actionable monitoring recipe.
  • Abnar et al. (Apple), “Parameters vs FLOPs: Scaling Laws for Optimal Sparsity for Mixture-of-Experts Language Models”, arXiv:2501.12370, 2025. [PAPER] Optimal sparsity trending toward 1, no observed reversal even at the lowest tested activation ratio.
  • StepFun, “Predictable Scale Part I: Step Law”, arXiv:2503.04715, and “Part II: Farseer”, arXiv:2506.10972. [PAPER] The largest public hyperparameter-scaling sweep to date, at roughly a third of DeepSeek-V3’s entire pretraining GPU-hour budget.
  • Su Jianlin, kexue.fm, ongoing. [BLOG] ★ Moonshot’s de facto public research notebook — QK-Clip’s MaxLogit mechanism, a reverse-engineering of DeepSeek-V4’s hash-routing table, months ahead of any English-language coverage.
  • Bernstein, “Modular Manifolds”, Thinking Machines Connectionism, Sept 26, 2025. [OFFICIAL-BLOG] ★ The direct theoretical continuation of Muon, constraining weight matrices to the Stiefel manifold.
  • Newhouse, “Duality, Weight Decay, and Metrized Deep Learning”, MIT MEng thesis, May 2025. [THESIS] ★ Unpublished-outside-MIT; quantifies Muon’s Newton-Schulz “noise floor” and how vacuous formal Lipschitz guarantees become at competitive model quality.
  • “Fantastic Pretraining Optimizers and Where to Find Them,” arXiv:2509.02046, Sept 2025, and its “Hyperball” rebuttal, arXiv:2606.16899, June 2026. [PAPER] An adversarial re-benchmark of nearly every optimizer-speedup claim in circulation, followed by a dated counter-rebuttal.

Precision and Numerics (Ch8)

  • NVIDIA, “Pretraining Large Language Models with NVFP4”, arXiv:2509.25149, Sept 2025. [PAPER] The reference 2-level-scaling, Random-Hadamard NVFP4 recipe, validated to 12B/10T tokens.
  • Kimi K2 team, community discussion, huggingface.co/moonshotai/Kimi-K2-Instruct/discussions/30. [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] Explicit rejection of FP8 pretraining compute for a 1T-parameter MoE after a stability study — the sharpest counter-example to “FP8 pretraining is solved.”
  • “Four Over Six,” arXiv:2512.02010, Dec 2025. [PAPER] Dual-scale NVFP4 fix closing 22% of the loss gap for under 15% overhead, validated on Nemotron-3-Nano.
  • ★ mufeezamjad, “NVFP4 Grouped GEMM”, 2026. [BLOG][CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] A 10x gap between naive and hand-optimized NVFP4 grouped GEMM on B200, with the exact architectural reason (TMEM capacity forcing 1 CTA/SM) spelled out — the sharpest public treatment of the “small-M problem” in the whole corpus.

Multimodal-Native Pretraining (Ch9)

  • Baidu, “ERNIE 5.0 Technical Report”, arXiv:2602.04705, Feb 2026. [PAPER] Explicitly reverses ERNIE 4.5’s own modality-isolated routing within eight months, naming the prior model as the design it’s abandoning.
  • Zhou et al. (Meta FAIR), “MoMa: Efficient Early-Fusion Pre-training with Mixture of Modality-Aware Experts”, arXiv:2407.21770, July 2024. [PAPER] The actual origin of modality-isolated MoE, predating ERNIE’s more commonly credited version.
  • DeepSeek-AI, “DeepSeek-OCR: Contexts Optical Compression”, arXiv:2510.18234, Oct 2025. [PAPER] Frames vision tokens as a general context-compression primitive, not an OCR trick — directly relevant to long-context token-budget planning.

The Data Supply Chain and Curation (Ch10–11)

  • Essential AI, “Essential-Web v1.0”, arXiv:2506.14111, June 2025. [PAPER] The reference curation architecture: 24T tokens, a 12-category taxonomy, an 0.5B distilled classifier.
  • ★ Li et al. (Apple/Stanford/UW), “Beyond a Single Extractor: Re-thinking HTML-to-Text Extraction for LLM Pretraining”, arXiv:2602.19548, Feb 2026. [PAPER] Only 39% of pages survive post-curation filtering across more than one extractor — extractors are complementary, not redundant, and running the union yields a 50–70% token-yield increase.
  • ★ Ma et al. (Shanghai AI Lab), “AICC: Parse HTML Finer, Make Models Better”, arXiv:2511.16397, Nov 2025. [PAPER] A 0.6B sequence-labeling extractor beating trafilatura by 18 ROUGE-F1 points.
  • ★ GAIR-NLP, “Data Darwinism Part II: DataEvolve,” arXiv:2603.14420, March 2026. [PAPER] An LLM literally writing and grading its own data-cleaning prompts across 30 generations — the most literal instantiation of “data agents” in the corpus, with a public repo and dataset.
  • ★ “Introspective X Training,” arXiv:2605.20285, May 2026. [PAPER] Metadata/feedback-conditioning experiments at 7.5–12B models over 18 trillion tokens seen — an order of magnitude past the technique’s earlier published scale, and almost certainly unseen by anyone on a pre-2026 reading list.
  • DatologyAI, “BeyondWeb”, Aug 2025. [OFFICIAL-BLOG] Real numbers from a paid vendor with zero independent replication found anywhere in this research — cite the direction, not the multiplier.

Midtraining and Curriculum (Ch12)

  • Allen Institute for AI, “2 OLMo 2 Furious”, arXiv:2501.00656, and “OLMo 3”, arXiv:2512.13961. [PAPER] The single richest primary sources in the beat — full Dolmino-mix tables, souping ablations, the special-chat-token GSM8K landmine.
  • Microsoft AI, “MAI-Thinking-1: Building a Hill-Climbing Machine”, June 2026. [OFFICIAL-BLOG] Explicitly excludes AI-generated text from its 30T-token base corpus — a live, frontier-scale disagreement with the DatologyAI/Nemotron synthetic-data-scaling consensus.

Dataloading and the Input Pipeline (Ch13)

  • ★ VAST Data, “Optimizing Checkpoint Bandwidth for LLM Training”, 2026. [OFFICIAL-BLOG, vendor] An 85,000-checkpoint, 40-production-run telemetry survey showing real global checkpoint bandwidth tops out at 50–200GB/s — a data point that should recalibrate any naive per-parameter checkpoint-storage sizing.
  • Li et al., “MegaScale-Data: Scaling Dataloader for Multisource Large Foundation Model Training”, EuroSys 2026. [PAPER] Documents a 3.2–6.9x per-microbatch FLOP imbalance from dataloader composition alone.

Hardware Ground Truth: GB200 and the Alternative-Silicon Question (Ch14)

  • NVIDIA “Blackwell” datasheet, ★ document #3384703, Dec 2024. [OFFICIAL-DOCS] The single authoritative side-by-side GB200 NVL72 vs. HGX B200 spec table (5,000 vs. 4,500 TFLOPS/GPU dense FP8; 186GB vs. 180GB HBM3e) that resolves a FLOPS conflation this book’s own research fleet found propagating into two of its own earlier notes; NVIDIA’s own CDN copy 403’d on direct fetch, so this points to the mirror the fleet actually retrieved and verified against.
  • ★ Glenn K. Lockwood, “NVIDIA B200”. [CREDIBLE-HEARSAY] An independently-compiled, footnoted, frequently-updated technical wiki that cross-validates cleanly against the official datasheet and is not indexed by generic tech-news search.
  • Jouppi et al. (Google), “Google’s Training Supercomputers from TPU v2 to Ironwood”, arXiv:2606.15870, June 2026. [PAPER] The single most quantified cross-generation hardware disclosure any lab has given the field, including the Ironwood Hardware Replay Unit’s zero-overhead SDC detection.
  • ★ Zyphra/AMD/IBM, “Training Foundation Models on a Full-Stack AMD Platform”, arXiv:2511.17127. [PAPER] By far the richest 2025–2026 AMD hard-numbers source, unusually candid about the platform’s own limitations.
  • AWS Neuron docs, Trainium2 and Trainium3 architecture pages, and AWS, “Announcing Neuron Agentic Development”, April 2026. [OFFICIAL-BLOG] AWS’s own written admission that its kernel language is still too hard for most ML engineers, three SDK generations in.

Kernels for Blackwell (Ch15)

  • Dao et al., “FlashAttention-4: Algorithm and Kernel Pipelining Co-Design for Asymmetric Hardware Scaling”, arXiv:2603.05451, March 2026. [PAPER] The primary FA4 source; verified in the corpus’s own code inspection to have a forward-only FP8 path as of July 2026.
  • DeepSeek-AI, DeepGEMM, DeepEP, and EPLB, GitHub. [CODE] The MoE-kernel reference implementations most Blackwell-generation frameworks build against.

Parallelism Math and Frameworks (Ch16–17)

  • Tazi, Mom, Zhao et al. (HuggingFace), the Ultra-Scale Playbook, 2025. [OFFICIAL-BLOG] Over 4,000 scaling experiments on up to 512 GPUs; the DeepSeek routing-locality constraint the scaling book’s own EP section omits.
  • ★ Arcee AI, “Trinity Large Technical Report”, arXiv:2602.17004, Feb 2026. [PAPER] An unusually candid production post-mortem — a forced MXFP8→BF16 fallback and six concurrent stability interventions on a real ~400B-total MoE, the single best “is this actually production grade” data point in the frameworks beat.
  • NVIDIA, “Scalable Training of Mixture-of-Experts Models with Megatron Core”, arXiv:2603.07685, March 2026. [PAPER] The canonical Megatron-Core MoE reference, superseding scattered blog posts.

Networking at 100k (Ch18)

  • Si et al. (Meta), “Collective Communication for 100k+ GPUs” (NCCLX), arXiv:2510.20171, Jan 2026. [PAPER] The richest source on production 100K+-GPU collectives engineering found anywhere in the corpus.
  • ★ arXiv, 2605.04333 — see the epistemics section above; also load-bearing here as the primary source for OpenAI’s actual scale-out design.
  • Qian et al. (Alibaba Cloud), “Alibaba HPN”, SIGCOMM 2024. [PAPER] Quantifies multi-chip chassis switches failing 3.77x more often than single-ASIC designs — the concrete reason the industry converged on single-ASIC fabrics.

Fault Tolerance and Checkpointing (Ch19)

  • ★ “Training LLMs with Fault Tolerant HSDP on 100,000 GPUs,” arXiv:2602.00277, Feb 2026. [PAPER] The single best-matched primary source for this beat, giving exact 100K-GPU failure rates and recovery-stall numbers; not yet widely cited elsewhere as of this writing.
  • Meta, “The Llama 3 Herd of Models”, arXiv:2407.21783, July 2024. [PAPER] The 419-unexpected-interruption/54-day ground truth every later fault-tolerance paper in the corpus benchmarks against.
  • ★ Hu et al. (Shanghai AI Laboratory), “Characterization of Large Language Model Development in the Datacenter”, NSDI 2024 (the Acme trace). [PAPER] Median job duration in a real frontier-adjacent datacenter is two minutes, not hours — 92.9% of jobs are tiny eval jobs, a scheduler-design fact easy to lose sight of while planning for the pretraining tail.
  • ★ Alibaba, “Boosting Large-Scale Parallel Training Efficiency with C4: A Communication-Driven Approach”, arXiv:2406.04594, Jun 2024 (venue/author list not independently confirmed in the research corpus). [PAPER] A 30x reduction in error-induced downtime (31.19%→1.16%) came almost entirely from cutting detection latency, not from any change in underlying hardware failure rate.
  • ★ “Lazarus: Resilient and Elastic Training of Mixture-of-Experts Models with Adaptive Expert Placement,” arXiv:2407.04656. [PAPER] 20–40-second failure-to-resume by exploiting MoE’s structurally redundant, independently-placeable experts — reliability engineering as an argument for MoE distinct from its usual compute-efficiency framing.

Operations, War Stories, and Historical Postmortems (Ch20)

  • Stas Bekman, ★ ml-engineering and ★ the-art-of-debugging, GitHub, both repos actively maintained through July 2026. [CODE/PRIMARY-SOURCE] The single densest practitioner-authored primary source in the entire corpus — a MAMF achievable-efficiency table, a fully-worked GB200 NVLink-C2C duplex measurement at 38% of spec, and (per its own maintainer runbook) evidence the book is itself agent-maintained.
  • Meta AI, “OPT-175B Logbook”, 2021–2022, and BigScience, “BLOOM chronicles”, 2022. [LOGBOOK] ★ The two canonical day-by-day training logbooks the entire genre is measured against — and, per note 53, a genre that has essentially vanished from frontier commercial labs since.
  • Imbue, “70B infrastructure”, June 2024. [OFFICIAL-BLOG] The most complete public “how do you actually bring up a 500-node IB fabric” gauntlet available, with exact MFU signatures per failure class.
  • Character.AI, “Squinch”, 2024. [OFFICIAL-BLOG] A rare admission of bandwidth-constrained (not reliability-constrained) training, and a custom 6-bit gradient compressor built specifically to make FSDP viable under that constraint.

Benchmarks and the Money Math (Ch21)

  • NVIDIA, Megatron-Bridge Performance Summary, 26.06 container. [OFFICIAL-DOCS] The primary source for the dense-vs-MoE MFU gap table — 32–39% dense vs. 9–18% MoE, same framework, same hardware, three GPU generations.
  • MLCommons, MLPerf Training v6.0 results, June 16, 2026. [BENCHMARK] The first standardized, third-party-audited large-MoE training benchmark — and the round where NVIDIA, in its own words, was “the only platform to submit on every test.”

The Surprise Index

The 36 individual findings across this corpus most likely to be new to a reader who has already internalized DeepSeek V3/V4 and the MAI-1 report, ranked roughly by how far they sit from the standard reading list rather than by topic.

  1. NVIDIA’s own GB200 NVL72 product page reports 2:4 structured-sparse FLOPS without labeling them as such — the scaling book that calls out this exact trick elsewhere doesn’t flag it for Blackwell. → 01-jax-scaling-book
  2. ECHO (Elastic Cloning for Hot Experts) dynamically clones overloaded experts to spare capacity slots specifically to make CUDA-Graph static buffers tractable for dropless MoE — merged into Megatron-Core in February 2026 and barely discussed outside its source paper. → 02-megatron-lm
  3. TorchTitan ships a dedicated HybridEPTokenDispatcher built for “GB200/NVLink72” in core, not an experiments folder. → 03-torchtitan
  4. MaxText’s own GPU support is frozen at a single "a3" (H100) entry as of June 2026 — zero A4X/GB200/B200 config entries, despite Google Cloud selling A4X since 2025. → 04-maxtext-tpu-stack
  5. DeepSeek’s V3 paper states its own comm budget could support 13 activated experts instead of the 8 it actually uses — the routing topology, not the token compute, is the binding constraint, and the lab deliberately leaves slack on the table. → 05-deepseek-stack
  6. Ling-1T’s MFU optimization waterfall (16.9%→32.8%) isolates each technique’s individual contribution — nearly unique in a corpus where most labs report only a single final number, if any. → 06-chinese-labs
  7. DeepSeek-V4 shipped with none of what the pre-release rumor mill predicted (no “Engram,” no “MODEL1”) — the real innovations are a CSA/HCA hybrid-compression attention scheme and Sinkhorn-projected hyper-connections. → 07-moe-architecture
  8. StepFun’s Step Law hyperparameter-scaling sweep cost roughly a third of DeepSeek-V3’s entire pretraining GPU-hour budget just to fit the laws — “fitting the ladder” is no longer meaningfully cheaper than training a serious mid-scale model. → 08-scaling-laws-modern
  9. NVIDIA’s NeMo Curator dedupes 1.96 trillion tokens in 30 minutes on 32 H100s — deduplication of an entire multi-trillion-token corpus is a same-afternoon operation, not a pipeline bottleneck. → 09-data-curation
  10. Common Crawl’s own bot is now blocked almost as often as OpenAI’s dedicated GPTBot (5.08% vs. 5.52% of DISALLOW rules) — “just use CC” no longer sidesteps the site-blocking fight it’s supposed to avoid. → 10-crawling-data-supply
  11. Best-Fit Packing’s padding overhead measures 0.0006%–0.003% — functionally zero, closing a “live tradeoff” most secondary literature still treats as open. → 11-dataloading-storage
  12. FlashAttention-4’s FP8 path is forward-only as of July 2026, verified directly in source (NotImplementedError) rather than inferred from documentation — anyone planning FP8 attention training on Blackwell needs a fallback. → 12-kernels-attention
  13. Kimi K2’s team explicitly rejected FP8 pretraining compute for a 1T-parameter MoE after a preliminary stability study — a named, citable counter-example to the “FP8 pretraining is fully solved” narrative most 2025–2026 commentary carries. → 13-precision-numerics
  14. Meta runs its entire 24K+-GPU RoCE backend with DCQCN turned off, relying purely on PFC plus receiver-driven admission control in the collective library itself — directly contradicting the “DCQCN is mandatory” assumption in most vendor documentation. → 14-networking-scale
  15. NVLink Paladin HD connectors are rated for roughly 200 mating cycles — a hard mechanical wear-out cap on how many times a GB200 NVL72 backplane can be serviced over a multi-year deployment. → 15-fault-tolerance
  16. The widely-circulated “58% MFU for DeepSeek-V3” claim does not survive reconstruction from the paper’s own stated GPU-hours and token count. → 16-mlperf-benchmarks
  17. Google’s April 2026 Virgo Network/TPU 8t-8i disclosure claims JAX+Pathways can orchestrate more than one million TPU chips as a single logical training cluster — a step-change in stated ambition three months old at the time of this research. → 17-frontier-lab-crumbs
  18. DeepSeek switched from AdamW (V3) to Muon (V4) between December 2024 and June 2026 — the strongest revealed-preference signal in the entire optimizer literature, from the lab with the best public track record for MoE stability. → 18-optimizers-stability
  19. Su Jianlin’s blog reverse-engineers DeepSeek-V4’s tid2eid hash-routing construction algorithm from a leaked checkpoint artifact — the earliest accessible technical description of that specific V4 design choice in any language. → 19-su-jianlin-theory
  20. Microsoft’s MAI-Thinking-1 (June 2026) actively filters AI-generated text out of its 30T-token base pretraining corpus at genuinely frontier scale (8,000 GB200s) — a live, unresolved strategic disagreement with the DatologyAI/Nemotron synthetic-data-scaling consensus. → 20-midtraining-curriculum
  21. A SemiAnalysis contractor spent roughly $10,000 in agentic (Claude/Codex) compute fuzzing NVIDIA’s closed-source ptxas compiler and found 40 distinct miscompiling programs in three days — one agent-discovered bug silently converts an atomic store into two non-atomic stores. → 21-local-semianalysis-pdfs
  22. AWS states outright that NVIDIA’s own GB200/InfiniBand backplane reliability saga — which took six to seven months and a firmware fix to resolve — directly motivated Trainium3’s redundant-lane, hot-swap-without-drain design. → 22-local-semianalysis-htmls
  23. Stas Bekman’s own nvbandwidth measurement on a DGX Station puts real-world NVLink-C2C duplex efficiency at 38% of the marketed 900GB/s spec, bottlenecked by Grace’s LPDDR5X bandwidth — not published anywhere by NVIDIA. → 23-stas-ml-engineering
  24. The JAX Scaling Book contradicts itself within the same document — its main FLOPS table and its own Quiz Question 6 cite two different Blackwell SKUs under one undifferentiated “B200” label, and the resulting number gets copy-pasted, uncorrected, into a second note in this same corpus. → 30-gb200-nvl72-peak-flops-reconciliation
  25. ERNIE 5.0 explicitly reverses ERNIE 4.5’s own modality-isolated MoE routing within eight months, naming its own prior model as the design it is moving away from. → 31-multimodal-native-pretraining-integration
  26. Baidu’s router-orthogonalization loss bypasses the ordinary loss function entirely, patching directly into the Adam update instead — “analogous to AdamW,” in the paper’s own words — so a naive reimplementation that just adds the term to the loss would get it measurably wrong. → 32-baidu-ernie-paddlepaddle-infra
  27. The single richest technical account of a frontier MoE pretrain failure sat unread in this research fleet’s own paid-subscriber archive for an entire prior research pass, misfiled as “paywalled” when the full post-mortem was always inside the free-preview boundary. → 33-llama4-behemoth-failure-postmortem
  28. NVIDIA’s own frontier-model technical reports disclose zero MFU, step time, or training-cluster GPU count for their main pretraining runs — a striking gap given how much operational detail (RL infrastructure failure rates down to the specific NCCL bug) they disclose elsewhere. → 34-nemotron-3-architecture-primary-source
  29. The standard all_reduce_bench.py busbw formula systematically undercounts bandwidth on SHARP/NVLS-active fabrics — anyone running this widely-recommended script on a fresh GB200 NVL72 cluster gets a pessimistic number relative to what’s actually being achieved. → 40-stas-network-comms-debug
  30. H100’s headline 989 TFLOPS bf16 spec appears to assume a 1830MHz clock, not the commonly-quoted 1980MHz boost clock — reverse-derived from first principles, unexplained anywhere in NVIDIA’s own materials. → 41-stas-performance-parallelism-dtype
  31. The ml-engineering book is itself agent-maintained — a maintainer-only runbook explicitly written “for a code agent” governs its own consistency checks, unit conventions, and cross-book content-diffing. → 42-stas-storage-fault-orchestration
  32. BLOOM-176B ran its entire 176-billion-parameter training run with CUDA_LAUNCH_BLOCKING=1 — fully synchronous CUDA — and measured zero throughput regression, a direct counterexample to the reflexive assumption that synchronous CUDA always costs performance. → 43-art-of-debugging
  33. Character.AI trained on a cluster running at one-quarter the network bandwidth of state-of-the-art systems and built a custom 6-bit gradient compressor (Squinch) specifically to make FSDP viable — the inverse of almost every other lab’s bandwidth-adequate, reliability-constrained story. → 44-open-lab-war-stories
  34. Meta’s production RoCE fabric has already reached 129,000 GPUs, per its own @Scale 2025 disclosure — larger than the 24,576-GPU figure fixed in most readers’ mental model from the well-cited SIGCOMM’24 paper, achieved without a from-scratch transport redesign. → 45-conference-archive-mining
  35. ByteDance’s COMET has been running in the company’s own ten-thousand-GPU production clusters since early 2025, saving “millions of GPU-hours” — and has no Blackwell backend as of its last public commit, a real gap between production deployment and hardware currency. → 46-moe-systems-papers
  36. A classifier-cascade labeling pass for a 100T-token corpus costs only 0.2–0.5% of the training FLOPs it feeds, by three independently converging estimation methods — “can we afford to label everything” should be permanently retired as a planning question. → 47-data-processing-infra

What We Could Not Verify

The honesty this book owes its reader runs in both directions: every load-bearing claim is tagged with its evidence tier, and every genuine gap the research fleet hit is worth stating as plainly as the findings themselves.

The current frontier is genuinely dark. No source anywhere in this corpus — not Epoch AI, not the insider-journalism layer, not any lab’s own disclosure — offers a FLOP estimate, GPU count, or duration for Gemini 3 Pro, any Claude Opus 4.x model, Claude Sonnet 4/4.5, or GPT-5.1 and its successors. Several of SemiAnalysis’s most consequential pieces for this book’s purposes — the Colossus 2 gigawatt-datacenter analysis, the fuller Meta Superintelligence progress update — remain title-confirmed but paywall-blocked in this pass.

DeepSeek-V4’s actual training footprint is undisclosed. No source confirms V4’s training hardware (H800, growing Ascend capacity, or some mix), exact parallelism degrees, cluster size, or GPU-hour cost — a sharper silence than V3’s unusually transparent report, and one the corpus can only infer around from the absence of Ascend-specific kernel code in DeepSeek’s public repositories.

Whether MoE genuinely wants a different pretraining data mixture than a compute-matched dense model remains an open, and consequential, question. No DeepSeek, Kimi, Qwen, Ling, or Hunyuan technical report claims a MoE-specific mixture, and no controlled ablation isolating this variable was found anywhere in the corpus — this may reflect genuine mixture-invariance, or it may simply reflect nobody having published the experiment.

No joint EP+TP+PP+FSDP optimal-split closed form exists for GB200’s hierarchical topology in either the JAX Scaling Book or the HuggingFace Ultra-Scale Playbook — both handle individual parallelism axes well, but neither derives a true joint optimum across all four simultaneously for a two-tier NVLink-domain/scale-out-fabric hierarchy. This is a genuine open engineering problem, not a reading gap, and it recurs as an explicitly-flagged hole across three independent notes in this corpus.

Achieved GEMM efficiency at real MoE training shapes, on GB200, in MXFP8 or NVFP4, at scale, does not appear anywhere in the public record this research could reach. Every measured-efficiency number found is either an isolated microbenchmark tile shape or an H800/Hopper-generation figure — a load-bearing gap for anyone sizing a 10T/200B-active precision strategy.

Whether SHARP in-network reduction is actually load-bearing in any confirmed 100K+-GPU frontier pretraining run, or is capability marketing that isn’t exercised in practice at that scale, is unconfirmed. NCCL 2.27’s SHARP support is real and shipped; a documented NVSwitch-ALU silent-corruption incident implies someone is exercising in-network ALUs enough to have found the failure mode — but attribution and scale remain unconfirmed.

No public source gives a validated hot-spare ratio for a 100K-GPU-class mega-cluster. The only concrete number found — a 2% vendor-floor recommendation for NVL72 — is not derived against any specific mean-time-between-interruption curve at that scale.

DatologyAI’s headline multipliers (7.7x–43x “compute-equivalent”) remain entirely vendor-self-reported, with no independent replication located anywhere in this research, across a paid-vendor incentive structure this book flags every time the numbers recur.

The training-logbook genre has essentially vanished from frontier commercial labs. No lab training at genuine 2025–2026 frontier scale publishes anything resembling OPT’s or BLOOM’s day-by-day diary — DeepSeek’s entire public disclosure on this axis is a one-sentence “zero loss spikes” claim. A reader wanting this book’s Chapter 20 texture for a 10T-scale run has, as of this writing, no frontier-scale precedent to read; only extrapolation from smaller, still-instructive, non-frontier runs.

No aggregated, fleet-wide stack-dump tooling for hang forensics exists past roughly 40 nodes anywhere in the public record, and SLURM’s FairShare scheduling algorithm has no drop-in Kubernetes-native equivalent — both confirmed gaps, not search failures, and both belong on the build list this book’s Chapter 22 costs out.

Implications for the 10T/200B Run

Read as a single object, this atlas argues for a specific discipline rather than a specific number: weight every claim in this book — and every claim a vendor, a competitor’s paper, or a conference talk hands you during the actual run — by the evidentiary route it traveled, not by how confidently it’s stated. The corpus’s own worst failure modes recur in a pattern worth internalizing before the run starts. A number gets copy-pasted from a vendor spec sheet without checking whether it’s dense or 2:4-sparse, and it silently halves or doubles every downstream roofline calculation built on it — this happened inside this book’s own research fleet, caught only by a dedicated reconciliation pass (Note 30) built specifically because the error had already propagated into two other notes. A single unsourced blog post fans out into apparent multi-outlet consensus within months (Chapter 1’s Gemini-3-on-TPU case is the cleanest example, but it is not the only one in this corpus). A vendor’s own most candid admissions — AWS on Trainium’s kernel-language gap, Meta on running without DCQCN, NVIDIA’s silence on its own frontier-model MFU — surface only in primary documents, never in secondary summaries of them. And the field’s best-evidenced numbers are reliably its most stale ones: DeepSeek’s technical reports are the gold standard for verifiability in this entire corpus precisely because DeepSeek is not the lab currently defining the capability frontier.

For a team actually building the 10T/200B run, the operational lesson is to build the equivalent of this atlas as a living document from day one, not a retrospective. Tag every internal number by its evidentiary route the way this book tags its external ones — a vendor benchmark, a paper’s stated methodology, an internal ablation, a rumor from a competitor’s ex-employee — and revisit the tags as new evidence arrives, exactly as Note 30 forced a correction two other notes had already made. Budget explicitly for the gaps this atlas surfaces rather than assuming they’ll resolve themselves before they matter: the joint EP+TP+PP+FSDP optimum, the achieved-MXFP8-at-MoE-shape number, the FairShare-equivalent for a Kubernetes fleet, and the fleet-scale hang-forensics tooling are all genuine, checkable absences in the public record as of this writing, and a program at this scale will need to derive, benchmark, or build each one internally rather than finding it published. The frontier lets very little slip, and what it does let slip has to be triangulated, dated, and dated again before it holds enough weight to bet a nine-figure training run on.

Appendix A

Master Hard-Numbers Table

Compiled from the “Hard numbers” sections of all 42 research-note files in pretraining-2026-research/notes/ (01–53). Each row carries: the value, its context, the citing source, a provenance tag (how confident/primary the sourcing is), and a date. Where the same fact appears in multiple notes, the best-sourced instance is kept and duplicates are dropped silently. Where notes genuinely disagree — different numbers for what should be the same quantity — both rows are kept and marked [CONFLICT].

Silicon note: notes/30-gb200-nvl72-peak-flops-reconciliation.md is treated as authoritative for all GB200 NVL72 / HGX B200 per-GPU peak-FLOPS figures; it reconciles the NVIDIA Blackwell datasheet against every other note’s derived numbers and finds them consistent to rounding except where flagged. Downstream MFU calculations elsewhere in the corpus that used a slightly-off denominator (e.g., 4,500 vs. the correct 5,000 TFLOPS/GPU dense FP8 for GB200 NVL72) are noted where material.

Provenance tags: PAPER (arXiv/peer-reviewed), OFFICIAL (vendor/lab blog, docs, product page, statement), CODE (repo/README/benchmark script), BENCHMARK (independent measured benchmark), DERIVED (calculated by a note’s author from other primary numbers), HEARSAY (secondary press/aggregation/paywalled analysis), SPOKEN (podcast/interview claim, unverified), LOGBOOK (primary training chronicle), THESIS, UNVERIFIED (explicitly flagged unconfirmed by the sourcing note).
